CuratorialText:
In the early 1940s, decades into his artistic career, Henri Matisse (1869–1954) turned his focus to a new medium: cut paper. 

He had become bedridden and unable to paint following an excruciating abdominal surgery, and cut paper allowed him to continue to create in his relatively sedentary state. Encouraged by his friend, the book publisher Tériade (the pseudonym of Stratis Eleftheriades), Matisse furthered his exploration of this new technique. He mined his memories of Parisian music halls, the circus, trips to Tahiti, folktales, and mythology, and produced a series of 20 cut-paper maquettes.
persona1_adapted:
In the early 1940s, Henri Matisse (1869–1954) had been making art for decades. Then he turned his focus to a new material: cut paper.

After a painful stomach surgery, Matisse was stuck in bed and could no longer paint. But cut paper let him keep making art while resting. His friend Tériade, a book publisher, encouraged him to explore this new method. Matisse looked back on memories of Paris music halls, the circus, trips to Tahiti, folktales, and myths. From these ideas, he created 20 small models made from cut paper.

CuratorialText: These vibrant original compositions were then faithfully reproduced using pochoir (stencil) and combined with an original text written by Matisse to form an unbound book, Jazz, that was published in 1947—when Matisse was 77 years old. Jazz caused an immediate international sensation and reinvigorated the aging artist, setting him on a new course of artistic discovery.
persona1_adapted: These bright, original artworks were carefully copied using pochoir, a stencil printing method. They were paired with text written by Matisse himself. Together, they formed an unbound book called *Jazz*, published in 1947 when Matisse was 77. *Jazz* caused an instant stir around the world. It gave the aging artist new energy and set him on a fresh path of artistic discovery.

CuratorialText: Matisse’s extraordinary images of Jazz are not only captivating and provocative in their own right but also reflect a practice devoted to the exploration of color and line. Throughout the artist’s decades-long career, he pushed color from the purely visual to the nearly transcendental and progressively simplified his line to the most elemental. But until his cut-outs, his color and line existed separately, one modifying the other but never fully unified—at least not to Matisse’s own satisfaction.
persona1_adapted: Matisse’s images in *Jazz* are striking and thought-provoking. They also show his lifelong focus on color and line. Over many decades, he pushed color beyond what we see, making it feel almost spiritual. He also simplified his line drawings to their most basic form. But before his cut-outs, color and line stayed separate. Each one changed the other, but they never fully came together—at least not in a way that satisfied Matisse.

CuratorialText: This exhibition marks the first time Matisse’s Jazz will be shown in its entirety at the Art Institute; the museum acquired it in 1948. One of the most important livres d’artiste (artist books) of the 20th century, Jazz joins over 50 works from the museum’s renowned collection of paintings, sculpture, drawings, prints, illustrated books, and textiles to showcase the famed artist’s commitment across his 50-year career to continual innovation and the expressive power of color and line.
persona1_adapted: This exhibition is the first time Matisse’s *Jazz* will be shown in full at the Art Institute. The museum bought it in 1948. *Jazz* is one of the most important artist books of the 1900s. An artist book is a work of art made in book form. The show also includes over 50 other works from the museum’s collection. These include paintings, sculptures, drawings, prints, illustrated books, and textiles. Together, they show how Matisse, over his 50-year career, always tried new things. They also highlight his powerful use of color and line.

CuratorialText: From 6th-century gilt bronze Buddhist sculpture through Joseon dynasty painting and white porcelain and contemporary paintings of the late 20th century, the works of art in this exhibition demonstrate the artistic legacy produced on the Korean peninsula over millennia.
persona1_adapted: This exhibition shows art from the Korean peninsula over many centuries. You will see gilt bronze Buddhist sculptures from the 500s. Gilt bronze is a metal coated with a thin layer of gold. The display also includes paintings and white porcelain from the Joseon dynasty. Finally, you can explore paintings from the late 1900s. Together, these works reveal Korea's long artistic legacy.

CuratorialText: Once privately held, these artworks now belong to the Korean people thanks to a single groundbreaking gift from the family of Lee Kun-Hee, late chairman of Samsung Group. In 2021 the family donated over 23,000 works to the Korean government for public audiences to study and appreciate in perpetuity. Lee Kun-Hee and his father, Lee Byung-Chull, collected exceptional and storied objects from throughout Korean history as a means of preserving and celebrating the nation’s cultural heritage. This exhibition marks the first time the works are on public display.
persona1_adapted: These artworks were once owned by a private family. Now they belong to the people of Korea. That’s because of a single, historic gift from the family of Lee Kun-Hee, the late chairman of Samsung Group. In 2021, the family donated over 23,000 works to the Korean government. The public can now study and enjoy them forever. Lee Kun-Hee and his father, Lee Byung-Chull, collected rare and important objects from throughout Korean history. They wanted to preserve and celebrate the nation’s cultural heritage. This exhibition is the first time the works are on public display.

CuratorialText: Among the 140 artworks on view—including modern and historical painting, ceramics, and objects made for Buddhist worship and scholarly study—22 are officially recognized as National Treasures or Treasures by the Korean government. These objects are recognized as remarkable examples of their type and distinguished for their exceptional historic, artistic, and academic value.
persona1_adapted: This exhibition has 140 artworks. They include modern and historical paintings, ceramics, and objects made for Buddhist worship and scholarly study. Of these, 22 are officially called National Treasures or Treasures by the Korean government. These objects are seen as outstanding examples of their kind. They are valued for their great historical, artistic, and academic importance.

CuratorialText: The largest Art Institute exhibition devoted to Korean art in four decades, Korean National Treasures: 2,000 Years of Art charts the ideas, values, and traditions that have shaped the country’s creative production, from the Three Kingdoms period (57 BCE–676 CE) through the 1900s, offering insights into these objects’ rich meanings across centuries.
persona1_adapted: This exhibition is the largest show of Korean art at the Art Institute in forty years. It is called *Korean National Treasures: 2,000 Years of Art*. The show explores the ideas, values, and traditions that have shaped Korean art. It covers art from the Three Kingdoms period (57 BCE–676 CE) all the way to the 1900s. The objects on display reveal their deep meanings across many centuries.

CuratorialText: Epic and intricate, monumental and meticulous—the paintings of Kashmir-raised, London-based artist Raqib Shaw offer fantastical meditations on identity, transformation, and the redemptive power of beauty.
persona1_adapted: Artist Raqib Shaw grew up in Kashmir and now lives in London. His paintings are both huge and full of tiny details. They explore big ideas about identity, change, and how beauty can heal.

CuratorialText: Born in Calcutta and raised in the verdant Himalayan mountains of Kashmir, Shaw draws deeply on the landscapes and memories of his early life, many of which were fractured by political upheaval. Forced to leave Kashmir as a teenager, he relocated first to New Delhi and later to London, where he studied at Central Saint Martins College of Art and Design. His work is influenced by a broad range of sources—Mughal and Persian miniatures, Renaissance altarpieces, Old Masters paintings, Japanese arts of the Momoyama period, Kashmiri and Urdu poetry, and Hindu and Western mythology—and yet his visual vocabulary and technique are all his own. His signature method involves applying an acrylic liner on gesso to create a golden line almost like the leading of a stained glass window. He then applies automobile enamel paints with needle-fine syringes and manipulates those with a porcupine quill, often adding glittering inset stones and baubles that further enhance the magical and dreamlike quality of the depicted scenes.
persona1_adapted:
Shaw was born in Calcutta and grew up in the green Himalayan mountains of Kashmir. The landscapes and memories of his early life deeply influence his work. Many of those memories were broken apart by political conflict. When he was a teenager, he had to leave Kashmir. He moved first to New Delhi and later to London. There, he studied at Central Saint Martins College of Art and Design.

His work is inspired by many different sources. These include Mughal and Persian miniature paintings, Renaissance altarpieces, Old Masters, Japanese art from the Momoyama period, Kashmiri and Urdu poetry, and Hindu and Western mythology. Even so, his own style and techniques are unique.

Shaw’s signature method starts with a layer of gesso, a type of primer. On top of that, he uses an acrylic liner to create a golden line, similar to the lead in a stained glass window. Then he applies car enamel paint using very thin syringes. He moves the paint around with a porcupine quill. Often, he adds shiny stones and small decorations. These details make his scenes feel magical and dreamlike.

CuratorialText: At the Art Institute, Shaw debuts his more-than 100-feet-wide, 21-panel Paradise Lost (2009–25), the artist’s most ambitious and personal project to date. This magnificent allegorical painting takes viewers on a spellbinding journey, from the nocturnal solitude of the artist’s childhood in Kashmir to the frenzied daylight of the art world and the West to finally a fragile, renewed dawn. Each panel is dense with symbolism: mythical beasts, anthropomorphic hybrids, collapsing kingdoms, and natural beauty in various states of transformation. Throughout, the work is dotted with images of the artist, sometimes as a humanoid creature with different animal heads, at another time as a monkey looking with awe at the gleaming edifices and the wealth of the West, and sometimes unambiguously in full human form seated on a bed of saffron under a blossoming cherry tree, lost deep in his thoughts
persona1_adapted:
At the Art Institute, Shaw presents his largest and most personal work yet. It is called *Paradise Lost* (2009–25). The painting is over 100 feet wide and has 21 panels. This big, story-like painting takes viewers on a journey. It starts with the quiet, lonely nights of Shaw’s childhood in Kashmir. Then it moves to the busy, bright days of the art world and the West. Finally, it shows a fragile, new dawn.

Each panel is full of symbols. You will see mythical beasts, mixed human-animal creatures, falling kingdoms, and natural beauty that is changing. Throughout the work, Shaw includes images of himself. Sometimes he looks like a human with different animal heads. Other times he is a monkey staring in wonder at the shiny buildings and wealth of the West. And sometimes he is clearly a human, sitting on a bed of saffron under a blooming cherry tree, lost in his thoughts.

CuratorialText: The work is not a direct retelling of Milton’s 17th-century poem Paradise Lost, but rather a reflection on the many paradises lost across a lifetime: childhood innocence, creative freedom, mental tranquility, cultural belonging. “This is not just my story,” Shaw explained. “It is the story of each of us, and the story of our times.”
persona1_adapted: This artwork does not directly retell John Milton’s 17th-century poem *Paradise Lost*. Instead, it reflects on the many kinds of paradise people lose over a lifetime. These include childhood innocence, creative freedom, mental peace, and a sense of belonging to a culture. “This is not just my story,” Shaw explained. “It is the story of each of us, and the story of our times.”

CuratorialText: While Shaw has previously shown the first chapter of Paradise Lost, this exhibition marks the first time all four chapters will be displayed, showcasing the fullest extent yet of this epic masterpiece. “This painting was made not only to document a life,” Shaw shared, “but to offer something to those who come after, to offer a space of reflection for emotional recognition and perhaps for quiet rebellion against forgetfulness. I do hope that this work invites the viewer to slow down and to look carefully and to feel without haste.”
persona1_adapted: Shaw has shown the first chapter of *Paradise Lost* before. But this exhibition is the first time all four chapters are on display. It shows the full scope of this epic masterpiece so far. “This painting was made not just to record a life,” Shaw said. “It was also made to give something to future generations. It offers a space for reflection, for emotional understanding, and maybe for quiet rebellion against being forgotten. I hope this work makes viewers slow down, look closely, and feel without rushing.”

CuratorialText: Exquisitely crafted and shrouded in mystery, Saint-Porchaire ceramics are among the rarest treasures of the French Renaissance.
persona1_adapted: Saint-Porchaire ceramics are beautifully made and full of mystery. They are some of the rarest treasures from the French Renaissance.

CuratorialText: Only around 70 examples are known to exist worldwide, and each one dazzles and intrigues with its refined and delicate modeling and astonishingly intricate decoration. Created between about 1520 and 1570 and closely associated with the royal courts of François I and Henri II, these extraordinary works bear the emblems of power, prestige, and desire—including the interlaced cipher of Henri II and his famed mistress, Diane de Poitiers.
persona1_adapted: Only about 70 of these objects are known to exist in the world. Each one amazes viewers with its fine, careful modeling and incredibly detailed decoration. Artists made them between about 1520 and 1570. These works are closely linked to the royal courts of King François I and King Henri II. They carry symbols of power, status, and desire. One example is the linked initials of Henri II and his famous mistress, Diane de Poitiers.

CuratorialText: Saint-Porchaire ceramics embody the sophistication and inventive spirit of Renaissance court culture. Fashioned from lead-glazed earthenware, their sculptural forms echo precious metalwork, while their ornamental patterns draw inspiration from textiles, bookbinding, and other luxury arts. Together, they express a unique and daring artistry, playful elegance, and refined taste at the height of French royal patronage.
persona1_adapted: Saint-Porchaire ceramics show the elegance and creative energy of Renaissance court life. These objects are made from lead-glazed earthenware, a type of pottery coated with a shiny finish. Their shapes look like fancy metalwork, and their decorative patterns come from fabrics, book covers, and other luxury items. Together, they reveal a bold and original style, playful beauty, and refined taste during the peak of French royal support for the arts.

CuratorialText: Beginning in March, the Art Institute will present an exceptional group of Saint-Porchaire ceramics from a private collection—most on public view for the first time since the late 1800s—in the Deering Family Galleries of Medieval and Renaissance Art, Arms, and Armor. These rare works will be shown alongside other notable examples of French Renaissance ceramics drawn from the Art Institute’s collection, including several recent acquisitions.
persona1_adapted: Starting in March, the Art Institute will show a special group of Saint-Porchaire ceramics from a private collection. Most of these pieces have not been seen by the public since the late 1800s. They will be on display in the Deering Family Galleries of Medieval and Renaissance Art, Arms, and Armor. These rare works will be shown next to other important French Renaissance ceramics from the Art Institute’s own collection. Some of these pieces were recently acquired.

CuratorialText: Installed in the intimate James W. and Marilynn Alsdorf Gallery, this focused presentation invites close encounters with these remarkable jewel-like objects, while its placement within the broader context of the Deering Family Galleries highlights the rich dialogue between ceramics and the metalwork, textiles, and decorative arts that flourished during the Renaissance.
persona1_adapted: This display is in the small James W. and Marilynn Alsdorf Gallery. It lets you get close to these tiny, jewel-like objects. The gallery sits inside the larger Deering Family Galleries. This location shows how ceramics, metalwork, textiles, and other decorative arts influenced each other during the Renaissance.

CuratorialText: Over a long and distinguished career, Japanese artist Hamanishi Katsunori (born 1949) has focused on making mezzotint prints, perhaps the most demanding of all print techniques.
persona1_adapted: Japanese artist Hamanishi Katsunori (born 1949) has spent his long and respected career making mezzotint prints. Mezzotint is a printmaking method that is very hard to do.

CuratorialText: Mezzotints are known for their dark and atmospheric appearance. This is because the starting point for any mezzotint is the creation of a roughened surface, which produces a solid black background when printed. To draw the image that emerges from this dark background, the artist uses a series of burnishers and scrapers; the deepest gouges print as white areas on the finished print.
persona1_adapted: Mezzotints are known for their dark, moody look. That’s because the artist starts by roughing up the surface of the printing plate. When printed, this rough surface creates a solid black background. To make the image appear from that darkness, the artist uses tools called burnishers and scrapers. The deepest scratches show up as white areas in the final print.

CuratorialText: This presentation includes earlier, smaller-format works done without color, as well as more recent larger work that boast many hues. Among the earlier monochromatic prints is Hamanishi’s 1997 Viva Chicago series, which focuses on the city’s beloved public monuments like the Picasso and Miró sculptures. As the artist recalled, “When I first came here, I was unprepared for the impact this city would leave on my mind, such a vivid impression etched deep in my memory… . This is my ode to Chicago.”
persona1_adapted: This exhibit shows both earlier, smaller black-and-white works and newer, larger pieces full of color. One of the early black-and-white prints is Hamanishi’s 1997 Viva Chicago series. It focuses on the city’s favorite public monuments, like the Picasso and Miró sculptures. The artist remembered, “When I first came here, I was unprepared for the impact this city would leave on my mind, such a vivid impression etched deep in my memory… . This is my ode to Chicago.”

CuratorialText: The exhibition also celebrates several important gifts to the museum. Hamanishi’s 2022 Four Seasons series—shown here for the first time—is a recent gift from the artist. Each work in the series is reminiscent of the panels of a folding screen and features seasonal floral imagery. Many works in the show come from the 2013 gift to the museum from the Ninion and Sheldon Landy Collection, which gave the Art Institute the largest collection of Hamanishi’s prints in the world.
persona1_adapted: This exhibition also honors several important gifts to the museum. Hamanishi’s 2022 Four Seasons series is shown here for the first time. It is a recent gift from the artist. Each work in the series looks like the panels of a folding screen. They show flowers and plants from different seasons. Many works in the show come from a 2013 gift. That gift came from the Ninion and Sheldon Landy Collection. It gave the Art Institute the largest collection of Hamanishi’s prints in the world.

CuratorialText:
“There is no work by Carroll Dunham that does not use drawing as a foundation.”  
—Carroll Dunham. Over the course of five decades, Dunham has engaged in wide-ranging formal and thematic experimentation across various media, yet his drawings represent a distinct, interconnected body of work. Dunham’s mature artistic career began in 1970s New York amid a scene dominated by Minimalist aesthetics. Using simple elements such as line, shape, and color, he made a tremendous impact with work that nevertheless signaled a return to subject matter. In the intervening decades, Dunham has continued to produce drawings that test, and ultimately collapse, the porous boundaries between abstraction and figuration: bodies shapeshift into buildings, trees pose like models, the cellular resembles the cosmic, and internal organs or private parts are made very public.
persona1_adapted:
“There is no work by Carroll Dunham that does not use drawing as a foundation.”  
—Carroll Dunham  

For over fifty years, Dunham has tried many different styles and materials in his art. But his drawings form a special, connected group of works. His career as an artist began in New York in the 1970s. At that time, many artists focused on simple, clean styles called Minimalism. Dunham used basic tools like lines, shapes, and colors. Even so, his work made a big impact because it brought back real subjects and stories. Over the years, Dunham has kept making drawings that blur the line between abstract art (which doesn’t show real objects) and figurative art (which does). In his work, bodies turn into buildings, trees stand like models, tiny cells look like the universe, and private body parts become very public.

CuratorialText: Working in series, Dunham methodically tests out every possible outcome of a drawing’s composition or content. This approach seems to spontaneously yield new imagery and ideas that link one body of work to the next. His influences are likewise wide-ranging—art history, philosophy, psychoanalysis, and evolutionary science as well as science fiction and comic books—all of which inform his explorations of the tension between male and female, nature and culture, self and other.
persona1_adapted: Dunham works in series. He carefully tests every possible outcome of a drawing’s layout or subject. This method seems to naturally create new images and ideas. These ideas connect one group of works to the next. His influences come from many places. They include art history, philosophy, and psychology. He also draws from science, science fiction, and comic books. All of these help him explore the tension between male and female, nature and culture, and self and others.

CuratorialText: Dunham foregrounds the development of his work by precisely dating each drawing. This practice is evidenced in the thousands of drawings he has organized into an archive, from which this show is derived. Developed in conversation with the artist, this comprehensive survey is the first museum presentation focused exclusively on Dunham’s drawings. Encompassing a 50-year period and featuring many drawings making their public debut, it celebrates the medium central to Dunham’s expansive practice.
persona1_adapted: Dunham shows how his art has changed over time by dating each drawing exactly. He has done this with thousands of drawings, which he keeps in an organized collection. This exhibition comes from that collection. Created with the artist’s help, this is the first museum show to focus only on Dunham’s drawings. It covers 50 years of his work and includes many drawings shown to the public for the first time. The show celebrates drawing, which is the key part of Dunham’s wide-ranging art.

CuratorialText: In fall of 2024, the Art Institute unveiled a newly imagined and installed gallery for the arts of Korea—our first space fully dedicated to this cultural region.
persona1_adapted: In fall 2024, the Art Institute opened a new gallery for Korean art. It is our first space fully focused on this culture.

CuratorialText: The new installation presents a wider range of objects than previous displays—extending from ceramics to textiles and painting and spanning 2,000 years from the Three Kingdoms period (about 57 BCE–676 CE) until today. Intentionally located between the Chinese and Japanese art galleries, the thoughtful display offers insight into how these artworks both reflect the religious and material culture of Korea and how they relate to the arts of Korea’s influential neighbors, China and Japan.
persona1_adapted: This new exhibit shows more types of objects than before. You will see ceramics, textiles, and paintings. The artworks span 2,000 years, from the Three Kingdoms period (about 57 BCE–676 CE) to today. The gallery sits between the Chinese and Japanese art rooms on purpose. This thoughtful setup helps visitors see how these artworks reflect Korea’s religious and material culture. It also shows how Korean art connects to the art of its powerful neighbors, China and Japan.

CuratorialText: Six themes introduce Korea’s long and rich history as well as its religious, political, and material cultures. The first section introduces objects that were made to help understand and spread the teachings of the Buddha. The centerpiece, an 18th-century Buddha statue, is on view for the first time since its acquisition after extensive conservation treatment. A section devoted to celadon and tea culture provides a glimpse into the practice of tea ceremonies during the Goryeo dynasty (918–1392), and objects grouped around the idea of symbolism illustrate how beautiful embellishments represent desired outcomes, such as a blissful marriage, career advancement, and long life.
persona1_adapted: Six themes explore Korea’s long and rich history, along with its religious, political, and material cultures. The first section shows objects made to help people understand and share the teachings of the Buddha. The main piece is an 18th-century Buddha statue. It is on display for the first time since the museum got it, after careful cleaning and repair. Another section focuses on celadon, a type of green-glazed pottery, and tea culture. It offers a look at how tea ceremonies were held during the Goryeo dynasty (918–1392). Objects grouped by the idea of symbolism show how beautiful decorations stand for things people wanted, like a happy marriage, a better career, and a long life.

CuratorialText: The remaining three sections focus on how material culture responded to a major ideological, political, and cultural shift during the Joseon dynasty (1392–1897) as evidenced in the creation of the earthy grey stoneware known as buncheong, elegant white porcelain, and scholars’ objects. The new installation also includes select contemporary works that meaningfully engage with traditional forms and materials.
persona1_adapted: The last three sections show how art and everyday objects changed during the Joseon dynasty (1392–1897). This was a time of major shifts in ideas, politics, and culture. We can see these changes in three types of objects: earthy grey stoneware called buncheong, elegant white porcelain, and items used by scholars. The new display also includes a few modern works that connect with these traditional forms and materials in meaningful ways.

CuratorialText: The transformed space explores aspects of life and the afterlife in the Nile Valley with the first new installation of works from the museum’s historic collection of ancient Egyptian art in a quarter-century. Striking artifacts—displayed along one wall of the gallery in a series of innovative cases that promote viewing from multiple vantage points—provide insight into the beliefs and practices of this illustrious North African culture.
persona1_adapted: The transformed gallery explores life and the afterlife in the Nile Valley. It features the first new display of works from the museum’s ancient Egyptian art collection in 25 years. Striking artifacts line one wall in special cases that let you view them from different angles. These objects reveal the beliefs and practices of this famous North African culture.

CuratorialText: Recurring themes throughout this fresh presentation of the collection consider the impact of Egypt’s natural environment, including the Nile River, on its visual culture; reveal the processes of ancient Egyptian artists; and explore the centrality of gods and goddesses to life (and death) along the Nile. Arresting sculptures, such as the statue of Shebenhor, reveal how ancient Egyptians chose to present themselves so that they would be remembered for eternity, while funerary works—such as a gilded funerary mask—unveil practices for preparing for and protecting oneself in the afterlife.
persona1_adapted:
This fresh look at the collection explores several big ideas. It shows how Egypt’s natural world, especially the Nile River, shaped its art. It also reveals how ancient Egyptian artists worked. And it looks at how gods and goddesses were central to life and death along the Nile.

Striking sculptures, like the statue of Shebenhor, show how Egyptians chose to be remembered forever. Funeral objects, such as a gold-covered funeral mask, reveal the practices they used to prepare for and protect themselves in the afterlife.

CuratorialText: For the first time in over 30 years, the Art Institute of Chicago unveils an elegant and thought-provoking new display of architectural fragments from buildings in and around Chicago. Since the 19th century, Chicago’s urban landscape has experienced continuous change. Over time, many  transformations of the city’s streets and neighborhoods have become invisible, preserved only in collective memory and archival photographs. Architectural fragments provide another avenue to understanding our urban past, including historical building practices and technologies, our dynamic communities, and evolving ways of life.
persona1_adapted: For the first time in over 30 years, the Art Institute of Chicago shows a new display of architectural pieces from buildings in and around Chicago. These pieces are elegant and make you think. Since the 1800s, Chicago’s cityscape has kept changing. Over time, many changes to the city’s streets and neighborhoods have become hidden. They survive only in shared memory and old photos. Architectural fragments offer another way to learn about our city’s past. They show us how buildings were made, how our communities changed, and how people’s ways of life evolved.

CuratorialText: In this new installation, sculptural elements from building facades and interiors are displayed in groups that allow visitors to learn about many architecturally or culturally significant structures and moments in Chicago. These range from celebrated buildings by well-known architects like Frank Lloyd Wright and Louis Sullivan to important local histories related to the South Side neighborhood of Bronzeville, labor, and preservation.
persona1_adapted: In this new exhibit, pieces from building fronts and insides are grouped together. They help visitors learn about important buildings and moments in Chicago. Some are famous structures by well-known architects like Frank Lloyd Wright and Louis Sullivan. Others tell local stories about the Bronzeville neighborhood, labor, and preservation.

CuratorialText: Featuring well-loved works from the permanent collection alongside important new acquisitions—such as a stained-glass window by Marion Mahony Griffin and a terracotta bust of an Egyptian pharaoh by Walter Bailey—this installation, designed by the Chicago-based practice Norman Kelley, offers dynamic encounters with the museum’s rich holdings of architectural fragments, an integral part of our shared material history.
persona1_adapted: This installation brings together favorite works from the museum’s collection and important new pieces. These include a stained-glass window by Marion Mahony Griffin and a terracotta bust of an Egyptian pharaoh by Walter Bailey. The design comes from the Chicago-based firm Norman Kelley. It offers exciting ways to explore the museum’s large collection of architectural fragments. These fragments are a key part of our shared material history.

CuratorialText: In the textiles of Southwest Asia and North Africa, embroidery is more than surface decoration. In this region, known as SWANA, artists have used, and continue to use, centuries-old embellishment techniques to transmit cultural expressions of identity, status, and long-held belief systems. Cultural groups throughout the region wear layers of dress and adornment to protect their bodies physically and spiritually. Garments and household items are ornamented with valuable materials that function as wearable wealth, including symbolic motifs and designs constructed with coins, thread, beads, amulets, ornaments, and jewelry.
persona1_adapted: In the textiles of Southwest Asia and North Africa, embroidery is more than just decoration. In this region, called SWANA, artists have used old techniques for centuries—and still use them today—to share cultural ideas about identity, status, and long-held beliefs. Different cultural groups in the area wear layers of clothing and accessories to protect their bodies and spirits. Clothes and household items are decorated with valuable materials that act like wearable wealth. These items include symbolic patterns and designs made with coins, thread, beads, amulets, ornaments, and jewelry.

CuratorialText: The vast area that spans from Morocco and Algeria in the west to Afghanistan and Uzbekistan in the east is connected by millennia-old trade networks. The works presented reveal pathways of deep cultural exchange shaped by diverse landscapes—from mountains and deserts to seas—along with the movement of nomadic peoples and the spread and interweaving of multiple religious traditions and urban fashions.
persona1_adapted: A huge region stretches from Morocco and Algeria in the west to Afghanistan and Uzbekistan in the east. For thousands of years, trade routes have linked these lands together. The artworks here show how cultures shared ideas across mountains, deserts, and seas. Nomadic people moved through these areas, and different religions and city styles mixed and blended along the way.

CuratorialText: Embroidered Traditions from Morocco to Afghanistan is the first exhibition at the Art Institute of Chicago devoted exclusively to the artistic and cultural traditions of SWANA and spectacularly showcases the region’s distinctive beauty and iconic visual expressions. Drawn primarily from the Art Institute’s rich collection of textiles, jewelry, and adornment, this display presents over 70 works from the 18th through 20th century—most of which have never been exhibited publicly—including four rare face veils on loan from a private collector based in Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.
persona1_adapted: Embroidered Traditions from Morocco to Afghanistan is the first show at the Art Institute of Chicago focused only on the art and culture of the SWANA region. It highlights the area’s unique beauty and famous visual styles. Most of the items come from the museum’s own large collection of textiles, jewelry, and body adornment. The exhibition includes over 70 works from the 1700s to the 1900s. Many of these have never been shown to the public before. Four rare face veils are also on display. They are on loan from a private collector who lives in Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.

CuratorialText: Among the many other highlights are elaborately decorated garments from Syria, Yemen, Niger, and Egypt; silver and coral “treasure necklaces” from Morocco’s Anti-Atlas Mountains; richly embroidered linen panels from the Art Institute’s exceptional collection of Ottoman Turkish textiles; and a wedding blanket embroidered by the Marsh Arab (Ahwari) peoples of Iraq.
persona1_adapted: Other highlights include decorated clothing from Syria, Yemen, Niger, and Egypt. There are silver and coral "treasure necklaces" from Morocco’s Anti-Atlas Mountains. You can also see richly embroidered linen panels from the Art Institute’s collection of Ottoman Turkish textiles. Finally, there is a wedding blanket embroidered by the Marsh Arab (Ahwari) people of Iraq.

CuratorialText: Barbara Hammer’s experimental film Sanctus depicts human bodies at risk. It features archival moving X-rays taken by doctor and filmmaker James Sibley Watson Jr. (1894–1982) of people making simple gestures and engaging in everyday activities: applying lipstick, drinking liquids, shaking hands, and shaving. Hammer rephotographed the original film and spliced the images together in a visceral montage set to overwhelming requiems by Neil B. Rolnick. She further transformed the X-rays with flashes of light, hand-painted colors, and other visual effects and by speeding up, looping, and reversing the movements. In presenting images captured by medical technology that uses large amounts of radiation, Hammer underscored her idea that “science advances through the demise of animal, including human, subjects in experiments” and that imaging, and even treatment, can cause harm.
persona1_adapted: Barbara Hammer’s experimental film *Sanctus* shows human bodies in danger. The film uses old moving X-rays taken by doctor and filmmaker James Sibley Watson Jr. (1894–1982). In these X-rays, people do simple things like putting on lipstick, drinking, shaking hands, and shaving. Hammer re-filmed the original movie and cut the images together in a powerful sequence set to sad requiem music by Neil B. Rolnick. She changed the X-rays even more by adding flashes of light, hand-painted colors, and other visual tricks. She also sped up, looped, and reversed the movements. By showing images made with medical technology that uses a lot of radiation, Hammer made her point clear: “science advances through the death of animal, including human, subjects in experiments.” She wanted viewers to understand that medical imaging and even treatment can cause harm.

CuratorialText: Sanctus emerged from Hammer’s initial engagement with medical themes, an interest incited in the early 1980s by the AIDS epidemic. Over the course of her 50-year career, Hammer worked in photography, 16mm film, and video with an unflinching focus on queer communities and on her encounters within the American medical system.
persona1_adapted: Sanctus came from Hammer’s first work on medical topics. Her interest in these themes started in the early 1980s because of the AIDS crisis. Over her 50-year career, Hammer used photography, 16mm film, and video. She focused directly on queer communities and her own experiences with the American medical system.

CuratorialText: Step back in time and immerse yourself in the spiritual, domestic, and chivalric worlds of the Middle Ages and Renaissance. The Deering Family Galleries of Medieval and Renaissance Art, Arms, and Armor present nearly 700 objects from the museum’s rich holdings of art from 1200 to 1600: monumental altarpieces, exquisite jewelry, and the beloved arms and armor collection.
persona1_adapted: Travel back in time and explore the spiritual, home, and knightly worlds of the Middle Ages and Renaissance. The Deering Family Galleries of Medieval and Renaissance Art, Arms, and Armor show nearly 700 objects from the museum’s collection of art from 1200 to 1600: large altarpieces, fine jewelry, and the popular arms and armor collection.

CuratorialText: The sumptuous display begins with a large room featuring architectural sculpture, treasury objects, and early painting that convey the austere sanctity of earlier medieval art. The centerpiece of the gallery is the meticulously conserved Ayala Altarpiece, commissioned in 1396 by the chancellor of Castile for his family’s funerary chapel in northern Spain. Spanning nearly 24 feet across and eight feet high, the painted wood altarpiece has undergone extensive treatment over the last three years and makes its restored debut in brilliant form. Two linked vaulted galleries showcase altarpieces and altarpiece fragments from Italy, Spain, and northern Europe, including the Art Institute favorite Saint George and the Dragon by Bernat Martorell. Each is displayed to suggest its original religious space, highlighting the church as a center of public life.
persona1_adapted: The exhibition opens with a large room filled with architectural carvings, treasury objects, and early paintings. These works show the serious and holy feeling of medieval art. The main piece is the Ayala Altarpiece, a painted wood artwork that was carefully restored. It was made in 1396 for a Spanish nobleman's family chapel. The altarpiece is almost 24 feet wide and eight feet tall. It took three years to restore, and now it looks bright and new. Two connected rooms with arched ceilings display altarpieces and altarpiece parts from Italy, Spain, and northern Europe. One highlight is Saint George and the Dragon by Bernat Martorell, a favorite at the Art Institute. Each piece is shown in a way that suggests its original church setting. This reminds us that the church was a center of public life.

CuratorialText: The spaces that follow are more intimate, focusing on the texture of late Gothic and Renaissance life. One gallery presents luxury goods and the accessories of feasting, while another displays works of art for the bedchambers of Tuscany’s merchant elite. Everyday objects from northern Europe, along with jewelry and items of personal display, complete the domestic picture of the period.
persona1_adapted: The next rooms feel more personal. They show what daily life was like in the late Gothic and Renaissance periods. One room displays fancy goods and items used for feasting. Another room shows art made for the bedrooms of wealthy Tuscan merchants. Everyday objects from northern Europe, along with jewelry and personal items, complete the picture of home life during this time.

CuratorialText: An elegant rotunda introduces the collection of arms and armor with equipment for the bodyguards of four rulers. From here, the space opens to a gallery filled with weaponry and armor dominated by two armored figures on horseback—one dressed for battle, the other for sport—and two armed and costumed figures engaged in foot combat. Finally, a gallery lined with cabinetry features the art of the sword, luxury firearms, and equipment for the hunt extending through the Baroque era.
persona1_adapted: A graceful round room welcomes you to the arms and armor collection. It shows equipment used by the bodyguards of four rulers. From there, the space opens into a gallery filled with weapons and armor. Two armored figures on horseback stand out—one is dressed for battle, the other for sport. Two more figures, armed and in costume, fight on foot. At the end, a gallery with cabinets displays the art of the sword, fancy firearms, and hunting gear from the Baroque period.

CuratorialText: The new installation expands the display of art of this fascinating period sixfold, premiering several recently conserved works and many significant new acquisitions—an elegant late Gothic breastplate and backplate, a rare 15th-century German portrait, and an outstanding group of 17th- and 18th-century swords. Subtle changes in the architecture of the galleries propel visitors through time and from religious to secular works, while digital labels provide an interactive multimedia experience. At the heart of this display are the gifts of major collectors whose donations serve as the collection’s foundation: the Ayala Altarpiece and Saint George and the Dragon from Charles Deering, Sienese and Florentine gold-ground paintings and early Northern works given by Martin Ryerson, a terracotta altarpiece by Florentine Benedetto Buglioni and a South German carved and painted Saint Catherine from Kate Buckingham, and George F. Harding’s impressive collection of arms and armor. All come together in the new Deering Family Galleries, offering a transporting experience to a time that continues to enchant.
persona1_adapted: The new installation shows six times more art from this fascinating period. It includes several recently cleaned works and many important new pieces—a fancy late Gothic chest and back plate, a rare 15th-century German portrait, and an outstanding group of swords from the 1600s and 1700s. Small changes in the gallery layout guide visitors through time, moving from religious art to everyday scenes. Digital labels offer an interactive multimedia experience. At the heart of this display are gifts from major collectors whose donations form the collection’s foundation: the Ayala Altarpiece and Saint George and the Dragon from Charles Deering; gold-backed paintings from Siena and Florence, plus early Northern works given by Martin Ryerson; a clay altarpiece by Florentine artist Benedetto Buglioni and a carved and painted Saint Catherine from South Germany, given by Kate Buckingham; and George F. Harding’s impressive collection of arms and armor. All come together in the new Deering Family Galleries, offering a journey to a time that still fascinates us today.

CuratorialText: Jill Mulleady paints by breathing new life into unseen histories. Drawing from art-historical, cinematic, and literary sources, her memorable images can haunt the viewer long after encountering them. This exhibition—Mulleady’s first solo show at a major museum—brings together approximately 20 paintings by the artist.
persona1_adapted: Jill Mulleady paints in a way that brings forgotten stories back to life. She takes ideas from art history, movies, and books. Her powerful images can stay with you long after you see them. This show is Mulleady’s first solo exhibition at a major museum. It features about 20 of her paintings.

CuratorialText: Mulleady uses various methods including staining, and at times incorporating materials such as copper, to achieve fragile, luminous surfaces that feel impossibly thin yet visually deep, holding tension between flatness and depth. Not limited to canvas or linen, she employs a range of supports, including glass and velvet. The resultant works balance different degrees of transparency and opacity and can be looked at and, sometimes, seen through.
persona1_adapted: Mulleady uses different methods to create her art. She stains surfaces and sometimes adds materials like copper. This makes her work look fragile and glowing. The surfaces feel impossibly thin but also deep. They create a tension between being flat and having depth. She doesn't just use canvas or linen. She also works on glass and velvet. Her finished pieces balance transparency and opacity. You can look at them, and sometimes you can see through them.

CuratorialText: Mulleady’s paintings often depict scenes that are enigmatic and dangerous, showing figures at risk or under threat—from appetite, predation, or compulsion. While these ambiguous scenes may imply narratives, they likewise refuse tidy resolutions. Her imagery draws actively on painting lineages—allegory and the Baroque, Symbolist unease, the grotesque—recasting genres and canonical images for the present. In contrast to the purity and rationality of 20th-century modernism, Mulleady prioritizes a tradition in which chaos, instinct, and history drive art making.
persona1_adapted: Mulleady’s paintings often show scenes that are mysterious and dangerous. The people in them face risks like hunger, being hunted, or strong urges. These unclear scenes might suggest a story, but they don’t give easy answers. Her art pulls from older painting styles—like allegory (using symbols to tell a story), the dramatic light of the Baroque, the uneasy feelings of Symbolism, and the strange, twisted look of the grotesque. She updates these old styles and famous images for today. Unlike the clean, logical art of the 1900s, Mulleady focuses on a tradition where chaos, instinct, and history drive the making of art.

CuratorialText: As Mulleady has said, she works with existing languages in order to invent a new one, and her staged tableaux are particularly resonant within the Art Institute galleries, as they rhyme with many works in the collection that have informed the artist.
persona1_adapted: Mulleady says she uses existing languages to create a new one. Her staged scenes feel especially meaningful in the Art Institute galleries. They connect to many works in the collection that have inspired her.

CuratorialText: After decades as a representational painter, in her seventies Alma Thomas turned to abstraction, creating shimmering, mosaic-like fields of color with rhythmic dabs of paint that were often inspired by forms from nature. The artist had been fascinated with space exploration since the late 1960s, and her later paintings often referenced America’s manned Apollo missions to the moon. Although she had never flown, Thomas began to paint as if she were in an airplane, capturing what she described as shifting patterns of light and streaks of color. “You look down on things,” she explained. “You streak through the clouds so fast… . You see only streaks of color.”
persona1_adapted:
Alma Thomas painted realistic art for decades. In her seventies, she switched to abstract art. She created bright, colorful paintings that looked like mosaics. She used small, rhythmic dabs of paint. Nature often inspired these patterns.

Thomas was fascinated by space exploration since the late 1960s. Her later paintings often referenced America’s Apollo missions to the moon. Even though she never flew, Thomas painted as if she were in an airplane. She captured what she called shifting patterns of light and streaks of color.

“You look down on things,” she explained. “You streak through the clouds so fast… . You see only streaks of color.”

CuratorialText: Starry Night and the Astronauts evokes the open expanse and celestial patterns of a night sky, but despite its narrative title, the work could also be read as an aerial view of a watery surface, playing with our sense of immersion within an otherwise flat picture plane. The viewer is immersed not only in the sense of organic expanse that this painting achieves, however, but also in an encounter with Thomas’s process: the surface here is clearly constructed stroke by stroke. Meanwhile, the glimpses of raw canvas between each primary-colored mark seem as vivid as the applied paint itself—almost as if the composition were backlit. Thomas relied on the enlivening properties of color throughout her late-blooming career. “Color is life,” she once proclaimed, “and light is the mother of color.” This painting was created in 1972, when the artist was eighty. In the same year, she became the first African American woman to receive a solo exhibition at a major art museum, the Whitney Museum of American Art in New York City.
persona1_adapted:
Starry Night and the Astronauts makes us think of a wide-open night sky filled with stars. But even though the title tells a story, the painting could also look like a view of water from above. It plays with how we see depth on a flat surface.

The painting pulls you in—not just with its wide, organic space, but also with the artist’s process. You can see that the surface was built stroke by stroke. Between each bright, primary-colored mark, you can spot bits of raw canvas. These gaps seem just as vivid as the paint itself, almost as if the painting is lit from behind.

Alma Thomas believed that color brings things to life. She said, “Color is life, and light is the mother of color.” She used this idea throughout her career, which took off later in her life.

This painting was made in 1972, when Thomas was eighty years old. That same year, she became the first African American woman to have a solo show at a major art museum—the Whitney Museum of American Art in New York City.

CuratorialText: Perhaps the most famous depiction of a bedroom in Western art history, this vibrant painting documents Vincent van Gogh’s sleeping quarters in his beloved “Yellow House” in Arles, France. The composition exists in three versions, the first of which Van Gogh conceived in October 1888, a month after he moved into the home. In a letter to his brother Theo, he described having painted “the walls pale lilac, the floor in a broken and faded red, the chairs and the bed chrome yellow, the pillows and the sheet very pale lemon green, the bedspread blood-red, the dressing table orange, the washbasin blue, the window green.” With its bold colors, thick and broken brushwork, and sharply receding lines, the picture might suggest a nervous energy. But the artist understood it as a calming and restful image.
persona1_adapted:
This is one of the most famous paintings of a bedroom in Western art. It shows Vincent van Gogh’s sleeping area in his “Yellow House” in Arles, France. Van Gogh made three versions of this scene. He painted the first one in October 1888, just a month after moving in.

In a letter to his brother Theo, Van Gogh described the room’s colors. He wrote that the walls were pale lilac, the floor was a faded red, and the chairs and bed were bright yellow. The pillows and sheet were light lemon green, the bedspread was blood-red, the dressing table was orange, the washbasin was blue, and the window was green.

The painting uses bold colors, thick and rough brushstrokes, and lines that seem to pull back sharply. This might make the room feel full of nervous energy. But Van Gogh saw it as a calm and restful scene.

CuratorialText: The painting in the Art Institute’s collection is Van Gogh’s second version of the scene, made nearly a year after the first, in September 1889. He produced a third, smaller version at the same time as a gift for his mother and sister.
persona1_adapted: This painting is Van Gogh’s second version of this scene. He made it in September 1889, almost a year after the first one. At the same time, he created a third, smaller version as a gift for his mother and sister.

CuratorialText: This meditating Buddha comes from the coastal town of Nagapattinam in southern India, which was, as a result of settlers from Srivijaya (Indonesia), one of the few places where Buddhism was still flourishing in the twelfth century. The Buddha—with his elongated earlobes, the wheel marks on his palms, the urna between his brows, and the cranial protuberance covered with snail-shell curls—is seated in the posture of meditation, with his hands resting on his lap (dhyanamudra), wearing a seemingly diaphanous monastic garment. As in other images from Nagapattinam, a flame emerges out of the Buddha’s cranial protuberance, probably signifying wisdom. This monumental granite sculpture originally would have graced a monastic site at Nagapattinam, which is also well known for its Buddhist bronzes. The Tamil inscription covering its back is no longer legible.
persona1_adapted:
This meditating Buddha comes from the coastal town of Nagapattinam in southern India. Because of settlers from Srivijaya (in modern-day Indonesia), it was one of the few places where Buddhism was still thriving in the 1100s.

The Buddha has several special features. His earlobes are long. There are wheel marks on his palms. He has a dot (called an urna) between his eyebrows. On top of his head is a bump covered with curls that look like snail shells. He sits in a meditation pose, with his hands resting on his lap. This hand position is called dhyanamudra. He wears a thin, almost see-through monk’s robe.

Like other statues from Nagapattinam, a flame rises from the bump on his head. This flame probably stands for wisdom. This large granite sculpture once decorated a monastery in Nagapattinam. The town is also famous for its Buddhist bronze statues. A Tamil inscription on the back of the statue is now too worn to read.

CuratorialText: Many Mansions is the first in Kerry James Marshall’s series of five large-scale paintings depicting public housing projects in Chicago and Los Angeles such as Rockwell Gardens, Wentworth Gardens, or, as in Many Mansions, Stateway Gardens. Struck by the absurdity of the term “garden” to describe these failed solutions to low-income housing, Marshall was inspired to represent the profound contradictions of living in such an environment. Many Mansions is filled with ironic and startling juxtapositions of the real and artificial—from the unnaturally cheerful landscape to the three haunting, ebony-skinned figures dressed in nostalgic Sunday best. The title of the painting, visible on the red ribbon above, is a variation on Christ’s oft-quoted remark found in John [14:2]: “In my Father’s house there are many mansions.”
persona1_adapted:
"Many Mansions" is the first in a series of five large paintings by Kerry James Marshall. These paintings show public housing projects in Chicago and Los Angeles. Examples include Rockwell Gardens, Wentworth Gardens, and Stateway Gardens.

Marshall thought the word "garden" was strange for these places. The housing projects were not successful solutions for low-income families. This inspired him to show the deep contradictions of living there.

The painting is full of ironic and surprising contrasts between what is real and what is fake. There is an unnaturally cheerful landscape. Three dark-skinned figures wear old-fashioned Sunday clothes. They look haunting.

The title appears on a red ribbon above. It comes from a famous line by Christ in the Bible, John 14:2: "In my Father's house there are many mansions."

CuratorialText: In Ferris Bueller’s Day Off, Ferris’s best friend Cameron Frye intensely studies this nearly ten-foot-wide painting during a scene set at the Art Institute. Featuring people of every age and social class on the banks of the River Seine, A Sunday on La Grande Jatte—1884 has captivated visitors ever since its arrival at the museum in 1924.
persona1_adapted: In *Ferris Bueller’s Day Off*, Ferris’s best friend Cameron Frye stares closely at this nearly ten-foot-wide painting. The scene takes place at the Art Institute. The painting shows people of all ages and social classes on the banks of the River Seine. *A Sunday on La Grande Jatte—1884* has fascinated visitors ever since it arrived at the museum in 1924.

CuratorialText: If we, like Cameron, come closer to the painting, figures and forms dissolve into dots and dashes of complementary colors laid side by side, characteristic of Seurat’s pointillist technique. Many smaller painted and drawn sketches and several larger canvases, in which Seurat laid out the parameters for the landscape and figures, led up to this majestically composed scene. Seurat returned to the work two years after its start date, amplifying the silhouettes of some figures and adding others. Some of these, like the monkey on the leash, seem so integral to the final composition that it is hard to imagine them as add-ons, but others, like the man carrying a rolled newspaper in the furthest distance, are barely noticeable. To the artist, however, every decision was essential to his aim of making a painting of modern life equivalent to a classical Greek frieze.
persona1_adapted: If we, like Cameron, step closer to the painting, the figures and shapes break into dots and dashes of colors placed side by side. This is typical of Seurat’s pointillist technique, where he used small dots of paint to build the image. Before this grand scene, Seurat made many smaller sketches and several larger canvases to plan the landscape and people. He returned to the work two years after starting it, making some figures’ outlines stronger and adding others. Some additions, like the monkey on a leash, feel so important to the final picture that it’s hard to think of them as later changes. Others, like the man holding a rolled newspaper far in the background, are barely noticeable. But for Seurat, every choice mattered. He wanted to create a painting of modern life that felt as timeless and balanced as a classical Greek frieze.

CuratorialText: Laboring at a backstrap loom in this painting is Luz Jiménez, a master weaver and Nahua, one of the largest Indigenous groups in Mesoamerica. With expertise and dexterity, Jiménez threads weft through warp, slowly building her intricately patterned textile, the completed portion resting on her lap. Diego Rivera, a leading modernist painter who came to prominence in the years after the Mexican Revolution (1910–20), promoted a vision of Mexican national identity rooted in Indigenous and folk cultures, distinct from the legacies of Spanish colonialism. By centering Jiménez in Weaving, Rivera claimed her traditions as part of his own.
persona1_adapted:
In this painting, Luz Jiménez works at a backstrap loom. She is a master weaver and a Nahua person. The Nahua are one of the largest Indigenous groups in Mesoamerica. With skill and care, Jiménez threads the weft through the warp. She slowly builds her textile with an intricate pattern. The finished part rests on her lap.

Diego Rivera painted this scene. He was a leading modernist painter. He became famous after the Mexican Revolution (1910–20). Rivera promoted a vision of Mexican national identity. This vision was rooted in Indigenous and folk cultures. It was separate from the legacy of Spanish colonialism. By placing Jiménez at the center of *Weaving*, Rivera claimed her traditions as part of his own.

CuratorialText: This monumental painting is Gustave Caillebotte’s largest, most ambitious work and has been a visitor favorite since the Art Institute acquired it in 1964, well before the artist’s contributions to the Impressionist movement were widely recognized. Caillebotte captured a busy intersection of Paris that was only a short walk from his home. In fact, his paintings of Paris rarely depict areas beyond his familiar neighborhoods.
persona1_adapted: This huge painting is Gustave Caillebotte’s biggest and most important work. Visitors have loved it since the Art Institute got it in 1964. At that time, people did not yet know how much Caillebotte had helped the Impressionist movement. He painted a busy street crossing in Paris, just a short walk from his home. In fact, most of his Paris paintings show places close to where he lived.

CuratorialText: Caillebotte demonstrated his affinity with the Impressionists through his subject of modern urban life but approached these settings with a more Realist technique. The dramatic cropping of the scene may have been inspired by the newly popularized medium of photography; the two central figures seem to be walking straight into the viewer as something captures their attention outside of the frame. At the time, artists rarely attempted to depict people in motion; Caillebotte carefully placed each of the background figures to evoke the feeling of bustling city streets. When he started exhibiting with the Impressionists, Caillebotte also focused on portraying the atmosphere of his scenes; here, instead of explicitly painting raindrops to indicate the weather, he suggests it through the incorporation of newly available retractable umbrellas and the subtle reflections on the wet cobblestones.
persona1_adapted: Caillebotte showed his connection to the Impressionists by painting modern city life. But he used a more realistic style. The way he cut off the scene at the edges may have been inspired by photography, which was becoming popular at the time. The two main figures seem to walk right toward the viewer. Something outside the frame catches their attention. Back then, artists rarely tried to show people in motion. Caillebotte carefully placed each person in the background to make the street feel busy and alive. When he started showing his work with the Impressionists, he also focused on capturing the mood of a scene. Here, instead of painting raindrops directly, he suggests rain by including new retractable umbrellas and the soft reflections on the wet cobblestones.

CuratorialText: Richard Hunt created Hero Construction in 1958, one year after graduating from the School of the Art Institute of Chicago. The sculpture is composed of found objects—old pipes, bits of metal, and automobile parts—that the artist discovered in junkyards and on the street. Using a torch like a paintbrush, Hunt welded these elements into a form that is abstract yet recognizable.
persona1_adapted: Richard Hunt made *Hero Construction* in 1958, just one year after he finished art school in Chicago. The sculpture is built from found objects. These are things like old pipes, pieces of metal, and car parts. Hunt discovered them in junkyards and on the street. He used a torch like a paintbrush to weld these parts together. The final shape is abstract, but you can still recognize parts of it.

CuratorialText: Hunt was fascinated with mythology, and Hero Construction can be related to other sculptures past and present, from ancient statues of Greek heroes to modern monuments of statesmen. The modest but monumental stance of the figure suggests a contemporary hero who maintains strength in the face of the uncertainties and dangers of the present age.
persona1_adapted: Hunt was fascinated by mythology. His sculpture *Hero Construction* connects to other artworks from different times—from ancient Greek statues of heroes to modern monuments of leaders. The figure stands in a simple but powerful pose. It suggests a modern-day hero who stays strong even when facing the unknowns and dangers of today's world.

CuratorialText: The Mexicas, or Aztec Empire, ruled central Mexico from 1428 until 1521. This monument uses glyphs to record the history of the Mexica universe up to 1503. On the back, 1 Rabbit represents the beginning of time. On the front, the four previous cosmic eras are inscribed in the corners, while the current cosmic era 4 Movement appears in the center. 1 Crocodile (above) and 11 Reed (in the square frame) indicate the date July 15, 1503, when the ninth emperor Moctezuma Xocoyotzin was coronated. Just as these glyphs recurred throughout the Mexica capital, Tenochtitlan, they can also be found in Chicago: 4 Movement appears in a mural at the 18th Street “L” stop and on manhole covers in Pilsen—a neighborhood that has long been home to Chicago’s vibrant Mexican community.
persona1_adapted: The Mexicas, also known as the Aztec Empire, ruled central Mexico from 1428 to 1521. This monument uses picture symbols called glyphs to tell the history of the Mexica universe up to 1503. On the back, the symbol 1 Rabbit marks the beginning of time. On the front, the four previous cosmic eras are carved in the corners. The current cosmic era, 4 Movement, appears in the center. The symbols 1 Crocodile (above) and 11 Reed (in the square frame) show the date July 15, 1503. On that day, the ninth emperor, Moctezuma Xocoyotzin, was crowned. These same glyphs appeared all over the Mexica capital, Tenochtitlan. You can also find them in Chicago today. For example, 4 Movement appears in a mural at the 18th Street “L” stop and on manhole covers in Pilsen. Pilsen has long been home to Chicago’s lively Mexican community.

CuratorialText: A palpable energy and sense of movement enliven Nightlife, Archibald Motley’s portrayal of a crowded cabaret in the South Side neighborhood of Bronzeville in Chicago. With stylized figures, an array of diagonal lines, and heightened colors keyed to shades of magenta and violet, the artist captured the exuberance of city dwellers out on the town. Motley created a network of gestures and glances among the people, drawing attention to the various social interactions that animate the scene. The composition is an exploration of artificial lighting. Motley was inspired, in part, to paint Nightlife after having seen Edward Hopper’s Nighthawks (1942.51), which had entered the Art Institute’s collection the prior year.
persona1_adapted: A lively energy fills *Nightlife*, Archibald Motley’s painting of a busy cabaret in Chicago’s Bronzeville neighborhood. The artist used stylized, or simplified, figures, many diagonal lines, and bright colors like magenta and violet. These choices show the excitement of city people enjoying a night out. Motley created a web of gestures and glances among the people. This highlights the different social interactions that bring the scene to life. The painting also explores how artificial light affects a space. Motley was partly inspired to paint *Nightlife* after seeing Edward Hopper’s *Nighthawks*, which the Art Institute had bought the year before.

CuratorialText: Although influenced by Abstract Expressionist artists in New York in the early 1950s, Joan Mitchell did not prioritize self-expression: her often exuberant abstractions were “about landscape, not about me,” she once explained. Mitchell painted large, light-filled canvases animated by loosely applied skeins of bright color—here infused with the energy of a large metropolis. The title suggests a relationship between the painting’s network of pigments and the nerves or arteries of an urban space. The sense of spontaneity conveyed in City Landscape, however, belies Mitchell’s methods. Unlike many of her contemporaries, who were dubbed “action painters,” Mitchell worked slowly and deliberately. “I paint a little,” she said. “Then I sit and look at the painting, sometimes for hours. Eventually, the painting tells me what to do.”
persona1_adapted: Joan Mitchell was influenced by Abstract Expressionist artists in New York in the early 1950s. But she did not focus on self-expression. Her lively abstract paintings were “about landscape, not about me,” she once said. Mitchell created large, bright canvases filled with loosely painted swirls of color. In this painting, the colors feel full of the energy of a big city. The title suggests that the painting’s web of paint is like the nerves or arteries of an urban space. *City Landscape* looks spontaneous, but that hides how Mitchell actually worked. Unlike many of her fellow artists, who were called “action painters,” Mitchell worked slowly and carefully. “I paint a little,” she said. “Then I sit and look at the painting, sometimes for hours. Eventually, the painting tells me what to do.”

CuratorialText: Around 1920 Georgia O’Keeffe painted a number of oils exploring, as she later recalled, “the idea that music could be translated into something for the eye.” In Blue and Green Music, O’Keeffe’s colors and forms simultaneously suggest the natural world and evoke the experience of sound. She was drawn to the theories of the Russian Expressionist painter Vasily Kandinsky, who, in his 1912 text Concerning the Spiritual in Art, argued that visual artists should emulate music in order to achieve pure expression free of literary references.
persona1_adapted: Around 1920, Georgia O’Keeffe painted several works that explored an idea. She later said it was "the idea that music could be translated into something for the eye." In *Blue and Green Music*, O’Keeffe’s colors and shapes suggest nature. They also make you think of the experience of sound. She was interested in the ideas of Russian painter Vasily Kandinsky. In his 1912 book *Concerning the Spiritual in Art*, he said that visual artists should copy music. This way, they could create pure expression without using references to stories or objects.

CuratorialText: Henri Matisse considered Bathers by a River to be one of the five most “pivotal” works of his career, and with good reason: it facilitated the evolution of the artist’s style over the course of nearly a decade. Originally, the work was related to a 1909 commission by the Russian collector Sergei Shchukin, who wanted two large canvases to decorate the staircase of his Moscow home. Matisse proposed three pastoral images, though Shchukin decided to purchase only two works, Dance II and Music (both State Hermitage Museum, Saint Petersburg).
persona1_adapted: Henri Matisse said that *Bathers by a River* was one of the five most important works of his career. He had a good reason for this. The painting shows how his style changed over nearly ten years. At first, the work was part of a 1909 order from a Russian collector named Sergei Shchukin. He wanted two large paintings for the stairs of his home in Moscow. Matisse offered three peaceful country scenes. But Shchukin chose to buy only two of them: *Dance II* and *Music*. Both are now in the State Hermitage Museum in Saint Petersburg.

CuratorialText: Four years later, Matisse returned to this canvas, the rejected third image, altering the idyllic scene and changing the pastel palette to reflect his new interest in Cubism. He reordered the composition, making the figures more columnar, with faceless, ovoid heads. Over the next years, Matisse transformed the background into four vertical bands and turned the formerly blue river into a thick black vertical band. With its restricted palette and severely abstracted forms, Bathers by a River is far removed from Dance II and Music, which convey a graceful lyricism. The sobriety and hint of danger in Bathers by a River may in part reflect the artist’s concerns during the terrible, war-torn period during which he completed it.
persona1_adapted: Four years later, Matisse returned to this canvas—the painting that had been rejected as the third image. He changed the peaceful scene and swapped the soft pastel colors for a style that showed his new interest in Cubism. Cubism is an art style that breaks objects into geometric shapes. Matisse rearranged the composition, making the figures look more like columns, with head shapes that were oval and had no faces. Over the next few years, he turned the background into four vertical strips. He also changed the river, which was once blue, into a thick black vertical band. With its limited colors and highly simplified forms, *Bathers by a River* is very different from *Dance II* and *Music*. Those earlier works feel graceful and songlike. The seriousness and hint of danger in *Bathers by a River* may partly reflect the artist’s worries during the terrible war years when he finished it.

CuratorialText: One of four posts sculpted for the palace of the king (ogoga) of Ikere, this evocative image of the Yoruba concept of a divine ruler is a masterwork of Olowe of Ise. The renowned artist’s dynamic style can be distinguished by the interrelationships between figures, their exaggerated proportions, and the use of open space. The sculptor’s diminutive portrayal of the king suggests his dependence on others, and his crown has been emphasized to evoke the spiritual power residing within it. The towering image of the king’s senior wife standing behind him underscores her superior status as a guardian of her husband’s interests, endowed with keen political understanding and supernatural knowledge. She would have placed the crown on the king’s head during his enthronement ceremony. The smaller figures at the king’s feet represent one of his junior wives and the flute-playing trickster god Eshu.
persona1_adapted:
This is one of four carved posts made for the palace of the king (ogoga) of Ikere. It shows the Yoruba idea of a divine ruler. The post is a masterwork by Olowe of Ise. He was a famous artist.

Olowe’s style is easy to spot. He shows how the figures relate to each other. He gives them exaggerated body parts. He also uses open space in the carving.

The king is shown as small. This suggests he depends on others. His crown is made large to show the spiritual power inside it.

The tall figure behind the king is his senior wife. She stands above him. This shows her high status. She guards her husband’s interests. She has sharp political sense and supernatural knowledge. She would have placed the crown on the king’s head during his crowning ceremony.

The smaller figures at the king’s feet are one of his junior wives and Eshu, the trickster god who plays the flute.

CuratorialText: About Nighthawks Edward Hopper recollected, “unconsciously, probably, I was painting the loneliness of a large city.” In an all-night diner, three customers sit at the counter opposite a server, each appear to be lost in thought and disengaged from one another. The composition is tightly organized and spare in details: there is no entrance to the establishment, no debris on the streets. Through harmonious geometric forms and the glow of the diner’s electric lighting, Hopper created a serene, beautiful, yet enigmatic scene. Although inspired by a restaurant Hopper had seen on Greenwich Avenue in New York, the painting is not a realistic transcription of an actual place. As viewers, we are left to wonder about the figures, their relationships, and this imagined world.
persona1_adapted: About *Nighthawks*, Edward Hopper said, “unconsciously, probably, I was painting the loneliness of a large city.” The scene takes place in an all-night diner. Three customers sit at the counter across from a server. Each person seems lost in thought and not connected to the others. The painting is tightly arranged and simple in details. There is no door to the diner and no trash on the streets. Hopper used balanced shapes and the bright glow of the diner’s lights to create a calm, beautiful, yet mysterious scene. Even though a real restaurant on Greenwich Avenue in New York inspired him, the painting is not a copy of an actual place. As viewers, we are left to wonder about the people, their connections, and this imagined world.

CuratorialText: The Assumption of the Virgin was the central image of El Greco’s first major commission, an altarpiece for the Church of Santo Domingo el Antiguo, a convent in Toledo, Spain. The painting’s subject, the raising of the Virgin Mary’s body into heaven, relates to the function of the church as the funerary monument of a wealthy noblewoman, Doña Maria de Silva; the iconography of the entire commission focuses on death and salvation.
persona1_adapted: The Assumption of the Virgin was the main image in El Greco’s first big job. He painted it for an altarpiece, a large artwork placed behind an altar, in a church called Santo Domingo el Antiguo. This church was a convent in Toledo, Spain. The painting shows the Virgin Mary’s body being lifted up into heaven. This subject connects to the church’s purpose as a burial monument for a wealthy noblewoman, Doña Maria de Silva. The whole set of images in the commission focuses on death and salvation.

CuratorialText: El Greco used broad, free brushwork, flickering hues, rich color harmonies, and bold figural arrangements to arouse devotional fervor in the viewer and impart a deep sense of faith. The composition is divided into two zones: the earthly sphere of the apostles and the celestial realm of the angels. El Greco masterfully compressed many figures into a tall and narrow format: In the earthly zone, the apostles’ faces and gestures express a range of emotions through their dynamic, agitated gestures and poses. They are so individually considered that it seems El Greco studied actual studio models. In contrast, the angels in the heavenly scene above are calm, graceful, and direct in their focus toward the looming figure of the Virgin Mary as she rises into their realm.
persona1_adapted:
