El Greco used broad, free brushstrokes and bright, flickering colors. He mixed rich color combinations and bold figure arrangements. His goal was to stir strong religious feelings in the viewer and create a deep sense of faith.

The painting is split into two parts. The lower part shows the apostles on Earth. The upper part shows the angels in heaven. El Greco packed many figures into a tall, narrow space. In the earthly section, the apostles’ faces and gestures show many emotions. Their poses are lively and full of movement. Each figure looks so individual that it seems El Greco studied real people as models.

In the heavenly scene above, the angels are calm and graceful. They all look directly at the Virgin Mary as she rises up into their realm.

CuratorialText: Katsushika Hokusai’s much celebrated series, Thirty-Six Views of Mount Fuji (Fugaku sanjûrokkei), was begun in 1830, when the artist was 70 years old. This tour-de-force series established the popularity of landscape prints, which continues to this day. Perhaps most striking about the series is Hokusai’s copious use of the newly affordable Berlin blue pigment, featured in many of the compositions in the color for the sky and water. Mount Fuji is the protagonist in each scene, viewed from afar or up close, during various weather conditions and seasons, and from all directions.
persona1_adapted: Katsushika Hokusai started his famous series *Thirty-Six Views of Mount Fuji* in 1830. He was 70 years old at the time. This powerful series made landscape prints popular, and they are still loved today. One striking feature is Hokusai’s heavy use of a new blue pigment called Berlin blue. It had just become affordable, and he used it for the sky and water in many prints. Mount Fuji is the main subject in every scene. We see it from far away or up close, in different weather and seasons, and from every direction.

CuratorialText: The most famous image from the set is the “Great Wave” (Kanagawa oki nami ura), in which a diminutive Mount Fuji can be seen in the distance under the crest of a giant wave. The three impressions of Hokusai’s Great Wave in the Art Institute are all later impressions than the first state of the design.
persona1_adapted: The most famous picture from the series is the "Great Wave" (Kanagawa oki nami ura). In it, a tiny Mount Fuji appears in the distance under the curl of a huge wave. The Art Institute has three copies of Hokusai’s Great Wave. All of them were printed later than the first version of the design.

CuratorialText: Pablo Picasso made The Old Guitarist while working in Barcelona. In the paintings of his Blue Period (1901-04), the artist restricted himself to a cold, monochromatic blue palette, flattened forms, and emotional, psychological themes of human misery and alienation related to the work of such artists as Edvard Munch and Paul Gauguin. The elongated, angular figure of the blind musician also relates to Picasso’s interest in Spanish art and, in particular, the great 16th-century artist El Greco. The image reﬂects the twenty-two-year-old Picasso’s personal struggle and sympathy for the plight of the downtrodden; he knew what it was like to be poor, having been nearly penniless during all of 1902.
persona1_adapted:
Pablo Picasso painted *The Old Guitarist* while he was living in Barcelona. During his Blue Period (1901–04), he used only cool shades of blue. He also made his figures look flat. His paintings focused on sad, lonely feelings and human suffering. These themes were similar to the work of artists like Edvard Munch and Paul Gauguin.

The blind musician in the painting has a long, skinny, and angular body. This style shows Picasso’s interest in Spanish art, especially the work of the 16th-century artist El Greco. The painting also reflects Picasso’s own struggles. He was only twenty-two years old when he made it. He knew what it was like to be poor—he had almost no money during all of 1902.

CuratorialText: Illustrator Norman Rockwell was one of the most popular artists of the 20th century, beloved in particular for the hundreds of cover images he created for the Saturday Evening Post. The Dugout is the largest of several studies for one such cover, published on September 4, 1948. It was inspired by a springtime doubleheader that he attended during which the hometown Boston Braves swept the visiting Chicago Cubs. The artist created an image contrasting dejected Cubs players and elated Braves fans.
persona1_adapted: Norman Rockwell was a famous illustrator and one of the most popular artists of the 1900s. He is best known for the many cover images he made for the Saturday Evening Post magazine. *The Dugout* is the largest of several studies, or practice versions, for one of those covers. It was published on September 4, 1948. Rockwell got the idea from a spring baseball doubleheader he went to. In that game, the home team, the Boston Braves, beat the visiting Chicago Cubs. The artist created a scene that shows sad Cubs players next to happy Braves fans.

CuratorialText: Rockwell staged the entire scene, which enabled him to take photographs for later reference. He recruited several family members of Braves players to act as fans. In the dugout, he positioned (from left to right) Cubs pitcher Bob Rush, manager Charlie Grimm, catcher Al “Rube” Walker, and pitcher Johnny Schmitz. The figure of the forlorn batboy, however, was not a member of the Cubs; Rockwell paid Braves batboy Frank McNulty $5 to don the Cubs uniform and pose.
persona1_adapted: Rockwell set up the whole scene so he could take photos to use later. He asked family members of Braves players to pretend to be fans. In the dugout, he placed (from left to right) Cubs pitcher Bob Rush, manager Charlie Grimm, catcher Al “Rube” Walker, and pitcher Johnny Schmitz. The sad-looking batboy was not a real Cubs player. Rockwell paid Braves batboy Frank McNulty $5 to wear a Cubs uniform and pose for the picture.

CuratorialText: By the time the issue was published, the Cubs had fallen to last place in their league. Rockwell’s cover helped bolster their growing reputation as the “loveable losers,” and it became one of the most recognized sports images of the 20th century.
persona1_adapted: By the time the magazine came out, the Cubs had dropped to last place in their league. Rockwell’s cover helped build their image as the “loveable losers.” It became one of the most famous sports pictures of the 1900s.

CuratorialText: The monumental stacks that Claude Monet depicted in his series Stacks of Wheat rose fifteen to twenty feet and stood just outside the artist’s farmhouse at Giverny. Through 1890 and 1891, he worked on this series both in the field, painting simultaneously at several easels, and in the studio, refining pictorial harmonies. In May 1891, Monet hung fifteen of these canvases next to each other in one small room in the Galerie Durand-Ruel in Paris. An unprecedented critical and financial success, the exhibition marked a breakthrough in Monet’s career, as well as in the history of French art. In this view, and in nearly all of the autumn views in the series, the conical tops of the stacks break the horizon and push into the sky. But in most of the winter views, which constitute the core of the series, the stacks seem wrapped by bands of hill and field, as if bedded down for the season. For Monet, the stack was a resonant symbol of sustenance and survival. He followed this group with further series depicting poplars, the facade of Rouen Cathedral, and, later, his own garden at Giverny. The Art Institute has the largest group of Monet’s Stacks of Wheat in the world.
persona1_adapted:
Claude Monet painted a series of huge stacks of wheat. These stacks were fifteen to twenty feet tall. They stood just outside Monet’s farmhouse in Giverny, France. From 1890 to 1891, Monet worked on this series. He painted outside using several easels at once. He also worked inside his studio to improve the colors and shapes in his paintings.

In May 1891, Monet hung fifteen of these paintings side by side in one small room at the Galerie Durand-Ruel in Paris. The show was a huge success. Critics and buyers praised it. This exhibition was a major breakthrough for Monet and for French art.

In this painting, and in most autumn views from the series, the pointed tops of the wheat stacks rise above the horizon and reach into the sky. But in most winter views, which are the heart of the series, the stacks look wrapped by hills and fields. They seem tucked in for the season.

For Monet, the wheat stack was a strong symbol of food and survival. After this series, he painted more groups of works. He painted poplar trees, the front of Rouen Cathedral, and later his own garden at Giverny. The Art Institute of Chicago has the largest collection of Monet’s *Stacks of Wheat* in the world.

CuratorialText: In 1886 Vincent van Gogh left his native Holland and settled in Paris, where his beloved brother Theo was a dealer in paintings. Van Gogh was apparently inspired by the energy of the city and by his introduction to Impressionism, and he created at least twenty-four self-portraits during his two-year stay in the French capital.
persona1_adapted: In 1886, Vincent van Gogh left his home country of Holland and moved to Paris. His brother Theo, whom he loved very much, worked there as a painting dealer. The city’s energy and van Gogh’s first look at Impressionism—a style of art that focuses on light and color—inspired him. During his two years in Paris, he painted at least twenty-four self-portraits.

CuratorialText: This early example is modest in size and was painted on prepared artist’s board rather than canvas. Its densely dabbed brushwork, which became a hallmark of Van Gogh’s style, reﬂects the artist’s response to Georges Seurat’s revolutionary pointillist technique in A Sunday on La Grande Jatte—1884. What was for Seurat a method based on the cool objectivity of science became in Van Gogh’s hands an intense emotional language. The surface of the painting dances with particles of color—intense greens, blues, reds, and oranges. Dominating this dazzling array of staccato dots and dashes are the artist’s deep green eyes and the intensity of their gaze. “I prefer painting people’s eyes to cathedrals,” Van Gogh once wrote to Theo. “However solemn and imposing the latter may be—a human soul, be it that of a poor streetwalker, is more interesting to me.” From Paris, Van Gogh traveled to the southern town of Arles for fifteen months. At the time of his death, in 1890, he had actively pursued his art for only five years.
persona1_adapted: This early painting is small and was done on artist’s board instead of canvas. The thick, dotted brushstrokes became a key part of Van Gogh’s style. They show how he reacted to Georges Seurat’s new pointillist technique—a method of painting with tiny dots of color—in the work *A Sunday on La Grande Jatte—1884*. For Seurat, this was a scientific way to paint. For Van Gogh, it became a way to express strong emotions. The surface of the painting seems to dance with specks of color—bright greens, blues, reds, and oranges. Standing out among these quick dots and dashes are the artist’s deep green eyes and their intense stare. “I prefer painting people’s eyes to cathedrals,” Van Gogh once wrote to his brother Theo. “No matter how grand and impressive cathedrals are—a human soul, even that of a poor streetwalker, is more interesting to me.” After Paris, Van Gogh moved to the southern town of Arles for fifteen months. When he died in 1890, he had only been making art for five years.

CuratorialText: Van Gogh’s career as a painter was actually very brief. From Paris, he traveled to the southern town of Arles for ﬁ ft een months. At the time of his death, in 1890, he had actively pursued his art for just ﬁve years.
persona1_adapted: Van Gogh’s career as a painter was actually very short. From Paris, he moved to the southern town of Arles for fifteen months. When he died in 1890, he had been making art for only five years.

CuratorialText: In American Gothic, Grant Wood directly evoked images of an earlier generation by featuring a farmer and his daughter posed stiffly and dressed as if they were, as the artist put it, “tintypes from my old family album.” They stand outside of their home, built in an 1880s style known as Carpenter Gothic. Wood had seen a similar farmhouse during a visit to Eldon, Iowa. When it was exhibited at the Art Institute in 1930, the painting became an instant sensation, its ambiguity prompting viewers to speculate about the figures and their story. Many understood the work to be a satirical comment on midwesterners out of step with a modernizing world. Yet Wood intended it to convey a positive image of rural American values, offering a vision of reassurance at the beginning of the Great Depression.
persona1_adapted: In *American Gothic*, Grant Wood wanted to show people from an earlier time. He painted a farmer and his daughter standing stiffly. They are dressed like old-fashioned photos from the artist’s family album. The two stand outside their home, which was built in a style from the 1880s called Carpenter Gothic. Wood saw a similar farmhouse when he visited Eldon, Iowa. When the painting was shown at the Art Institute in 1930, it became an instant hit. People were unsure what the painting meant, so they made up their own stories about the two figures. Many thought the painting made fun of midwesterners who seemed out of touch with the modern world. But Wood actually wanted to show a positive view of rural American values. He offered a comforting image at the start of the Great Depression.

CuratorialText: Little did Claude Monet know that the water garden he created three years after buying his Giverny, France, property in 1890 would become his primary inspiration over the next two and a half decades. These paintings, numbering around 250, mark Monet’s artistic journey from more straightforward depictions of the pond spanned by the wooden Japanese bridge to the monumental and near-abstract series on the water lily theme he made in preparation for his murals at the Musée de l’Orangerie in Paris between 1914 and 1926.
persona1_adapted: In 1890, Claude Monet bought a house in Giverny, France. Three years later, he built a water garden there. He had no idea that this garden would become his main source of inspiration for the next 25 years. Monet painted the garden about 250 times. His early paintings show a clear view of the pond and the wooden Japanese bridge. Over time, his style changed. His later paintings of water lilies became large and almost abstract. He made these works between 1914 and 1926 as studies for the murals now at the Musée de l’Orangerie in Paris.

CuratorialText: Water Lilies, one of a group of paintings on the subject made between 1903 and 1908, comes at the midpoint of Monet’s developing style and spatial experimentations. The nearly square format underscores his move away from painting the conventional zones of land, sky, and water to focus solely on the water’s surface. Clusters of water lilies at bottom left and top right frame a watery path, while the water’s surface reflects trees and clouds. Although the dreamlike quality of floating forms might seem to be a natural development for the artist, he considered these works “an obsession,” a sentiment borne out by technical examinations on the Art Institute’s canvas, revealing many significant changes made to the painting in progress.
persona1_adapted: Water Lilies is one of a group of paintings on this subject that Monet made between 1903 and 1908. It comes from the middle of his career, when he was trying new styles and experimenting with space. The painting is almost square. This shape shows that Monet moved away from painting the usual areas of land, sky, and water. Instead, he focused only on the water’s surface. Groups of water lilies in the bottom left and top right frame a watery path. The water’s surface also reflects trees and clouds. The floating shapes give the painting a dreamlike feel. You might think this style came naturally to Monet, but he called these works “an obsession.” Technical studies of the Art Institute’s canvas show that Monet made many big changes to the painting while he worked on it.

CuratorialText: Pierre-Auguste Renoir considered the Restaurant Fournaise in Chatou (a village along the Seine River) the perfect spot to enjoy nature and good company away from the urban distractions of Paris. His painting Two Sisters (On the Terrace), which he exhibited at the Impressionist exhibition of 1882, is among the last of his paintings set there.
persona1_adapted: Pierre-Auguste Renoir thought the Restaurant Fournaise in Chatou was the perfect place to enjoy nature and good company. Chatou is a village along the Seine River, away from the busy city of Paris. His painting *Two Sisters (On the Terrace)* is one of the last paintings he made there. He showed this painting at the Impressionist exhibition in 1882.

CuratorialText: Sometimes, as in his most famous work on this subject, Luncheon of the Boating Party (1882; Phillips Collection, Washington, DC), Renoir used his close friends as models. For Two Sisters, however, despite the title of the painting, he paid unrelated models to create this intimate and perfectly balanced pairing of youth and nature. The older girl, wearing the blue flannel of a female boater’s outfit, sports a luxurious red hat, but it is her younger companion who captivates. Her oversize headdress composed of a floral bouquet is echoed in the brightly colored skeins of yarn in the basket that she gently touches. As was typical for the artist, Renoir painted the faces of his models with controlled and precise brushwork creating a porcelain-like surface that contrasts brilliantly with the diaphanous, impressionist backdrop of river, boats, and blossoming trees just beyond the railing of the terrace.
persona1_adapted: Sometimes, in his most famous painting on this topic, *Luncheon of the Boating Party* (1882), Renoir used his close friends as models. But for *Two Sisters*, even though the title suggests otherwise, he paid strangers to pose. He wanted to create a calm and perfectly balanced scene of youth and nature. The older girl wears a blue flannel boater’s outfit and a fancy red hat. But it is her younger sister who really grabs your attention. Her oversized headdress is made of flowers. That floral pattern is repeated in the bright yarn in the basket she gently touches. As was typical for Renoir, he painted the faces with careful, controlled brushstrokes. This gave their skin a smooth, porcelain-like look. That smoothness stands out against the soft, blurry background of the river, boats, and blooming trees just beyond the terrace railing.

CuratorialText: Willem de Kooning was a central figure in Abstract Expressionism, an art movement that espoused the painterly actions of the artist as a sign of his or her emotions. De Kooning completed Excavation in June 1950, just in time for it to be exhibited in the twenty-fifth Venice Biennale. His largest painting up to that date, the work exemplifies the Dutch-born innovator’s style, with its expressive brushwork and distinctive organization of space into sliding planes with open contours. According to the artist, the point of departure for the painting was an image of women working in a rice field in Bitter Rice, a 1949 Neorealist film by Italian director Giuseppe de Santis. The mobile structure of hooked calligraphic lines defines anatomical parts—bird and fish shapes, human noses, eyes, teeth, necks, and jaws—that seem to dance across the painted surface, revealing the particular tension between abstraction and figuration that is inherent in de Kooning’s work. The original white pigment has yellowed over the years, diluting somewhat the flashes of red, blue, yellow, and pink that punctuate the composition. Aptly titled, the painting reflects de Kooning’s technically masterful painting process: an intensive building up of the surface and scraping down of its paint layers, often for months, until the desired effect was achieved.
persona1_adapted:
Willem de Kooning was a key artist in Abstract Expressionism. This art movement focused on the artist's physical painting actions to show their emotions. De Kooning finished *Excavation* in June 1950, just in time for the twenty-fifth Venice Biennale, a major international art show. It was his largest painting up to that point.

The work shows de Kooning’s style with bold brushwork and a unique way of organizing space. He used sliding planes and open outlines. The artist said the painting was inspired by a scene from the 1949 Italian film *Bitter Rice*. In it, women work in a rice field.

The painting uses hooked, flowing lines that look like calligraphy. These lines form body parts—bird and fish shapes, noses, eyes, teeth, necks, and jaws. They seem to dance across the canvas. This creates a tension between abstract shapes and recognizable figures, a key feature of de Kooning’s work.

Over time, the original white paint has yellowed. This slightly dulls the flashes of red, blue, yellow, and pink in the painting. The title *Excavation* fits the work well. It describes de Kooning’s skillful process: he built up layers of paint and then scraped them down, often for months, until he got the effect he wanted.

CuratorialText: White Crucifixion is the first in Marc Chagall’s series of compositions that feature Jesus as a Jewish martyr and dramatically call attention to the persecution and suffering of Jews in 1930s Germany at the hands of the National Socialist Party. Chagall stressed Jesus’s religious identity by depicting him and the biblical figures above him in traditional Jewish garments. The surrounding images show the devastation of pogroms, violent attacks against Jewish communities often organized or sanctioned by local governments. Combining the Crucifixion with contemporary events, Chagall’s painting links the martyred Jesus with the Jewish people being persecuted across Europe and implicitly compares the Nazis with Jesus’s tormentors.
persona1_adapted: White Crucifixion is the first painting in Marc Chagall’s series that shows Jesus as a Jewish martyr. It strongly draws attention to the persecution and suffering of Jews in 1930s Germany under the National Socialist Party. Chagall highlighted Jesus’s religious identity by showing him and the biblical figures above him wearing traditional Jewish clothing. The scenes around them show the destruction caused by pogroms—violent attacks on Jewish communities that were often organized or approved by local governments. By mixing the Crucifixion with current events, Chagall’s painting connects the martyred Jesus with the Jewish people being persecuted across Europe. It also quietly compares the Nazis to those who tormented Jesus.

CuratorialText: The Child’s Bath is a tender portrayal of familial closeness, a subject that Mary Cassatt explored throughout her career. The caregiver’s cheek brushing the child’s shoulder, her encircling embrace, and the child’s pudgy hand on her knee suggest an emotional bond between the two. Captivated by a large exhibition of Japanese prints in Paris in 1890, Cassatt set out to produce a series of color prints influenced by Japanese aesthetics. She then continued her investigation across media, culminating in this bold composition, with its dramatically flattened picture plane, decorative patterning, and bright palette.
persona1_adapted:
The Child’s Bath shows a quiet, loving moment between a caregiver and a child. Mary Cassatt often painted this kind of family closeness. In the painting, the caregiver’s cheek gently touches the child’s shoulder. Her arms wrap around the child, and the child’s small hand rests on her knee. These details show a strong emotional bond between them.

In 1890, Cassatt saw a big exhibition of Japanese prints in Paris and was fascinated by them. She decided to make a series of color prints inspired by Japanese art. Later, she explored the same ideas in different art forms. This painting is the result. It has a flat-looking space, decorative patterns, and bright colors.

CuratorialText: René Magritte painted Time Transfixed at a moment when he was attempting to elicit “poetic secrets” through his works. With both philosophy and psychology in mind, the artist challenged himself to juxtapose two images in such a way that they would suggest in the viewer’s mind a third un-pictured thing. Here he connected two highly disparate objects—a train and a fireplace—in a composition that became one of his most iconic works. Although the fireplace is domestic and the train is industrial, they are linked by the smoke. Magritte understood that viewers would connect the locomotive’s billowing exhaust with the plumes of smoke that travel up chimneys, uncovering the painting’s poetic secret.
persona1_adapted: René Magritte painted *Time Transfixed* at a time when he was trying to reveal "poetic secrets" through his art. He thought about both philosophy and psychology. The artist challenged himself to put two images together in a way that would make the viewer imagine a third thing that wasn’t actually painted. In this work, he connected two very different objects—a train and a fireplace—in a scene that became one of his most famous paintings. Even though the fireplace belongs in a home and the train belongs in an industrial world, they are linked by smoke. Magritte knew that viewers would see the train’s thick exhaust and think of the smoke rising from a chimney. That connection is the painting’s poetic secret.

CuratorialText: One of the most avant-garde aspects of the Impressionists was their choice of subject matter, which frequently included scenes derived from modern, industrial Paris, from iron bridges to exhibition halls to train sheds. The train station at Saint-Lazare would have been a familiar, meaningful sight to Claude Monet in the 1870s. The terminal linked Paris to Normandy, where the artist developed his technique of painting outdoors in the 1860s. It was also the point of departure for the towns and villages west and north of Paris that the Impressionists frequently visited. Monet completed eight of his twelve known paintings of the Gare Saint-Lazare in time for the third Impressionist exhibition, in 1877, probably displaying them in the same gallery.
persona1_adapted:
One of the most daring things about the Impressionists was what they chose to paint. They often showed scenes from modern, industrial Paris. These included iron bridges, exhibition halls, and train sheds.

In the 1870s, the train station at Saint-Lazare was a familiar and meaningful sight for Claude Monet. The station connected Paris to Normandy. There, in the 1860s, Monet had learned to paint outdoors. The station was also the starting point for towns and villages west and north of Paris. The Impressionists visited these places often.

Monet finished eight of his twelve known paintings of the Gare Saint-Lazare in time for the third Impressionist exhibition in 1877. He probably showed them all in the same gallery.

CuratorialText: Monet chose to focus his attention here on the glass-and-iron train shed, where he found an appealing combination of artiﬁ cial and natural effects—the rising steam of locomotives trapped within the structure and the daylight penetrating large, glazed sections of the roof, for instance. Monet’s depictions of the station inaugurated what was to become for him an established pattern of painting a speciﬁc motif repeatedly in order to capture subtle and temporal atmospheric changes, as in his famous series of stacks of wheat. But the Saint-Lazare paintings also represented his last attempt to capture urban life: from this point on in his career, Monet largely devoted himself to landscapes.
persona1_adapted: Monet focused on the glass-and-iron train shed. He liked how artificial and natural effects mixed there. For example, steam from the trains rose inside the shed, while daylight came through the large glass sections of the roof. Monet’s paintings of the station started a pattern he would use often: painting the same subject many times to capture small, changing atmospheric effects. He did the same thing later in his famous series of wheat stacks. But the Saint-Lazare paintings were also his last try at capturing city life. After this, Monet mostly painted landscapes.

CuratorialText: Salvador Dalí, Surrealism’s most publicized practitioner, created monstrous visions of a world turned inside out, which he made even more compelling through his extraordinary technical skills. When the Art Institute acquired Inventions of the Monsters in 1943, the artist wrote his congratulations and explained.
persona1_adapted: Salvador Dalí was the most famous artist of Surrealism. He created strange, dreamlike images of a world turned upside down. His amazing painting skills made these visions even more powerful. In 1943, the Art Institute bought his painting *Inventions of the Monsters*. Dalí wrote to congratulate them and explained his work.

CuratorialText: “According to Nostradamus the apparition of monsters presages the outbreak of war. The canvas was painted in the Semmering mountains near Vienna a few months before the Anschluss [the 1938 political union of Austria and Germany] and has a prophetic character. Horse women equal maternal river monsters. Flaming giraffe equals masculine apocalyptic monster. Cat angel equals divine heterosexual monster. Hourglass equals metaphysical monster. Gala and Dalí equal sentimental monster. The little blue dog is not a true monster.”
persona1_adapted: According to Nostradamus, seeing monsters means war is coming. This painting was made in the Semmering mountains near Vienna a few months before the Anschluss—the 1938 union of Austria and Germany. It has a prophetic, or fortune-telling, quality. Horse women stand for motherly river monsters. A flaming giraffe stands for a male monster that brings the end of the world. A cat angel stands for a divine, or god-like, heterosexual monster. An hourglass stands for a supernatural monster. Gala and Dalí stand for a sentimental, or emotional, monster. The little blue dog is not a real monster.

CuratorialText: Inventions of the Monsters has an ominous mood. It is rife with threats of danger, from the menacing fire in the distance to the sibylline figure in the foreground with an hourglass and a butterfly, both symbols of the inevitability of death. Next to this figure sit Dalí and his wife and muse, Gala. With his native Catalonia embroiled in the Spanish Civil War, the artist surely felt great anxiety over a world without a safe haven, a world that indeed had allowed for the invention of monsters.
persona1_adapted: Inventions of the Monsters feels dark and threatening. Danger is everywhere in this painting. In the distance, a scary fire burns. In the front, a mysterious figure holds an hourglass and a butterfly. Both are symbols that death is unavoidable. Next to this figure sit Dalí and his wife and muse, Gala. At the time, Dalí’s home region of Catalonia was caught in the Spanish Civil War. The artist likely felt deep worry about a world with no safe place. It was a world that had allowed monsters to be invented.

CuratorialText: Shiva, one of the most important Hindu divinities, is here depicted as the Lord of the Dance (Nataraja), an iconic image in Indian art. Shiva’s cosmic dance sets in motion the rhythm of life and death; it pervades the universe, as symbolized by the ring of fire that is filled with the loose, snakelike locks of the god’s hair. One pair of his arms balances the flame of destruction and the hand drum (damaru) that beats the rhythm of life while another performs symbolic gestures: the raised right hand means “fear not,” and the left hand (gajahasta) pointing down toward his raised left foot signifies release from the ignorance that hinders realization of the ultimate reality. Shiva is shown perfectly balanced, with his right leg planted on the demon of darkness (Apasmara), stamping out ignorance. The tiny figure of the personified river goddess, Ganga, is caught up in his matted, flowing locks. Shiva was believed to break the fall of the great Ganges River as it descends from the Himalayas by standing beneath the waters, which divide over his hair, becoming the seven holy rivers of India. This classic bronze comes from the Chola period in the south of India. Icons such as this were carried in procession during religious ceremonies.
persona1_adapted:
Shiva is one of the most important gods in Hinduism. Here, he is shown as the Lord of the Dance, or Nataraja. This is a famous image in Indian art. Shiva’s cosmic dance creates the rhythm of life and death. It fills the whole universe, shown by the ring of fire around him. The ring is filled with the loose, snakelike locks of his hair.

One pair of his arms holds the flame of destruction and a small hand drum called a damaru. The drum beats the rhythm of life. His other arms make symbolic gestures. His raised right hand means “fear not.” His left hand points down toward his raised left foot. This gesture shows freedom from ignorance, which blocks people from understanding the ultimate truth.

Shiva stands perfectly balanced. His right leg is planted on the demon of darkness, named Apasmara. This shows him crushing ignorance. A tiny figure of the river goddess Ganga is caught in his matted, flowing hair. People believed that Shiva broke the fall of the great Ganges River as it came down from the Himalayas. He stood under the waters, and the river divided over his hair, becoming the seven holy rivers of India.

This classic bronze statue comes from the Chola period in southern India. Icons like this were carried in processions during religious ceremonies.

CuratorialText: Albert Bierstadt is best remembered for his paintings of the American West, but during his early career, the ambitious artist also created New England landscapes, particularly of the White Mountains, as seen here. When this painting was first exhibited in 1863, critics declared it Bierstadt’s “best work” and praised the artist’s deft contrast of “light and shade,” which brought a heightened realism to the image. Bierstadt’s affinity for the White Mountains mirrored a growing interest in the region as one of America’s premier tourist attractions. Photographs of the area from the 19th century suggest that Bierstadt utilized landscape elements from the Flume, a popular New Hampshire tourist site, in this imaginary composition.
persona1_adapted:
Albert Bierstadt is best known for his paintings of the American West. But early in his career, this ambitious artist also painted landscapes of New England, especially the White Mountains. You can see one of those paintings here.

When this painting was first shown in 1863, critics called it Bierstadt’s “best work.” They praised how he used “light and shade” to make the scene look very real.

Bierstadt’s love for the White Mountains matched a growing interest in the area as one of America’s top tourist spots. Photos from the 1800s show that Bierstadt used real features from the Flume, a popular New Hampshire tourist site, to create this imaginary scene.

CuratorialText: In this large-scale canvas, one of several Paul Gauguin sent back to France in 1897, women of varying ages appear to go about daily life within a colorful, stylized outdoor environment. Like much of the artist’s work, this painting combines observed detail with artistic fantasy to create a dreamlike scene whose narrative resists simple interpretation. The work’s deliberately provocative title enhances this effect, leaving us to wonder if the “you” refers to someone in the painting or to the viewer.
persona1_adapted: In this large painting, one of several Paul Gauguin sent back to France in 1897, women of different ages seem to go about their daily lives in a colorful, stylized outdoor setting. Like much of the artist’s work, this painting mixes real details with artistic imagination to create a dreamlike scene whose story is hard to explain simply. The painting’s deliberately bold title adds to this effect, making us wonder if the “you” refers to someone in the painting or to the viewer.

CuratorialText: Motivations for travel throughout history have been almost as varied as the means of travel. Whether people traveled alone or in groups, on foot, on animals, in caravans, on ships, or using modern modes of transportation like planes, trains, and automobiles, art can capture how and why we move through the world.
persona1_adapted: People have traveled for many different reasons throughout history. The ways they traveled have also changed a lot. Some people traveled alone, while others went in groups. They walked, rode animals, joined caravans, or sailed on ships. Today, we use planes, trains, and cars. Art can show us both how and why people move around the world.

CuratorialText: Marco Polo (1254–1324) was a Venetian merchant who spent about seventeen years in China—from around 1274 to 1292—during the reign of the Mongol emperor Khubilai Khan (reigned 1260–94), founder of the Yuan dynasty. In 1276, Khubilai’s armies conquered the Chinese capital at Hangzhou with minimal destruction. Sometime after this, Marco Polo visited the city and its West Lake.
persona1_adapted: Marco Polo (1254–1324) was a merchant from Venice, Italy. He spent about seventeen years in China, from around 1274 to 1292. This was during the rule of Khubilai Khan, the Mongol emperor who founded the Yuan dynasty. In 1276, Khubilai’s armies took over the Chinese capital at Hangzhou. They did very little damage to the city. Sometime after that, Marco Polo visited Hangzhou and its famous West Lake.

CuratorialText: Travel is a common theme in traditional Chinese landscape painting. From ancient times, the emperors of China maintained a network of roads and waterways that unified their vast realm and facilitated the movement of people and goods. Owing to this imperial association, images of ordinary travelers on the empire’s roads and rivers connoted the benevolent rule of an enlightened sovereign and evoked an era of peace and prosperity. Rather than documenting the hardships or actual circumstances of any particular journey, images of anonymous characters traveling or working together capture some of the noteworthy sights and scenes a traveler might encounter along the way. Simultaneously, they promote the fundamental ideals of a harmonious society and a well-ordered state.
persona1_adapted: Travel is a common theme in traditional Chinese landscape painting. Long ago, China’s emperors built a network of roads and waterways. These routes connected their huge empire and helped people and goods move around. Because of this link to rulers, pictures of everyday travelers on the empire’s roads and rivers suggested a wise and kind leader. They also brought to mind a time of peace and wealth. Instead of showing the hard parts of a real trip, these images of unknown people traveling or working together capture interesting sights a traveler might see along the way. At the same time, they support the basic ideas of a peaceful society and a well-run state.

CuratorialText: One of the most enduring motivations for travel is religious devotion. Nearly every major religion—Christianity, Judaism, Islam, Buddhism, Hinduism, Jainism, and Sikhism, among others—has a tradition of pilgrimage: intentional travel to a site of spiritual significance. Christian pilgrims traveled to the Holy Land to walk in the footsteps of Christ or visited more local destinations connected to the lives or relics of the saints. In Islam, pilgrimage (hajj) to Mecca is one of the principal duties of all believers. Other holy sites were also visited on a regular basis. Over many centuries, hundreds of Chinese, Japanese, and Korean pilgrims braved countless dangers to visit places where the Historical Buddha lived and taught. Pilgrims would sometimes bring gifts, called votive offerings, with them as a donation to the destination site.
persona1_adapted:
One of the oldest reasons people travel is religious faith. Almost every major religion—like Christianity, Judaism, Islam, Buddhism, Hinduism, Jainism, and Sikhism—has a tradition of pilgrimage. A pilgrimage is a journey to a place that has spiritual meaning.

Christian pilgrims traveled to the Holy Land to follow the path of Christ. They also visited local sites connected to the lives or remains of saints. In Islam, the pilgrimage to Mecca, called hajj, is a main duty for all believers. People also visited other holy places on a regular basis.

For many centuries, hundreds of pilgrims from China, Japan, and Korea faced great dangers to visit places where the Historical Buddha lived and taught. Sometimes pilgrims brought gifts, called votive offerings, as donations to the site they visited.

CuratorialText: Trade was another common motivation for travel and occurred in many forms, but the most famous trade route in history was the Silk Road, the overland trade routes that linked the prosperous Tang empire (618–907) with Central, West, and South Asia. On the Silk Road, foreign merchants joined Buddhist missionaries, diplomatic envoys, translators, craftsmen, entertainers, and other skilled immigrants in a wealthy, worldly environment that offered a ready market for exotic imports, including silver and gold objects, delicate glassware, and even grape wine.
persona1_adapted: People also traveled for trade. Trade happened in many different ways. The most famous trade route in history was the Silk Road. This was a network of land routes that connected the powerful Tang empire (618–907) with Central, West, and South Asia. Along the Silk Road, foreign merchants traveled with Buddhist missionaries, diplomats, translators, craftspeople, entertainers, and other skilled newcomers. They all moved through a rich and worldly environment. This place had a strong demand for rare goods from other lands. These included items made of silver and gold, delicate glassware, and even grape wine.

CuratorialText: In the twentieth century, travel offered a key means for diplomacy and cultural exchange. Alice Roosevelt Longworth (1884–1980), Theodore Roosevelt’s daughter, embarked on a tour of Asia accompanying William Howard Taft’s goodwill mission in 1905. This voyage is immortalized in a series of photographs but also through diplomatic gifts that dignitaries, such as the emperor of Japan, bestowed on Roosevelt.
persona1_adapted: In the 1900s, travel was an important way for countries to build friendships and share cultures. Alice Roosevelt Longworth (1884–1980), the daughter of President Theodore Roosevelt, went on a trip to Asia in 1905. She joined William Howard Taft’s goodwill mission. This journey is remembered through many photos. It is also remembered through special gifts that leaders, like the emperor of Japan, gave to Roosevelt.

CuratorialText: Adventurers, archaeologists, and scholars have long been traversing the globe to conduct research and to explore foreign lands. In the early twentieth century, leisure travel among Europeans and North Americans also became increasingly common. Fascinated by the cultural heritage of Asia and the Middle East, tourists began to expand their horizons by venturing there.
persona1_adapted: Adventurers, archaeologists, and scholars have long traveled the world to study and explore new places. In the early 1900s, more Europeans and North Americans started traveling for fun. Tourists were fascinated by the culture and history of Asia and the Middle East, so they began to visit those regions.

CuratorialText: Artists who traveled played a pivotal role in documenting and popularizing their visions of the distant places they visited. Their interactions with other artists and the things they experienced on their journeys contributed to the dispersal of different artistic styles and motifs.
persona1_adapted: Traveling artists played a key role in recording and sharing their views of faraway places. The artists they met and the things they saw helped spread different art styles and ideas.

CuratorialText: While travel has mostly positive connotations in popular culture today, it’s important to remember the darker sides of travel as well. Not all travel is undertaken by choice: people can be forced to travel as a result of political exile, as well as due to war, famine, disease, economic hardship, environmental disaster, and colonialism. Even when travel is voluntary, it can be dangerous, and this was particularly true in ages past. The physical act of traveling exposed people to potential injuries and diseases, the natural elements were unpredictable and threatened modes of transportation, and there was an inherent unpredictability to being on the road far from home. The recognition of these dangers shaped stories and cultural beliefs.
persona1_adapted: Today, we often think of travel as a good thing. But travel also has a darker side. Not everyone travels by choice. People may be forced to leave home because of war, hunger, disease, money problems, natural disasters, or colonialism. Even when travel is voluntary, it can be risky. This was especially true in the past. Traveling put people in danger of getting hurt or sick. The weather was unpredictable and could damage ships or carts. Being far from home meant facing many unknowns. These dangers helped shape stories and beliefs about travel.

CuratorialText: Objects themselves have rich histories and often undertook journeys of their own, whether through trade, gift giving, or collecting. For conservators, the journey of an item is embedded within its material presence; marks, scars, repairs, and other interventions describe the actions of others who have contributed to its present state. The process of conservation involves the synthesis of material evidence and its placement in time, not just to understand an item’s past but to envision its future preservation. Anthropologists have described the trajectories of material items in terms of their lives, biographies, or itineraries, but such notions are intuitively familiar to conservators, whose actions are fundamentally additive to an item’s journey, both materially and through time.
persona1_adapted:
Objects have rich histories. They often traveled through trade, gifts, or collecting. For conservators—people who care for and preserve objects—an item’s journey shows in its materials. Marks, scars, and repairs tell us about others who shaped how it looks today.

Conservation means studying these material clues and placing them in time. It’s not just about understanding an object’s past. It’s also about planning how to keep it safe for the future.

Anthropologists describe an object’s path as its life, biography, or itinerary. These ideas feel natural to conservators. Their work always adds something new to an object’s journey, both in its materials and its history.

CuratorialText: Individual travelers carried things with them that were essential for the journey itself. Clothing and equipment suitable to traversing long distances sometimes set them apart from local residents, and travelers carried personal effects and accessories, such as small books, drinking flasks, and protective amulets, to make the journey easier.
persona1_adapted: Travelers brought items they needed for the trip. Their clothes and gear for long journeys sometimes made them stand out from locals. They also carried personal things like small books, drinking flasks, and protective charms to make traveling easier.

CuratorialText: Travelers brought objects acquired on their journeys home with them as well. Holy objects acquired by pilgrims allowed them to bring the blessings of their pilgrimage back from their destination. Images made during travel—whether sketches, paintings, or photographs—captured the experience of the journey for future reflection. Souvenirs purchased during a trip also offered travelers a way of commemorating a journey and gave them something tangible on which to reflect. The advent of commercial global travel networks in many cultures and contexts was accompanied by a flood of photographs, drawings, postcards, mementos, and other means of recording scientific and sentimental experiences abroad. Perhaps the most recognizable artifact of travel over the past 150 years is the picture postcard. These were collected as mementos or mailed to family and friends from abroad, accruing the additional prestige of a foreign stamp and postmark.
persona1_adapted: Travelers also brought home objects they found on their trips. Pilgrims brought back holy items to share the blessings of their journey. Images made during travel—like sketches, paintings, or photos—captured the experience so travelers could remember it later. Souvenirs bought on a trip helped travelers remember their journey and gave them something real to look back on. As global travel networks grew, people created many photos, drawings, postcards, and other items to record their adventures. The most familiar travel item from the last 150 years is the picture postcard. People collected them as keepsakes or mailed them to family and friends. A foreign stamp and postmark made the postcard even more special.

CuratorialText: Most travel we think of involves physically moving between places, but virtual travel (colloquially, “armchair travel”) that takes place in the mind spans time and cultures and is facilitated by books and works of art. Through focused contemplation of a travel account, a map, a cherished souvenir, or images of far-off places, memory and imagination combine to transport someone mentally and to simulate the experience of travel.
persona1_adapted: Most travel means moving your body from one place to another. But there is also virtual travel, sometimes called "armchair travel." This kind of travel happens in your mind. It can take you across time and through different cultures. Books and works of art help make this possible. When you look closely at a travel story, a map, a special souvenir, or pictures of faraway places, your memory and imagination work together. They can carry you away in your thoughts and make you feel like you have really traveled.

CuratorialText: While it may not be the first thing we think of when looking at a work of art, environmental factors have shaped everything about it, from how it was made, to how it has been perceived and appreciated, to how it has been preserved for today’s audiences. Dive into the complex and interconnected relationships between art and the environment—from images of the natural world to recycling ancient jades—to see our collections in a whole new way.
persona1_adapted: When you look at a work of art, you might not think about the environment first. But the environment has shaped everything about it. It affects how the art was made, how people saw and valued it, and how it was saved for us today. Explore the deep connections between art and the environment—from pictures of nature to the reuse of ancient jade objects—and see our collections in a fresh way.

CuratorialText: While environmental conditions differ from place to place, contact with nature has been a universal human experience. It’s therefore not surprising that natural motifs—plants, animals, water, the sky—adorn works of art across all cultures and are a consistent source of inspiration and meaning.
persona1_adapted: Nature looks different depending on where you live. But every human has experienced the natural world. That’s why plants, animals, water, and the sky show up in art from all cultures. These natural images have always inspired artists and carried deep meaning.

CuratorialText: Depictions of landscapes can serve as valuable documentation of specific places, but they can also evoke strong emotions. Images of the natural world can remind us of the beauty and power of nature, evoking feelings of tranquility, wonder, awe, and contemplation.
persona1_adapted: Landscape paintings can show us what a place really looks like. But they can also make us feel strong emotions. Pictures of nature can remind us how beautiful and powerful the natural world is. They might make us feel calm, amazed, or thoughtful.

CuratorialText: From the earliest Japanese poetry anthology, compiled in the eighth century, to the present day, the beauty of the Japanese landscape has been celebrated in literature and art. At first, landscapes were not an independent subject but were incorporated into the backgrounds of Buddhist paintings, seasonal subjects (shikinami-e), and paintings of famous scenic or sacred sites (meisho-e). Beginning around the thirteenth century, possibly through the influence of the introduction of new modes of ink painting from China and Korea, landscape became an independent subject in Japanese painting. Landscapes of Japan were most often, but not exclusively, painted in the full color mode, which effectively expressed the seasonal changes that are inextricably linked with natural imagery in Japanese poetry and art. Ink painting was usually chosen for varied and dramatic landscapes of China, which most Japanese artists understood through their study of imported paintings, and later, of painting manuals as well. Whether rendered in rich, deep colors or in subtle tones of ink, landscapes by Japanese artists are imbued with a sense of intimate understanding and appreciation of the transient beauty and spiritual significance of the natural world.
persona1_adapted:
The beauty of Japan’s landscape has been celebrated in art and literature for over a thousand years. The earliest Japanese poetry collection, from the 700s, already praised nature. At first, landscapes were not the main focus. They appeared in the background of Buddhist paintings, seasonal scenes, and images of famous places.

Around the 1200s, landscape became a subject of its own. This change may have come from new ink painting styles introduced from China and Korea. Japanese artists often painted their own country’s landscapes in bright, full color. These colors showed the changing seasons, which are closely tied to nature in Japanese poetry and art.

Ink painting was usually used for dramatic Chinese landscapes. Most Japanese artists learned about these scenes from imported paintings and later from painting manuals. Whether in rich colors or soft ink tones, Japanese landscape paintings show a deep understanding and appreciation of nature’s beauty and spiritual meaning.

CuratorialText: Travel to misty mountain valleys and foggy ocean beaches through the eyes of great Japanese artists from the sixteenth century on, accompanied by the sounds of the shakuhachi (bamboo flute), in this exploration of nature as an inspiration for Japanese art and music, guided by the virtuoso Kurahashi Yodo II and curator Frank Feltens.
persona1_adapted: Explore misty mountain valleys and foggy ocean beaches through the eyes of great Japanese artists from the 1500s onward. As you look, listen to the sounds of the shakuhachi, a bamboo flute. This exhibit shows how nature inspires Japanese art and music. Your guides are the master musician Kurahashi Yodo II and curator Frank Feltens.

CuratorialText: For Chinese scholar-officials, viewing landscape paintings not only provided aesthetic delight, but a landscape setting was often vested with meaning, which could range from providing a clue about the season of the painting to conveying more complex meanings. Seeing an image of figures in a remote landscape often referred to the key cultural and political concept of eremitism. The literati, or scholar class, believed in the Confucian notion that government service was a high moral duty. However, if the emperor was corrupt, or if a scholar was faced with serving a dynasty founded by usurping foreign rulers then, following the tradition of eremitism, a true gentleman refused government office. Instead, he became a mountain recluse, or at least assumed the demeanor of a hermit or rustic fisherman, to protest the corruption of moral order. Vested with meaning as a symbol of ethical reclusion, landscape motifs had poignant significance.
persona1_adapted:
For Chinese scholar-officials, looking at landscape paintings was more than just a beautiful experience. A landscape scene often carried a deeper meaning. It could hint at the season of the painting or express more complex ideas.

For example, showing people in a faraway landscape often pointed to a key cultural and political idea called eremitism. Eremitism is the practice of withdrawing from society to live alone, often as a form of protest.

The literati, or scholar class, believed in the Confucian idea that serving the government was a high moral duty. But if the emperor was corrupt, or if a scholar had to serve a dynasty founded by foreign rulers who had seized power, then a true gentleman would refuse to hold office. Following the tradition of eremitism, he would become a mountain recluse. Or at least, he would act like a hermit or a simple fisherman to protest the breakdown of moral order.

Because these landscape scenes were filled with meaning as symbols of ethical withdrawal, they held deep emotional significance.

CuratorialText: Careful observation of nature did not only translate to depictions of real places. Artists used their knowledge of the natural world to create imagined landscapes as well.
persona1_adapted: Artists didn't just paint real places by studying nature. They also used what they knew about the natural world to create imaginary landscapes.

CuratorialText: A full appreciation of Chinese art includes embracing the category of “scholar’s rocks,” alternatively called “spirit stones.”  Scholar’s rocks consist of wondrously shaped and textured rocks, often of limestone, that are collected for placement in a designed garden or are brought indoors for display in a scholar’s study. The size can range from twelve feet or more to tiny enough to be enclosed in a fist. Philosophic and religious texts as well as poetry have a long history of singling out mountains and rocks for special consideration. A saying from the time of Confucius (551 BCE–479 BCE) notes, “The wise take joy in water; the humane take joy in mountains.” At the same time, Daoists pictured mountains as sanctuaries or paradises for immortal beings and used minerals in elixirs for infinite life. Placement of evocative rocks in gardens and homes helped turn a mundane space into a paradise, or at least into an environment conducive to cultivating personal longevity or to the pursuit of eternal life.
persona1_adapted:
To fully enjoy Chinese art, it helps to understand a category called “scholar’s rocks,” also known as “spirit stones.” These are rocks with amazing shapes and textures, often made of limestone. People collect them to place in designed gardens or to display indoors in a scholar’s study. They can be as big as twelve feet tall or small enough to fit in your fist.

For a long time, Chinese philosophy, religion, and poetry have focused on mountains and rocks. A saying from the time of Confucius (551 BCE–479 BCE) says, “The wise take joy in water; the humane take joy in mountains.” Daoists saw mountains as safe places or paradises for immortal beings. They also used minerals in special drinks meant to give eternal life.

Placing these special rocks in gardens and homes helped turn an ordinary space into a paradise. It could also create a setting that encouraged long life or the search for eternal life.

CuratorialText: In Chinese tradition, the educated class delighted in rocks as tangible embodiments of the powers of creation and cosmic transformation, and they sometimes rhapsodized about them as eternally faithful “friends.” Gazing upon them could encourage imaginary travel deep into nature. Chinese scholars, poets, painters, and emperors all engaged in collecting rocks, and painters often studied the rocks they collected, treating them as both muse and model for their landscape paintings.
persona1_adapted: In Chinese tradition, educated people loved rocks because they saw them as real examples of nature's power and change. Some even called rocks their forever loyal "friends." Looking at rocks could make the mind wander deep into nature. Chinese scholars, poets, painters, and emperors all collected rocks. Painters often studied the rocks they owned. They used these rocks as both inspiration and a model for their landscape paintings.

CuratorialText: From intimate courtyards to monumental temple, tomb, and pleasure gardens, Asia has been central to the development of cultivated landscapes. The earliest known garden—the biblical Garden of Eden—may have been located in West Asia at the confluence of the Tigris and the Euphrates in present-day Iraq. The very word “paradise” is derived from the walled orchard gardens and hunting parks of ancient Iran, referred to as pardis. This type of enclosed garden became an integral part of Persian architectural landscape for centuries and also often included pavilions to provide shade and relief from the sun. According to written sources, the earliest gardens in China, dating to the Zhou period (ca. 1050–256 BCE), consisted of enclosed hunting grounds reserved for the royal elite. Chinese gardens were often characterized by carefully positioned rocks and pools intended to recreate microcosms of nature at large. In Japan, gardens followed a more naturalistic design and incorporated rolling hills and languid ponds to underline harmony between humans and their surroundings. In the arid landscapes of West and South Asia, one of the most common garden plans depended on a series of interconnected pools and axial watercourses.
persona1_adapted:
Asia has played a major role in shaping how people design gardens. These gardens range from small, private courtyards to huge temple, tomb, and pleasure gardens. The earliest known garden might be the Garden of Eden from the Bible. It may have been located in West Asia, where the Tigris and Euphrates rivers meet in modern-day Iraq.

The word "paradise" comes from ancient Iran. There, people built walled gardens and hunting parks called *pardis*. These enclosed gardens became a key part of Persian architecture for centuries. They often included small buildings called pavilions, which offered shade and relief from the sun.

In China, the earliest gardens date back to the Zhou period (around 1050–256 BCE). Written records show they were enclosed hunting grounds for royalty. Chinese gardens often used carefully placed rocks and pools. These features were meant to recreate small versions of nature.

Japanese gardens followed a more natural design. They included rolling hills and calm ponds. This style highlighted harmony between people and their environment.

In the dry landscapes of West and South Asia, a common garden plan used a series of connected pools and straight water channels.

CuratorialText: Throughout history, art has served as a reflection of and response to environmental change. Sometimes this change is deliberately brought about through human beings adapting to and altering their surrounding environment. Cultural landscapes—including the ways that humans have built on, altered, and imagined natural environments—play a significant role in how we understand the world we inhabit.
persona1_adapted: Throughout history, art has shown how people respond to changes in the environment. Sometimes, people cause these changes by adapting to or reshaping the world around them. Cultural landscapes—how humans have built on, changed, and imagined nature—help us understand the world we live in.

CuratorialText: Works of art can bear witness to the environmental changes that have taken place throughout recorded history. Depictions of animals from regions where those animals no longer exist, for example, can tell us about migrations or extinctions.
persona1_adapted: Art can show us how the environment has changed over time. For example, paintings of animals in places where those animals no longer live can tell us about animal movements or extinctions.

CuratorialText: Humans have always had an impact on the environments where they live, but the scale of that impact dramatically increased with industrialization. In the United States and Europe, factories and advances in transportation sprung up to create a range of consumer goods and ways to travel with new ease and speed. Largely coal powered, these machines spewed pollution that artists such as Whistler sought to visualize in paint.
persona1_adapted: People have always affected the places where they live. But that impact grew much bigger with industrialization. In the United States and Europe, factories and new ways to travel appeared. They made many consumer goods and let people move faster and easier. These machines mostly ran on coal. They released pollution that artists like Whistler tried to show in their paintings.

CuratorialText: Similarly, the infusion of Western technology into Japan from the beginning of the Meiji era (1868–1912) spurred widespread visual documentation of the new developments. Railroad transportation, telegraphic communication, gas-fueled lighting, steam-powered river and ocean vessels, and new materials and designs for bridges and buildings were crammed into the Japanese landscape over two decades.
persona1_adapted: When Western technology entered Japan at the start of the Meiji era (1868–1912), people began making many pictures and records of these new changes. In just twenty years, Japan’s landscape filled with railroads, telegraphs, gas lights, steamships, and new types of bridges and buildings.

CuratorialText: Contemporary artists are increasingly exploring the impact of climate change on the natural world and human communities, whether their goal is to raise awareness about the urgent need to address climate change, to highlight the social and political issues that are bound up in environmental degradation, or to envision alternative futures and inspire hope and action.
persona1_adapted: Today’s artists are looking closely at how climate change affects nature and people. Some want to raise awareness about why we need to act now. Others focus on the social and political problems tied to damage to the environment. Still, some imagine different futures to inspire hope and action.

CuratorialText: The creation of art itself impacts and is impacted by the environment. Stones and precious metals need to be mined. Wood needs to be harvested. Clay is extracted from the earth. Plants need to be gathered. Many works of art make use of animal products, such as parchment, feathers, horn, and ivory. Different production techniques require burning fuel sources for melting, firing, and glazing. The gathering of raw materials and their transformation into art objects is not without impact on the environment and has at times come at the expense of the exploitation of natural resources and people.
persona1_adapted: Making art affects the environment, and the environment affects art. Stones and precious metals must be mined. Wood needs to be cut down. Clay is taken from the earth. Plants have to be collected. Many artworks use animal products like parchment, feathers, horn, and ivory. Different methods of making art require burning fuel for melting, firing, and glazing. Getting raw materials and turning them into art objects does have an impact on the environment. Sometimes this has led to the overuse of natural resources and the mistreatment of people.

CuratorialText: While recycling may seem like a very recent phenomenon, materials have long been reused in the creation of works of art, whether due to the scarcity of materials or to the value placed on an existing object. For example, since jade was such a prestigious medium and a valuable commodity in ancient China, broken or out-of-fashion articles were not discarded but were instead recycled to make new objects. This appears to be especially true in the late Shang dynasty (ca. 1250–1050 BCE). Archaeologists working at the late Shang capital of Anyang have found that, possibly due to the scarcity of resources at a time of high demand, earlier Stone Age (ca. 5000–1700 BCE) objects were being transformed into decorated jewelry and other items that held more interest and meaning to the Shang people. All three pendants shown here were made by cutting up a special kind of Neolithic square tube called a cong, which had lost its ritual significance by Shang times. It is noteworthy that the recycled cong used here represent different Stone Age cultures dispersed across north and south China.
persona1_adapted:
Recycling might seem like a modern idea, but people have reused materials to make art for a very long time. Sometimes they did this because materials were hard to find. Other times, they valued the original object itself.

In ancient China, jade was a precious material. So when jade items broke or went out of style, people did not throw them away. Instead, they recycled the jade to create new objects. This was especially common during the late Shang dynasty (around 1250–1050 BCE).

Archaeologists working at the Shang capital of Anyang found evidence of this. They think that because jade was scarce and in high demand, people took older Stone Age objects (from about 5000–1700 BCE) and turned them into decorated jewelry and other items. These new objects had more meaning for the Shang people.

The three pendants shown here were all made by cutting up a special type of Neolithic square tube called a cong. By Shang times, the cong had lost its original ritual meaning. It is interesting to note that the recycled cong used here came from different Stone Age cultures across northern and southern China.

CuratorialText: Environmental conditions can significantly impact the preservation of works of art. Factors such as temperature, humidity, light, and pollution affect the chemical and physical properties of materials in different ways, causing damage and degradation to objects over time. On a larger scale, major environmental changes such as temperature shifts and more destructive weather patterns can threaten heritage sites around the world. Conservators play a pivotal role in safeguarding the objects in the museum’s collections. Read about conservation and scientific research initiatives at the National Museum of Asian Art. The Smithsonian Institution acknowledges climate change as the existential threat of our time. Learn more about the Smithsonian’s sustainability initiatives.
persona1_adapted: The environment can have a big effect on how well artworks survive. Things like temperature, humidity, light, and pollution affect the materials in art in different ways. Over time, these factors can cause damage and wear. On a larger scale, major changes in the environment—like shifting temperatures and more extreme weather—can threaten historic sites around the world. Conservators play a key role in protecting the objects in the museum’s collections. Read about conservation and scientific research at the National Museum of Asian Art. The Smithsonian Institution sees climate change as one of the biggest threats of our time. Learn more about the Smithsonian’s sustainability efforts.

CuratorialText: A color of vitality, passion, good fortune, and celebration, red has undeniable energy. It catches our eye and stirs our blood, forcing us to take notice. From glittering stones to ruddy earth, from insects to minerals, learn about various ways that people have made and understood red through examples drawn from across the museum’s collections.
persona1_adapted: Red is a color full of life, strong feelings, good luck, and celebration. It has an energy you can’t ignore. Red catches your eye and gets your heart pumping. It makes you pay attention. People have made red from shiny gems, reddish soil, insects, and minerals. Look at examples from the museum’s collections to see how different cultures have created and understood this powerful color.

CuratorialText: Several metal compounds (that include iron, mercury, or lead) form commonly used historic colored pigments. These compounds may be found, mined, or produced. Iron compounds in earth pigments can have different colors. Red iron earths are readily found or can be manufactured and have been discovered in many applications, including pottery and paints.
persona1_adapted: Many old paints and glazes get their colors from metal compounds. These compounds often contain iron, mercury, or lead. People found them in nature, mined them, or made them. Iron compounds in earth pigments can create different colors. Red iron earths are easy to find or make. People have used them in many ways, such as in pottery and paint.

CuratorialText: Similarly, mercury-based pigments are made from grinding the red mineral cinnabar. Synthetic versions have long been produced and are referred to as vermilion. This deep red pigment, though toxic, was used in burial ceremonies, incorporated in lacquers, and adorns paintings and documents.
persona1_adapted: Mercury-based pigments come from grinding up a red mineral called cinnabar. People have made fake versions of this pigment for a long time, and they call it vermilion. This deep red color is poisonous, but it was still used in burial ceremonies, added to lacquers, and painted onto artworks and documents.

CuratorialText: While red lead has a naturally forming mineral equivalent (minimum), it was manufactured far more commonly for use as a pigment. In general, lead compounds were heated under conditions favorable to produce the desired colored material and minimizing the formation of others.
persona1_adapted: Red lead is a pigment that people usually made in a lab, even though it also exists as a natural mineral called minium. To make it, workers heated lead compounds in a controlled way. This process helped create the right color and stopped other unwanted colors from forming.

CuratorialText: Red copper oxide or cuprite is found as a degradation product on bronzes and forms by the reaction of the copper from the bronze and the atmosphere.
persona1_adapted: Red copper oxide, also called cuprite, forms on bronze objects. It happens when the copper in the bronze reacts with the air.

CuratorialText: Plants and animals can also be sources of reds used in artworks. Madder root and safflower each may have their color extracted, purified, and prepared for use in objects such as textiles and prints. The conditions in which the dye or lake is prepared can change the final color. Scale insects (such as lac and cochineal) are another source. Such insects were harvested, with the red color extracted and prepared for use as a pigment or dye. When possible, determining the colorants used in artworks can shed light on historical trade routes due to the limited native habitat of these insects.
persona1_adapted: Plants and animals can also give us reds for art. People can take color from madder root and safflower. They clean it and get it ready for use in things like fabric and prints. How the dye or lake is made can change the final color. Scale insects, like lac and cochineal, are another source. People collected these bugs. They took out the red color and got it ready to use as a pigment, which is a coloring material. When possible, finding out what colorants were used in artworks can tell us about old trade routes. That’s because these insects only live in certain areas.

CuratorialText:
Dried cochineal insects. Image courtesy of Conservation and Scientific Research, National Museum of Asian Art, Smithsonian Institution
Red also occurred naturally in the form of precious and semi-precious stones, such as rubies, garnets, spinels, and carnelians. Prized for their color and rarity, they were valuable gifts and were often worn by elites or used to adorn luxury items.
persona1_adapted: Red also came from natural sources like rubies, garnets, spinels, and carnelians. These are precious and semi-precious stones. People valued them for their color and rarity. They made great gifts and were often worn by wealthy people or used to decorate luxury objects.

CuratorialText: Rubrication, the practice of writing portions of a text in red ink, helped visually underscore certain information and became particularly common in European manuscripts after the seventh century. Red was sometimes used for the angular Kufic script in in copies of the Qur’an made between the eighth and eleventh centuries CE to indicate vocalization; it stood out next to black- or brown-inked calligraphy. Red was also commonly used in the paste for the inked seals that were added to finished works of Chinese painting or calligraphy; it contrasted against black ink writing and images. The seal might give the name of the artist or his studio, the collector, or other private individuals, and it occasionally expresses a favorite brief saying.
persona1_adapted:
Rubrication means writing parts of a text in red ink. It helped highlight important information. After the 7th century, this practice became common in European manuscripts.

In copies of the Qur’an made between the 8th and 11th centuries CE, red was sometimes used for the angular Kufic script. This showed how to pronounce words. The red stood out next to black or brown ink calligraphy.

Red was also used in the paste for inked seals on finished Chinese paintings or calligraphy. The red seal contrasted with black ink writing and images. The seal might show the artist’s name, the studio name, the collector’s name, or another person’s name. Sometimes it included a short favorite saying.

CuratorialText: Love, passion, importance—red carries many positive associations today, and has throughout history. In ancient Egypt, pharaohs were depicted in red due to its association with royalty and passage through the afterlife. In China, red is auspicious—associated with life-generating energy (the sun, blood, and fire)—and is the color of celebrations and prosperity.
persona1_adapted: Red has many positive meanings today, and it has had them throughout history. People often connect it with love, passion, and importance. In ancient Egypt, artists showed pharaohs in red. This color was linked to royalty and the journey through the afterlife. In China, red is a lucky color. It is tied to life-giving energy, like the sun, blood, and fire. Red is also the color of celebrations and wealth.

CuratorialText: Red is still associated with festivities and happiness in Chinese culture today. Learn more about Lunar New Year, when families decorate windows with red paper cuttings and adorn doors with couplets expressing auspicious wishes. Watch and learn how Chinese paper cuttings are made, from the Smithsonian Center for Folklife and Cultural Heritage.
persona1_adapted: In Chinese culture today, red is still linked to celebrations and happiness. Discover more about Lunar New Year. During this holiday, families decorate windows with red paper cuttings. They also hang couplets on doors that express good wishes. Watch a video from the Smithsonian Center for Folklife and Cultural Heritage to learn how Chinese paper cuttings are made.

CuratorialText: Red can carry more negative associations as well, such as with warfare, rage, and destruction. The color of blood, there is a visceral quality to red that resonates uniquely with the human psyche. Haunted by the traces that the human body leaves behind, contemporary artist Chiharu Shiota’s 2015 installation “Over the Continents” amassed personal memories through an accumulation of nearly four hundred individual shoes.
