Collected by the artist, each shoe came with a note from the donor describing lost individuals and past moments. Almost four miles of red yarn wrapped around the shoes and attached to a single point in the museum’s Sackler pavilion, evoking the artist’s attention to the fundamental ties that connect humans to one another and the world.
persona1_adapted:
Red can also have negative meanings. It can stand for war, anger, and destruction. Red is the color of blood. It has a strong, physical effect on how people feel.

Artist Chiharu Shiota is interested in the marks people leave behind. In her 2015 artwork “Over the Continents,” she collected nearly 400 shoes. Each shoe came with a note from the person who gave it. The notes described lost people and past memories.

Shiota wrapped the shoes in almost four miles of red yarn. The yarn was attached to one point in the museum’s Sackler pavilion. This shows how the artist thinks about the basic connections that link people to each other and to the world.

CuratorialText: Whether it’s in the form of growing plants, precious stones, or deliberately blended pigments, there’s a human impulse to surround ourselves with green in the art we create, the environments we inhabit, and the spaces we build. Green evokes fertile landscapes, growth and prosperity, and even health and wellness. Learn about how green has been made and understood across various cultures and traditions represented in the museum’s collections.
persona1_adapted: People have always wanted to include green in the art they make and the spaces they live in. Green can come from plants, gems, or mixed paints. The color reminds us of rich landscapes, growth, success, and even health. Explore how different cultures and traditions in the museum’s collection have created and understood the color green.

CuratorialText: While there aren’t many plant sources of green dyes in the art world, some Indian paintings use beetle wings to mimic shiny green emeralds. In very special works of art, emeralds could be carved to make unique objects, or were made into jewelry.
persona1_adapted: Green dyes from plants are rare in art. Some Indian paintings use beetle wings to look like shiny green emeralds. In very special artworks, emeralds were carved into unique objects or made into jewelry.

CuratorialText: The green patina on copper and bronze objects occurs as the copper corrodes. Patina occurs naturally when copper comes into contact with salty or acidic surroundings. See, for instance, how a range of corrosion products affects the surface color of the ancient Chinese bronze ritual vessel below: most of the surface is covered with a smooth, tin oxide-stained pale green with copper compounds, but there are also numerous scattered small patches of dull red cuprite, and the bright, powdery green seen here and there represents copper chloride. Explore this object in 3D.
persona1_adapted: The green coating on copper and bronze objects forms as the copper wears away over time. This coating, called a patina, happens naturally when copper touches salty or acidic environments. Look at the ancient Chinese bronze ritual vessel below. You can see how different types of wear change the surface color. Most of the surface has a smooth, pale green color from tin oxide and copper compounds. There are also small patches of dull red cuprite scattered around. The bright, powdery green spots you see here and there come from copper chloride. Explore this object in 3D.

CuratorialText: Copper corrosion products can be taken advantage of to create man-made copper green pigments; more commonly, however, the green mineral malachite can be ground to make paints. Malachite is often used in paintings in China.
persona1_adapted: Artists can use copper rust to make green paint. But more often, they grind up a green mineral called malachite. Malachite is commonly used in Chinese paintings.

CuratorialText:
Fine-ground malachite. Image courtesy of Conservation and Scientific Research, National Museum of Asian Art, Smithsonian Institution
Copper, together with lead, is often the basis of green glazes in ceramics. Mixtures of copper with yellow particles of lead tin oxide or lead antimony oxide are also used for green enamels.
persona1_adapted:
Fine-ground malachite. Image courtesy of Conservation and Scientific Research, National Museum of Asian Art, Smithsonian Institution

Copper and lead are often the main ingredients in green glazes for ceramics. To make green enamels, artists also mix copper with tiny yellow particles. These yellow particles can be lead tin oxide or lead antimony oxide.

CuratorialText: “Celadon” refers to a large family of high-fired ceramics. Their glazes are colored by small percentages of iron oxide. When fired in an oxygen-deprived, or reducing, atmosphere, the glazes turn variable shades of bluish green, traditionally described with the French word céladon. The word is commonly believed to have been derived from a seventeenth-century French drama, L’Astrée, in which a shepherd named Céladon appears dressed in a gray-green robe. Some modern scholars prefer the term “green ware,” but the poetic French name is more likely to remind a reader that celadon ceramics can exhibit a broad range of colors, from sky blue to sea green.
persona1_adapted: "Celadon" is a name for a large group of high-fired ceramics. These ceramics have glazes that get their color from small amounts of iron oxide. When they are fired in a kiln with little oxygen, the glazes turn different shades of bluish green. This color is called céladon in French. Many people believe the word comes from a 1600s French play, L’Astrée. In the play, a shepherd named Céladon wears a gray-green robe. Some modern scholars prefer the term "green ware." But the poetic French name helps remind us that celadon ceramics can range in color from sky blue to sea green.

CuratorialText: Jade was the first true luxury medium to emerge in ancient China. Although the term is used to refer to a range of hard, beautifully figured stones, most Chinese Neolithic, Bronze Age, and early imperial period examples have been scientifically identified as nephrite, geologically different from the bright, apple green jadeite used in much later times. Composed of interlocked, fibrous crystals and occurring as pebbles and boulders in a range of colors (due to iron, chromium, manganese, or similar materials fused in the rock), nephrite is an incredibly hard stone. It cannot be carved or even scratched by metal but instead must be ground into shape using quartz and other tough minerals. These qualities and its rarity inspired ancient Chinese people to value it very highly and to reserve its use for special purposes, such as for jewelry and ceremonial and ritual objects.
persona1_adapted: Jade was the first truly valuable luxury material in ancient China. The word "jade" can refer to many different hard, beautiful stones. However, most jade from ancient China is actually a type called nephrite. This is different from the bright green jadeite that became popular much later. Nephrite is made of tightly packed, fibrous crystals. It comes in many colors because of minerals like iron, chromium, or manganese mixed into the rock. Nephrite is extremely hard. You cannot carve or scratch it with metal. Instead, people had to grind it into shape using quartz and other tough minerals. Because nephrite was so rare and hard to work with, ancient Chinese people valued it very highly. They saved it for special uses, like making jewelry and objects for ceremonies and rituals.

CuratorialText: According to his close friend Agnes Meyer, when Charles Lang Freer was ill near the end of his life, he often held a piece of jade in his hand, believing with an almost religious faith in the comforting and restorative powers of the stone. Freer collected ancient Chinese jades in large numbers, when most other Western collectors showed little interest. Ultimately, there were more than 500 pieces in his collection, spanning more than 5,000 years.
persona1_adapted: According to his close friend Agnes Meyer, Charles Lang Freer often held a piece of jade in his hand when he was sick near the end of his life. He believed, almost like a religious faith, that the stone could comfort and heal him. Freer collected many ancient Chinese jades, even though most other Western collectors were not interested in them. In the end, his collection had more than 500 pieces, covering more than 5,000 years of history.

CuratorialText: Green is so prevalent in the flora of the natural world that the color easily conjures lush environments teeming with plants. Life, growth, and regeneration are all concepts that come to mind in the context of verdant landscapes. In Southeast Asia, during the ninth through the thirteenth century, constructing cities required carving out spaces from the verdant, tropical environment. The jungle had to be systematically cultivated; water had to be diverted into reservoirs, moats, and canals; and the landscape had to be carefully tended. When population centers shifted due to political, ecological, and religious changes, the tropical landscape readily encroached on the built environment, transforming once urban landscapes back into jungle. This environment informed Southeast Asian artists: on temple walls and sculptures, plants and flowers grow on thick stalks, vines curlicue, and sprays of foliage transform surfaces into lush landscapes.
persona1_adapted:
Green is everywhere in nature. It makes us think of lush, plant-filled environments. When we see green landscapes, we often think about life, growth, and new beginnings.

Between the 9th and 13th centuries, people in Southeast Asia built cities by cutting spaces out of the tropical jungle. They had to carefully clear the land, move water into reservoirs, moats, and canals, and take care of the landscape. When cities moved or were abandoned because of political, environmental, or religious changes, the jungle quickly grew back. It turned old cities back into forest.

This environment inspired Southeast Asian artists. On temple walls and sculptures, they carved plants and flowers with thick stalks, curly vines, and sprays of leaves. These designs turned flat surfaces into rich, green landscapes.

CuratorialText: Set in a garden, the image depicts a group of angels, referred to as huris or paris in Persian, entertaining their queen by playing music, dancing, and serving food and wine. The drawing's lively mood echoes other Persian representations of outdoor banquets or bazm (a form of celebration with wine and music) ceremonies, with the exception that this fine drawing features heavenly creatures--a favorite subject matter in sixteenth-and seventeenth-century Iran and Turkey--instead of the princely figures seen in illustrations to lyrical poetry by Hafiz and Assar.
persona1_adapted: This scene takes place in a garden. It shows a group of angels, called *huris* or *paris* in Persian. They are entertaining their queen by playing music, dancing, and serving food and wine. The drawing has a lively mood. It is similar to other Persian artworks that show outdoor banquets or *bazm* celebrations. A *bazm* is a party with wine and music. However, this drawing is special because it shows heavenly creatures instead of princes. In the 1500s and 1600s, artists in Iran and Turkey loved to paint angels. Usually, princes appeared in illustrations for poems by Hafiz and Assar.

CuratorialText: Referred to as peri winged celestial creatures are among some of the most popular subjects in sixteenth-century Ottoman and Persian drawings and paintings. The exquisitely rendered composition typifies a distinct style of Ottoman drawing that combines sweeping, calligraphic lines with soft, feathery strokes, as is evident in the minute designs on the figure's robe, cup, and bottle. Light washes of color add a sense of volume and texture to the form of this heavenly being.
persona1_adapted: Artists in the 1500s often drew and painted winged celestial creatures called peri. These were popular subjects in Ottoman and Persian art. This drawing shows a typical Ottoman style. It combines flowing, calligraphic lines with soft, feathery strokes. You can see this in the tiny patterns on the figure's robe, cup, and bottle. Light washes of color give the heavenly being a sense of shape and texture.

CuratorialText: Four-part inro in form of flattened rectangle with rounded corners (probably derived from the coin called ryo). The inset rim of each of the three compartments fits into the base of the compartment above it or into the lid. The four components are secured by a blue-green silk cord passing through two holes in each of the elements, tied in a bow below and secured by the bead (ojime) above and atttached to the toggle (netsuke) at the end.
persona1_adapted: This is a four-part inro. An inro is a small container worn on a belt. This one is shaped like a flat rectangle with rounded corners. Its shape probably comes from an old coin called a ryo. The inro has three compartments stacked on top of each other. The rim of each compartment fits into the one above it or into the lid. A blue-green silk cord holds all four parts together. The cord passes through two holes in each piece. It is tied in a bow at the bottom. A small bead, called an ojime, secures the cord above. At the end of the cord is a toggle, called a netsuke.

CuratorialText: Box, small cylindrical, with cover of equal size with the box proper. Clay: hard, grayish. Glaze: pale greenish-gray celadon, with faint lilac tints. Decoration: inlaid in white and black under glaze: medallion of two flying cranes surrounded by geometric borders.
persona1_adapted: This is a small, round box with a lid that is the same size as the box itself. It is made of hard, grayish clay. The glaze is a pale greenish-gray color called celadon, with faint hints of lilac. The decoration is inlaid under the glaze using white and black materials. It shows a medallion with two flying cranes, surrounded by geometric borders.

CuratorialText: A straight-sided cup which angles inward about three-quarters of the way toward a flattened bottom. The walls of the cup are very thin and this type of ware has been referred to as egg-shell ware. The fabric is a pinkish beige color. The inside of the cup is unpainted. The details on the outside of the cup are painted in brownish-black and red on an upainted background. The same motif of a red lotus bud shape outlined in brownish-black and tied with ribbons of the same brownish-black color is repeated seven times around the cup. The ornament color is repeated seven times around the cup. The ornament extends from the edge of the cup to about the middle of the cup. Within the cup are remains of a black powdery substance.
persona1_adapted:
This cup has straight sides that curve inward near the bottom. The walls are very thin. This type of pottery is sometimes called "egg-shell ware" because it is so delicate. The cup is made of a pinkish-beige clay. The inside is not painted.

On the outside, the artist painted details in brownish-black and red on the unpainted clay. A red lotus bud shape, outlined in brownish-black, appears seven times around the cup. Ribbons of the same brownish-black color tie the lotus buds. The decoration runs from the rim to about the middle of the cup.

Inside the cup, there are remains of a black, powdery substance.

CuratorialText: This is one of several paintings of shop fronts that Whistler completed while in St. Ives. Sympathetic critics singled it out for enthusiastic praise when Whistler exhibited it in London in 1884. One declared, it "is probably the most perfect little thing of its kind that was ever wrought by an artist who has learnt how to see….It is without detail, without apparent labour, without dramatic interest; but it is exquisite in colour, faultless in tone, and its well-considered mystery has, at least, the interest of suggestiveness."
persona1_adapted: Whistler painted several pictures of shop fronts while he was in St. Ives. This is one of them. When he showed it in London in 1884, some supportive critics gave it high praise. One critic said, "This is probably the most perfect little painting of its kind ever made by an artist who has learned how to see. It has no small details. It does not look like hard work. It has no dramatic story. But its colors are beautiful. Its shades of light and dark are perfect. And its thoughtful mystery makes you wonder."

CuratorialText:
Plate with a flat rim and peacock in the center. Clay: buff-colored. Glaze: transparent over blue, green, red and black painted over a white paste. Decoration: Peacock amidst flowers in the center with a scroll of half-palmettes on the rim; eight blossoms on the exterior.
Greek letters on label: (^o^) and No. 48.
persona1_adapted: This plate has a flat rim. In the center, there is a painted peacock. The plate is made of buff-colored clay. A clear glaze covers the surface. Under the glaze, artists painted blue, green, red, and black colors on a white base. The center shows a peacock surrounded by flowers. The rim has a pattern of curved, leaf-like shapes called half-palmettes. On the outside of the plate, there are eight flower blossoms. A label on the plate has Greek letters and the number 48.

CuratorialText: Molded Yue-ware vessels like this were made in large quantities, but their function is unclear. They may have held a candle, or perhaps scholars dipped their writing brushes in the water-filled interior. In the Jin dynasty (265-420 CE), mythical lions, with exaggeratedly curled manes, and winged chimeras were protective images; huge stone examples guarded important tombs.
persona1_adapted: Molded Yue-ware vessels like this one were made in large numbers. Their exact use is unknown. They might have held a candle. Or maybe scholars dipped their writing brushes into the water inside. During the Jin dynasty (265-420 CE), people used protective images. These included mythical lions with big, curly manes and winged chimeras. Large stone versions of these creatures guarded important tombs.

CuratorialText: A pair of blue and white molded porcelain beaker vases [F1992.47.1 and .2] decorated with freely drawn feather, foliate scroll and scattered flowerheads, each with a leaf or conch shell "emblem" mark on base. Part of a five-piece garniture set with three jars.
persona1_adapted: These two blue and white porcelain vases are shaped like tall cups. They are decorated with hand-painted feathers, curling leaves, and scattered flower heads. On the bottom of each vase, there is a small mark shaped like a leaf or a seashell. These vases were part of a five-piece set that also included three jars.

CuratorialText:
The Kiyotaki Waterfall is located along the route of the Tokaido, the great road passing through the eastern provinces of Japan and linking the city of Edo (now Tokyo), seat of the Tokugawa shogunate, with Kyoto, the capital and home of the emperor.
In this print from the series, Shokoku taki meguri (Famous Waterfalls in Various Provinces), the Kiyotaki Waterfall descends in branching rivulets over a sheer, rock face. In the foreground, pilgrims ascend from a teahouse toward a shrine dedicated to Kannon, a Buddhist deity associated with compassion. The remoteness and altitude of this scene is emphasized by the bands of clouds at the lower left.
persona1_adapted:
The Kiyotaki Waterfall sits along the Tokaido road. This was a major route in Japan that connected Edo (now Tokyo) to Kyoto. Edo was the seat of the Tokugawa shogunate, while Kyoto was the capital and home of the emperor.

This print is from a series called *Shokoku taki meguri* (Famous Waterfalls in Various Provinces). In the image, the Kiyotaki Waterfall flows down a steep rock face in small, branching streams. In the front of the scene, pilgrims walk up from a teahouse toward a shrine. The shrine is dedicated to Kannon, a Buddhist deity of compassion. The clouds in the lower left corner show how remote and high up this place is.

CuratorialText: Small dish with pleated rim. Clay: buff pottery. Glaze: transparent glaze with olive greenish tone (Ofuke glaze), large irregular cracks stained brown; glaze deeper green in tone where thick. Interior of footrim glazed; glazed wiped off outside of footrim and surrounding base. Decoration: pleating on narrow everted rim. The form of this dish with fluted rim distantly echoes the shape of Chinese Longquan-ware celadon dishes.
persona1_adapted: This is a small dish with a pleated, or folded, rim. It is made from buff-colored clay. The dish has a transparent glaze with an olive-green tint. This type of glaze is called Ofuke glaze. The glaze has large, uneven cracks that are stained brown. The glaze looks darker green where it is thicker. The inside of the footrim, or the base ring, is glazed. The glaze was wiped off the outside of the footrim and the area around the base. The decoration is the pleating on the narrow, outward-turned rim. The shape of this dish, with its fluted rim, is a distant echo of Chinese Longquan-ware celadon dishes.

CuratorialText:
The Khamsa, or Quintet, by Nizami ranks among the great masterpieces of Persian literature. The romance of Khosraw and Shirin, one of the five poems that constitute the Khamsa, narrates the adventures of the Sasanian king Khusraw Parviz and the Armenian princess Shirin. Attributed to the early fifteenth century, the Freer Khamsa is one of the earliest known illustrated copies of this work.
Khosraw at the Gate of Shirin's Castle is among the most poignantly expressive manuscript paintings of the fifteenth century. Displeased with Khosraw's behavior and his marriage to another woman, Shirin has withdrawn to her castle. Following the death of his queen, Khosraw repeatedly attempts to see Shirin, who finally yields to his request. Shielded by the royal umbrella and standing on precious brocades and carpets, the king gestures in supplication toward Shirin, who gazes down from a window in her castle. Although the text specifies that Khosraw visited Shirin at dawn in the dead of winter, the artist has situated the reconciliation in a lush garden setting softly illuminated by a waning moon. The elegant, mustachioed Khosraw in these paintings bears a striking resemblance to known depictions of the Timurid patron Baysunghur Mirza (1399-1434).
persona1_adapted:
The *Khamsa* (or *Quintet*) by Nizami is one of the great masterpieces of Persian literature. It contains five poems. One of them is the romance of Khosraw and Shirin. This poem tells the story of the Sasanian king Khusraw Parviz and the Armenian princess Shirin. The Freer *Khamsa* was made around the early 1400s. It is one of the earliest known copies of this work that includes pictures.

*Khosraw at the Gate of Shirin's Castle* is one of the most emotional and expressive paintings from the 1400s. Shirin is upset with Khosraw because of his behavior and his marriage to another woman. So she has gone to her castle and refuses to see him. After his queen dies, Khosraw tries many times to visit Shirin. Finally, she agrees to meet him. In the painting, the king stands under a royal umbrella on expensive carpets and cloth. He gestures and begs Shirin to forgive him. Shirin looks down at him from a window in her castle. The poem says that Khosraw visited Shirin at dawn in the middle of winter. But the artist shows the meeting in a lush garden, softly lit by a fading moon. The elegant Khosraw, with his mustache, looks very much like known images of the Timurid prince Baysunghur Mirza (1399–1434), who may have paid for the book.

CuratorialText: Set in the London home of Whistler's brother-in-law Seymour Haden, this painting was originally titled The Morning Call. The woman reflected in the mirror at the left is Whistler's half sister Deborah Haden. The seated girl is his niece, Annie Haden. The standing woman, Miss Isabella Boott, was connected to the Haden family by marriage. Instead of trying to create a convincing illusion of three-dimensional space, Whistler adopted a color scheme and sharply angled perspective that emphasize the flatness of the picture plane.
persona1_adapted: This painting takes place in the London home of Whistler’s brother-in-law, Seymour Haden. It was first called *The Morning Call*. The woman you see in the mirror on the left is Whistler’s half sister, Deborah Haden. The girl sitting down is his niece, Annie Haden. The woman standing is Miss Isabella Boott. She was related to the Haden family by marriage. Whistler did not try to make the scene look deeply three-dimensional. Instead, he used a specific color scheme and a sharp, angled view. This choice makes the painting’s flat surface more obvious.

CuratorialText: Popular tales and lively images played a role in transmitting beliefs and values to the Jain community. The Shripalarasa is a narrative that recounts the wondrous adventures of the Jain Prince Shripala, who travels to distant lands in search of trade and wealth. In this unusually large and vivacious painting, the artist represents the Prince's ship as a British vessel. The inscriptions scattered across the painting, which repetively identify parts of the ship, indicate a fascination with seafaring. The depiction of the Union Jack lends immediacy to a story that took place long before the British came to India.
persona1_adapted: Popular stories and lively pictures helped share beliefs and values within the Jain community. The *Shripalarasa* is a tale about the exciting adventures of the Jain Prince Shripala. He travels to faraway lands in search of trade and wealth. In this unusually large and colorful painting, the artist shows the Prince's ship as a British vessel. Small labels scattered across the painting identify parts of the ship over and over again. This shows a strong interest in seafaring. The image of the Union Jack makes a story feel immediate and real, even though it takes place long before the British arrived in India.

CuratorialText: Funeral vase: tall, slender, on hollowed, spreading feet. Wood stand. Clay: soft, light-weight, gray-white. Glaze: thin, transparent green celadon; close brown crackle. Areas of stain and of over-baked wrinkled glaze. Decoration: hieratic figures and a dragon supporting the sun-disk - in applied relief, under glaze.
persona1_adapted: This is a funeral vase. It is tall and slender. It stands on a hollow, flared base made of wood. The vase is made of clay that is soft, light, and gray-white. The glaze is a thin, see-through green color called celadon. It has a fine brown crackle pattern. Some areas are stained or have a wrinkled glaze from over-baking. The decoration shows formal figures and a dragon holding up a sun disk. These designs are built up on the surface in relief, under the glaze.

CuratorialText: The Tale of Shuten Doji has long been popular in Japan; the earliest-known illustrated version of this tale dates to the fourteenth century. Minamoto no Yorimitsu (948-1021), a historical figure of great martial prowess, was transformed in popular literature into a legendary hero who rescued the realm from demonic forces. Shuten Doji, a monster who lived southwest of Kyoto, terrorized the population, dining on locals and holding young maidens captive. With divine assistance and various means of subterfuge, Yorimitsu beheaded the ogre and returned the region to tranquility. Kyosai's interest in the narrative format and his careful connoisseurship of ancient works of art are evident in his paintings and sketchbooks. Riddled with macabre humor and images of the grotesque, his own paintings catered to the tastes of late Edo (1615-1868) and early Meiji (1868-1912) audiences.
persona1_adapted: The story of Shuten Doji has been popular in Japan for a long time. The earliest known illustrated version of this tale comes from the 1300s. Minamoto no Yorimitsu (948-1021) was a real person known for his great fighting skills. In popular stories, he became a legendary hero who saved the land from evil demons. Shuten Doji was a monster who lived southwest of Kyoto. He terrorized the people by eating locals and keeping young women prisoner. With help from the gods and clever tricks, Yorimitsu cut off the ogre’s head and brought peace back to the area. Kyosai was interested in telling stories through pictures. He also studied old artworks very carefully. You can see this in his paintings and sketchbooks. His own paintings are full of dark humor and strange, scary images. These works matched what audiences liked in the late Edo period (1615-1868) and early Meiji period (1868-1912).

CuratorialText: Hassam visited Oregon during the summer of 1908, completing this view of Portland's Chinatown after his return to New York. The subject matter is unusual for Hassam, but recalls a series of shop-front scenes painted by Whistler in the 1880s. Like Whistler, Hassam treated the street as a shallow stage set, emphasizing the geometrical patterns created by the play of variously colored rectangular shaped-bricks, signs, windows, doors, walls, sidewalk, alley, and the white diagonal of broken light splayed across the near foreground.
persona1_adapted: In the summer of 1908, Hassam visited Oregon. He painted this scene of Portland's Chinatown after he returned to New York. The subject is unusual for Hassam, but it reminds us of a series of shop-front paintings by Whistler from the 1880s. Like Whistler, Hassam treated the street like a shallow stage. He focused on the geometric patterns made by different colored rectangular shapes—bricks, signs, windows, doors, walls, sidewalk, and alley. In the front, a white diagonal of broken light spreads across the scene.

CuratorialText: Tall, blue and white glazed porcelain vase, with long neck, round bulbous body and "garlic"-shaped neck/mouth. Round ring base, slightly uneven. The body decorated with scrolling, flowering vines and animals.
persona1_adapted: This is a tall vase made of porcelain. It has a blue and white glaze, which is a glass-like coating. The vase has a long neck and a round, bulbous body. Its mouth and neck are shaped like a garlic bulb. The base is a round ring, and it is slightly uneven. The body is decorated with twisting vines, flowers, and animals.

CuratorialText: Legendary and theatrical references abound in the decoration of the cover of this lacquer box for an inkstone, a water dropper, and writing brushes. The Wisteria Maiden (Fuji Musume), whose silvery facial features have oxidized to bluish black, is a common subject of Otsu-e, a type of folk painting sold in the town of Otsu near Lake Biwa. In nineteenth-century kabuki, the Wisteria Maiden became the focus of a dance expressing unrequited love to the vocal and instrumental music of a nagauta (literally, "long song"). With her is a praying demon, another figure often seen in Otsu-e. The Thunder God rushes down from above, beating his ring of drums as the couple takes shelter from a rainstorm. The ornate pictorial decoration of this box was created by an artist who had mastered maki-e techniques, which employ powders and particles of gold and other material to create intricate designs.
persona1_adapted: The cover of this lacquer box is decorated with stories from legends and theater. The box holds an inkstone, a water dropper, and writing brushes. The Wisteria Maiden, whose silver face has turned bluish black over time, is a common figure in Otsu-e. Otsu-e is a type of folk painting sold in the town of Otsu near Lake Biwa. In 1800s kabuki theater, the Wisteria Maiden became the focus of a dance about unrequited love. The dance was performed to the music of a nagauta, which means "long song." A praying demon, another figure from Otsu-e, stands with her. The Thunder God rushes down from above, beating his ring of drums, as the couple takes shelter from a rainstorm. The detailed decoration on this box was made by an artist skilled in maki-e. Maki-e uses powders and bits of gold and other materials to create intricate designs.

CuratorialText: Bowl, conical, flaring; deep foot with four silica spur-marks underneath on base inside footrim. Six notches in rim. Clay: dense, sonant. Glaze: gray-green celadon; crackled in long lines. Decoration: molded and incised in paste under glaze: rosette in bottom; hibiscus flowers and foliage filling sides: single line incised below rim.
persona1_adapted: This bowl has a cone-like shape that flares outward at the top. It sits on a tall, deep foot. On the bottom, inside the foot rim, you can see four small spur marks left from the kiln. The rim has six notches cut into it. The clay is dense and makes a ringing sound when tapped. The glaze is a gray-green celadon color, with long crack lines running through it. The decoration was pressed and carved into the clay before the glaze was applied. A rosette sits in the center of the bowl. Hibiscus flowers and leaves cover the sides. A single carved line runs just below the rim.

CuratorialText: Depicts a hilly landscape, with a scraggly tree, and a Central Asian groom in a red jacket holding the bridle of a dappled horse with trappings and a saddle. Spurious inscription and one seal, no colophons or labels.
persona1_adapted: This painting shows a hilly landscape. It includes a scraggly tree and a groom from Central Asia. The groom wears a red jacket and holds the bridle of a dappled horse. The horse has a saddle and other gear. The painting has a fake inscription and one seal. It has no colophons or labels.

CuratorialText: A rectangular, off-white frit tile with an opaque white underglaze. Overglaze painted with yellow, green, turquoise, blue and manganese black/purple outlines depicting a bird perched on a fruit tree branch. Sides and back are unglazed.
persona1_adapted: This tile is rectangular and off-white. It is made from frit, a type of glassy material. The tile has a white base layer underneath the glaze. On top, artists painted a bird sitting on a branch of a fruit tree. The colors used are yellow, green, turquoise, blue, and blackish-purple outlines. The sides and back of the tile are not glazed.

CuratorialText: Painting is one of a series of eight views of Tokyo by Ogata Gekko. This view of a racetrack focuses on the action in the foreground, where spectators are enthralled by the competition. Contained in modern wooden box with lid.
persona1_adapted: This painting is part of a series of eight views of Tokyo by Ogata Gekko. The scene shows a racetrack. The main action happens in the foreground, where spectators are excited by the race. The artwork comes in a modern wooden box with a lid.

CuratorialText: Folio from a dispersed copy of Yusuf u Zulaykha by Jami; Persian in black nasta'liq script on gold-flecked paper; heading in red; recto: text: 2 columns, 14 lines; verso: text: 2 columns, 14 lines; one of a group of 13. Border: The recto is set in gold, green, and black rulings in a margin with birds, floral, and leaf scrolls. The verso is set in gold, green, and black rulings in a margin with lions, bulls, foxes, rabbits,and birds in a landscape.
persona1_adapted:
This page comes from a book called *Yusuf u Zulaykha*, written by the poet Jami. The text is written in Persian using a script called *nasta'liq*. The letters are black, and the paper has gold flecks. Some headings are in red.

Each side of the page has two columns of text, with 14 lines per column. This page is one of a set of 13.

On the front side, the text is framed by gold, green, and black lines. The border around it shows birds, flowers, and curling leaves.

On the back side, the text is also framed by gold, green, and black lines. But here the border shows a landscape with lions, bulls, foxes, rabbits, and birds.

CuratorialText: Squared, hollow cylinder of the type ts’ung [cong] 琮; narrow projecting collar at both ends; rich cream and tans; decoration: incised and in low relief, stylized masks and double bands of fine lines on corners. (Collar chipped.)
persona1_adapted: This object is a hollow, square cylinder called a *ts’ung* (pronounced “cong”). It has a narrow, raised collar at both the top and bottom. The colors are rich cream and tan. The decoration is carved into the surface, with some parts cut in and others raised slightly. The design shows stylized masks and double bands of thin lines on the corners. (The collar is chipped.)

CuratorialText: One of a pair of blue and white vases, Kangxi period, each of trumpet shape form with flared foot, after a bronze Ku, each of the three sections with large loose lotus scroll, the central lobed section encircled by a diaper filled band.
persona1_adapted: This is one of a pair of blue and white vases from the Kangxi period. Each vase has a trumpet-like shape with a wide, flared foot. The design copies an ancient bronze vessel called a Ku. The vase is divided into three sections. Each section is decorated with large, flowing lotus scrolls. The middle section has a band filled with a repeating diamond pattern.

CuratorialText: Pitcher, of a full-bodied pear form, on a high spreading foot; plaster restorations including handle and foot. Clay: soft, sandy, white. Glaze: turquoise green, partly disintegrated and having a silver iridescence.
persona1_adapted: This pitcher is shaped like a full, round pear. It stands on a tall, wide base. The handle and base have been restored with plaster. The clay is soft, sandy, and white. The glaze is a turquoise green. Some of the glaze has worn away, and parts of it have a shiny, silver look.

CuratorialText: Bowl, deep ovoidal; low foot with kiln-slag attachments. Clay: hard. Glaze: brilliant olive-gray, finely crackled. Decoration: molded in the paste in light relief, under glaze; single large floral motif (peony?) filling bottom, lower half of walls, difficult to decipher under glaze.
persona1_adapted: This bowl is deep and egg-shaped. It has a short base with small bits of kiln material stuck to it. The clay is hard. The glaze is a shiny olive-gray color with fine cracks. The decoration is molded into the clay in light relief, which means the design stands out slightly from the surface. This design is under the glaze. A single large flower, possibly a peony, fills the bottom and lower half of the bowl. The pattern is hard to see clearly under the glaze.

CuratorialText: Clay: dense, resonant, buff stoneware. Glaze: transparent, crackled, over white slip; unglazed area stained blackish-brown. Decoration: in cobalt blue, under glaze: linear patterns representing maple leaves and water or pampas grass; geometric bands on sides of lid and base. Square seal on underside of lid.
persona1_adapted: This object is made from a dense, buff-colored stoneware clay. It has a clear, crackled glaze over a white slip. The unglazed parts are stained a dark brownish-black. The decoration is painted in cobalt blue under the glaze. The blue lines show patterns of maple leaves and water or pampas grass. There are also geometric bands on the sides of the lid and base. A square seal is stamped on the underside of the lid.

CuratorialText: Tall blue and white glazed porcelain vase, with bulbous lower body, cylindrical center body with pinched "snake" handles and slightly outflaring rim. Porcelain body with dark blue cobalt underglaze with gray-blue-white glaze. Floral decorative patterns.
persona1_adapted: This is a tall vase made of porcelain, a type of fine ceramic. It is glazed in blue and white. The lower part of the vase is round and bulbous. The middle section is shaped like a cylinder. It has pinched handles that look like snakes. The rim flares out slightly. The porcelain body is painted with dark blue cobalt under the glaze. The glaze itself is a mix of gray, blue, and white. The vase is decorated with floral patterns.

CuratorialText: Dish, low, with rounded sides; one chip on rim; hair-line crack. Clay: white porcelain. Glaze: clear, feldspathic; a few pinholes and impurity flecks. Decoration: painted in underglaze cobalt blue of deep tone, copying a traditional design of the Ch'eng-hua period. See F1951.10 and .11. Center inside, "three friends of winter" (pine, plum, and bamboo) with garden rock, and fungus, enclosed by double line band which is repeated at rim. Outside, following model, a scene of figures on a garden terrace with plants and trees.
persona1_adapted:
This is a low dish with rounded sides. It has a small chip on the rim and a hairline crack. The dish is made of white porcelain. The glaze is clear and feldspathic, which means it contains a mineral called feldspar. There are a few tiny holes and small specks of dirt in the glaze.

The decoration is painted in deep blue underglaze cobalt. This means the blue color was applied before the final glaze was added. The design copies a traditional style from the Ch'eng-hua period.

In the center of the dish, there is a design called the "three friends of winter." These are pine, plum, and bamboo. They are shown with a garden rock and a fungus. A double line band surrounds this scene, and the same band repeats at the rim.

On the outside, following the original model, there is a scene of people on a garden terrace with plants and trees.

CuratorialText: Individual serving bowl (mukozuke [JPN]). Black lacquer rim. Red lacquer repairs. Clay: hard, light-fawn. Shiny brown on surface. Glaze: yellow and gray over blue-green; inside--brilliant brown and blue. Outside: compounded with iron and rice-straw ash. Inside: clear glaze.
persona1_adapted: This is a small serving bowl called *mukozuke* in Japanese. It has a black lacquer rim with red lacquer repairs. The clay body is hard and light-fawn in color. The surface is shiny brown. The glaze on the outside is yellow and gray over a blue-green base. The inside is brilliant brown and blue. The outside glaze is made with iron and rice-straw ash. The inside has a clear glaze.

CuratorialText: White clay, pinkish buff where exposed. Decoration of grasses incised under clear brown wash; hatching incised under clear glaze (body indented in each area); and flower and grasses painted in black overglaze enamel. Kiln mark stamped on base inside foot rim. Thick iron wash over half, appearing dull yellowish brown; clear glaze on other half of body, appearing gray where it partially overlaps wash. Five spur marks (from clay wads) on foot rim.
persona1_adapted: This pot is made of white clay, which looks pinkish-buff where it is not covered. The decoration includes grasses carved into the clay under a clear brown wash. Hatching—a pattern of crossed lines—is also carved under a clear glaze. The body is slightly indented in each carved area. Flowers and grasses are painted on top of the glaze with black enamel. A kiln mark is stamped on the base inside the foot rim. Half of the pot is covered with a thick iron wash that looks dull yellowish-brown. The other half has a clear glaze that looks gray where it overlaps the wash. There are five spur marks on the foot rim from clay wads used during firing.

CuratorialText: Bowl, small, ovoidal, on a bold, spreading foot. Cracked and repaired. Clay: soft, grayish-white. Glaze: inside, a deep aubergine; outside now covered with an iridescent patina. Decoration: painted in black under glaze.
persona1_adapted: This small, egg-shaped bowl sits on a wide, flared base. It has been cracked and repaired. The clay is soft and grayish-white. Inside, the glaze is a deep purple color. On the outside, the glaze now has a shiny, rainbow-like layer. The decoration was painted in black before the glaze was applied.

CuratorialText: Mounted with F1937.39. Detached folio from a dispersed copy of Nuzhatnama-yi ala'i (Excellent book of counsel) by Shahmardan ibn Abi al-Khayr; text: Persian in black and red naskhi script; recto: illustration and text: Medicinal property of the mulberry tree, 2 lines, 3 paintings; verso: illustration and text: Pharmacological property of the olive tree, its leaves and fruits, 4 lines, 2 paintings; one of a group of three folios.
persona1_adapted: This page is part of a book called *Nuzhatnama-yi ‘Ala’i* (The Excellent Book of Counsel), written by Shahmardan ibn Abi al-Khayr. The text is in Persian, written in black and red ink in a script called *naskhi*. On the front side, there is an illustration and text about the medicinal uses of the mulberry tree. It has 2 lines of writing and 3 paintings. On the back side, there is an illustration and text about the medicinal uses of the olive tree, its leaves, and its fruits. It has 4 lines of writing and 2 paintings. This page is one of three in a group.

CuratorialText: Both Corvus, "The Raven," and Centaurus, "The Centaur," are constellations of the southern hemisphere. Corvus is composed of seven stars while Centaurus, whose forepart is made up of a man and hindpart of a horse, consists of 37. Al-Kazwini records that the last two stars of Centaurus are called "the two sworn upon," and according to tradition one of these passes very close to the path of Corvus. Some people, writes al-Kazwini, believe that the star belongs to "the Raven" while others insist that it is part of "The Centaur."
persona1_adapted: Corvus, "The Raven," and Centaurus, "The Centaur," are both constellations in the southern sky. Corvus has seven stars. Centaurus has 37 stars. Its front half looks like a man, and its back half looks like a horse. The scholar Al-Kazwini wrote that the last two stars of Centaurus are called "the two sworn upon." According to tradition, one of these stars passes very close to the path of Corvus. Al-Kazwini says some people believe that star belongs to "The Raven." Others insist it is part of "The Centaur."

CuratorialText: Bowl, large, deep, ovoidal; heavy foot; cracked. Clay: heavy, coarse, gray. Glaze: white, with tinges of green; crackled and discolored to a brownish tone. Chipped along the rim. Decoration: painted in blue and black under glaze.
persona1_adapted: This is a large, deep bowl shaped like an egg. It has a heavy base and a crack in it. The bowl is made from thick, rough, gray clay. The glaze, or glassy coating, is white with hints of green. It has a cracked pattern and has turned a brownish color over time. The rim is chipped. The decoration was painted in blue and black before the glaze was applied.

CuratorialText: Black-lacquered (shin nuri) cypress-wood utensil stand (shin daisu) with four black-lacquered posts and two shelves; four black-lacquered (shin nuri) cypress boards (shikiita) of various sizes for use with formal tea procedures.
persona1_adapted: This is a utensil stand made from cypress wood. It has a shiny black lacquer finish. The stand has four black posts and two shelves. It also includes four black-lacquered cypress boards. These boards come in different sizes. People use them during formal tea ceremonies.

CuratorialText: Vessel in the shape of a Shang dynasty ceremonial "ku;" copper decorated with cloisonne enamels in light blue, dark blue, green, red, yellow, and white; inside the foot is an inscription of eight characters in relief giving a K'ang-hsi date. A separate flower holder fits inside the mouth. During the Qing dynasty (1644-1911), the display of ancient objects as well as contemporary ones made in an archaistic style was considered a sign of learning and refined taste. The shape and decoration of this vase, including the mask-like faces on the base, reproduce aspects of a Bronze Age ritual vessel called a gu.
persona1_adapted:
This vessel is shaped like a "ku," a type of ancient Chinese cup used in ceremonies during the Shang dynasty. It is made of copper and decorated with cloisonné enamel. Cloisonné is a technique where thin wires are used to create small areas, which are then filled with colored enamel. The colors here are light blue, dark blue, green, red, yellow, and white.

Inside the foot of the vase, there is an inscription of eight raised Chinese characters. These characters give a date from the K'ang-hsi period. A separate flower holder fits inside the mouth of the vase.

During the Qing dynasty (1644–1911), people thought that displaying old objects—and new ones made to look old—showed that you were educated and had good taste. The shape and decoration of this vase, including the mask-like faces on its base, copy features of a Bronze Age ritual vessel called a gu.

CuratorialText: Polychrome. Clay: fine white porcelain. Glaze: transparent, high fired. Decoration: dragons amid clouds incised in the paste and covered with brilliant green enamel; six-character Hung-chih mark on base. (Pair with F1952.20).
persona1_adapted: This artwork is made of fine white porcelain clay. It has a clear, high-fired glaze. The decoration shows dragons among clouds. These designs were carved into the clay and then covered with bright green enamel. The base has a six-character mark from the Hung-chih period. This piece is part of a pair with F1952.20.

CuratorialText: Bulb-bowl: circular, on three cloud-scroll feet. Two rows of bosses. Incised numeral 1 (one) and spur-marks on base. Clay: hard, fine, light-gray. Glaze: inside:olive, strong lavender-blue and lighter green-blue; outside: rose-purple, blue, gray and olive.
persona1_adapted: This bowl is round and sits on three feet shaped like cloud scrolls. Two rows of raised bumps decorate the outside. On the bottom, you can see the number "1" scratched into the clay and small marks from the kiln supports. The clay is hard, fine, and light gray. Inside, the glaze has olive, strong lavender-blue, and lighter green-blue colors. Outside, the glaze shows rose-purple, blue, gray, and olive.

CuratorialText:
The brilliant paintings on this pair of folding screens are regarded as masterpieces among only six surviving sets of screens by Sotatsu, a talented and innovative artist who headed a painting workshop known as Tawaraya. As a townsman, Sotatsu produced paintings such as fans for popular consumption. By the late 1620s, however, Sotatsu was painting for the imperial court, and his works survive in the collections of the Kyoto imperial palace. For his artistic merit, he was granted the honorary Buddhist ecclesiastical title Hokkyo (Bridge of the Law), which is included in his signature on these screens.
Matsushima (Pine Islands) is the name of a famous site (meisho) near Sendai, a city in Miyagi Prefecture in northeastern Japan. Its beauty inspired both poets and painters. In Sotatsu's screens the rock from which pine trees grow are rendered in brilliant mineral colors of green, blue, and brown, highlighted with gold. Waves in animated forms are delineated in alternating lines of ink and gold, producing a luminous effect. Clouds and embankments are rendered in particles of gold leaf accented with silver, which has darkened over time to a soft black tone.
Sotatsu's innovative composition creates a dynamic interplay among the land and cloud forms, the bending pines, and churning waves. His lifelong interest in pictorial composition left a lasting legacy. Later painters of the Rimpa school, such as Ogata Korin (1658-1716), repeated the Matsushima theme in their work. The Matsushima screens were probably painted in the early Kan'ei era (1624-44), a period of cultural efflorescence in Kyoto.
persona1_adapted:
The brilliant paintings on this pair of folding screens are considered masterpieces. They are among only six surviving sets of screens by Sotatsu. Sotatsu was a talented and creative artist who led a painting workshop called Tawaraya. As a townsman, he made paintings like fans for everyday people. By the late 1620s, Sotatsu was painting for the imperial court. His works still exist in the collections of the Kyoto imperial palace. Because of his artistic skill, he was given the honorary Buddhist title Hokkyo, which means "Bridge of the Law." This title appears in his signature on these screens.

Matsushima, or Pine Islands, is the name of a famous place near Sendai, a city in Miyagi Prefecture in northeastern Japan. Its beauty inspired both poets and painters. In Sotatsu's screens, the rocks where pine trees grow are painted with bright mineral colors like green, blue, and brown, highlighted with gold. Waves in lively shapes are drawn with alternating lines of ink and gold, creating a glowing effect. Clouds and embankments are made with particles of gold leaf, accented with silver. Over time, the silver has darkened to a soft black tone.

Sotatsu's creative arrangement creates a lively interaction between the land and cloud forms, the bending pines, and the churning waves. His lifelong interest in picture composition left a lasting impact. Later painters of the Rimpa school, such as Ogata Korin (1658-1716), repeated the Matsushima theme in their own work. The Matsushima screens were probably painted in the early Kan'ei era (1624-44), a time of cultural growth in Kyoto.

CuratorialText:
The Tale of Shuten Doji has long been popular in Japan; the earliest-known illustrated version of this tale dates to the fourteenth century. Minamoto no Yorimitsu (948-1021), a historical figure of great martial prowess, was transformed in popular literature into a legendary hero who rescued the realm from demonic forces. Shuten Doji, a monster who lived southwest of Kyoto, terrorized the population, dining on locals and holding young maidens captive. With divine assistance and various means of subterfuge, Yorimitsu beheaded the ogre and returned the region to tranquility.
Kyosai's interest in the narrative format and his careful connoisseurship of ancient works of art are evident in his paintings and sketchbooks. Riddled with macabre humor and images of the grotesque, his own paintings catered to the tastes of late Edo (1615-1868) and early Meiji (1868-1912) audiences.
persona1_adapted:
The story of Shuten Doji has been popular in Japan for a long time. The earliest known illustrated version of this tale comes from the 1300s. Minamoto no Yorimitsu (948-1021) was a real person known for his great fighting skills. In popular stories, he became a legendary hero who saved the land from evil demons. Shuten Doji was a monster who lived southwest of Kyoto. He terrorized the people by eating locals and keeping young women captive. With help from the gods and clever tricks, Yorimitsu cut off the ogre’s head and brought peace back to the area.

Kyosai’s interest in telling stories through pictures is clear in his paintings and sketchbooks. He also carefully studied old works of art. His own paintings are full of dark humor and strange, scary images. These artworks matched what audiences liked during the late Edo period (1615-1868) and early Meiji period (1868-1912).

CuratorialText: Ceremonial sickle in four parts: three of bronze inlaid with turquoise, one (the blade) of jade decorated in linear relief with notched back and ground edge. No objects of this type have yet been scientifically excavated in China, so it is difficult to know the purpose of this extremely rare axe. It consists of four separate pieces: a tubular bronze handle, two caps with dragon motifs, and an unusual serrated jade blade that might not be original. Traces of wood inside the handle suggest it was lengthened with additional elements that are now missing.
persona1_adapted:
This is a ceremonial sickle made of four parts. Three parts are bronze with turquoise inlay. The fourth part is a jade blade. The blade has a carved design, a notched back, and a ground edge.

No objects like this have been found in scientific digs in China. So, it is hard to know the purpose of this very rare axe. The sickle has four separate pieces: a tube-shaped bronze handle, two caps with dragon designs, and a special jagged jade blade. The blade might not be the original one.

Traces of wood inside the handle show it was once longer. It had extra parts that are now missing.

CuratorialText: This is one of a pair of folding screens illustrating two of the fifty-four chapters in The Tale of Genji, a narrative composed in the early eleventh century by a lady of the court known as Murasaki Shikibu. This fictional biography of Prince Genji, whose life was filled with romance and tragedy, was a frequent subject of Japanese paintings. This screen represents chapter fifty-one, Ukifune. The lady Ukifune and her lover Niou, drift together in a boat on a wintry day, sheltered by a folding screen and temporarily isolated in their tragic romance. The painter Kano Naonobu chose monochromatic ink and thin color washes for a theme that was more commonly illustrated in lavish color and gold. The dreamlike effect of the mists and washes is poetically evocative of the mood of each episode. Naonobu was an official painter who served the Tokugawa shogun.
persona1_adapted:
This is one of a pair of folding screens. They show scenes from two chapters of *The Tale of Genji*. That story was written in the early 1000s by a court lady named Murasaki Shikibu. It is a fictional biography of Prince Genji, whose life was full of romance and tragedy. Japanese artists often painted scenes from this story.

This screen shows chapter fifty-one, called *Ukifune*. In the scene, a lady named Ukifune and her lover Niou drift together in a boat on a cold winter day. They are sheltered by a folding screen and feel cut off from the world in their sad romance.

The painter Kano Naonobu used only black ink and thin color washes. Other artists usually painted this story in bright colors and gold. The misty, dreamlike look of the ink and washes helps capture the mood of each moment. Naonobu was an official painter who worked for the Tokugawa shogun.

CuratorialText: Ornament of roughly rectangular shape; translucent mottled tan and cream color with areas of white film of incipient disintegration; blunt blade edge is indicated by lightly incised line and beveling; upper edge has irregular notches and a narrow projection; satin smooth surface. (One small chip). Acquired with a box, now lost.
persona1_adapted: This ornament is roughly rectangular. It is a mix of tan and cream colors that let some light through. Some white patches show where the surface is starting to wear away. A light carved line and a slanted edge mark the blunt blade. The top edge has uneven notches and a thin point. The surface is smooth, like satin. There is one small chip. It came with a box that is now lost.

CuratorialText: Jar, large, ovoidal, on a low, thick foot rim. Clay: soft, grayish. Glaze: variable aubergine, frosted with whitish areas of partial disintegration, with a slight iridescence. Earthy attachments. Decoration: impressed, incised and modelled in the paste.
persona1_adapted: This large, egg-shaped jar sits on a short, thick base. It is made of soft, grayish clay. The glaze is a mix of purple shades, with frosty white patches where it has partly worn away. The surface has a slight rainbow-like shimmer. The jar has earthy-looking attachments. The decoration was made by pressing, carving, and shaping the clay before it was fired.

CuratorialText: Bowl: deep, ovoidal; low foot. Clay: hard, white. Glaze: pale green, mottled with blue-violet inside; gray-olive on rim and edge of flow; finely pitted.
persona1_adapted: This bowl is deep and egg-shaped. It has a short base. The clay is hard and white. The glaze is pale green. Inside, it is spotted with blue-violet. The rim and the edge of the glaze are gray-olive. The surface has many tiny pits.

CuratorialText: Chinese Han Glazed pottery " Hill-jar"; cylindrical vessel with cover; three bear-shaped feet; spur marks. Clay: reddish, sandy texture; fired medium. Glaze: lead silicate; deep green with areas of silvery iridescence, earth incrustations. Decoration: horizontal panels moulded in relief with animals a nd horsemen; two simulated mask and ring handles; cover represents mountains with animals in relief.
persona1_adapted: This is a Chinese glazed pottery jar from the Han dynasty. It is shaped like a cylinder and has a lid. The jar stands on three feet shaped like bears. You can see small marks left from where it was fired in a kiln. The clay is reddish and feels sandy. It was fired at a medium temperature. The glaze is made from lead and silica. It is a deep green color. Some parts have a silvery, shiny look. There are also crusts of dirt on the surface. The decoration is made of horizontal bands with raised images of animals and horsemen. There are two fake handles shaped like masks and rings. The lid shows mountains with raised animals on them.

CuratorialText: Dive into the artistic process of one of history’s most beloved and influential artists. A true titan of the Italian Renaissance, Raffaello di Giovanni Santi (1483–1520)—better known as Raphael—matched ambition with lyricism to create works with both intellectual heft and emotional depth, a necessary skill in the complex political landscape of Renaissance courts. In his short life of only 37 years, he achieved such profound success as a painter, designer, and architect that he was regarded as the pinnacle of artistic perfection for centuries after his death. Raphael: Sublime Poetry is the first comprehensive exhibition on Raphael in the United States, bringing together more than 170 of the artist’s greatest masterpieces and rarely seen treasures to illuminate the brilliance of Raphael’s extraordinary creativity. The son of a painter and poet, Raphael engaged with the foremost writers and thinkers of his age in Rome, displaying a poetic sensibility that captivated his peers and generations that followed. Follow the full breadth of his life and career, from his origins in Urbino to his rise in Florence, where he began to emerge as a peer of Leonardo da Vinci and Michelangelo, to his final, prolific decade at the papal court in Rome. To underscore the range of his genius, this presentation brings together important drawings, paintings, and tapestries from public and private collections across Europe and the United States, many of which have never been shown together. With particular attention to Raphael’s portrayal of women—from his use of nude female models for the first time in Western art to his tender depictions of the Madonna and Child—and recent scientific discoveries made using state-of-the-art technology, this exhibition offers a rare opportunity to experience the genius of an artist who helped shape the course of art history.
persona1_adapted:
Explore the creative process of one of history’s most loved and influential artists. Raphael (1483–1520) was a giant of the Italian Renaissance. He combined big ideas with beauty to create works that were both smart and emotional. This skill was needed in the tricky politics of Renaissance courts.

In his short life of just 37 years, Raphael became a hugely successful painter, designer, and architect. For centuries after his death, people saw him as the perfect artist. *Raphael: Sublime Poetry* is the first big exhibition on Raphael in the United States. It brings together over 170 of his greatest works and rare treasures. These pieces show the brilliance of his amazing creativity.

Raphael’s father was a painter and poet. Raphael worked with the top writers and thinkers of his time in Rome. He showed a poetic style that amazed his peers and future generations. Follow his full life and career, from his start in Urbino to his rise in Florence. There, he became a rival of Leonardo da Vinci and Michelangelo. Then see his final, busy decade at the pope’s court in Rome.

To show the range of his genius, this exhibition brings together important drawings, paintings, and tapestries. These come from public and private collections across Europe and the United States. Many have never been shown together before. The exhibition pays special attention to Raphael’s portraits of women. This includes his use of nude female models for the first time in Western art. It also shows his gentle images of Mary and baby Jesus. With recent scientific discoveries made using modern technology, this exhibition offers a rare chance to see the genius of an artist who helped shape art history.

CuratorialText: One of the greatest influencers of all time, Raphael (1483 –1520) was idolized for more than three centuries, in Europe and beyond, as the painter of supreme perfection and the role model for artists in training. During his short life, his contemporaries celebrated his enormous creative facility, and later his fame towered above that of other Italian Renaissance stars. Born Raffaello di Giovanni Santi, the ambitious young artist soon left behind his modest origins in the Marche region of central Italy in search of important patrons in Umbria and Tuscany. In Florence, then the capital of the European art world, he emerged as a peer to the elder Leonardo and Michelangelo. Raphael spent his final decade as the favorite artist of the popes in Rome, where he was praised as the “prince of painters.” A savvy businessman and impresario, he worked with well-organized teams of assistants and collaborators to propagate his inventions and style, spawning a new generation of noteworthy artists.
persona1_adapted:
Raphael (1483–1520) was one of the most influential artists ever. For over 300 years, people in Europe and beyond admired him as the perfect painter. Young artists looked up to him as a role model. During his short life, other artists praised his amazing creativity. Later, his fame grew even bigger than other famous Italian Renaissance artists.

He was born Raffaello di Giovanni Santi. The ambitious young artist soon left his modest home in central Italy. He went looking for important supporters in Umbria and Tuscany. In Florence, the art capital of Europe at the time, he became an equal to the older masters Leonardo and Michelangelo.

Raphael spent his last ten years in Rome as the favorite artist of the popes. There, people called him the "prince of painters." He was a smart businessman and organizer. He worked with well-trained teams of helpers to spread his ideas and style. This helped create a new generation of important artists.

CuratorialText: This first comprehensive U.S. exhibition on Raphael provides an immersive look at his meteoric career. Together, the drawings, paintings, prints, and tapestries on view present a portrait of his dashing artistic personality and astonishingly creative mind. This is also the story of an artist who was born to a poet-painter, became an intimate friend to literary figures, and tried his hand at composing sonnets. Raphael lived and worked in a culture accustomed to thinking of painting and poetry as intertwined sister arts. The elegance and dramatic force of his imagery still have the power to invoke the ancient dictum, much discussed in his time, that “painting is a mute poetry, and poetry is a blind painting.”
persona1_adapted: This first major U.S. exhibition on Raphael offers an up-close look at his fast-moving career. The drawings, paintings, prints, and tapestries on display show his bold artistic style and brilliant imagination. This is also the story of an artist whose father was a poet and painter. Raphael became close friends with writers and even tried writing sonnets himself. He lived in a time when people saw painting and poetry as connected art forms. The beauty and power of his images still remind us of an old saying from his era: “Painting is silent poetry, and poetry is blind painting.”

CuratorialText: Raphael was born on April 6, 1483, in Urbino, a hill town set in a spectacular, rugged landscape in the Marche region, in east central Italy. The artist’s roots in Urbino, with its refined court life and unique intellectual heritage, played a defining role in his life and career. He proudly identified with his birthplace and signed his paintings “RAPHAEL VRBINAS” to the end of his days.
persona1_adapted: Raphael was born on April 6, 1483, in Urbino, Italy. Urbino is a hill town in the Marche region of east central Italy. The area has a dramatic, rocky landscape. Urbino had a refined court life and a unique intellectual history. These qualities shaped Raphael’s life and career. He was proud of his hometown. Until the end of his life, he signed his paintings “RAPHAEL VRBINAS.”

CuratorialText: Raphael received his earliest training from his father, Giovanni Santi (ca. 1439–1494), a court painter and prolific poet. During Raphael’s childhood, Giovanni was busy composing an epic poem on Federico III da Montefeltro (1422–1482), the famous ruler who had brought peace, wealth, and fame to the Duchy of Urbino. A formidable general, scholar, and patron of art and architecture, Federico transformed his court into an internationally celebrated center for the arts and humanist learning. He founded a fabled library and rebuilt Urbino’s imposing Palazzo Ducale, whose rhythmic arcades and harmonic geometry embodied the humanist dedication to mathematics that distinguished the culture of Urbino. Those same ideals are evident in the vibrant architectural visions—enlivened with perspectival effects and classical references—created by Urbino painters.
persona1_adapted: Raphael got his first art lessons from his father, Giovanni Santi. Giovanni was a court painter and a poet who wrote many poems. When Raphael was a child, his father was working on a long epic poem about Federico III da Montefeltro. Federico was a famous ruler who brought peace, wealth, and fame to the Duchy of Urbino. He was a strong military leader, a scholar, and a supporter of art and architecture. Federico turned his court into a famous center for the arts and humanist learning. Humanism was a way of thinking that focused on human values and the study of classical texts. Federico built a legendary library and rebuilt Urbino’s grand palace, the Palazzo Ducale. The palace had repeating arches and balanced shapes that showed the humanist love for math. These same ideas appear in the lively city scenes painted by Urbino artists. Their works used perspective—a technique that makes things look three-dimensional—and included references to ancient Greek and Roman styles.

CuratorialText: Raphael’s father soon brought his precocious son to study with Pietro Perugino (1446/50–1523), an artist he likely knew through local projects. A brilliant, prolific painter and draftsman with active workshops in Perugia and Florence, Perugino relied heavily on collaboration with assistants to meet the demands of patrons all over Italy. Raphael coursed quickly through the stages of pupil and apprentice to become first a collaborator to Perugino and then an independent artist. Perugino’s elegant figures, breathtaking command of technique, and efficient methods for reproducing designs left their mark on the younger artist.
persona1_adapted: Raphael’s father soon sent his talented son to study with Pietro Perugino (1446/50–1523). Perugino was an artist Raphael’s father likely knew from local projects. Perugino was a brilliant and busy painter and draftsman. He had active workshops in Perugia and Florence. To meet the demands of clients all over Italy, he relied heavily on help from his assistants. Raphael moved quickly through the stages of student and apprentice. He first became a collaborator with Perugino, then an independent artist. Perugino’s elegant figures, amazing skill with technique, and efficient ways of repeating designs left a strong influence on the younger artist.

CuratorialText: Ambitious, studious, and disciplined, the young Raphael first sought to establish himself as an independent professional in Città di Castello, a small, wealthy town near Perugia, where he painted notable church altarpieces. Confraternities, religious institutions for regular citizens devoted to works of charity, were also major sources of patronage for artists in Umbria and the Marche. Gathered here are paintings and related studies done for confraternities by Perugino and the young Raphael—including the first painting fully in Raphael’s hand, as confirmed by its recent conservation treatment.
persona1_adapted: Raphael was ambitious, hardworking, and focused. As a young artist, he first tried to build his career in Città di Castello, a small but wealthy town near Perugia. There, he painted important altarpieces for churches. Confraternities were religious groups for regular people who did charity work. These groups were major supporters of artists in Umbria and the Marche. In this room, you can see paintings and related studies made for confraternities by Perugino and the young Raphael. One of these is the first painting fully done by Raphael himself, as confirmed by recent conservation work.

CuratorialText: From 1500 to 1507, the young Raphael endeavored to cultivate patrons by painting both monumental altarpieces and small-scale devotional works in the regions of the Marche, Umbria, and Tuscany. An iconic example is the large, multipart painting at the center of this gallery, made for nuns in Perugia and now known as the Colonna Altarpiece (after a later owner). The full ensemble is reunited here as a complete work for the first time since it was dismembered around 1663 and the individual paintings dispersed. Other notable projects include a mammoth altarpiece for the Baronci family chapel in Città di Castello, which was later destroyed in an earthquake and survives only in fragments and preliminary drawings, and a remarkable altarpiece for the Oddi family chapel in Perugia, represented here by studies and the painting that provided its base.
persona1_adapted: Between 1500 and 1507, the young Raphael worked hard to find supporters for his art. He painted both huge church altarpieces and small religious works in the regions of the Marche, Umbria, and Tuscany. A famous example is the large, multi-part painting in the center of this gallery. It was made for nuns in Perugia and is now called the Colonna Altarpiece (named after a later owner). For the first time since it was taken apart around 1663, the whole set is back together here. Other important projects include a giant altarpiece for the Baronci family chapel in Città di Castello. That piece was later destroyed in an earthquake. Only fragments and early drawings survive. There is also a remarkable altarpiece for the Oddi family chapel in Perugia. It is shown here through studies and the painting that formed its base.

CuratorialText: Full-scale drawings for the Oddi Altarpiece reveal the workshop practices that Raphael had absorbed during his training and collaboration with Perugino. Dazzling studies on paper also show how he worked in a variety of media—black chalk, pen and ink, and metalpoint on prepared paper—often with a virtuosic command of technique. Drawings such as these likely served as his calling card and undoubtedly impressed his patrons.
persona1_adapted: Full-scale drawings for the Oddi Altarpiece show how Raphael learned his craft. He picked up these skills while training and working with Perugino. Raphael's brilliant studies on paper also reveal that he used many different materials. He worked with black chalk, pen and ink, and metalpoint on special paper. He had amazing control over these techniques. Drawings like these probably acted as his business card. They surely impressed the people who hired him.

CuratorialText: Raphael may have been drawn to Florence after hearing fellow painters rave about rival full-scale designs (cartoons) for spectacular murals there by Leonardo and Michelangelo. Related works in this gallery convey the electrifying novelty and arresting sculptural power of those unrealized projects. While in Florence between 1504 and 1508, Raphael also studied Michelangelo’s sculptures and Leonardo’s paintings and drawings, promptly and seamlessly adapting aspects of their styles and techniques in his work.
persona1_adapted: Raphael might have moved to Florence because other painters couldn't stop talking about amazing wall paintings planned there. Two famous artists, Leonardo and Michelangelo, had created large-scale designs for these murals. The artworks in this gallery show how exciting and powerful those unfinished projects were. While living in Florence from 1504 to 1508, Raphael studied Michelangelo's sculptures and Leonardo's paintings and drawings. He quickly and smoothly borrowed parts of their styles and methods for his own work.

CuratorialText: Raphael also learned from his peers’ revolutionary approaches to inventing compositions. Leonardo’s theories about “sketching quickly,” in the fervor of artistic inspiration, transformed Raphael’s methods. Asserting that the effort to finish drawings and puzzle out details could dry up fresh ideas, Leonardo advised working out concepts in “rough sketches,” like a poet scrawling the first draft of a verse in messy handwriting with crossed-out words. Before Florence, Raphael had tended to develop compositions in a more artisanal way, figure by detailed figure, as he had learned from Perugino. Now, spontaneous sketching led him to more organic compositions pulsating with movement and vivid expression.
persona1_adapted: Raphael also learned from his peers' new ways of creating compositions. Leonardo's ideas about "sketching quickly," while caught up in artistic inspiration, changed how Raphael worked. Leonardo believed that spending too much time finishing drawings and working out small details could kill fresh ideas. He advised developing ideas in "rough sketches," like a poet writing a messy first draft with crossed-out words. Before moving to Florence, Raphael created his compositions in a more step-by-step way, drawing one figure at a time, as his teacher Perugino had taught him. Now, sketching freely led him to create more natural compositions full of movement and strong emotion.

CuratorialText: The exposure to Leonardo’s and Michelangelo’s art infused Raphael’s compositions with a sense of space, sculptural monumentality, and expressive force. This was the result of an enormous amount of experimentation on paper. Sequences of drawings—from a sheet of sketches to studies of an arrangement based on three-dimensional clay or wax models—reveal Raphael’s increasingly disciplined approach to the design process. Working in orderly steps, for example, he gradually animated a pyramidal composition (favored by Leonardo), which could seem static, by building a dynamic interplay of gestures and gazes among the figures.
persona1_adapted: Raphael saw Leonardo’s and Michelangelo’s art. It changed his own work. His paintings gained a stronger sense of space, bigger-looking figures, and more powerful emotions. This came from a huge amount of drawing and experimenting on paper. Raphael made many drawings in a row. He started with quick sketches. Then he studied arrangements using small clay or wax models. These steps show how Raphael became more careful and organized in his design process. For example, he worked step by step to bring life to a pyramid-shaped composition. (Leonardo liked this shape.) A pyramid composition can look still or boring. But Raphael made it feel active by building a lively give-and-take of gestures and looks among the people in the scene.

CuratorialText: In 1507 the twenty-four-year-old Raphael painted his most significant work before his move to Rome, an altarpiece of Christ’s entombment for the Baglioni family chapel in Perugia. The preliminary drawings show him in full command of his powers as a storyteller, and demonstrate how he constructed a dramatic composition with a climactic narrative. Through arduous study, aided by his knowledge of classical sculpture, the artist evolved a static arrangement of figures into an emotionally arresting action scene. Raphael had absorbed Leonardo’s principle that the physical gestures of the body express the “motions” of the mind and soul.
persona1_adapted:
