In 1507, Raphael was twenty-four years old. He painted his most important work before moving to Rome. It was an altarpiece showing Christ being placed in the tomb. The painting was made for the Baglioni family chapel in Perugia.

His early sketches show he was a master storyteller. They reveal how he built a dramatic scene with a powerful climax. Through hard work and study of ancient Roman statues, Raphael turned a still group of figures into an emotional, action-filled moment. He learned from Leonardo da Vinci that the way people move their bodies shows what they think and feel.

CuratorialText: For centuries Raphael has been widely admired for Madonna and Child paintings that include a display of tenderness. From about 1300, Byzantine icons of the Eastern Orthodox faith had featured a form of the Madonna known as the Virgin Eleousa, from the Greek for “compassionate,” in which Mary embraces the Christ Child as he touches his cheek to hers. Disseminated widely, the imagery inspired numerous medieval and Renaissance artists, including Italian sculptors and painters. Raphael drew upon this model and thoroughly transformed it.
persona1_adapted: For centuries, people have admired Raphael’s paintings of Mary and the baby Jesus. These works show a gentle, loving bond between mother and child. Around the year 1300, Byzantine religious art from the Eastern Orthodox faith featured a type of Mary called the Virgin Eleousa. “Eleousa” comes from a Greek word meaning “compassionate.” In these images, Mary holds the Christ Child close as he touches his cheek to hers. This style of art spread widely and inspired many artists from the Middle Ages and the Renaissance, including sculptors and painters in Italy. Raphael used this older model as a starting point, but he changed it completely to create his own unique version.

CuratorialText: Raphael’s ability to capture a mood of vitality and innocent playfulness, especially while maintaining a superior command of anatomical realism, is striking in light of the historical realities of childbirth, infancy, and parenthood in his time. The rare account book displayed here records funerary expenses for Raphael’s mother, who died of childbirth-related complications in 1491, and for two of his siblings who died in infancy. Knowledge of gynecology and obstetrics was scarce, pregnancy and childbirth extremely dangerous, and infant mortality rates astronomically high. These facts, along with a desire among Renaissance artists to humanize religious subjects, all contributed to the appeal of the tender Madonna and Child as votive figures and subjects in art.
persona1_adapted: Raphael was great at showing energy and innocent playfulness in his art. He also had a strong skill for painting the human body in a realistic way. This is surprising when you think about how dangerous childbirth and infancy were during his time. The rare account book on display here lists funeral costs for Raphael’s mother. She died from problems related to childbirth in 1491. It also lists costs for two of his siblings who died as babies. People back then knew very little about women’s health and childbirth. Pregnancy and giving birth were very risky. Many babies did not survive. These facts help explain why Renaissance artists often painted tender images of Mary and baby Jesus. People used these images as symbols of hope and protection in their homes and churches.

CuratorialText: Despite his great ambitions, Raphael was largely thwarted in his attempts to break into the competitive art market in Florence. He found his most feasible source of patronage among the city’s wealthy merchants, who commissioned portraits and tender devotional paintings, mainly of the Madonna and Child. Raphael continued to produce works on this subject for private patrons well after his move to Rome in 1508, and many of his collaborators followed his example.
persona1_adapted: Raphael had big dreams, but he struggled to succeed in Florence’s tough art market. His best chance for work came from the city’s rich merchants. They hired him to paint portraits and gentle religious scenes, mostly of Mary and baby Jesus. Raphael kept making this type of art for private buyers even after he moved to Rome in 1508. Many of his assistants copied his style.

CuratorialText: Raphael sought to bring the lauded ethereal physical beauty of the Madonna into radiant being. He was not immune to the convention, sung by poets both within and beyond his circle, that favored an elegant, aristocratic lady with dainty facial features and golden hair, but he also strove for long-established Christian ideals. Sermons, prayers, songs, and poetry inspired by early church writings had praised the Virgin’s beauty and grace as mirrors of her unique spiritual perfection. He infused his representations with a humanity and psychological presence revealed through gestures and reactions. Raphael’s paintings display an arresting command of light, color, space, and geometry, and his renderings of infants are more naturalistic in their proportions, poses, and expressions than those of many of his peers.
persona1_adapted: Raphael wanted to paint the Virgin Mary with a radiant, heavenly beauty. Like many poets of his time, he often showed her as an elegant, noble lady with delicate features and golden hair. But Raphael also followed old Christian traditions. Sermons, prayers, songs, and poems praised Mary’s beauty as a sign of her perfect spiritual goodness. Raphael gave his paintings a human feel. He showed emotion through the figures’ gestures and reactions. His works show a strong command of light, color, space, and shape. He also painted babies with more natural body proportions, poses, and expressions than many other artists of his day.

CuratorialText: Raphael’s portraits communicate a profound empathy and reflect years of exercising his hand in drawings to achieve an intimate, concerted contemplation of the sitter. The regal pose of Leonardo’s Mona Lisa, begun in 1503, offered a revolutionary model for the likeness of a luxuriously dressed person: a three-quarter view, head turned to the left, torso erect, and hands resting gracefully at the waist. The elegance of the poses here also suggests a striving for the cultivated manners of the Italian Renaissance courts and the ideals of beauty celebrated by famous poets. Raphael was an intimate friend of Baldassarre Castiglione (1478–1529), whose book on fashionable behavior at court promoted a model of comportment and grace that prized sprezzatura, a studied nonchalance or insouciance.
persona1_adapted: Raphael’s portraits show deep understanding of people. They also reflect years of practice drawing to capture a quiet, focused look at the sitter. Leonardo’s *Mona Lisa*, started in 1503, set a new standard for portraits of richly dressed people. The pose shows the person from a three-quarter angle, head turned left, body straight, and hands resting gently at the waist. The elegant poses in these paintings also hint at the polished manners valued in Italian Renaissance courts. They reflect the beauty ideals praised by famous poets of the time. Raphael was a close friend of Baldassarre Castiglione (1478–1529). Castiglione wrote a book about proper behavior at court. It promoted a model of grace and ease called *sprezzatura*—a kind of studied nonchalance, or coolness.

CuratorialText: Many Renaissance portraits of women were painted at the time of betrothal and marriage, a financial-dynastic arrangement in which romantic love had no place. Elegant fashion and jewelry were meant to impress the future husband’s family, as dowries and property were negotiated. Overt appraisals of women’s appearances by poets and artists also contributed to a culture in which young women could become innamorate—platonic beloveds or actual mistresses—of a male aristocracy.
persona1_adapted: Many Renaissance portraits of women were painted when they got engaged or married. Back then, marriage was about money and family power, not romantic love. Fancy clothes and jewelry were meant to impress the groom’s family while they discussed dowries and property. Poets and artists often praised women’s looks. This created a culture where young women could become innamorate—meaning either a platonic beloved or an actual mistress—of a wealthy, powerful man.

CuratorialText: At some point in 1508, Raphael arrived in Rome, where he would become the favorite court artist of Popes Julius II (r. 1503–13) and Leo X (r. 1513–21). An intriguing question is how exactly the twenty-five-year-old Raphael managed to supersede an earlier generation of painters working in the Vatican Palace and take control of the fresco decoration of the most important of the rooms, the Stanza della Segnatura. Surely artistic genius and personal savvy were key factors, but research shows that networks of political influence between Rome and his hometown of Urbino also paved the way, including support from his friend and countryman Donato Bramante (1444–1514), the pope’s favorite architect.
persona1_adapted: Around 1508, Raphael arrived in Rome. He became the favorite court artist for two popes: Julius II and Leo X. A big question is how this 25-year-old artist beat out older painters working in the Vatican Palace. He took over the fresco painting of the most important room, the Stanza della Segnatura. His artistic talent and personal skill were key. But research shows that political connections between Rome and his hometown, Urbino, also helped. His friend and fellow countryman Donato Bramante, the pope’s favorite architect, supported him too.

CuratorialText: Gathered here are Raphael’s studies for those Vatican frescoes: the School of Athens, with its gathering of philosophers, and the Disputa, representing Roman Catholic theology. These works show an artist in full command of the expressive potential of different drawing media. Rapidly sketched pen-and-ink sheets teem with the energy of ideas in the making, while studies in black chalk appear monumental despite their small size. In the next gallery, a digital video conveys the power of Raphael’s finished frescoes in four rooms of the Vatican Palace.
persona1_adapted: Here are Raphael’s studies for his famous Vatican frescoes. One is the *School of Athens*, which shows a group of philosophers. The other is the *Disputa*, which represents Catholic theology. These drawings show an artist who fully controls the expressive power of different drawing materials. Quickly sketched pen-and-ink sheets are full of the energy of ideas being formed. Studies in black chalk look grand and important, even though they are small. In the next gallery, a digital video shows the power of Raphael’s finished frescoes in four rooms of the Vatican Palace.

CuratorialText: By about 1510 Raphael was collecting accolades as the most visible and prolific painter at the papal court in Rome; meanwhile, shut away in the Sistine Chapel, the secretive Michelangelo labored to paint his ceiling frescoes, which would deeply influence the younger artist, who snuck in to see them. Drawings here relate to projects that occupied Raphael as he concluded his first series of frescoes in the Vatican Palace and began the second, in the Stanza di Eliodoro, which communicate with greater monumentality and dramatic force. After Pope Julius II died in 1513 and Leo X ascended to the throne, some learned imagery vanished in favor of blunter papal messaging.
persona1_adapted: By around 1510, Raphael was earning praise as the most famous and hardworking painter at the pope’s court in Rome. At the same time, the secretive Michelangelo was working alone in the Sistine Chapel on his ceiling frescoes—paintings made on wet plaster. These works deeply influenced Raphael, who snuck in to see them. Some drawings here show projects Raphael worked on as he finished his first set of frescoes in the Vatican Palace and began a second set in the Stanza di Eliodoro. These later frescoes feel bigger and more dramatic. After Pope Julius II died in 1513 and Leo X became pope, some of the learned, symbolic images disappeared. They were replaced by simpler messages that promoted the pope’s power.

CuratorialText: Other drawings and prints illustrate Raphael’s collaboration with Marcantonio Raimondi (ca. 1480–1534), a Bolognese printmaker and close friend. In their fruitful partnership, beginning in 1510, they created engravings of tremendous technical skill and artistic power. This medium enabled the reproduction of imagery in multiples and ensured a long afterlife for Raphael’s compositions. The prints were copied by artists well into the late 1800s and repurposed in other media, such as the maiolica plates in a nearby case.
persona1_adapted: Other drawings and prints show how Raphael worked with Marcantonio Raimondi (about 1480–1534). Raimondi was a printmaker from Bologna and a close friend of Raphael. Their partnership began in 1510 and was very successful. Together, they made engravings, which are images carved into metal plates. These prints showed great skill and artistic power. The engraving process allowed images to be copied many times. This helped Raphael’s art live on for a long time. Artists copied these prints into the late 1800s. They also used the designs in other art forms, like the painted ceramic plates in a nearby case.

CuratorialText: In the next gallery is a digital video showing Raphael’s monumental fresco cycles in four rooms of the Vatican Palace. The display provides a sense of scale and context for the many related drawings in this exhibition. Between 1508 and 1524, the frescoes were painted by Raphael and his workshop in the nearly square rooms now known as the Stanza della Segnatura, Stanza di Eliodoro, and Stanza dell’Incendio, and in the gargantuan oblong Sala di Costantino (completed by his assistants after his death in 1520).
persona1_adapted: In the next gallery, a digital video shows Raphael’s huge fresco cycles in four rooms of the Vatican Palace. The video helps you understand the size and setting of the many drawings in this exhibition. Between 1508 and 1524, Raphael and his workshop painted these frescoes in nearly square rooms. These rooms are now called the Stanza della Segnatura, Stanza di Eliodoro, and Stanza dell’Incendio. They also painted in the very large, long room called the Sala di Costantino. Raphael’s assistants finished that room after he died in 1520.

CuratorialText: The durable but difficult fresco technique requires expertise, the use of full-scale drawings (cartoons), and a willingness to anticipate challenges. Water-based colors applied to moist surface plaster (intonaco) dry and carbonate in a chemical process, permanently bonding the colors to the wall. Executed primarily by Raphael between 1508 and 1512, the frescoes in the Stanza della Segnatura are the most famous and include the School of Athens, the Disputa, the personifications of Justice, and the Parnassus. In the following three rooms Raphael worked with a greater involvement of collaborators who followed his designs closely.
persona1_adapted: Painting a fresco is a difficult but long-lasting technique. It requires skill, full-scale drawings called cartoons, and careful planning. The artist applies water-based paint onto wet plaster. As the plaster dries, a chemical reaction bonds the colors to the wall forever. Raphael painted most of the frescoes in the Stanza della Segnatura between 1508 and 1512. These are his most famous works. They include the School of Athens, the Disputa, the personifications of Justice, and the Parnassus. In the next three rooms, Raphael worked with more assistants. They closely followed his designs.

CuratorialText: The paintings here represent the breathtaking visual eloquence of Raphael’s later years. New harmonies of color are transformed by a dark chiaroscuro reminiscent of Leonardo. Sculptural forms seem to project from dense compositions. Charged gazes, poses, and gestures communicate a sense of impending drama.
persona1_adapted: These paintings show Raphael’s incredible skill in his later years. He uses new color combinations that are changed by dark shadows and light, a style similar to Leonardo da Vinci. Figures look like they are popping out of the crowded scenes. Intense looks, poses, and hand movements suggest that something dramatic is about to happen.

CuratorialText: Between 1514 and 1520, the demanding Pope Leo X rained project after project upon Raphael. He produced few paintings independently; using his drawings as a guide, skilled assistants painted most of the frescoes in the Vatican’s Stanza dell’Incendio. He was often disparaged for this practice by rivals, including his fierce enemy Michelangelo. But Raphael and his well-organized workshop completed an astonishing number of large-scale projects in his final six years. Executive help freed the artist to concentrate his creative energies on inventing new designs and exploring new forms. The involvement of workshop assistants in paintings and in certain types of drawings has inevitably led to debates about authorship. Labels in this exhibition attempt to indicate instances of their likely intervention, and a nearby display allows comparison of studies by Raphael and his fellow artists in an effort to characterize their individual styles.
persona1_adapted: Between 1514 and 1520, the demanding Pope Leo X gave Raphael one project after another. Raphael painted very few works alone. Instead, skilled assistants used his drawings as a guide to paint most of the frescoes in the Vatican’s Stanza dell’Incendio. His rivals, including his fierce enemy Michelangelo, often criticized him for this. But Raphael and his well-organized workshop completed an amazing number of large projects in his final six years. Having helpers freed Raphael to focus his creative energy on inventing new designs and exploring new forms. Because workshop assistants worked on paintings and some drawings, experts often debate who made what. Labels in this exhibition show where assistants likely helped. A nearby display lets you compare studies by Raphael and his fellow artists to see how their individual styles differ.

CuratorialText: The imperious Pope Leo X charged Raphael with the daunting task of designing a set of monumental tapestries to hang in the Sistine Chapel during special occasions—a project that put Raphael in direct competition with Michelangelo and his ceiling frescoes above. Starting in 1515, Raphael began drawing small preliminary studies. Aided by workshop assistants, he then painted in color gouaches on paper the enormous full-scale cartoons that would guide the weavers. Woven in Brussels using rich materials, the stratospherically expensive tapestries contributed to the bankruptcy of Leo’s papacy. With their inventive compositions, striking color harmonies, and monumental scale, they were soon the envy of monarchs across Europe. Kings commissioned second editions woven from Raphael’s cartoons, and the set owned by King Philip II of Spain (1527–1598) is represented here by three stunning examples.
persona1_adapted:
Pope Leo X gave Raphael a huge task. He asked Raphael to design a set of giant tapestries for the Sistine Chapel. These tapestries would hang there during special events. This project put Raphael in direct competition with Michelangelo, who had painted the ceiling above.

Raphael started in 1515. He drew small sketches to plan his ideas. Then, with help from his assistants, he painted full-size color guides on paper. These guides, called cartoons, showed the weavers exactly what to make.

The tapestries were woven in Brussels using rich materials. They cost an enormous amount of money. In fact, they helped cause Pope Leo’s papacy to go bankrupt.

The tapestries had creative designs, striking colors, and a huge size. Kings all over Europe soon wanted them. Many rulers ordered second copies made from Raphael’s original cartoons. The set owned by King Philip II of Spain is shown here with three stunning examples.

CuratorialText: As the tapestry designs progressed, the Raphael team largely took over the fresco work in the Vatican Palace, endeavoring to complete the Stanza dell’Incendio and assisting Raphael as he began to conceive decorations for the final space. Giulio Romano (1499?–1546) acted as his studio foreman and went on to enjoy a brilliant independent career.
persona1_adapted: As the tapestry designs moved forward, Raphael’s team took over most of the fresco painting in the Vatican Palace. They worked to finish the Stanza dell’Incendio, a room decorated with wall paintings. They also helped Raphael plan decorations for the last room. Giulio Romano (1499?–1546) served as the head of Raphael’s workshop. He later had a very successful career on his own.

CuratorialText: Raphael’s last and largest altarpiece is the enormous Transfiguration, commissioned by Cardinal Giulio de’ Medici (the future Pope Clement VII) around 1517. Spurred by competition with artistic rivals, Raphael produced a work markedly more dramatic, ambitious, and complex than his earlier altarpieces. Although its execution was interrupted by his untimely death in 1520, the Transfiguration became Raphael’s artistic testament. The main event is Christ’s Transfiguration on Mount Tabor, during which he transformed before the stunned apostles Peter, James, and John to reveal his divinity.
persona1_adapted: Raphael’s final and biggest altarpiece is the huge *Transfiguration*. It was ordered by Cardinal Giulio de’ Medici (who later became Pope Clement VII) around 1517. Pushed by competition with other artists, Raphael created a work that was much more dramatic, bold, and complex than his earlier altarpieces. Even though his sudden death in 1520 stopped him from finishing it, the *Transfiguration* became Raphael’s artistic legacy. The main scene shows Christ’s Transfiguration on Mount Tabor. During this event, Christ changed form in front of the stunned apostles Peter, James, and John to reveal his divine nature.

CuratorialText: Raphael’s studies for the Transfiguration, displayed in the niche at left, represent the culmination of his career as a draftsman. They are reunited here to show the range of his design process. Raphael used red chalk primarily when drawing nude models from life. He reserved black chalk for the full-scale studies of apostles’ heads and hands, a type of drawing called an “auxiliary cartoon” because the design starts from a dotted chalk outline transferred from another sheet.
persona1_adapted: Raphael’s drawings for the Transfiguration are on display in the niche to your left. These works show the peak of his skill as an artist. They are brought together here to reveal how he planned his art. Raphael mainly used red chalk when he drew nude models from real life. He used black chalk for large studies of the apostles’ faces and hands. This kind of drawing is called an “auxiliary cartoon.” The name comes from the dotted chalk outline that was copied from another sheet.

CuratorialText: Raphael rarely interrupted his projects for Popes Julius II and Leo X to make room for other commissions. The notable exception was his powerful friend Agostino Chigi (1466–1520), whom he met in about 1510. Born in Siena, Chigi became the richest banker in Europe, and his legendary wealth financed the popes’ cultural and military enterprises. Raphael’s work for Chigi included fresco paintings, as well as designs for his remodeled stables and the multimedia ensembles of his two chapels.
persona1_adapted: Raphael rarely stopped working on projects for Popes Julius II and Leo X to take on other jobs. But he made an exception for his powerful friend Agostino Chigi (1466–1520), whom he met around 1510. Chigi was born in Siena and became the richest banker in Europe. His huge fortune helped pay for the popes’ art projects and military campaigns. For Chigi, Raphael painted frescoes (wall paintings on wet plaster) and designed his remodeled stables. He also created the artwork for the two chapels belonging to Chigi.

CuratorialText: Displayed here are a variety of Raphael’s preparatory drawings for Chigi’s projects. In chalk studies of nude figures, Michelangelo’s language of contorted poses and powerful muscularity is transformed with an effortless grace and the sensuality of living flesh. The drawings also allude to the legacy of Raphael’s approach to the human figure, which led to the style known as Mannerism after his death. Male and female forms here anticipate prototypical Mannerist poses—as with Raphael’s primeval personification of an earthquake in a Sistine Chapel tapestry design, which spawned the figural vocabulary that his former assistant Giulio Romano would exploit to spectacular effect in his later frescoes.
persona1_adapted: Here are some of Raphael’s early drawings for Chigi’s projects. In these chalk studies of nude figures, Raphael took Michelangelo’s style of twisted poses and strong muscles. He changed it into something graceful and lifelike. These drawings also show how Raphael’s way of drawing the human body led to a style called Mannerism after he died. The male and female forms here look like early Mannerist poses. For example, Raphael’s drawing of an earthquake for a Sistine Chapel tapestry created a set of poses. His former assistant, Giulio Romano, later used those poses in a dramatic way in his own wall paintings.

CuratorialText: Rome and its ancient monuments transformed Raphael’s art, and his work likewise changed the face of Rome. Following his arrival in the Eternal City in 1508, he threw himself into archaeological studies and made drawings of monuments that demonstrate a nearly scientific rigor. Pope Leo X tasked Raphael with creating an enormous drawing of ancient Rome, “making those members that are entirely ruined and are completely invisible correspond with those that are still standing and can be seen.” A letter to Leo, written on Raphael’s behalf, attests to the artist’s deep archaeological knowledge and technical skill, including a highly original method for using a magnetic compass in conducting surveys of ruins.
persona1_adapted: Rome and its ancient buildings changed Raphael’s art. In return, his work also changed the look of Rome. When he arrived in the Eternal City in 1508, he dove into studying old ruins. He made drawings of monuments with almost scientific care. Pope Leo X asked Raphael to create a huge drawing of ancient Rome. The pope wanted him to “make the parts that are completely ruined and invisible match up with the parts that still stand and can be seen.” A letter written for Raphael to the pope shows the artist’s deep knowledge of archaeology and his technical skill. It also describes a very original method he used: a magnetic compass to survey ruins.

CuratorialText: Around 1503 Pope Julius II had charged Bramante, Raphael’s mentor and close friend from Urbino, to design a new Saint Peter’s Basilica that would convey the majesty of the papacy in the aesthetic language of ancient Rome. Drawings and prints here illuminate Raphael’s career as an architect after he took over this massive project upon Bramante’s death. In 1517 Raphael bought the Palazzo Caprini, designed by Bramante and now destroyed but pictured in two works here, where he lived out his final years in Rome as the “prince of painters.”
persona1_adapted: Around 1503, Pope Julius II asked Bramante to design a new Saint Peter’s Basilica. Bramante was Raphael’s teacher and close friend from Urbino. The pope wanted the building to show the power of the church using the style of ancient Rome. Drawings and prints here show Raphael’s work as an architect after he took over this huge project when Bramante died. In 1517, Raphael bought the Palazzo Caprini. Bramante had designed this building, which is now gone but appears in two works here. Raphael spent his last years in Rome living there as the “prince of painters.”

CuratorialText: The Met acquired its first Chinese painting in 1902. Since then, the Museum has added more than two thousand works of painting and calligraphy, building one of the most comprehensive collections in the world. Spanning fifteen hundred years of cultural production and featuring a variety of genres, techniques, and styles, The Met's collection has become a key resource for the study of Chinese painting and calligraphy. This exhibition presents a rich selection of works from the collection arranged in a largely chronological display. The final gallery tells the story of Wen C. Fong (1930–2018), chairman of the Museum’s Department of Asian Art from 1971 to 2000, and his teacher Li Jian (1881–1956). As a young calligraphy prodigy in Shanghai in the 1940s, Fong studied brush arts with Li. The display centers on a group of fans, painted and written by Li, that were intended to transmit the canonical models of the classical tradition from teacher to student. Brought to the United States in 1949 and treasured by the Fong family since then, these fans are presented publicly for the first time.
persona1_adapted:
The Met got its first Chinese painting in 1902. Since then, the museum has added more than two thousand works of painting and calligraphy. Calligraphy is the art of beautiful handwriting. This makes the Met’s collection one of the most complete in the world. The works span 1,500 years of culture. They show many different types, methods, and styles. The Met’s collection is now a key resource for studying Chinese painting and calligraphy.

This exhibition shows a rich selection of works from the collection. They are arranged mostly in order of time. The last room tells the story of Wen C. Fong (1930–2018) and his teacher Li Jian (1881–1956). Fong was the head of the museum’s Asian Art department from 1971 to 2000. In the 1940s, as a young calligraphy prodigy in Shanghai, Fong studied brush arts with Li. A prodigy is someone with great talent at a young age. The display focuses on a group of fans. Li painted and wrote on them. He meant for them to pass on the classic models of the tradition from teacher to student. These fans were brought to the United States in 1949. The Fong family has treasured them ever since. Now they are shown to the public for the first time.

CuratorialText: The hand-held fan was an unexpected muse for some of the most innovative artists in 19th-century Europe. Fans became hugely popular across many levels of society during this period, serving as functional and fashionable objects of adornment and communication. Well-known French Impressionists such as Edgar Degas and Camille Pissarro not only featured this feminine accessory in their work but also adopted it as an experimental format for their art. Fanmania investigates why avant-garde artists incorporated fans into their work and sheds light on themes of gender, courtship, consumerism, and appropriation. Artists were attracted to the semicircular form for myriad reasons, including fascination with fans from Asia and Spain, commercial ambition, and their interest in formal and technical innovation. Displaying more than 75 artworks from across The Met collection, this multimedia exhibition features painted and printed fans from Europe and Asia as well as artworks that depict women wielding fans to explore the phenomenon of “fanmania.”
persona1_adapted: The hand-held fan inspired some of the most creative artists in 19th-century Europe. Fans became very popular across all levels of society during this time. People used them as both a stylish accessory and a way to communicate. Famous French Impressionists like Edgar Degas and Camille Pissarro included this feminine item in their paintings. They also used fans as a new and experimental surface for their art. The exhibition *Fanmania* explores why forward-thinking artists added fans to their work. It also looks at ideas about gender, dating, shopping, and cultural borrowing. Artists liked the half-circle shape for many reasons. Some were drawn to fans from Asia and Spain. Others saw a chance to sell their work. Many were interested in trying new techniques and forms. This multimedia show displays more than 75 artworks from across The Met collection. It includes painted and printed fans from Europe and Asia. It also shows artworks of women using fans to help explain the “fanmania” craze.

CuratorialText: During the nineteenth century, handheld fans achieved unprecedented popularity throughout European society. A newfound artistic appreciation for these objects also emerged, fueled by cultural crazes for all things Japanese and Spanish as well as by exhibitions and publications devoted to fan making and its history. “Almost all the great artists of the century have contributed . . . to the decoration of the best fans,” wrote the author Octave Uzanne in 1882. For the fourth Impressionist exhibition of 1879, Edgar Degas envisioned an entire gallery devoted to fans. Though the fan-shaped paintings his colleagues submitted were ultimately dispersed throughout the show, they nevertheless elicited strong responses from the public, including bewilderment, as illustrated below. Today, these works continue to raise questions—mainly, why did ambitious European artists adopt this accessory as a format for their work? Bringing together artworks from across The Met, Fanmania explores the reasons artists were drawn to this semicircular form, including its commercial potential, its fashionability, and the opportunities it offered for formal and technical innovation. Although avant-garde fans were primarily designed for display rather than use, they retain many of the associations of functional fans. Themes of gender, courtship, appropriation, and experimentation unfold in this examination of fans as objects of adornment, decoration, and communication in the nineteenth century.
persona1_adapted: In the 1800s, handheld fans became very popular across Europe. People also started to see fans as art. This was partly because of a craze for Japanese and Spanish culture. Exhibitions and books about fan-making also helped. In 1882, the writer Octave Uzanne said, “Almost all the great artists of the century have contributed to the decoration of the best fans.” For the fourth Impressionist exhibition in 1879, artist Edgar Degas planned a whole room just for fans. The fan-shaped paintings his friends submitted were spread throughout the show. Still, they got strong reactions from the public, including confusion, as shown below. Today, these works still raise questions. Mainly, why did serious European artists use this accessory as a format for their art? The exhibition *Fanmania* brings together artworks from across The Met. It explores why artists liked this half-circle shape. Reasons include its potential to sell, its fashion appeal, and the chance to try new forms and techniques. Although avant-garde fans were mostly made for display, not use, they still carry many meanings of real fans. Themes of gender, courtship, borrowing from other cultures, and trying new ideas come out in this look at fans as objects of decoration, communication, and fashion in the 1800s.

CuratorialText: Handheld folding fans are multimedia objects that involve many types of artistry and artisanal skill. This show focuses primarily on the role of painters and printmakers in the production of the fan’s semicircular leaf. In Europe, prior to 1700, most of these designs were unsigned. In the nineteenth century, major artists grew increasingly interested in making fans and began signing their work, as did preeminent fan-making houses, which evolved out of earlier guilds. Some of the precedents for these practices are on display here.
persona1_adapted: Handheld folding fans are objects that combine different kinds of art and craft skills. This show focuses mainly on the painters and printmakers who made the fan’s semicircular leaf. Before 1700 in Europe, most of these designs were not signed. In the 1800s, major artists became more interested in making fans and began signing their work. So did the top fan-making companies, which grew out of earlier guilds. Some examples of these earlier practices are on display here.

CuratorialText: Historically, European fans have been most closely associated with the eighteenth century, when their use became widespread. The examples here show the various roles fans played, including as tools for flirtation, amusement, education, propaganda, observation, and masquerade. Fan leaves communicated through their subjects and style, while users brought them to life through their gestures. Women were not only the consumers of fans but also among their producers, as fan making was considered an art form appropriate for female practitioners, whether amateur or professional.
persona1_adapted: Historically, European fans are most linked to the 1700s, when people started using them widely. The fans here show many different roles. They were tools for flirting, having fun, learning, spreading ideas, watching others, and wearing at costume parties. The fan’s surface sent messages through its pictures and style. The person using the fan brought it to life with their movements. Women did not just buy and use fans. They also helped make them. Fan making was seen as a proper art form for women, whether they did it as a hobby or as a job.

CuratorialText: Handheld fans found perhaps their most prominent public display in the opera house. The atmosphere of spectatorship and performance—present both onstage and among the audience—heightened attention to fans in the hands of female theatergoers. The emphasis on seeing and being seen even gave rise to fan designs that incorporated opera glasses, or lorgnettes (from the French lorgner, “to ogle”), and small mirrors. A lorgnette fan is on view in the case nearby. Fan makers also developed a genre of fans devoted to the opera. These fans incorporated scores, lyrics, and homages to celebrated composers.
persona1_adapted: Handheld fans were most famously used in the opera house. There, watching and being watched was a big part of the experience—both onstage and among the crowd. This made people pay extra attention to the fans held by women in the audience. Because seeing and being seen mattered so much, some fans even included opera glasses (also called lorgnettes, from the French word for "to ogle") or small mirrors. You can see a lorgnette fan in the case nearby. Fan makers also created a special type of fan just for the opera. These fans featured music scores, lyrics, and tributes to famous composers.

CuratorialText: As theatergoing grew more popular later in the century, Impressionist artists seized on the novel subject of spectators in the theater loge. The fashionability and ubiquity of fans at the opera had become fully apparent by the early 1880s, as evidenced by the cartoon below.
persona1_adapted: As going to the theater became more popular later in the 1800s, Impressionist artists started painting a new subject: people watching the show from theater boxes. By the early 1880s, it was clear that fans were both fashionable and everywhere at the opera. The cartoon below shows this.

CuratorialText: Movement is an essential aspect of the handheld fan. The nineteenth-century critic Charles Blanc wrote that the fan is “above all things . . . a means or motive of gracious movements, under pretext of agitating the air for the sake of coolness.” The expressive qualities of a fan’s motions are among the ways it can convey meaning and reveal the user’s personality, mood, or intentions. Fans can flutter, vibrate, waft, snap, open, shut, rise, and even fall (or be deliberately dropped) at a particularly opportune moment before a potential suitor.
persona1_adapted: Movement is a key part of a handheld fan. The 19th-century critic Charles Blanc wrote that the fan is “above all things . . . a means or motive of gracious movements, under pretext of agitating the air for the sake of coolness.” The way a fan moves can show meaning and reveal the user’s personality, mood, or intentions. Fans can flutter, vibrate, waft, snap, open, shut, rise, and even fall (or be dropped on purpose) at just the right moment in front of a possible romantic interest.

CuratorialText: Artists in the late nineteenth century understood the fan as a moving object and attempted to capture its dynamism in myriad ways. The fleeting and ephemeral nature of its movement surely made it an appealing subject for the Impressionists. Fan designs frequently incorporate metallic paints, gold leaf, feathers, spangles, and sequins, the visual impacts of which are heightened when in motion.
persona1_adapted: In the late 1800s, artists saw the fan as a moving object. They tried to capture its energy in many different ways. The fan’s quick, short-lived movement made it a favorite subject for the Impressionists. Fan designs often use shiny paints, gold leaf, feathers, and sparkly decorations. These details look even more striking when the fan is in motion.

CuratorialText: The term “Japonisme” was first used by French critics in the early 1870s to describe the passion for Japanese art and culture they observed among their contemporaries. Japanese goods became increasingly available in Europe after the United States forced Japan to open its ports to international trade in 1853–54. The 1867 and 1878 Universal Exhibitions in Paris were pivotal events in Europeans’ exposure to the arts of Japan. Many Impressionist artists and their friends collected such works, including fans, acquiring them from newly established specialist dealers and shops. By the late 1880s, Japanese fans were imported in the millions to France, where they were used as women’s accessories and decoration.
persona1_adapted: In the early 1870s, French critics first used the word “Japonisme.” It describes the strong interest in Japanese art and culture that many people had at that time. Japanese goods became easier to find in Europe after the United States forced Japan to open its ports to world trade in 1853–54. Two big events in Paris—the Universal Exhibitions of 1867 and 1878—helped Europeans see Japanese art for the first time. Many Impressionist artists and their friends collected Japanese items, like fans. They bought them from new shops and dealers who specialized in these goods. By the late 1880s, millions of Japanese fans were imported to France. People used them as fashion accessories and home decoration.

CuratorialText: European artists focused on the aesthetic value of these objects and borrowed from them liberally, often with little understanding of their original contexts. Today, some would call this cultural appropriation, though subtleties of interpretation varied. Certainly, the history of Japonisme is fraught, but the admiration for Japanese fans freed European artists to imagine new techniques and strategies for their art.
persona1_adapted: European artists valued these objects for their beauty. They borrowed ideas from them freely, often without knowing their original meanings. Today, some people would call this cultural appropriation, though opinions on it differ. The history of Japonisme is complicated. Still, the love for Japanese fans helped European artists explore new methods and approaches in their work.

CuratorialText: In the nineteenth century, Spain became a center of fan production at the same time that the country’s tourism industry emerged and its culture was increasingly exported throughout Europe. Reporting from his travels to the Iberian Peninsula in the early 1840s, the French writer Théophile Gautier claimed, “A woman without a fan is something I have not yet seen in this happy country.” The fan’s prominent role in Spanish flamenco dance is likely another reason it became inextricably linked with Spain in the nineteenth-century imagination, and remains so today. In Paris at mid-century, Spanish culture was in vogue, a phenomenon known as Hispagnolisme. Spanish-born empress Eugénie de Montijo, who married Napoleon III in 1853, exerted considerable influence on fashion and taste. Spanish dance troupes and musicians performed often in the capital, and avant-garde artists, such as Edouard Manet, took them up as subjects. For some artists who made fans in this period, it was Spain that they had foremost in mind. Manet and Degas both chose Spanish themes for their earliest fan designs.
persona1_adapted: In the 1800s, Spain became a major center for making fans. At the same time, Spain’s tourism industry was growing, and Spanish culture was spreading across Europe. The French writer Théophile Gautier traveled to Spain in the early 1840s. He wrote, “A woman without a fan is something I have not yet seen in this happy country.” Fans also played a big role in Spanish flamenco dance. This is another reason why fans became closely linked with Spain in the 1800s, and they still are today. In Paris around the mid-1800s, Spanish culture was very popular. This trend was called Hispagnolisme. Empress Eugénie de Montijo was born in Spain. She married Napoleon III in 1853 and had a strong influence on fashion and taste. Spanish dance groups and musicians often performed in Paris. Avant-garde artists, like Edouard Manet, used them as subjects for their work. Avant-garde means artists who try new, experimental ideas. For some artists who made fans during this time, Spain was their main inspiration. Both Manet and Degas chose Spanish themes for their earliest fan designs.

CuratorialText: The theme of the bourgeois woman with her fan in a domestic setting proliferated in art of the last quarter of the nineteenth century. In his 1882 book The Fan, the French writer Octave Uzanne described the role of fans to his female readers: “In all solitude, in every despair, it will remain your confidante.” Whether at home or on ventures out, the handheld fan served as a companion, trusted protector, and elegant accoutrement.
persona1_adapted: In the late 1800s, many artists painted middle-class women holding fans at home. In his 1882 book *The Fan*, French writer Octave Uzanne told his female readers: “In all solitude, in every despair, it will remain your confidante.” Whether at home or out and about, the handheld fan was a companion, a trusted protector, and a stylish accessory.

CuratorialText: As fashion accessories, fans signaled facets of their user’s identity through their materials and stylistic form as well as in the way they were handled. “One wave of the Fan is sufficient by itself to distinguish between a princess and a countess, a marchioness and a plebeian,” remarked one female observer cited by Uzanne. A Japanese uchiwa fan in the hands of the Impressionist Berthe Morisot or a plumed fan held by one of Mary Cassatt’s sitters displayed their alignment with current taste and confirmed their social status.
persona1_adapted: Fans were more than just fashion accessories. They showed parts of a person's identity through their materials, style, and how they were used. One woman, quoted by writer Uzanne, said, "One wave of the Fan is enough to tell a princess from a countess, or a marchioness from a commoner." When Impressionist artist Berthe Morisot held a Japanese uchiwa fan, or when one of Mary Cassatt's subjects held a feathered fan, it showed they followed the latest trends and confirmed their place in society.

CuratorialText: Fans were among the first souvenirs, used since the seventeenth century to commemorate events and to remember visits to important sites. As travel increased in the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries, fans became popular as small, practical, and artistic objects that could serve as mementos. The Universal Exhibitions of the second half of the nineteenth century played a crucial role in promoting the French fan industry and exposing the European public to fans from other cultures. Souvenir fans were produced for each of these events, providing visitors with information and maps while also recording the remarkable buildings erected specifically for these occasions.
persona1_adapted: Fans were some of the first souvenirs. People have used them since the 1600s to remember special events and visits to important places. As travel grew in the 1700s and 1800s, fans became popular as small, useful, and artistic keepsakes. The World's Fairs in the late 1800s helped boost the French fan industry. They also showed European audiences fans from other cultures. Souvenir fans were made for each of these fairs. They gave visitors information and maps. They also showed the amazing buildings built just for these events.

CuratorialText: This monumental work was one of a series of carved architectural supports designed for the exterior courtyard of a Yoruba palace. It was commissioned by a king from the most renowned master sculptor in the history of Yoruba art, Olowe of Ise. The two-tiered composition embodies a formal dynamism that established Olowe's exceptional artistic reputation. Its principal personage, an equestrian warrior, is depicted frontally above a female caryatid with arms raised in three-quarter view. Through this contrast in their alignment, the figures at once reflect distinct attitudes while relating to each other fluidly. The compressed style of the upper half also contrasts with the greater degree of openness below. At the base two smaller figures radiate outward at oblique angles. In addition to the inventive dynamism of their overall design, works by Olowe are noted for the attention given to surface detail. The mounted warrior holds a spear and pistol in either hand, and his vest, saddle, and horse's headgear are articulated through deeply carved linear motifs. While innovative in its formal interpretation, the subject of this work is a classic emblem of regional leadership. In Yoruba art such equestrian figures identify their patrons with martial conquest achieved through physical might.
persona1_adapted:
This large carved post was part of a set made for the outdoor courtyard of a Yoruba palace. A king ordered it from Olowe of Ise, the most famous master sculptor in Yoruba art history.

The two-level design shows a lively sense of movement that made Olowe famous. The main figure is a warrior on horseback, shown facing forward. Below him stands a female figure with her arms raised, shown at a slight angle. This contrast in their poses shows different attitudes, yet the figures connect smoothly with each other.

The upper half is carved in a tight, compact style. The lower half is more open and spread out. At the base, two smaller figures lean outward at angles.

Olowe’s works are also known for their detailed surfaces. The mounted warrior holds a spear in one hand and a pistol in the other. His vest, saddle, and the horse’s headgear are decorated with deep, carved lines.

Even though the design is fresh and new, the subject is a classic symbol of local leadership. In Yoruba art, horse-riding figures show that their owners gained power through military strength.

CuratorialText: This saltcellar is both an extraordinary example of skilled workmanship and an artifact that epitomizes a singularly important convergence of cultures. In the second half of the fifteenth century, Portuguese explorers and traders were impressed by the considerable talent of ivory carvers along the coast of West Africa. As a result, they were inspired to commission works of this kind for their patrons, which ingeniously combine both European and African aesthetics and forms. During this period, salt and pepper were costly commodities and elaborate receptacles were appropriate for their storage in princely homes. This work contains imagery relating to early Temne and Bullom worldviews. The four snakes, associated with mystical wealth, appear to confront four growling dogs. According to regional traditions, dogs are considered spiritually astute animals able to see spirits and ghosts that are invisible to humans. This depiction of the dogs, with teeth bared, hair bristling, and ears laid back, may relate to that ability. However, the level of animation in this scene could also derive from chivalric hunting scenes in European woodcuts, which were furnished to local African artists by their European patrons.
persona1_adapted:
This saltcellar is a great example of skilled craftsmanship. It also shows an important meeting of different cultures. In the late 1400s, Portuguese explorers and traders noticed the amazing talent of ivory carvers along the West African coast. They asked these artists to make works like this one for their wealthy patrons back home. The pieces cleverly mix both European and African styles and shapes.

At that time, salt and pepper were expensive. Rich families needed fancy containers to store them. This saltcellar includes images from early Temne and Bullom beliefs. Four snakes, which stand for mystical wealth, seem to face four growling dogs. Local traditions say dogs are spiritually aware. They can see spirits and ghosts that people cannot see. The dogs in this carving show their teeth, bristle their fur, and flatten their ears. This might show that special ability.

But the lively scene could also come from European hunting pictures. European patrons gave those images to local African artists as examples to follow.

CuratorialText: By the early nineteenth century, Chokwe chiefs in the savanna of present-day Democratic Republic of Congo and Angola had become active trading partners with merchants from Europe and the New World. Profiting from commerce in ivory, rubber, wax, and African slaves, they emerged as important regional rulers whose prestige and power was reflected in the art they commissioned for their courts at this time. This figure depicts a Chokwe chief, or mwanangana, and epitomizes the balance of power and refinement that is characteristic of Chokwe court art. His large, spreading hands, muscular shoulders, and aggressive posture epitomize the strength and vigor of a ruler. A bulging, domelike forehead and large eyes suggest mental acuity and keen eyesight. The sweeping curves and swelling volumes of the distinctive headdress, rendered here in wood, depict an actual Chokwe crown made of cloth-covered basketry. Horns of the tiny duiker antelope, which bear associations with hunting and the dangerous, mysterious practices of medicine and sorcery, are placed on the sides of the headdress. The chief holds a lamellophone, or "thumb piano," an instrument whose music often accompanies recitations of oral histories and the praise songs of important individuals. Instrument in hand, the chief is cast as a repository and guardian of historical and cultural knowledge. While depicting a nineteenth-century Chokwe chief, this sculpture also evokes the culture hero Cibinda Ilunga, a legendary hunter from the powerful Luba kingdom. Cibinda Ilunga is said to have introduced new principles of governance to the Chokwe peoples, and figures such as this one honor the productive union of these two cultures.
persona1_adapted:
By the early 1800s, Chokwe chiefs in the savanna of what is now the Democratic Republic of Congo and Angola were active trading partners with European and New World merchants. They made money from trading ivory, rubber, wax, and enslaved African people. These chiefs became important regional rulers. Their power and status showed in the art they ordered for their courts.

This figure shows a Chokwe chief, called a mwanangana. It captures the mix of power and elegance found in Chokwe court art. The chief’s large, open hands, strong shoulders, and bold pose show his strength and energy. A bulging, dome-shaped forehead and big eyes suggest he is smart and sharp-sighted.

The headdress has sweeping curves and swelling shapes. It is made of wood but copies a real Chokwe crown made from cloth-covered basketry. On the sides of the headdress are horns from a tiny duiker antelope. These horns connect to hunting and to the dangerous, mysterious practices of medicine and sorcery.

The chief holds a lamellophone, also called a “thumb piano.” Music from this instrument often goes with spoken stories of history and praise songs for important people. By holding the instrument, the chief is shown as a keeper and protector of history and culture.

Although this sculpture shows a 19th-century Chokwe chief, it also brings to mind the culture hero Cibinda Ilunga. He was a legendary hunter from the powerful Luba kingdom. People say Cibinda Ilunga brought new ways of ruling to the Chokwe. Figures like this one honor the good mix of these two cultures.

CuratorialText: The Dogon live in the elevated rocky heights of Mali's Bandiagara Escarpment. They rely on agriculture and manage to wrest subsistence crops from poor soil in an area that receives little rain. This monumental figure is one of the largest Dogon sculptures known. The region's dry climate has preserved Dogon works far longer than is usual for African wood sculpture, and radiocarbon testing of this work suggests that it may date as far back as the fifteenth century. Dogon sculpture is primarily concerned with the spirits responsible for the fertility of both land and people. These include a family's real and mythical ancestors, the souls of women who died in childbirth, and water spirits. While the exact functions and meanings of individual works often remain obscure, scholars agree that Dogon sculptures were created for shrines. The most distinctive subject rendered by Dogon sculptures is that of a single figure standing with raised arms. This posture has usually been interpreted as a gesture of prayer—an effort to link earth and heavens—and it has been suggested that it may represent an appeal for rain. This masterpiece of Dogon figurative art depicts a man with a well-modeled body in a naturalistic stance. The artist has carefully rendered the figure's musculature, anatomical detail, and body ornament. His stylized beard identifies him as an elder and an individual whose age and experience entitle him to participate in the most important religious, political, and social affairs of Dogon society. The figure wears wristlets, armlets, and anklets that indicate his status, as well as a belt and neck pendants resembling leather talismans that also suggest his spiritual importance.
persona1_adapted:
The Dogon people live in the rocky highlands of Mali's Bandiagara Escarpment. They farm for food, growing crops in poor soil with very little rain. This large statue is one of the biggest Dogon sculptures ever found. The dry climate has helped preserve Dogon artworks much longer than most African wood sculptures. Radiocarbon testing shows this piece may date back to the 1400s.

Dogon sculpture focuses on spirits that help the land and people stay fertile. These spirits include real and mythical family ancestors, the souls of women who died in childbirth, and water spirits. Scholars are not sure what each sculpture was used for, but they agree that Dogon sculptures were made for shrines (special sacred spaces).

The most common subject in Dogon sculpture is a single figure standing with raised arms. Experts think this pose is a prayer gesture—a way to connect earth and sky. Some believe it may be a plea for rain.

This masterpiece shows a man with a well-shaped body in a natural pose. The artist carefully carved the figure's muscles, body details, and decorations. His stylized beard marks him as an elder—someone old and wise enough to take part in the most important religious, political, and social events of Dogon society. The figure wears wristlets, armlets, and anklets that show his high status. He also has a belt and neck pendants that look like leather charms, hinting at his spiritual power.

CuratorialText: When works from Equatorial Africa in this refined style began to enter Western consciousness in the early twentieth century, they were a great enigma to art critics. Many speculated about the sources of their exotic aesthetic and even proposed possible Asian influence, though the art form was in fact indigenous to southern Gabon. Such masks were worn by virtuosic male performers of a stilt dance called "mukudj," which involved towering impressively while executing complex choreography and astonishing feats of acrobatics. The creator of a "mukudj" mask would attempt to capture the likeness of the most beautiful woman in his community. The subject of this particular idealized and stylized portrait was embellished in classic nineteenth-century fashion with a coiffure composed of a central lobe and two lateral tresses and with cicatrization motifs on the forehead and temples. Kaolin taken from riverbeds, which was associated with healing and with a spiritual, ancestral realm of existence, was applied to the surface of the face. By using this material, the artist both celebrated the beauty of a mortal woman and transformed her into a transcendent being.
persona1_adapted:
In the early 1900s, art critics in the West first saw works from Equatorial Africa in this refined style. They found them very puzzling. Many guessed about where this exotic style came from. Some even thought it might have been influenced by Asian art. But in fact, this art form came from southern Gabon.

These masks were worn by male performers who were very skilled. They danced on stilts in a performance called "mukudj." The dancers would tower high above the ground. They performed complex dance moves and amazing acrobatic tricks.

The artist who made a "mukudj" mask tried to capture the face of the most beautiful woman in his community. This particular mask shows a stylized, idealized portrait. It follows classic nineteenth-century style. The hair has a central lobe with two side braids. There are also scar patterns on the forehead and temples.

The artist applied kaolin to the face. Kaolin is a white clay taken from riverbeds. People believed it had healing powers and was linked to the spirit world of ancestors. By using this material, the artist celebrated the beauty of a real woman. He also turned her into a being that goes beyond ordinary life.

CuratorialText: A lü me was typically commissioned by a man of standing in honor of a favorite wife. The subject’s distinguishing traits, including her facial features, cicatrices, and braided coiffure, would be captured by the artist through direct observation. The unveiling of a completed figure was celebrated with lavish feasting sponsored by its patron. Named for their subjects and stored in special baskets, such portraits were subsequently displayed on special occasions. This example was carved by celebrated early 20th century carver, Zlan of Belewale, and his wife, Sonzlanwan. Zlan’s calling was revealed to him as an adolescent through the miraculous appearance of his signature tool, an adze, falling from the branches of an oil palm. The sculptor honed his skills on soft tuber roots and soapstone before his mastery of wood earned him the name Zlan (sometimes spelled Sra), meaning "creator" or "god." As the most sought-after Dan artist of the first half of the twentieth century, Zlan mentored scores of apprentices. Many of these trainees were sent by regional chiefs to acquire his expertise for their own communities. Through his efforts, the small Wè village of Belewale—where his father had also been a sculptor—became an important carving center. Zlan’s works, including masks, ladles, and figurative portraits, have been collected globally. Defying the convention that woodcarving was a man’s vocation, Sonzlanwon (literally, "a snail, if god agrees") collaborated with her husband on many of his greatest works. She accompanied him on commissions for important patrons—the intense concentration required to execute these was such that they often worked side by side in seclusion. Sonzlanwon is credited with blocking out Zlan’s works’ initial form, using wood felled by his apprentices. Following his refinement of the contours, she handled final detailing, including the braided raffia-palm coiffures added to figures and ladles. As Zlan aged, it is believed that Sonzlanwon’s role in their collaboration expanded. Documentation of her contributions derives from interviews conducted by German anthropologist Hans Himmelheber shortly before Zlan’s death, as well as from remembrances of surviving relatives and acquaintances.
persona1_adapted:
A lü me was usually made for an important man to honor his favorite wife. The artist would study the woman closely to capture her unique features, like her face, scars, and braided hair. When the carving was finished, the person who paid for it threw a big feast to celebrate. These portraits were named after the women they showed and kept in special baskets. They were brought out only for special events.

This portrait was carved by Zlan of Belewale, a famous artist from the early 1900s, and his wife, Sonzlanwon. When Zlan was a teenager, he discovered his calling in a strange way: his special tool, an adze (a type of axe for carving wood), seemed to fall from a palm tree branch. He first practiced carving on soft roots and soapstone. After he mastered wood, people called him Zlan (sometimes spelled Sra), which means "creator" or "god."

Zlan became the most popular Dan artist in the first half of the 1900s. He taught many students. Local chiefs often sent these students to learn from him and bring his skills back to their own villages. Because of Zlan, the small Wè village of Belewale—where his father had also been a carver—became an important place for making carvings. Zlan’s works, like masks, spoons, and figure portraits, are now collected all over the world.

Sonzlanwon (her name means "a snail, if god agrees") broke the rule that woodcarving was only for men. She worked with her husband on many of his best pieces. When they took on jobs for important clients, they often worked alone together because the work needed so much focus. Sonzlanwon is known for carving the basic shape of Zlan’s works, using wood that his students had cut down. After Zlan smoothed out the details, she added the final touches, like the braided raffia-palm hair on figures and spoons. As Zlan got older, people believe Sonzlanwon did more of the work.

We know about her role from interviews a German anthropologist named Hans Himmelheber did just before Zlan died, and from what his surviving family and friends remembered.

CuratorialText: Central African power figures are among the ubiquitous genres identified with African art. Conceived to house specific mystical forces, they were collaborative creations of Kongo sculptors and ritual specialists. This example belongs to the most ambitious class of that tradition, attributed to the atelier of a master active along the coast of Congo and Angola at the end of the nineteenth century and identified with Mangaaka, the preeminent force of jurisprudence. That power was represented as a presiding authority and enforcing lord or king. Its crowning element is the distinctive mpu headdress worn by chiefs or priests. The figure's posture and gesture, leaning forward with hands placed akimbo on the hips, is the aggressive attitude of one who challenges fearlessly. Traces of a missing beard-a sign of seniority-survive in the form of nails along the contours of the chin. There are also vestiges of an abdominal cavity for medicinal matter that originally attracted the figure's defining force. The various metals embedded in the figure's expansive torso attest to its central role as witness and enforcer of affairs critical to its community. They document vows sealed, treaties signed, and efforts to eradicate evil. Ultimately, this work inspired reflection on the consequences of transgressing established codes of social conduct.
persona1_adapted:
Central African power figures are some of the most well-known types of African art. People believed these figures held special spiritual powers. They were made together by Kongo sculptors and ritual experts.

This example is one of the most important kinds of these figures. It was made by a master artist and his workshop. They worked along the coast of Congo and Angola in the late 1800s. The figure represents Mangaaka, the main force of justice. Mangaaka was shown as a ruler or king who had authority and enforced the law.

The figure wears a special hat called an mpu headdress. Only chiefs or priests wore this hat. The figure leans forward with hands on its hips. This pose shows an aggressive attitude, like someone who is not afraid to challenge others.

There are traces of a beard that is now missing. The beard was a sign of old age and wisdom. You can see nails along the chin where the beard used to be. The figure also had a space in its belly for medicine. That medicine was what gave the figure its power.

Many different metals are stuck into the figure's chest. These metals show that the figure was a witness and enforcer for important events in the community. They mark promises made, treaties signed, and efforts to get rid of evil. In the end, this work made people think about what happens when you break the rules of society.

CuratorialText: Throughout the twentieth century, members of Poro, a Senufo initiation association, wore small, finely carved face masks as insignia. The masks, known as kpeliye'e, feature delicate oval faces with geometric projections at the sides. Raised and incised scarification patterns ornament their smooth, glossy surfaces. Considered feminine, the masks honor deceased Senufo elders with their grace and beauty. They provide a complement to the aggressive Senufo helmet masks also sponsored by fraternal organizations in the region. The feathers and animal horns attached to this example are unusual, and may have reflected its owner's power to counteract negative forces in the community.
persona1_adapted: Throughout the 1900s, members of Poro—a Senufo group for young men—wore small, finely carved face masks as symbols of their rank. These masks are called *kpeliye'e*. They have delicate oval faces with geometric shapes sticking out from the sides. Raised and carved scar patterns decorate their smooth, shiny surfaces. People see these masks as feminine. They honor dead Senufo elders with their grace and beauty. The masks balance the fierce helmet masks also worn by men’s groups in the area. The feathers and animal horns attached to this mask are unusual. They may have shown the owner’s power to fight off bad forces in the community.

CuratorialText: This cross was created in the northern Highlands, near the Red Sea, the birthplace of Ethiopia's earliest kingdom and of Christianity in Africa. A small corpus of Ethiopian Christian works from before the seventeenth century has survived. This exceptional creation, in which a highly unusual interplay of materials affords rich tonal contrasts, is a tour de force. The solidity of the carved-wood structure is permeated by finely applied metal inlays. The complexity of this work, with each side elaborately distinct, is unrivaled. It has been attributed to Ezra (ca. 1460-1522), a member of the Stephanite monastic order. The Stephanites came to be recognized as the foremost carvers of crosses and illuminators of manuscripts. During a period of the order's exile in Egypt, Ezra is reputed to have enhanced his training as a craftsman and learned the technique of marquetry. It has been suggested that he may have produced this work at the Gunda Gunde monastery, renowned for its manuscripts. In the Ethiopian church, the wooden cross is perceived as having been sanctified by Christ's blood, which conferred upon it infinite power to heal and to bless. Foliate and organic interlace designs, branching from the foot of the cross, are interpreted as symbolizing the Tree of Paradise, and thus a vibrant life-giving force. Commissioned by Ethiopian royalty, such works were presented to important monasteries to be carried in liturgical processions.
persona1_adapted: This cross was made in the northern Highlands, near the Red Sea. That area is where Ethiopia’s earliest kingdom and Christianity in Africa began. Only a small number of Ethiopian Christian artworks from before the 1600s still exist. This piece is exceptional. It uses a rare mix of materials to create rich contrasts in color and texture. The solid wood frame is covered with finely placed metal inlays. Each side of the cross is very different from the other, making it one of a kind. Experts believe it was made by Ezra (about 1460–1522), a monk from the Stephanite order. The Stephanites became known as the best carvers of crosses and painters of manuscripts. While the order was in exile in Egypt, Ezra is said to have improved his skills and learned marquetry, a technique of inlaying pieces of wood or metal. Some think he made this cross at the Gunda Gunde monastery, which was famous for its manuscripts. In the Ethiopian church, the wooden cross is seen as holy because of Christ’s blood. That blood gave the cross the power to heal and bless. Leaf and vine patterns that branch out from the base of the cross are seen as symbols of the Tree of Paradise. This represents a strong, life-giving force. Ethiopian royalty ordered works like this and gave them to important monasteries to be carried in religious processions.

CuratorialText: This illuminated manuscript of the Four Gospels was created at a monastic center in northern Ethiopia. Twenty full-page paintings depict scenes from the life of Christ and four portraits of the evangelists introduce the respective Gospel texts. The New Testament was translated from Greek into Geez, the classical language of Ethiopia, in the sixth century. Both this text and its pictorial format draw upon Byzantine prototypes, which were transformed into a local idiom of expression. Stylistically consistent, the paintings reflect the hands of two distinct artists. The color scheme consists of red, yellow, green, and blue. A stylized uniformity is reflected in the abbreviated definition of facial features and the bold linear articulation of the human form in black and red. Figures' heads are depicted frontally, their bodies often in profile. Bodies are treated as columnar masses encased in textiles composed of striated fields juxtaposed against one another. This work is evidence of sub-Saharan Africa's historically complex interrelationships with Arabia, Egypt, and the eastern Mediterranean. Early complex societies in the Highlands developed primarily through local processes beginning in the late second millennium B.C.E. Those societies attracted trade from across the Red Sea, Mediterranean, and Indian Ocean, which spurred migrations, religious exchanges, and greater concentrations of wealth. By the early first millennium C.E., the Kingdom of Aksum emerged as a powerful civilization that, at its height, extended from Ethiopia's Highlands in the South, to the Eastern Desert in the North, to southern Arabia in the East. Emperor Ezana (r. ca. 320s-360 C.E.) was the first monarch to convert to Christianity, and adopted it as the state religion. Subsequent kingdoms, including the Zagwe Dynasty (ca. 1137-1270 C.E.) and the Ethiopian Empire (1270-1974 C.E.) continued to embrace Christianity. Monasteries were founded as centers of learning responsible for disseminating knowledge and consolidating the power and influence of the ruling elite. In the Ethiopian Orthodox Church, the text of the Gospels was considered the most important holy writing; the miniatures at the beginning of this manuscript were intended to be viewed during liturgical processions. Such works were frequently presented to churches by distinguished patrons; they reflected both the prestige of royal benefactors and the erudition of the monastic scriptoria in which they were created. Recent research suggests that a member of Ethiopia's ruling elite may have commissioned this manuscript at Dabra Hayg Estifanos monastery for presentation to his or her favored church or monastery. Brief notations indicate that the church in question was dedicated to the Archangel Michael.
persona1_adapted:
This handwritten and painted book of the Four Gospels was made at a monastery in northern Ethiopia. It has twenty full-page paintings showing scenes from the life of Christ. Four portraits of the evangelists—the writers of the Gospels—introduce each Gospel text.

The New Testament was translated from Greek into Geez, the classical language of Ethiopia, in the sixth century. Both the writing and the pictures follow Byzantine models, but they were changed into a local Ethiopian style. The paintings are consistent in style but show the work of two different artists. The colors used are red, yellow, green, and blue.

Faces are simplified and shown in a uniform way. The human form is drawn with bold black and red lines. Heads face forward, but bodies often face sideways. Bodies look like column-like shapes wrapped in cloth made of striped sections placed next to each other.

This book shows that sub-Saharan Africa had complex connections with Arabia, Egypt, and the eastern Mediterranean long ago. Early complex societies in the Highlands grew mostly through local processes starting in the late second millennium B.C.E. These societies attracted trade from across the Red Sea, Mediterranean, and Indian Ocean. That trade led to migrations, religious exchanges, and more wealth.

By the early first millennium C.E., the Kingdom of Aksum became a powerful civilization. At its height, it stretched from Ethiopia's Highlands in the south to the Eastern Desert in the north and southern Arabia in the east. Emperor Ezana (ruled around 320s-360 C.E.) was the first king to convert to Christianity and made it the state religion. Later kingdoms, including the Zagwe Dynasty (around 1137-1270 C.E.) and the Ethiopian Empire (1270-1974 C.E.), continued to embrace Christianity.

Monasteries were founded as learning centers. They spread knowledge and helped the ruling elite keep power and influence. In the Ethiopian Orthodox Church, the Gospel text was considered the most important holy writing. The miniatures at the start of this book were meant to be seen during church processions. Such works were often given to churches by important patrons. They showed both the prestige of royal donors and the skill of the monastery workshops that made them.

Recent research suggests that a member of Ethiopia's ruling elite may have ordered this book at Dabra Hayg Estifanos monastery. It was likely given to a church or monastery he or she favored. Brief notes show that the church was dedicated to the Archangel Michael.

CuratorialText: Phillips pursued a long and successful career over fifty years, taking to the road as an itinerant artist painting portraits of clusters of friends, relatives, and neighbors in New York State and New England. His stock in trade was the plain likeness, devoid of shadows or modeling. Although he depended on repetitious formulas to work efficiently as he traveled the countryside, he was nevertheless able to individualize his sitters imaginatively. In "Mrs. Mayer and Daughter," Phillips’s masterful design abilities are evident in the cleanly contained forms of the mother and child, enriched by the use of brilliant, saturated color and careful detail.
persona1_adapted:
Phillips had a long and successful career that lasted over fifty years. He traveled from place to place as an itinerant artist, which means he moved around to find work. He painted portraits of groups of friends, relatives, and neighbors in New York State and New England.
