CuratorialText: At the end of the 19th century, a new art style emerges. It is a reaction by artists who rebelled against the historicism prevailing at the time, which harked back to the styles of previous centuries. The new art, aptly named Art Nouveau, is characterized by natural forms, such as flowers, leaves, animals, and insects. The emphasis lies on flowing lines, geometric shapes, and asymmetrical compositions. With Paris, Brussels, and Munich as centers, Art Nouveau spread as an international movement in Western Europe and the United States. During this period, which lasted from approximately 1890 until the beginning of the First World War in 1914, Art Nouveau became visible in all fields of the arts.
persona1_adapted: In the late 1800s, a new art style appeared. Artists created it because they were tired of always copying older styles from past centuries. This new style was called Art Nouveau. It used natural shapes like flowers, leaves, animals, and insects. The art focused on flowing lines, geometric shapes, and designs that were not perfectly balanced. Art Nouveau spread from cities like Paris, Brussels, and Munich to other parts of Western Europe and the United States. This period lasted from about 1890 until the start of World War I in 1914. During that time, Art Nouveau showed up in all kinds of art.

CuratorialText: Rembrandt is not only a successful painter, but also exceptionally talented as a draftsman and etcher. The Rijksmuseum collection contains approximately 1,300 prints by his hand, including sometimes multiple copies and different states of a single print. The subjects of his etchings range from biblical and mythological scenes to everyday scenes, landscapes, and (self-)portraits. Rembrandt's development in technique, style, and artistic approach can be clearly seen in these works. His loose drawing style and the high-contrast rendering of light and shadow are unique. The choice to depict the most emotional moments is also remarkable for his time. It makes his work human and relatable. Rembrandt has thus had a major influence on artists of his own time and for a long time thereafter.
persona1_adapted: Rembrandt was not just a great painter. He was also a very talented draftsman and etcher. An etcher is an artist who makes prints by drawing on a metal plate with acid. The Rijksmuseum has about 1,300 prints made by Rembrandt. Some prints exist in several copies or different versions. His etchings show many subjects. These include scenes from the Bible, myths, everyday life, landscapes, and portraits of himself and others. You can clearly see how Rembrandt’s skill, style, and artistic choices changed over time. His loose drawing style and strong contrast between light and dark are unique. He often chose to show the most emotional moments in a scene. This was unusual for his time. It makes his work feel human and easy to relate to. Because of this, Rembrandt had a big influence on other artists during his life and for many years after.

CuratorialText: Under the patronage of Augustus the Strong, Elector of Saxony, alchemist Johann Friedrich Böttger succeeded in becoming the first in Europe to produce porcelain in 1708. Not long afterwards, the first European porcelain factory was established in Meissen. The factory played an important role in the dream of August the Strong: a Japanese Palace, in which he wanted to exhibit his collection of Asian and Meissen porcelain. Since the end of the 16th century, porcelain, also known as 'white gold', had been imported from Asia and collected by the wealthy members of European courts and the elite. The fact that porcelain was now also being produced in Europe is one of the most important moments in the history of the European applied arts. The Rijksmuseum holds one of the most important collections of Meissen porcelain, including pieces from the factory's early production from the collection of Margarethe and Franz Oppenheimer.
persona1_adapted:
Augustus the Strong was the Elector of Saxony. Under his support, an alchemist named Johann Friedrich Böttger became the first person in Europe to make porcelain. This happened in 1708. Soon after, the first European porcelain factory opened in Meissen. This factory helped make Augustus the Strong’s dream come true. He wanted to build a Japanese Palace. There, he planned to show off his collection of Asian and Meissen porcelain.

Since the late 1500s, porcelain—often called “white gold”—had been brought from Asia. Wealthy people in European courts and high society collected it. The fact that Europe could now make its own porcelain was a huge moment in the history of European decorative arts. The Rijksmuseum has one of the most important collections of Meissen porcelain. This includes early pieces from the factory that once belonged to Margarethe and Franz Oppenheimer.

CuratorialText: The Rijksmuseum has been collecting Asian ceramics since its founding in 1885. The largest part of this is Chinese (approx. 2,300 pieces) and Japanese (approx. 600), plus another 50 pieces from other Asian countries. After all, the Dutch have been importing porcelain from Asia since the founding of the Dutch East India Company (VOC, 1602). From the 17th century onwards, thin-fired porcelain with Chinese blue decorations became immensely popular. Consequently, Chinese and Japanese porcelain play an important role in Dutch interiors. Since 1952, the collection of the Royal Society of Friends of Asian Art (KVVAK) has also been housed in the Rijksmuseum, comprising pieces that are part of art production in Asia: tea ceramics from Japan, Song bowls and Tang funerary sculptures from China, and ceramics from Southeast Asia and Korea.
persona1_adapted: The Rijksmuseum has collected Asian ceramics since it opened in 1885. Most of the collection is Chinese (about 2,300 pieces) and Japanese (about 600 pieces). Another 50 pieces come from other Asian countries. The Dutch have been bringing porcelain from Asia since the Dutch East India Company (VOC) started in 1602. Starting in the 1600s, thin porcelain with blue Chinese designs became very popular. Because of this, Chinese and Japanese porcelain became important in Dutch homes. Since 1952, the museum has also held the collection of the Royal Society of Friends of Asian Art (KVVAK). This collection includes tea ceramics from Japan, Song bowls and Tang funerary sculptures from China, and ceramics from Southeast Asia and Korea.

CuratorialText: From 1610 onwards, around one hundred thousand pieces of Chinese porcelain were imported into the Netherlands annually. To withstand the Chinese competition, Dutch potters began to refine their products. The earthenware becomes thinner, whiter, and shinier. Blue paintings are applied to a base of white tin glaze, a color scheme strongly associated with Asian porcelain. This earthenware is called 'plateel' or 'faience', but in the 17th and 18th centuries it acquires the name 'Hollants porceleyn'. From 1650 onwards, Delft becomes the center of earthenware production, hence the eventual name 'Delft earthenware'. During the same period, trade in Chinese porcelain comes to a virtual standstill due to the civil war in China (1644-1684). This gives Delft earthenware the opportunity to flourish and gain popularity among the European elite.
persona1_adapted: Starting in 1610, about one hundred thousand pieces of Chinese porcelain were brought to the Netherlands every year. To compete with this Chinese pottery, Dutch potters began to improve their own work. They made their earthenware thinner, whiter, and shinier. They painted blue designs on a base of white tin glaze. This blue-and-white look was strongly linked to Asian porcelain. This type of earthenware was called "plateel" or "faience." But in the 1600s and 1700s, people started calling it "Hollants porceleyn," or "Dutch porcelain." After 1650, the city of Delft became the main center for making this earthenware. That is why it is now known as "Delft earthenware." Around the same time, trade in Chinese porcelain nearly stopped because of a civil war in China (1644-1684). This gave Delft earthenware a chance to grow and become popular with Europe’s wealthy elite.

CuratorialText: The bronze collection of the Rijksmuseum contains a collection of small sculptures from the Renaissance, primarily from Italy. A number of these originated in the late 15th century in the university city of Padua. These are small bronze utilitarian objects inspired by classical antiquity: candlesticks, perfume burners, oil lamps, and inkwells, all decorated with mythological figures. Many small workshops cast such luxury utensils in series, but unique sculptures and objects were also made in bronze. A good example is the large candlestick that Verrocchio designed in 1469 for Palazzo Vecchio, the city hall of Florence. Bronze casting in the style of classical antiquity also flourished in other Italian cities, such as Rome and Venice. In the 16th and 17th centuries, these figurines found their way into the studies of humanist scholars, art cabinets of wealthy citizens, and Kunstkammern of princes and nobility.
persona1_adapted:
The Rijksmuseum has a collection of small bronze sculptures from the Renaissance, mostly from Italy. Some of these were made in the late 1400s in the university city of Padua. These are small, useful bronze objects inspired by ancient Greek and Roman art. They include candlesticks, perfume burners, oil lamps, and inkwells. All of them are decorated with figures from mythology.

Many small workshops made these luxury items in large numbers. But artists also created unique bronze sculptures and objects. A good example is the large candlestick designed by Verrocchio in 1469 for the Palazzo Vecchio, the city hall of Florence.

Bronze casting in the style of ancient Greece and Rome also became popular in other Italian cities, like Rome and Venice. In the 1500s and 1600s, these small figures ended up in the studies of humanist scholars, the art cabinets of wealthy citizens, and the Kunstkammern (or wonder rooms) of princes and nobles.

CuratorialText: Originally, artists in seascapes primarily captured historical events at sea, so that posterity could relive the heroic deeds. 'War correspondent' and artist Willem van de Velde I created eyewitness paintings of famous Dutch naval battles. His son Willem was a master at capturing calm or, conversely, stormy seas. In the 18th and 19th centuries, Ludolf Bakhuyzen and Wijnand Nuijen opted for a dramatized depiction of ships in distress. Bakhuyzen even went out to sea to study weather effects up close. Thanks to painters such as Andreas Schelfhout, Louis Meijer, and father and son Schotel, Dutch art abroad became almost synonymous with the seascape. For the artists of the Hague School, the effect of light and cloudy skies played a major role, and they paid more attention to the harsh life of fishermen.
persona1_adapted:
At first, artists mainly painted historical events at sea. They wanted future generations to remember heroic moments. Artist Willem van de Velde I worked like a war reporter. He painted eyewitness scenes of famous Dutch naval battles. His son, Willem van de Velde II, was a master at painting calm or stormy seas.

In the 1700s and 1800s, artists like Ludolf Bakhuyzen and Wijnand Nuijen chose to show dramatic scenes of ships in trouble. Bakhuyzen even went out to sea to study weather effects up close. Thanks to painters such as Andreas Schelfhout, Louis Meijer, and father and son Schotel, Dutch art became almost a symbol of seascapes around the world.

For the artists of the Hague School, light and cloudy skies were very important. They also paid more attention to the hard lives of fishermen.

CuratorialText: In the 17th century, the marksmen of the civic guard were regularly immortalized in group portraits. At that time, the powerful cities themselves were responsible for security. The militia ensured the maintenance of order and security. Every adult man up to the age of 60 is expected to join, although he must be able to purchase his own weaponry. The group portraits are a costly undertaking, as each marksman pays for his own portrait. The earliest marksman portraits date from the 16th century and are rather static. In the course of the 17th century, painters opted for a somewhat looser composition, with Rembrandt's Night Watch and the lively Banquet of the Marksmen by Bartholomeus van der Helst as highlights. Not only marksmen's guilds commission group portraits; other urban, provincial, and national organizations and individuals also commission them.
persona1_adapted: In the 1600s, members of the city guard were often painted in group portraits. Back then, powerful cities were in charge of their own safety. The militia kept order and security. Every adult man up to age 60 was expected to join, but he had to buy his own weapons. These group portraits were expensive because each guard paid for his own picture. The earliest guard portraits come from the 1500s and look quite stiff. Over the 1600s, painters started using looser, more lively arrangements. Two famous examples are Rembrandt's *Night Watch* and Bartholomeus van der Helst's *Banquet of the Marksmen*. Group portraits were not just for guards. Other city, regional, and national groups, as well as individuals, also ordered them.

CuratorialText: Approximately 230 paintings in the collection of the Rijksmuseum are still lifes. As early as the 16th century, Flemish painters specialized in this genre. These are arranged displays featuring, for example, shells, books, food and drink, tableware, skulls, or dead animals, but also flowers, insects, and sometimes a person in the background. Life literally stands still for a moment. Separate specializations soon emerged within the genre. For instance, Rachel Ruysch primarily painted flowers, while Pieter Claesz and Willem Heda enjoyed creating arrangements with food and eating and drinking utensils. Another variant, the vanitas still life, which often depicts a skull, refers to the transience of life and the material world.
persona1_adapted:
The Rijksmuseum has about 230 still life paintings. A still life is a type of art that shows arranged objects. As early as the 1500s, Flemish painters began to specialize in this style. These paintings often include shells, books, food, drinks, dishes, skulls, or dead animals. They might also show flowers, insects, or sometimes a person in the background. In a still life, life literally stands still for a moment.

Soon, artists created different types of still lifes. For example, Rachel Ruysch mainly painted flowers. Pieter Claesz and Willem Heda liked to paint food and eating tools. Another type is the vanitas still life. It often shows a skull. This type reminds us that life and material things do not last forever.

CuratorialText: The Rijksmuseum has an enormous collection of portraits from the late Middle Ages to the present day. In addition to their artistic value, these more than 1,700 portraits give the past a face. They describe what people looked like: what clothing and jewelry they wore, what their hairstyles were like, and sometimes what the interiors looked like at the time. There are also significant differences, as each century emphasizes different aspects. In the Renaissance, portraits seem to become increasingly realistic, whereas artists from the 20th and 21st centuries move away from that realism. Throughout the centuries, governments, organizations, and individuals have commissioned portraits for all kinds of occasions: to confirm profession or status, for a wedding, or as a memorial after someone's death. The Rijksmuseum collection also contains many self-portraits by famous painters such as Jan Steen, Rembrandt, and Van Gogh.
persona1_adapted:
The Rijksmuseum has a huge collection of portraits, from the late Middle Ages to today. These more than 1,700 portraits are not just art. They give a face to the past. They show what people looked like—their clothes, jewelry, and hairstyles. Sometimes they even show what rooms looked like back then.

Each century focuses on different things. In the Renaissance, portraits became more realistic. But artists from the 1900s and 2000s moved away from that realism. Over time, governments, groups, and individuals paid for portraits for many reasons. They wanted to show a person’s job or status, celebrate a wedding, or remember someone after death.

The Rijksmuseum also has many self-portraits by famous painters like Jan Steen, Rembrandt, and Van Gogh.

CuratorialText: Genre paintings are paintings depicting scenes from everyday life. They are lively scenes that often take place in kitchens, living rooms, inns, and markets. They capture people in their natural environment: children romping, a family huddled together in the kitchen, or a merchant hawking his wares. The first genre paintings emerged in 16th-century Flemish painting, but in the Netherlands, genre art did not flourish until a century later. The works of Johannes Vermeer, Jan Steen, and Judith Leyster became famous. They often concealed a mischievous content or, conversely, a moral message in their work. The term 'genre paintings' did not emerge until the 19th century, when the popularity of genre art reached a peak. The Rijksmuseum collection also includes works by 19th-century painters such as Jozef Israëls, known for his lively scenes of farmers and fishermen.
persona1_adapted: Genre paintings show scenes from everyday life. These lively scenes often take place in kitchens, living rooms, inns, or markets. They capture people in their natural surroundings: children playing, a family gathered in the kitchen, or a merchant selling his goods. The first genre paintings appeared in 16th-century Flemish art. But in the Netherlands, this type of painting did not become popular until a century later. The works of Johannes Vermeer, Jan Steen, and Judith Leyster became well known. These artists often hid a playful or moral message in their paintings. The term "genre paintings" was not used until the 1800s, when this art style was at its peak. The Rijksmuseum collection also includes works by 19th-century painters like Jozef Israëls. He is known for his lively scenes of farmers and fishermen.

CuratorialText: A history piece depicts a memorable historical story, which often originates from a written source. This can be a story from the Bible or mythology, but also events from history. Multiple figures are often visible in these narrative pieces. For instance, around 1530, Jan Jansz. Mostaert depicted a scene from the conquest of America, while in 1824, Jan Willem Pieneman captured The Battle of Waterloo , the largest painting in the Rijksmuseum. In the 17th century, painters also chose Roman scenes as a metaphor for the political situation in the Netherlands. For instance, the Batavian revolt against the Romans, depicted by Ferdinand Bol in the painting Claudius Civilis, refers to the struggle against the Spaniards.
persona1_adapted: A history painting tells a memorable story from the past. The story often comes from a written source, like the Bible, mythology, or real historical events. These paintings usually show many people in action. For example, around 1530, Jan Jansz. Mostaert painted a scene from the conquest of America. In 1824, Jan Willem Pieneman created *The Battle of Waterloo*, the largest painting in the Rijksmuseum. In the 1600s, artists also used Roman scenes to comment on politics in the Netherlands. For instance, Ferdinand Bol’s painting *Claudius Civilis* shows the Batavian revolt against the Romans. This story was a way to refer to the Dutch struggle against the Spanish.

CuratorialText: “We, the people of Indonesia, hereby declare the independence of Indonesia.” By uttering these words, the nationalist leader Sukarno declared his country, Indonesia, independent on August 17, 1945. Former colonizer the Netherlands refuses to recognize that independence and attempts to reoccupy Indonesia. In defense of its independence, Indonesia wages war against the Netherlands on its own soil. From the beginning, Indonesia seeks and receives support from the international community. Under international pressure, the Netherlands is forced to recognize Indonesia's independence in 1949. In 1950, Indonesia becomes a member state of the United Nations, and the international confirmation of independence becomes a reality.
persona1_adapted: “We, the people of Indonesia, hereby declare the independence of Indonesia.” With these words, the leader Sukarno declared his country free on August 17, 1945. The Netherlands, the country that once ruled Indonesia, refused to accept this independence. It tried to take back control of Indonesia. To protect its freedom, Indonesia fought a war against the Netherlands on its own land. From the start, Indonesia asked for and received help from other countries around the world. Because of this international pressure, the Netherlands had to accept Indonesia’s independence in 1949. In 1950, Indonesia joined the United Nations. This was the final step that made its independence official worldwide.

CuratorialText: Water is crucial for the development of the Netherlands as a trading nation. The economy grows thanks to the raw materials that enter via rivers and the world's oceans. Consider bulk goods such as grain and timber from the Baltic region, wool from England, wine from France, and raw salt from the Iberian Peninsula. Using these raw materials, the Dutch produce items such as beer, cloth (woolen fabric), fine salt (for the herring trade), weapons, and ships. Many of these products are subsequently exported, often again by water. Cities are important links in this international trade. In the 15th and 16th centuries, Antwerp is the largest port city of the Netherlands. After the (re)conquest by the Spaniards during the Dutch Revolt, Holland, and especially Amsterdam, takes over the role as a staple market. International trade leads to the emergence of a financial market, significant employment, and strong urban growth.
persona1_adapted: Water was key to the Netherlands becoming a trading nation. The economy grew because raw materials came in by rivers and oceans. For example, grain and timber arrived from the Baltic region. Wool came from England, wine from France, and raw salt from the Iberian Peninsula. The Dutch used these materials to make beer, cloth (woolen fabric), fine salt (for the herring trade), weapons, and ships. Many of these products were then shipped out again, often by water. Cities played a big role in this international trade. In the 1400s and 1500s, Antwerp was the largest port city in the Netherlands. After the Spanish took it back during the Dutch Revolt, Holland—especially Amsterdam—became the main market hub. International trade created a financial market, lots of jobs, and fast city growth.

CuratorialText: What is money actually worth? Why does a small piece of metal worth a few euro cents suddenly have a much higher value due to the '2 euro' imprint? Whoever holds the right to mint coins chooses the appearance, the amount of precious metal, and the value. Through trade contacts, gold and silver coins spread across the world for centuries. Coins with little gold or silver circulated primarily locally, because once abroad, the metal value was of paramount importance. The Amsterdam Exchange Bank was for a long time a key hub for the trade in precious metals. As early as the Eighty Years' War, a large portion of the silver that Spain obtained from Spanish America ended up in Amsterdam. Until well into the 18th century, veritable silver fleets sailed from Spain to the Republic. From Amsterdam, money traveled the world, such as to Asia via the VOC.
persona1_adapted: What is money actually worth? Why is a small piece of metal worth just a few euro cents, but suddenly worth much more because it has a '2 euro' stamp on it? The person or group who has the right to make coins gets to choose how they look, how much precious metal they contain, and what value they have. For hundreds of years, gold and silver coins spread around the world through trade. Coins that had little gold or silver mostly stayed close to home. That’s because, in other countries, the value of the metal itself mattered most. For a long time, the Amsterdam Exchange Bank was a key center for trading precious metals. Even during the Eighty Years' War, a large amount of the silver that Spain got from Spanish America ended up in Amsterdam. Until well into the 1700s, whole fleets of silver ships sailed from Spain to the Dutch Republic. From Amsterdam, money traveled all over the world, including to Asia with the Dutch East India Company (VOC).

CuratorialText: William of Orange is regarded as the 'Father of the Fatherland' because of his leading role in the struggle for independence. The House of Orange owes its princely title to the insignificant French city-state of Orange on the Rhône, yet manages to carve out a place for itself among the great European royal houses through its wealth and prestige. The wives of the stadtholders play an important role in these networks. Nevertheless, it seems to be the end for the House of Orange at the end of the 18th century: Stadtholder William V flees to England, and the Batavian Republic is proclaimed. But in 1813, his eldest son returns and becomes King William I. From that moment on, the Netherlands is a 'constitutional monarchy,' and a member of the House of Orange-Nassau has always sat on the throne.
persona1_adapted: William of Orange is called the 'Father of the Fatherland' because he led the fight for Dutch independence. The House of Orange gets its princely title from a small city-state called Orange, located on the Rhône River in France. Even though it was not a major power, the family used its wealth and influence to become one of the great royal houses of Europe. The wives of the stadtholders—the leaders of the Dutch Republic—played a key role in building these connections. Still, by the end of the 1700s, the House of Orange seemed finished. Stadtholder William V fled to England, and the Batavian Republic was declared. But in 1813, his oldest son returned and became King William I. From then on, the Netherlands became a 'constitutional monarchy,' meaning the king or queen shares power with a parliament. Since that time, a member of the House of Orange-Nassau has always been on the throne.

CuratorialText: Whose object was it before it came to the museum? The lives, relationships, and circumstances of the owners tell something about the significance of the object. Provenance research reveals stories of pre-war owners and reminds us of the people who were persecuted during the Holocaust. Even if objects were not looted, their provenance provides insight into their history. The Rijksmuseum collection primarily tells the stories of collectors from the period between 1933 and 1945. Research into the collection can therefore not possibly provide a complete picture of all persecuted owners. After all, many everyday objects did not end up in museums or are more difficult to identify. Nevertheless, the objects in the Rijksmuseum remind us of the diversity of pre-war Europe. Provenance research shows that the people persecuted by the Nazis actively contributed to Dutch culture and history. Objects in museums are still the silent witnesses of this today.
persona1_adapted:
Who owned this object before it came to the museum? The lives, relationships, and backgrounds of the owners tell us something about why the object matters. Provenance research—the study of an object’s history of ownership—reveals stories of owners from before World War II. It reminds us of the people who were persecuted during the Holocaust. Even if objects were not stolen, their ownership history gives us insight into their past.

The Rijksmuseum collection mainly tells the stories of collectors from the years 1933 to 1945. Because of this, research into the collection cannot give a complete picture of all persecuted owners. Many everyday objects did not end up in museums, or are harder to identify. Still, the objects in the Rijksmuseum remind us of the diversity of pre-war Europe. Provenance research shows that the people targeted by the Nazis actively contributed to Dutch culture and history. Today, objects in museums remain silent witnesses to this past.

CuratorialText: Before the Europeans arrive, centuries-old trade routes already exist around the Indian Ocean. The Dutch East India Company (VOC) forcibly expands these networks and exploits areas such as the Moluccas and Sri Lanka for spices. Batavia (Jakarta) and the Cape of Good Hope become important centers for sugar, coffee, and rice. In the Atlantic region, Dutch merchants trade in products such as sugar and coffee, which leads to the establishment of the Dutch West India Company (WIC). Over time, slave labor and the slave trade take place at all trading posts. Even for a craftsman in the Netherlands, there is money to be made from colonial trade, for example as a supplier of products for the slave trade or for daily life in the colonies. The transport of the products to Europe and their further processing also generates revenue. In this way, colonial trade stimulates the national economy.
persona1_adapted: Before Europeans arrived, trade routes around the Indian Ocean had already existed for hundreds of years. The Dutch East India Company, or VOC, forcefully took over these networks. They used areas like the Moluccas and Sri Lanka to get spices. Batavia (now Jakarta) and the Cape of Good Hope became major centers for sugar, coffee, and rice. In the Atlantic region, Dutch merchants traded goods like sugar and coffee. This led to the creation of the Dutch West India Company, or WIC. Over time, slavery and the slave trade happened at all trading posts. Even a craftsman in the Netherlands could make money from colonial trade. For example, they could supply products for the slave trade or for daily life in the colonies. Transporting goods to Europe and processing them also brought in money. In this way, colonial trade helped grow the national economy.

CuratorialText: The Netherlands was a colonial power for more than 350 years and established trading posts and colonies worldwide in the 17th century, including in Indonesia, South Africa, Sri Lanka, South America, and the Caribbean. Dutch colonial settlement often took place through the conclusion of trade contracts, but also through military force. The Rijksmuseum possesses many (art) objects that were brought to the Netherlands during and after this period, ranging from war booty to items acquired through trade. It is important to be open about the history of this collection and to reflect on the role of the Netherlands in the colonial past. Through research and collaboration with countries of origin, the Rijksmuseum aims to provide a nuanced and diverse picture of this shared past.
persona1_adapted: The Netherlands was a colonial power for over 350 years. In the 1600s, it set up trading posts and colonies around the world. These included places like Indonesia, South Africa, Sri Lanka, South America, and the Caribbean. The Dutch often settled through trade agreements, but they also used military force. The Rijksmuseum holds many objects from this time. Some were taken as war booty, while others were gained through trade. It is important to be open about how these items came to the Netherlands. We also need to think about the Netherlands' role in its colonial past. Through research and work with the countries where these objects came from, the Rijksmuseum aims to share a full and fair picture of this shared history.

CuratorialText: For 750 years, Amsterdam has been a city that grows, flourishes, and buzzes. Residents from all corners of the world have made the city what it is today. The first inhabitants of Amsterdam were farmers and fishermen, living along the River Amstel. In the late Middle Ages, Amsterdam grew into a small port city amidst the peatlands. Partly due to the establishment of the trading companies VOC and WIC, the city transformed rapidly into a global metropolis with more than 200,000 inhabitants during the 17th century, where almost every Amsterdammer was directly or indirectly involved in colonial trade. From rope makers to cartographers, from notaries to confectioners. To manage the population growth, a plan was drawn up to expand the city. It was the beginning of the famous canal belt.
persona1_adapted: For 750 years, Amsterdam has been a city that grows, thrives, and buzzes with activity. People from all over the world have shaped the city into what it is today. The first people to live in Amsterdam were farmers and fishermen. They settled along the River Amstel. During the late Middle Ages, Amsterdam grew into a small port city in the peatlands. Because of the trading companies VOC and WIC, the city quickly became a global center. By the 1600s, it had over 200,000 residents. Almost everyone in Amsterdam was involved in colonial trade in some way. This included rope makers, mapmakers, notaries, and candy makers. To handle the growing population, city leaders made a plan to expand. This was the start of the famous canal belt.

CuratorialText: The tulip originates from Turkey and was introduced to Europe in the late 16th century. The flower proved to thrive particularly well in the sandy soil of the Dutch coastal region. In the 17th century, traders of tulips and other exotic flowers began publishing 'Florilegia', books with detailed illustrations serving as catalogues of their wares. Enthusiasts of unusual flowers and plants could also turn to painters and draftsmen. Flower still lifes became a popular genre in art. Real flowers wilt, but paintings could serve as durable substitutes. Moreover, these flower still lifes sometimes cost even less than the flowers depicted on the canvas.
persona1_adapted:
The tulip originally came from Turkey. It was brought to Europe in the late 1500s. The flower grew very well in the sandy soil of the Dutch coast.

In the 1600s, tulip traders and sellers of other rare flowers started making "Florilegia." These were books with detailed drawings that worked like catalogs of what they had for sale.

People who loved unusual flowers could also hire painters or sketch artists. Paintings of flowers in vases became a popular type of art. Real flowers die, but paintings last much longer. Sometimes, these flower paintings even cost less than the real flowers shown in them.

CuratorialText: Throughout history, the role of pets changes significantly, from useful working animals to beloved companions. It was not until between 500 and 1500 that noblemen began keeping dogs for their company. In paintings from the 17th century, these four-legged creatures often appear in that role. For a long time, cats were primarily used as mouse exterminators and had a bad reputation due to their medieval association with witchcraft. It was not until the Victorian era that people accepted cats as pets. A beautiful pedigree cat, such as a British Shorthair or the Persian Longhair, became a status symbol for wealthy citizens. From the 19th century onwards, dogs and cats, and later birds and fish, became everyday pets for all social classes. Artist Henriëtte Ronner-Knip (1821–1909) specialized in painting cats, which brought her fame. She was regularly commissioned to immortalize the beloved pets of aristocratic and bourgeois families.
persona1_adapted: Throughout history, the role of pets has changed a lot. They went from working animals to beloved companions. Between 500 and 1500, noblemen started keeping dogs just for their company. In 17th-century paintings, dogs often appear in this role. For a long time, cats were mainly used to catch mice. They also had a bad reputation because people in the Middle Ages linked them to witchcraft. It wasn't until the Victorian era that people accepted cats as pets. A beautiful purebred cat, like a British Shorthair or a Persian Longhair, became a status symbol for wealthy people. Starting in the 19th century, dogs and cats, and later birds and fish, became everyday pets for people of all social classes. Artist Henriëtte Ronner-Knip (1821–1909) specialized in painting cats, which made her famous. Wealthy families often hired her to paint their beloved pets.

CuratorialText: Sculpture. From the Middle Ages to the present day, in every shape and size and encompassing a wide range of materials. This expanding online catalog presents a comprehensive overview of the latest scholarly insights into the Rijksmuseum's vast and highly diverse collection of European sculpture. This online catalog includes every work of European sculpture in the Rijksmuseum's art collection. The most recently published catalog of sculpture dates from 1973. Prompting a thorough update of this valuable, but by now outdated reference work was the ever-growing body of art historical research and the long list of acquisitions made in recent decades.   The Rijksmuseum (and its precursors: the Royal Cabinet of Rareities and later the Dutch Museum of History and Art) began acquiring sculpture on a systematic basis only in the last quarter of the nineteenth century. Additions to the collection prior to this time are few in number. One early example is the Dutch government's purchase ofArtus Quellinus's magnificent marble bust portrait of Andries de Graeff in 1817. Initially, the museum's sculpture acquisition policy was primarily oriented to works of national interest. It was not until the twentieth century that this focus gradually began to shift, largely thanks to the efforts of Adriaan Pit (museum director from 1898 to 1917) and Jaap Leeuwenberg (curator of sculpture from 1948 to 1969 and compiler of the 1973 catalogue), who sought to actively acquire international works. As a result, sculptures of non-Netherlandish origin today make up approximately half of the collection. This international scope received an additional impulse when, in 1952, the Dutch government charged the Rijksmuseum with the care of Italian and German works formerly in the possession of Fritz Mannheimer and recovered from Germany after the Second World War. This enhancement of the collection has since served to stimulate the museum's policy of acquiring international works.
persona1_adapted:
Sculpture. From the Middle Ages to today, it comes in every shape and size and uses many different materials. This growing online catalog gives a full look at the latest expert knowledge about the Rijksmuseum's large and varied collection of European sculpture. It includes every European sculpture in the museum's art collection. The last printed catalog of sculpture came out in 1973. That old reference work needed a major update because art historians have learned a lot more since then, and the museum has added many new pieces over the past few decades.

The Rijksmuseum (and its earlier versions: the Royal Cabinet of Rareities and later the Dutch Museum of History and Art) only started collecting sculpture in a planned way in the late 1800s. Before that, the museum added very few sculptures. One early example is the Dutch government's purchase in 1817 of Artus Quellinus's beautiful marble bust of Andries de Graeff. At first, the museum focused on buying sculptures that were important to the Netherlands. It wasn't until the 1900s that this focus slowly changed, mostly thanks to two people: Adriaan Pit (museum director from 1898 to 1917) and Jaap Leeuwenberg (sculpture curator from 1948 to 1969 and creator of the 1973 catalog). They worked to actively buy sculptures from other countries. As a result, about half of the collection today comes from outside the Netherlands. This international focus got another boost in 1952, when the Dutch government asked the Rijksmuseum to take care of Italian and German works that had belonged to Fritz Mannheimer and were recovered from Germany after World War II. This addition to the collection has since encouraged the museum to keep buying international works.

CuratorialText:
The Rijksmuseum holds a world-class collection of Japanese works on paper: woodblock prints, illustrated books and drawings. Among the almost 4000 prints, more than 800 are exclusive, privately published surimono. This online catalog features 203 surimono donated to the museum between 1991 and 1999 by the collector JHW Goslings (1943-2011). The entries in this catalog are taken from Matthi Forrer's 2013 collection catalog Surimono in the Rijksmuseum Amsterdam.
Poetry and image combined
“Surimono (literally 'printed things') constitute one of the most delicate genres in Japanese printmaking. This genre fascinates because it combines poetry and image and because it presents a pictorial puzzle, which provides the viewer with a particular insight into the intellectual and literary world of the late 18th- and early 19th-century Edo (today's Tokyo). Major artists such asKatsushika Hokusai,Utagawa Kunisada,Toyota HokkeiandYashima Gakutei, to name but a few, provided imagery to accompany the poetic exploits of poetry club members.
persona1_adapted:
The Rijksmuseum has a world-class collection of Japanese art on paper. This includes woodblock prints, illustrated books, and drawings. Among the nearly 4,000 prints, more than 800 are special, privately published works called *surimono*. This online catalog features 203 *surimono* that were given to the museum between 1991 and 1999 by the collector JHW Goslings (1943-2011). The entries in this catalog come from Matthi Forrer's 2013 collection catalog *Surimono in the Rijksmuseum Amsterdam*.

**Poetry and Image Combined**

*Surimono* (which means "printed things") are one of the most delicate types of Japanese printmaking. This art form is fascinating because it mixes poetry with pictures. It also presents a visual puzzle. Solving that puzzle gives viewers a special look into the intellectual and literary world of late 18th- and early 19th-century Edo (today's Tokyo). Famous artists like Katsushika Hokusai, Utagawa Kunisada, Toyota Hokkei, and Yashima Gakutei created images to go along with the poems written by members of poetry clubs.

CuratorialText: Portrait of Maria Trip (1619-83), sister of Jacobus Trip and later the wife of Balthasar Coymans. Standing, half-length, facing left. Her left arm rests on a banister, holding a fan.
persona1_adapted: This is a portrait of Maria Trip (1619-1683). She was the sister of Jacobus Trip. Later, she became the wife of Balthasar Coymans. In the painting, she stands and faces left. We see her from the waist up. Her left arm rests on a banister, and she holds a fan.

CuratorialText: He sits with outstretched wings on a cloud placed on a round plinth, holding his right index finger to the mouth of his mischievously smiling face, while reaching with his left hand for an arrow in the quiver lying beside him. His left leg is drawn up, and the foot of his right leg, positioned diagonally in front of him, extends over the round plinth. Beside him lies a flowering rose branch. The quiver filled with arrows displays, among other things, fluting, a wreath of laurel leaves, and godrons. The pedestal, a short column, has a profiled base decorated with a wreath of laurel leaves, a shaft with curved fluting containing culots transitioning into a smooth upper section interrupted by a profiled edge, and furthermore, a profiled, overhanging cover plate decorated with egg-and-dart molding, which is rotatable by means of two brass knobs.
persona1_adapted: He sits on a cloud resting on a round base. His wings are spread open. He holds his right index finger to his mouth, and his face has a mischievous smile. With his left hand, he reaches for an arrow in a quiver lying beside him. His left leg is pulled up. His right leg is stretched diagonally in front of him, with the foot hanging over the edge of the round base. Next to him lies a flowering rose branch. The quiver, which is full of arrows, is decorated with carved grooves, a wreath of laurel leaves, and curved patterns called godrons. The pedestal is a short column. Its base has a wreath of laurel leaves. The middle part has curved grooves that end in leaf-shaped decorations. The upper part is smooth but has a raised ring. On top, there is a cover plate decorated with an egg-and-dart pattern. This plate can be turned using two brass knobs.

CuratorialText: Indoor room with a mother removing lice from her child's hair, known as 'Mother's Duty'. Interior with mother and child sitting in front of a box bed on the right. A high chair on the right; on the left, a dog sits on the tiled floor and looks at the open window in the room behind. Above the door is a small painting of a landscape.
persona1_adapted: This painting is called "Mother's Duty." It shows a mother removing lice from her child's hair. They sit inside a room, in front of a box bed on the right. A high chair stands nearby. On the left, a dog sits on the tiled floor. The dog looks at an open window in the back of the room. Above the door hangs a small landscape painting.

CuratorialText: This portrait of Alida Assink presents her in her finest attire, depicted from head to toe. That is quite exceptional, as the painter Kruseman typically reserved such a full-length portrait exclusively for royalty. Assink was 23 years old when this likeness was commissioned by her guardian, a wealthy landowner. The rural setting with hunting dog and garden vase harks back to the English portrait style, which Kruseman admired.
persona1_adapted: This portrait shows Alida Assink from head to toe, wearing her finest clothes. That is very unusual. The painter Kruseman usually only painted full-length portraits of royalty. Assink was 23 years old when her guardian, a wealthy landowner, asked for this portrait. The countryside scene with a hunting dog and a garden vase looks like the English portrait style that Kruseman admired.

CuratorialText: The Dordrecht artist Aert Schouman was no scientist, but he was deeply interested in true-to-life depictions of the bird realm. His keen powers of observation are demonstrated by the way in which this red-billed toucan grasps the branch, namely with two, rather than three toes like other species. The landscape airily painted in transparent shades of green and blue evokes the bird’s native regions in Guyana and North Brazil.
persona1_adapted: Aert Schouman was an artist from Dordrecht, not a scientist. But he cared deeply about painting birds that looked real. His sharp observation skills show in how this red-billed toucan grips the branch. It uses two toes, not three like other birds. The background is painted lightly in see-through greens and blues. This suggests the bird’s home in Guyana and northern Brazil.

CuratorialText: Still life with roses. Also included are a butterfly and two snails.
persona1_adapted: This painting is a still life. It shows a vase of roses. You can also see a butterfly and two snails.

CuratorialText: Here the Duke of Wellington is receiving the message that Prussian forces are coming to his aid. Wellington, commander of the Anglo-Dutch troops, is the central figure in this group portrait of the major players at Waterloo. Lying wounded on a stretcher in the left foreground is the Dutch Crown Prince, later King William II. Originally intended for Wellington, the painting remained in the Netherlands thanks to William I who bought the picture for his son.
persona1_adapted: In this painting, the Duke of Wellington learns that Prussian troops are coming to help him. Wellington, who led the Anglo-Dutch army, is the main figure in this group portrait of the key people at the Battle of Waterloo. On the left, lying wounded on a stretcher, is the Dutch Crown Prince. He later became King William II. The painting was first meant for Wellington, but it stayed in the Netherlands because King William I bought it for his son.

CuratorialText: Of the noblemen who rebelled against Phillip II’s Spanish rule of the Netherlands, William of Orange rose to become the great leader of the Dutch Revolt. He defended his own interests, strove for greater independence and advocated freedom of religion. In 1580 Phillip II put a price on his head, and he was assassinated four years later.
persona1_adapted: William of Orange was one of the noblemen who rebelled against King Philip II's Spanish rule in the Netherlands. He became the great leader of the Dutch Revolt. William fought for his own interests, pushed for more independence, and supported freedom of religion. In 1580, Philip II placed a bounty on his head. Four years later, William was assassinated.

CuratorialText: This still life invites close examination of all the details and elicits marvel at the richness of God’s creation: the colourful flowers, the exotic shells, but certainly also the beautiful insects. At the same time, decay is spreading. Fruit shows the first rotten spots, leaves are eaten away by caterpillars and snails and ants drag a dead fly. The painting is thus also about impermanence.
persona1_adapted: This still life asks you to look closely at every detail. It makes you wonder at the richness of nature: the colorful flowers, the unusual shells, and the beautiful insects. But decay is also spreading. Some fruit has rotten spots. Caterpillars and snails have eaten parts of leaves. Ants are dragging a dead fly. So this painting is also about how nothing lasts forever.

CuratorialText: Presented here are two squirrel monkeys from Central America, two white sulphur-crested cockatoos from Australia, a grey parrot from Africa and a purple-naped lory – on a chain at the lower left – from Indonesia. Hondecoeter combined these creatures and several other splendid birds in this painting, which was destined for Het Loo, the palace of William III. It hung above the door of the king’s private apartment.
persona1_adapted: This painting shows animals from around the world. You can see two squirrel monkeys from Central America. There are also two white sulphur-crested cockatoos from Australia. A grey parrot from Africa sits nearby. At the lower left, a purple-naped lory from Indonesia is on a chain. The artist, Hondecoeter, brought these creatures and other beautiful birds together in one scene. The painting was made for Het Loo, the palace of King William III. It hung above the door of the king’s private room.

CuratorialText: This is a panoramic, sun-drenched river landscape – somewhat hazy in the distance – with sketchily drawn clouds. Two Dutch officers halt at the river, and water their horses next to a herder and his resting cattle. This idyllic scene does not unfold in Italy, but along the Waal between Nijmegen and Cleves, where Cuyp made landscape drawings that he took back to his workshop in Dordrecht.
persona1_adapted: This is a wide, sunlit river scene. The distance looks a little hazy. The clouds are drawn with quick, loose strokes. Two Dutch officers stop by the river. They water their horses next to a herder and his resting cattle. This peaceful scene does not take place in Italy. It happens along the Waal River, between Nijmegen and Cleves. Cuyp made drawings of this landscape and brought them back to his studio in Dordrecht.

CuratorialText: Ice entertainment near a village. Winter village scene with many figures skating, playing, and bowling on the ice. On the left, two people have fallen through the ice. In the distance, a drawbridge and a windmill.
persona1_adapted: People enjoy winter fun on the ice near a village. Many figures skate, play, and bowl on the frozen surface. On the left, two people have fallen through the ice. In the distance, you can see a drawbridge and a windmill.

CuratorialText: This dolls’ house is exceptionally realistic. All the contents have been made of authentic materials, and the proportions are exactly correct. The fine cabinet, of tortoiseshell decorated with pewter inlays, was made by a cabinetmaker from France, who worked in Amsterdam for several years. Petronella Oortman was married to the Amsterdam merchant Johannes Brandt.
persona1_adapted: This dolls’ house looks incredibly real. Everything inside is made from genuine materials, and the sizes are perfectly accurate. The fancy cabinet, made of tortoiseshell with pewter decorations, was built by a French cabinetmaker. He worked in Amsterdam for several years. Petronella Oortman was married to Johannes Brandt, a merchant from Amsterdam.

CuratorialText: This scene is painted on a copper panel. Its smooth surface was well suited to meticulous and detailed finish. Van Kessel made dozens of such little ‘coppers’ of insects and other animals, which he would arrange in a larger composition. They may have also served as decorative panels for a small cabinet of curiosities.
persona1_adapted: This scene was painted on a copper panel. The smooth surface allowed for very fine, detailed work. Van Kessel created dozens of these small copper paintings of insects and other animals. He would later arrange them into a larger composition. They might have also been used as decorative panels for a small cabinet of curiosities.

CuratorialText: 18 June 1648: a banquet is taking place at the Amsterdam crossbowmen’s guild. The occasion was the signing of the Treaty of Münster, which marked an end to the war with Spain. The captains of the civic guard company shake hands as a sign of peace, and the drinking horn is passed around. The poem on the drum proclaims the joy of Amsterdam’s armed militia that their weapons can henceforth be laid to rest.
persona1_adapted: On June 18, 1648, a banquet was held at Amsterdam’s crossbowmen’s guild. The event celebrated the signing of the Treaty of Münster. This treaty ended the war with Spain. The captains of the city guard shake hands as a sign of peace. The drinking horn is passed around. A poem on the drum shares the joy of Amsterdam’s armed militia. It says their weapons can now be put to rest.

CuratorialText: The Fishing for Souls: an allegory of the jealousy of the various religions during the Twelve Years' Truce (1609-1621) between the Dutch Republic and Spain. In a river landscape, large groups of people are situated on both banks of the river; several rowboats are on the water. On the horizon, a rainbow. On the left side are the Protestants and their supporters: the Princes of Orange, Maurice and Frederick Henry, Frederick V of the Palatinate, James I, King of England, and the youthful Louis XIII, King of France, with his mother, Maria de' Medici. In the boat in the foreground sit the Protestant fishermen; their nets are marked with Fides, Spes, and Charitas. On the right side stand the Catholics and their supporters: Archdukes Albert and Isabella, General Ambrosio Spinola, and the Pope carried by cardinals. In the small Catholic boat, priests are fishing under the leadership of a bishop.
persona1_adapted:
"Fishing for Souls" is a painting that uses symbols to tell a story. It shows the jealousy between different religions during the Twelve Years' Truce (1609–1621), a pause in the war between the Dutch Republic and Spain.

In the scene, large groups of people stand on both sides of a river. Several rowboats float on the water. A rainbow appears on the horizon.

On the left side are the Protestants and their supporters. These include the Princes of Orange, Maurice and Frederick Henry, Frederick V of the Palatinate, James I (King of England), and the young Louis XIII (King of France) with his mother, Maria de' Medici. In the boat at the front, Protestant fishermen sit. Their nets are marked with the words Fides, Spes, and Charitas (Latin for faith, hope, and charity).

On the right side stand the Catholics and their supporters. These include Archdukes Albert and Isabella, General Ambrosio Spinola, and the Pope, who is carried by cardinals. In a small Catholic boat, priests fish under the leadership of a bishop.

CuratorialText: Wedding portrait of Isaac Abrahamsz Massa and Beatrix van der Laen, married in Haarlem on April 25, 1622. The couple is seated full-length on a raised platform under a tree. On the left, a thistle; on the right, ivy. On the right in the background, elegant couples are walking in a garden with a fountain at a country house.
persona1_adapted: This wedding portrait shows Isaac Abrahamsz Massa and Beatrix van der Laen. They got married in Haarlem on April 25, 1622. The couple sits full-length on a raised platform under a tree. On the left, there is a thistle plant. On the right, ivy grows. In the background on the right, well-dressed couples walk in a garden. The garden has a fountain and a country house.

CuratorialText: Figure of painted porcelain. The figure depicts a parrot (macaw) sitting on a tree stump with leaves. The parrot has a blue-scaled neck and head and a white body. The tail and the tips of the wings are painted blue, yellow, and white. A branch with cherries lies beneath the right leg. The figure is unmarked.
persona1_adapted: This figure is made of painted porcelain. It shows a parrot, specifically a macaw, sitting on a tree stump with leaves. The parrot has a blue-scaled neck and head and a white body. The tail and wing tips are painted blue, yellow, and white. A branch with cherries sits under its right leg. The figure has no maker's mark.

CuratorialText: Formal long-sleeved kimono for an unmarried young woman (furisode), decorated all over with more than two hundred flying cranes. Black silk crepe (chirimen) with yuzen decoration in white, red, and grey tones, with gold foil detailing. Red silk lining. Single bamboo crest (mon).
persona1_adapted: This is a formal, long-sleeved kimono called a furisode. It was worn by an unmarried young woman. The kimono is covered with over two hundred flying cranes. It is made of black silk crepe, a fabric called chirimen. The design uses a dyeing technique called yuzen, in white, red, and grey tones. Gold foil adds small, shiny details. The inside lining is red silk. The kimono also has a single bamboo crest, or mon, which is a family symbol.

CuratorialText: Still life with flowers. A bouquet of flowers in a metal vase standing in a niche. The bouquet contains roses, irises, poppies, a wheat stalk, tulips, and other flowers. A snail, a caterpillar, and a beetle crawl among the flowers and over the niche. Pendant of SK-A-337.
persona1_adapted: This painting is called *Still Life with Flowers*. It shows a bouquet of flowers in a metal vase. The vase is sitting in a small, arched alcove. The bouquet includes roses, irises, poppies, tulips, and a stalk of wheat. A snail, a caterpillar, and a beetle crawl among the flowers and along the edge of the alcove. This painting is the matching piece to another painting in the museum, numbered SK-A-337.

CuratorialText: Mary Magdalene sitting under a tree with the ointment jar on her lap. The saint is seated in a landscape with mountains and rugged rock formations.
persona1_adapted: Mary Magdalene sits under a tree. She holds an ointment jar on her lap. The saint is seated in a landscape with mountains and rocky cliffs.

CuratorialText: Portrait of the twenty-year-old Marten Soolmans standing full-length on a checkered tile floor in an undefined space. He is dressed in an expensive black suit with a lace collar, bows, cuffs, and garters, wears a black hat and shoes with rosettes. In his left hand, he holds a glove.
persona1_adapted: This is a full-length portrait of Marten Soolmans, who is twenty years old. He stands on a checkered tile floor in a space that is not clearly defined. He wears an expensive black suit with a lace collar, bows, cuffs, and garters. He also has a black hat and shoes with rosettes. In his left hand, he holds a glove.

CuratorialText: Portrait of 23-year-old Oopjen Coppit standing full-length on a tiled floor in an otherwise undefined space. She is dressed in an expensive black dress, lace collar and cuffs, and a black veil. She wears precious jewelry. In her right hand, she holds a fan of black ostrich feathers.
persona1_adapted: This is a full-length portrait of 23-year-old Oopjen Coppit. She stands on a tiled floor in a space with no other details. She wears an expensive black dress, a lace collar, and lace cuffs. A black veil covers her head. She also wears precious jewelry. In her right hand, she holds a fan made of black ostrich feathers.

CuratorialText: Still life with a golden ceremonial goblet, a silver holder on which stands a Chinese dish, an orange, a peeled lemon, grapes, peaches, a lobster, a plate of oysters and a roll.
persona1_adapted: A still life painting shows a golden ceremonial cup, a silver stand holding a Chinese dish, an orange, a peeled lemon, grapes, peaches, a lobster, a plate of oysters, and a roll.

CuratorialText: Portrait of Marie Fargues, the artist's wife, in Turkish costume. Full-length, seated on a divan. Her head resting on her right hand. On the divan lie a book and a basket containing a mirror, comb, and bonnet. On the floor are an Oriental rug and a small vase with carnations
persona1_adapted: This painting shows Marie Fargues, the artist’s wife, dressed in a Turkish costume. She sits on a long, low sofa called a divan. Her head rests on her right hand. On the divan are a book and a basket. Inside the basket are a mirror, a comb, and a bonnet. On the floor lies an Oriental rug. Next to it sits a small vase with carnations.

CuratorialText: A cheerful violinist holding a birch flask. A laughing man leans out of a window covered by an Oriental rug. In his right hand, he holds up a birch flask filled with wine; in his left hand, a violin with a bow. On his head, a feathered hat.
persona1_adapted: A cheerful violinist holds a birch flask. A laughing man leans out of a window covered by an Oriental rug. In his right hand, he lifts a birch flask filled with wine. In his left hand, he holds a violin with a bow. On his head, he wears a feathered hat.

CuratorialText: View of Olinda in Dutch Brazil. On the left, on a hill, a group of people by the ruins of the church. In the foreground, tropical plants, fruits, and animals (armadillo, monkey, sloth, anteater, lizard).
persona1_adapted: This painting shows a view of Olinda, a city in Brazil when it was ruled by the Dutch. On the left side, a group of people stands on a hill near the ruins of a church. In the front of the painting, you can see tropical plants, fruits, and animals. These animals include an armadillo, a monkey, a sloth, an anteater, and a lizard.

CuratorialText: Winter landscape. View of a wide, snow-covered lane leading past a forest. In the foreground, three boys with sleds. Further back, a wagon drawn by two horses stands on the road in front of a farm.
persona1_adapted: A winter landscape shows a wide, snowy lane. The lane leads past a forest. In the front, three boys stand with sleds. Farther back, a wagon pulled by two horses waits on the road in front of a farm.

CuratorialText: Italian landscape with draftsman. A road through the landscape with a bridge over a mountain stream. On the road, a man with a mule. In the foreground, some seated figures.
persona1_adapted: An Italian landscape shows a road winding through the countryside. A bridge crosses a mountain stream. On the road, a man walks with a mule. In the front of the scene, a few people sit.

CuratorialText: Still life with turkey pie. Table with a white damask tablecloth on which stand pewter plates with a pie, oysters, lemon, salt and pepper, and olives (?). Also a pewter pitcher, roemer, knife, nuts, bread rolls, a nautilus cup, and a Wan Li bowl with fruit. In the background stands a large pie on which a turkey has been placed.
persona1_adapted: A table is covered with a white damask tablecloth. On it are pewter plates holding a pie, oysters, lemon, salt, pepper, and what might be olives. There is also a pewter pitcher, a wine glass called a roemer, a knife, nuts, bread rolls, a nautilus cup, and a Chinese bowl from the Wan Li period filled with fruit. In the back, a large pie sits with a turkey placed on top.

CuratorialText: The stone bridge. Landscape with a stone bridge over a river or canal. On the water are two small boats with figures. Various people are walking on the road, a wagon on the left. In the middle, a hay barn between trees, a church tower in the distance on the right.
persona1_adapted: This painting shows a stone bridge crossing a river or canal. Two small boats with people float on the water. Several people walk along the road. A wagon sits on the left side. In the middle, a hay barn stands between trees. A church tower rises in the distance on the right.

CuratorialText: A stone bust of Flora in an ornamental cartouche surrounded by a festoon of various types of flowers, including tulips, roses, delphinium, foxglove, hollyhocks, carnations, and ivy.
persona1_adapted: A stone carving shows the head of Flora, the Roman goddess of flowers. It sits inside an ornamental frame shaped like a shield. Around the frame is a garland made of many different flowers, such as tulips, roses, delphinium, foxglove, hollyhocks, carnations, and ivy.

CuratorialText: The overturned bouquet. A cat, playing with a mousetrap, knocks over a vase containing a bouquet of flowers. In the bouquet: tulips, irises, poppies, and carnations. Flying among the flowers are butterflies, a spider, a dragonfly, and snails.
persona1_adapted: A cat plays with a mousetrap and knocks over a vase. The vase holds a bouquet of flowers. The bouquet includes tulips, irises, poppies, and carnations. Butterflies, a spider, a dragonfly, and snails fly or move among the flowers.

CuratorialText: The Fall of Man. Adam and Eve standing before the Tree of Knowledge. A snake offers Eve an apple. Surrounding the figures are various animals: monkey, cat, dog, slugs, hedgehog, frog, fox, and owl. In the background on the left, Adam and Eve are warned by God not to eat from the tree.
persona1_adapted: The Fall of Man shows Adam and Eve standing in front of the Tree of Knowledge. A snake offers Eve an apple. Around them are many animals, including a monkey, cat, dog, slugs, hedgehog, frog, fox, and owl. In the background on the left, God warns Adam and Eve not to eat fruit from the tree.

CuratorialText: View of an orchard with plum blossoms, seen through the branches of a plum tree in the foreground; picnicking visitors in the background; part of the entrance gate on the left in the foreground; against a bright red sky.
persona1_adapted: You see an orchard full of plum blossoms. A plum tree’s branches frame the front of the scene. In the background, visitors are having a picnic. Part of the entrance gate is on the left, close to the viewer. The sky is bright red.

CuratorialText: The painting is executed in a pointillist technique. The sitter is positioned on the right, facing the viewer, while studying a book in the light of a kerosene lamp. She is dressed in a pink gown, known as reform clothing, and has her hair simply styled in an updo. The kerosene lamp is partially visible on the table on the left, placed next to a small vase of roses on a small table covered with a floral tablecloth. Flowers in a large vase in the background are visually incorporated into the pattern of the decorative wallpaper.
persona1_adapted: The artist painted this work using small dots of color, a style called pointillism. The woman sits on the right side of the painting, facing the viewer. She is reading a book by the light of a kerosene lamp. She wears a pink gown, which was a type of reform clothing at the time. Her hair is styled simply in an updo. The kerosene lamp sits on the table to the left, next to a small vase of roses. The table is covered with a floral tablecloth. In the background, flowers in a large vase blend into the pattern of the decorative wallpaper.

CuratorialText: Kitten game. A small cat plays with dominoes on a table. There is also an ashtray with a cigar on the table.
persona1_adapted: A small cat plays with dominoes on a table. An ashtray with a cigar sits nearby.

CuratorialText: Interior in which a family celebrates the Feast of Saint Nicholas. In the center, a girl holding a John the Baptist doll and a bucket of candy in her arms. On the left, a crying boy who has received a rod in his shoe. Next to the mother with outstretched arms stands a laughing boy with a club. On the floor is a basket of cakes and rolls; by the fireplace, several young people are singing a Sinterklaas song. In the background, an older man and woman.
persona1_adapted: A family celebrates Saint Nicholas Day inside their home. In the center, a girl holds a doll of John the Baptist and a bucket of candy. On the left, a boy is crying because he got a rod in his shoe. Next to the mother, who has her arms stretched out, a laughing boy stands with a club. On the floor, there is a basket of cakes and rolls. By the fireplace, several young people sing a Sinterklaas song. In the background, an older man and woman are watching.

CuratorialText: Jeremiah mourning over the destruction of Jerusalem. The old prophet sits on the rocky slope of a mountain, his head resting on his left hand. On a stone beside him lie a golden bowl, a pitcher, and books, sanctuaries from the destroyed temple. Below to the left, the burning buildings of the city and soldiers can be seen.
persona1_adapted: Jeremiah mourns as Jerusalem is destroyed. The old prophet sits on a rocky mountain slope, resting his head on his left hand. Beside him on a stone are a golden bowl, a pitcher, and books—items saved from the ruined temple. Below to the left, the city’s buildings burn and soldiers can be seen.

CuratorialText: Still life with fruit, oysters, and a porcelain bowl. On a stone table, a silver plate with oysters, fruit, a porcelain bowl, an inverted roemer, a glowing wick, and a bunch of grapes on a wooden box. Also snails and insects.
persona1_adapted: A still life painting shows fruit, oysters, and a porcelain bowl on a stone table. A silver plate holds oysters and fruit. Next to it are a porcelain bowl, an upside-down wine glass, a glowing wick, and a bunch of grapes on a wooden box. You can also see snails and insects.

CuratorialText: Painting titled 'View at the Geestbrug'. River landscape with the Trekvliet near Rijswijk, with De Binckhorst Castle in the distance on the left and the Laakmolen on the right. A woman with children stands on the road to the farm, and boats are sailing on the water.
persona1_adapted: A painting called 'View at the Geestbrug' shows a river scene. It features the Trekvliet near Rijswijk. In the distance on the left is De Binckhorst Castle. On the right is the Laakmolen windmill. A woman and her children stand on the road leading to a farm. Boats sail across the water.

CuratorialText: The well-stocked kitchen, with Jesus with Martha and Mary in the background. In the kitchen, two maidservants are busy preparing food. One is plucking a chicken, the other is skewering a chicken. To the left, others are sitting by the hearth. In the foreground, all kinds of game and poultry, vegetables, and fruits are laid out. Among these are also various glasses, jugs, pitchers, and plates.
persona1_adapted: This painting shows a well-stocked kitchen. In the background, you can see Jesus with Martha and Mary. Two maidservants are busy preparing food. One is plucking a chicken. The other is skewering a chicken. On the left, more people sit by the hearth. In the front, many types of game, poultry, vegetables, and fruits are laid out. You can also see different glasses, jugs, pitchers, and plates among them.

CuratorialText: Still life with flowers and fruit. On a plinth stands a stone vase with flowers, fruit, and vines. On the plinth is a glass, and lying are a pineapple, peaches, grapes, and a rose. On the wall hangs a painting of a woman with a flower basket handing a child a flower wreath.
persona1_adapted: A still life shows flowers and fruit. A stone vase sits on a base, filled with flowers, fruit, and vines. On the base, there is a glass. A pineapple, peaches, grapes, and a rose lie nearby. A painting hangs on the wall. It shows a woman with a flower basket giving a flower wreath to a child.

CuratorialText: Six warships lie on a vast, calm sea. The sails are being hoisted, the anchors weighed. The stern of the ship on the right in the foreground is decorated with the coat of arms of Amsterdam, supported by two standing lions. This likely depicts part of a war fleet preparing for departure. A sloop with dignified figures, probably from the Admiralty, sails past the ships to the sound of trumpets and salutes, while a little further away the last provisions and crew are still being taken on board. The enormous accuracy in the rendering of details—rigging, sails, and cannons—is striking.
persona1_adapted: Six warships sit on a wide, calm sea. Crews are raising the sails and lifting the anchors. The back of the ship on the right, in the front, has the coat of arms of Amsterdam. Two standing lions hold up the coat of arms. This scene likely shows part of a war fleet getting ready to leave. A small boat with important people, probably from the Admiralty, sails past the ships. Trumpets play and cannons fire in salute. A little farther away, workers are still loading the last supplies and crew members. The artist paid very close attention to every detail—the ropes, sails, and cannons all look incredibly real.

CuratorialText: Still life with a hammered bronze vase containing a bouquet of flowers: tulips, roses, anemones, and marigolds. On the tabletop lie a dead frog, a cluster of berries, and a caterpillar.
persona1_adapted: A still life shows a hammered bronze vase holding a bouquet of flowers. The flowers include tulips, roses, anemones, and marigolds. On the table next to the vase are a dead frog, a bunch of berries, and a caterpillar.

CuratorialText: Portrait of Giuliano da Sangallo (c. 1445-1516). Architect, son of Francesco Giamberti da Sangallo. Bust facing right, with a landscape in the background. A compass and pen lie on the plinth at the bottom of the image. Part, with SK-C-1367, of a diptych.
persona1_adapted: This is a portrait of Giuliano da Sangallo, who lived from about 1445 to 1516. He was an architect and the son of Francesco Giamberti da Sangallo. The painting shows his upper body facing to the right, with a landscape behind him. A compass and a pen rest on the ledge at the bottom of the image. This artwork is part of a two-panel painting, or diptych, along with another piece labeled SK-C-1367.

CuratorialText: An old woman, probably Rembrandt's mother, Neeltgen Willemsdr van Zuydtbroeck (died 1640), presumably depicted as the prophetess Anna. A seated old woman, reading a large book lying open on her lap.
persona1_adapted: An old woman, likely Rembrandt's mother, Neeltgen Willemsdr van Zuydtbroeck (died 1640), is shown as the prophetess Anna. She sits in a chair, reading a large book that lies open on her lap.

CuratorialText: Allegorical musical group. On the left, a man in Oriental clothing and a turban plays a viola da gamba. Next to him stands a young man with a harp. A young woman sits beside a small table with an open songbook on her lap and sings. Behind her, an old woman with a cloth over her head looks on. In the foreground lie musical instruments (lute, violin) and stacks of books. On the wall is a painting of Lot's Flight from Sodom.
persona1_adapted: This group of people is a symbol, not just a real scene. On the left, a man in a turban and foreign-style clothes plays a viola da gamba, an old string instrument like a cello. Next to him, a young man holds a harp. A young woman sits by a small table with an open songbook on her lap and sings. Behind her, an old woman with a cloth over her head watches. In the front, musical instruments like a lute and violin lie on the floor next to stacks of books. A painting on the wall shows Lot's Flight from Sodom, a Bible story.

CuratorialText: Venus and Adonis. The half-naked Venus and Cupid try to prevent Adonis from going hunting. Two white doves in the lower right. Adonis's quiver hangs from the tree. Adonis has two hunting dogs with him.
persona1_adapted: Venus and Adonis are shown here. Venus, who is half-naked, and Cupid try to stop Adonis from going hunting. In the lower right corner, there are two white doves. Adonis's quiver, or arrow holder, hangs from a tree. Two hunting dogs stand beside Adonis.

CuratorialText: The Cannon Shot. A warship in calm with slack sails fires a cannon shot. Two longboats on either side, in the distance another warship with furled sails. Pendant to SK-A-1848.
persona1_adapted: A warship sits calmly on the water with its sails hanging loose. It fires a cannon shot. Two longboats float on either side of the ship. In the distance, another warship has its sails rolled up. This painting is a companion piece to SK-A-1848.

CuratorialText: The word Sufi often conjures up images of Turkish whirling dervishes, turning gracefully round themselves in a mystical ceremony. But outside the world of Islamic mysticism, not much is known of the beliefs and practices in which such ceremonies are rooted, or about the communities that embrace them.
persona1_adapted: The word "Sufi" often makes people think of Turkish whirling dervishes. These dancers spin gracefully in a mystical ceremony. But outside of Islamic mysticism, most people don't know much about the beliefs and practices behind these ceremonies. They also don't know much about the communities that follow them.

CuratorialText: This display will explore Sufi life and art through a selection of objects that represent Sufi communities in the Middle East, North and Sub-Saharan Africa, and northern India. This mystical movement, which first appeared in the Islamic world during the eighth century AD, has been defined as a pathway to a personal experience with God. Among the British Museum objects on display will be Islamic artefacts imbued with Sufi connotations. Some, such as alms bowls, are linked overtly to dervishes, while others have a more subtle connection, such as calligraphic works, miniature paintings and wall tiles. Sufis come from all walks of life, crossing the boundaries of class, ethnicity and regionality. The display will explore such themes as mystical love, poverty and asceticism, as well as Sufi influence upon the wider societies they belong to.
persona1_adapted: This display explores Sufi life and art through objects from Sufi communities in the Middle East, North and Sub-Saharan Africa, and northern India. Sufism is a mystical movement that began in the Islamic world during the 700s AD. It is a path for people to have a personal experience with God. The British Museum objects on display include Islamic items with Sufi meanings. Some objects, like alms bowls, are clearly linked to dervishes. Others have a more subtle connection, such as calligraphy, miniature paintings, and wall tiles. Sufis come from all backgrounds, crossing class, ethnicity, and region. The display looks at themes like mystical love, poverty, and self-denial, as well as how Sufism influences the wider societies around it.

CuratorialText: Hawaiʻi: a kingdom crossing oceans is a dazzling celebration of the artistry and history of Hawaiʻi. The exhibition brings together remarkable objects – from feathered cloaks worn by chiefs and finely carved deities, to powerful shark-toothed weapons and bold contemporary works by Kānaka ʻŌiwi (Native Hawaiian) artists.
