In 1824, the young King Liholiho and Queen Kamāmalu travelled across vast oceans on a journey that would mark a turning point in the history of their kingdom, Hawaiʻi. The exhibition commemorates over 200 years since this ill-fated royal visit and sheds light on Hawaiʻi's history and culture through an exploration of the complex and enduring relationship between the Hawaiian and British nations. Building on years of collaboration with Native Hawaiian artists, practitioners and scholars, the show centres on indigenous knowledge, shining new light on exceptional objects and extraordinary stories. Shaped together with Hawaiian knowledge-bearers, the exhibition showcases seldom-seen international loans alongside the remarkable collections at the British Museum – one of the largest in the world outside of Hawaiʻi.
persona1_adapted: Hawaiʻi: a kingdom crossing oceans is a stunning celebration of Hawaiian art and history. The exhibition brings together amazing objects. You will see feathered cloaks worn by chiefs, finely carved gods, powerful weapons made with shark teeth, and bold modern works by Kānaka ʻŌiwi (Native Hawaiian) artists. In 1824, the young King Liholiho and Queen Kamāmalu traveled across vast oceans. Their journey would change the history of their kingdom, Hawaiʻi. The exhibition marks over 200 years since this royal visit, which ended in tragedy. It explores Hawaiʻi's history and culture by looking at the deep and lasting bond between the Hawaiian and British nations. Built on years of work with Native Hawaiian artists, experts, and scholars, the show puts indigenous knowledge at the center. It shines new light on rare objects and amazing stories. Created together with Hawaiian knowledge-bearers, the exhibition features seldom-seen loans from other countries. It also highlights the British Museum’s remarkable collection, one of the largest outside of Hawaiʻi.

CuratorialText: Explore prints by Scottish poet, writer, artist and gardener Ian Hamilton Finlay (1925–2006) on the theme of revolution and its relationship to contemporary society. Finlay became known from the late 1950s for his poetry but in the 1960s his output expanded to include the production of prints and three-dimensional works of art in stone, metal, neon and glass, which he produced in collaboration with various craftspeople. He also became a dedicated gardener, developing a conceptual garden at his remote home on the edge of the Pentland Hills, southwest of Edinburgh, which incorporated many of his works of art. He named his garden Little Sparta after the ancient Greek city, a militaristic rival to Athens, reflecting his deep interest in classical history.
persona1_adapted: Explore prints by Scottish poet, writer, artist, and gardener Ian Hamilton Finlay (1925–2006). His work focuses on revolution and how it relates to today’s society. Finlay became known for his poetry in the late 1950s. By the 1960s, he started making prints and three-dimensional art. He worked with different craftspeople to create pieces in stone, metal, neon, and glass. Finlay was also a dedicated gardener. He built a conceptual garden at his remote home near the Pentland Hills, southwest of Edinburgh. The garden included many of his artworks. He named it Little Sparta after the ancient Greek city. Sparta was a militaristic rival to Athens. This name shows Finlay’s deep interest in classical history.

CuratorialText: In the 1980s, Finlay's interest in the French Revolution began to dominate his work. He was fascinated by revolutions and ruptures, and how high ideals could easily lead to violence and corruption. The 12 prints in this display, made between 1984 and 1992, form part of Finlay's engagement with the French Revolution as a subject. They are from a group of 26 prints donated in 2025 by Prof Stephen Bann, an art historian who has written extensively on Finlay and who corresponded with the artist for many years.
persona1_adapted: In the 1980s, Finlay’s work focused more and more on the French Revolution. He was fascinated by revolutions and sudden changes. He also thought about how good ideas could turn into violence and corruption. The 12 prints in this display were made between 1984 and 1992. They are part of Finlay’s work on the French Revolution. These prints come from a group of 26 that were donated in 2025 by Professor Stephen Bann. He is an art historian who has written a lot about Finlay and exchanged letters with him for many years.

CuratorialText: In a career that lasted from the 1760s to the 1830s, Pierre Alexandre Wille (1748–1837) experienced some of the most turbulent events in French history. A pupil of Jean Baptiste Greuze (1725–1805), Wille established himself by imitating the genre scenes that won Greuze renown at the Salons of the 1760s, showing an inclination for delicacy that suited the tastes of the ancien régime.
persona1_adapted: Pierre Alexandre Wille (1748–1837) worked as an artist from the 1760s to the 1830s. During that time, he lived through some of the most dramatic events in French history. Wille studied under Jean Baptiste Greuze (1725–1805). He built his career by copying the everyday life scenes that made Greuze famous at the art shows of the 1760s. Wille’s work showed a gentle style that matched the tastes of the old French royal government.

CuratorialText: Yet Wille the man embraced the Revolution, even joining the republican army, and he busied himself chronicling the personalities of the day. Ultimately, however, the Revolution proved more of a challenge than an opportunity. The younger Wille had embraced a variety of styles but, in later life, he found it difficult to adapt to the more austere taste of Napoleonic France. By the time he died, he had long been reduced to poverty. During his lifetime and since, Wille was renowned more for his drawings than his paintings. The British Museum has been acquiring his work since the 19th century, and its collection has recently doubled in size following a bequest from the dealer and philanthropist Colin Clark (1935–2020). This gift included some of the finest finished drawings by Wille; one of the most powerful is the portrait which claims to show the 'Angel of Assassination' Charlotte Corday, made in her prison cell days or even hours before her execution. This celebration of Clark's bequest illustrates Wille's world through his drawings and those of his peers, revealing him to have been a talented draughtsman whose works offer a fascinating insight into French society before, during and after the Revolution.
persona1_adapted: Yet Wille embraced the Revolution. He even joined the republican army. He spent his time recording the famous people of his day. But in the end, the Revolution brought him more trouble than opportunity. As a young man, Wille tried many different art styles. Later in life, he struggled to adjust to the simpler tastes of Napoleonic France. By the time he died, he had been poor for many years. During his life and after, Wille was better known for his drawings than his paintings. The British Museum has collected his work since the 1800s. Its collection recently doubled in size thanks to a gift from the dealer and philanthropist Colin Clark (1935–2020). This gift included some of Wille's finest finished drawings. One of the most powerful is a portrait said to show Charlotte Corday, the 'Angel of Assassination.' It was made in her prison cell just days or even hours before her execution. This celebration of Clark's gift shows Wille's world through his drawings and those of his peers. It reveals him as a talented artist whose works give a fascinating look into French society before, during, and after the Revolution.

CuratorialText: Made in England between 1340 and 1405, the Asante Ewer later travelled to West Africa. In the 1880s it was photographed in a courtyard of the royal palace of the Asantehene, king of the Asante people, in Kumasi (in present-day Ghana). During the Anglo-Asante War of 1896 it was looted by British forces and subsequently purchased by the British Museum. A second ewer, also taken in 1896 and shown in the same photograph, was presented to the Prince of Wales's Own (West Yorkshire) Regiment. This display reunites these two jugs. A major new research project into the Asante Ewer – one of the finest examples of late medieval English bronze casting – is helping to further trace the story of its journey to Ghana, and its status and significance in West Africa. Its location in the centre of the palace courtyard, in what appears to be a shrine, embedded in the roots of sacred trees, suggests it was an object of significant cultural or ritual value. Remarkable for surviving the long-distance journey from Europe to West Africa and back, the Asante Ewer has had vastly different lives, from a luxury English jug to sacred West African vessel, to colonial loot taken by British troops.
persona1_adapted:
The Asante Ewer was made in England between 1340 and 1405. Later, it traveled to West Africa. In the 1880s, someone photographed it in the courtyard of the royal palace of the Asantehene, the king of the Asante people, in Kumasi (in present-day Ghana). During the Anglo-Asante War of 1896, British forces took the ewer as loot. Then the British Museum bought it. A second jug, also taken in 1896 and shown in the same photo, was given to the Prince of Wales's Own (West Yorkshire) Regiment. This display brings these two jugs back together.

A major new research project is studying the Asante Ewer. It is one of the finest examples of late medieval English bronze casting. The project helps trace its journey to Ghana and its importance in West Africa. The ewer sat in the center of the palace courtyard, in what looks like a shrine, buried in the roots of sacred trees. This suggests it was an object with great cultural or ritual value. It is amazing that the Asante Ewer survived the long trip from Europe to West Africa and back. It has had very different lives: a luxury English jug, a sacred West African vessel, and colonial loot taken by British troops.

CuratorialText: This display forms part of a three-year research project, The age of copper, ivory and gold, supported by The British Academy and Wolfson Foundation, exploring the connections between Western Europe and West Africa during the Middle Ages and Early Modern period. It is the result of close collaboration with colleagues at the Manhyia Palace Museum in Kumasi, York Army Museum and many others across Ghana, the US and the UK.
persona1_adapted: This display is part of a three-year research project called *The Age of Copper, Ivory and Gold*. The project was supported by The British Academy and Wolfson Foundation. It looks at the connections between Western Europe and West Africa during the Middle Ages and Early Modern period. The work was done in close partnership with colleagues at the Manhyia Palace Museum in Kumasi, the York Army Museum, and many others across Ghana, the US, and the UK.

CuratorialText: In the 1400s and 1500s, the Low Countries were a powerhouse of artistic innovation. This was a transformative period for Netherlandish drawings, as they evolved from preparatory studies to works of art in their own right.
persona1_adapted: In the 1400s and 1500s, the Low Countries were a major center of new ideas in art. During this time, drawings in the Netherlands changed. They started as practice sketches for paintings. Later, they became valuable artworks on their own.

CuratorialText: Featuring around 120 works by artists including Rogier van der Weyden, Lucas van Leyden, Pieter Bruegel the Elder and Hendrick Goltzius, this exhibition charts the development of drawing in the Low Countries (present-day Belgium, Luxembourg and the Netherlands) before 1600. The exceptional breadth and quality of the British Museum's holdings provides a unique opportunity to present a comprehensive account of drawing from this region.  Early Netherlandish drawings are extremely rare, as they were made as functional objects in the workshop and were not typically retained. This was unlike Italy or the German-speaking lands, where the taste for collecting drawings in the 1500s ensured the survival of a higher number of works from this period. Drawings were central to the design and production of works of art in different media, including tapestries, paintings, stained glass and prints – and surviving examples shed light on the creativity and collaboration of Netherlandish artists.
persona1_adapted:
This exhibition shows about 120 drawings by artists like Rogier van der Weyden, Lucas van Leyden, Pieter Bruegel the Elder, and Hendrick Goltzius. It follows how drawing grew in the Low Countries—today’s Belgium, Luxembourg, and the Netherlands—before 1600. The British Museum has a large and high-quality collection of these works. That gives us a rare chance to tell the full story of drawing from this area.

Early Netherlandish drawings are very hard to find. Artists made them as tools in their workshops, not as pieces to keep. That was different from Italy or German-speaking lands. There, people started collecting drawings in the 1500s, so more works from that time survived.

Drawings were key to making art in many forms, like tapestries, paintings, stained glass, and prints. The ones that still exist help us see how Netherlandish artists came up with ideas and worked together.

CuratorialText: Based on a research project and supported by a grant from the International Music and Art Foundation, the exhibition highlights new findings that have come from combining curatorial and conservation expertise along with scientific analysis. New research into drawings previously only connected to Rogier van der Weyden and his circle, for instance, reveals they originate directly from Rogier's workshop and must have been drawn by his pupils as part of their training.
persona1_adapted: This exhibition is based on a research project. It was supported by a grant from the International Music and Art Foundation. The show highlights new discoveries made by combining curatorial work, conservation, and scientific analysis. For example, new research into drawings once linked only to Rogier van der Weyden and his circle shows they actually come from his workshop. His pupils must have drawn them as part of their training.

CuratorialText: Be among the first to view designs for a permanent memorial to Queen Elizabeth II in central London.
persona1_adapted: Be one of the first to see plans for a permanent memorial to Queen Elizabeth II in central London.

CuratorialText: Featuring a 10ft bronze statue of Elizabeth as a young Queen, on a 14ft plinth, the memorial in St James' Park will commemorate the late monarch and her lifelong commitment to public service. The memorial is expected to be completed in 2028. Designed by acclaimed sculptor Martin Jennings, to be sculpted in bronze, the statue will show Her Majesty in an early stage of her reign, dressed in the robes of the Order of the Garter, using a famous painting by Pietro Annigoni as inspiration.
persona1_adapted: A memorial in St James’s Park will honor the late Queen Elizabeth and her lifelong dedication to public service. It will feature a 10-foot bronze statue of her as a young queen, set on a 14-foot stone base. The statue is expected to be finished in 2028. Famous sculptor Martin Jennings will create the bronze statue. It will show the Queen early in her reign, wearing the robes of the Order of the Garter. The design takes inspiration from a well-known painting by Pietro Annigoni.

CuratorialText: Mr Jennings will also sculpt a statue of Prince Philip, the country's longest-serving consort, at a similar age wearing a uniform of Admiral-of-the-Fleet. His statue will be located close to Queen Elizabeth's, in recognition of the support he gave her during her 70-year reign. Other elements of the memorial have been designed by Foster + Partners in collaboration with Michel Desvigne Paysagiste. The British Museum display features maquettes of the statues and a scale model of the wider memorial park. The King and Queen, other Members of the Royal Family and the Prime Minister viewed the designs at the Museum on 21 April to mark the 100th anniversary of Queen Elizabeth II's birth. The permanent memorial will also feature: a new cast-glass bridge inspired by Queen Mary's Fringe Tiara, which was worn by Queen Elizabeth on her wedding day; a bust of Her Majesty in her later years by sculptor Karen Newman; and The Commonwealth Wind Sculpture, a new abstract work by Yinka Shonibare.
persona1_adapted:
Mr. Jennings will also make a statue of Prince Philip. He was the country's longest-serving royal partner. The statue will show him at a similar age, wearing his Admiral-of-the-Fleet uniform. It will stand close to Queen Elizabeth's statue. This honors the support he gave her during her 70-year reign.

Other parts of the memorial were designed by Foster + Partners, working with Michel Desvigne Paysagiste. The British Museum display includes small models of the statues. It also has a scale model of the whole memorial park.

The King and Queen, other members of the Royal Family, and the Prime Minister saw the designs at the Museum on April 21. This date marked the 100th anniversary of Queen Elizabeth II's birth.

The permanent memorial will also include:
- A new glass bridge, shaped like Queen Mary's Fringe Tiara. The Queen wore this tiara on her wedding day.
- A bust of Her Majesty in her later years, made by sculptor Karen Newman.
- The Commonwealth Wind Sculpture, a new abstract work by Yinka Shonibare.

CuratorialText: The design also includes gardens dedicated to the Commonwealth and to the nations of the United Kingdom, creating spaces for relaxation and reflection. The memorial has been developed in consultation with the Queen Elizabeth Memorial Committee following a design competition held last year. As well as the memorial park, recommendations made by the Queen Elizabeth Memorial Committee include a new charitable trust for regenerating community assets across the UK, and an online Digital Memorial(Opens in new window) to allow people to submit their own memories of the late Queen.
persona1_adapted: The design also includes gardens for the Commonwealth and the nations of the United Kingdom. These spaces are meant for relaxation and quiet thought. The memorial was created with help from the Queen Elizabeth Memorial Committee. This followed a design competition held last year. Along with the memorial park, the committee suggested other ideas. These include a new charity to improve community spaces across the UK. There is also an online Digital Memorial where people can share their own memories of the late Queen.

CuratorialText:
Discover how people lived and died in ancient Egypt and explore their hopes and aspirations for the afterlife. 
From human remains, painted coffins, amulets, shabtis and tomb models of daily life to spectacular wall paintings and papyri; investigate what tombs, human remains and burial goods tell us about the lives and afterlives of ancient Egyptian people. The tour will explore the preparation of the deceased for burial, including mummification, the furnishing of the tomb for an eternal restful existence, and the use of magic to guide and protect people on their perilous journey to the afterlife. It will also show how funerary remains can tell us about the lives of the ancient Egyptians. This tour visits displays that include human remains. The British Museum is committed to curating human remains with care, respect and dignity. Find out more about our principles governing the holding, display and study of human remains.
persona1_adapted:
Discover how people lived and died in ancient Egypt. Learn about their hopes for the afterlife. You will see human remains, painted coffins, amulets, and small statues called shabtis. There are also tomb models showing daily life, plus wall paintings and papyri. These objects help us understand what life and the afterlife meant to ancient Egyptians.

The tour explains how people prepared bodies for burial, including mummification. It shows how tombs were set up for an eternal, peaceful rest. You will also learn how magic was used to guide and protect people on their dangerous journey to the afterlife. These remains tell us a lot about how ancient Egyptians lived.

This tour includes displays of human remains. The British Museum treats these remains with care, respect, and dignity. You can learn more about our rules for holding, showing, and studying human remains.

CuratorialText:
Discover the fascinating history of ancient Egypt and explore one of the most iconic spaces in the Museum before it opens to the public.
In the Egyptian sculpture gallery (Room 4), you can see impressive statues of kings and gods, monumental tomb architecture and ancient tomb reliefs spanning 3,000 year. These include the imposing colossal bust of Ramesses II and the Gayer-Anderson Cat. Learn about the importance of large-scale sculpture in ancient Egyptian temples, tombs and much more in this special guided tour.
persona1_adapted:
Discover the amazing history of ancient Egypt. Explore one of the most famous rooms in the Museum before it opens to the public.

In the Egyptian sculpture gallery (Room 4), you can see huge statues of kings and gods. You will also see large tomb buildings and ancient stone carvings from tombs. These objects cover 3,000 years of history. They include the giant bust of Ramesses II and the Gayer-Anderson Cat. On this special guided tour, learn why large sculptures were so important in ancient Egyptian temples and tombs.

CuratorialText: Founded in 1753, the British Museum opened its doors to visitors in 1759. The Museum tells the story of human cultural achievement through a collection of collections. This room celebrates some of the collectors who, in different ways, have shaped the Museum over four centuries, along with individuals and organisations who continue to shape its future. The adjoining galleries also explore aspects of collecting. Room 1: Enlightenment, tells the story of how, in the early Museum, objects and knowledge were gathered and classified. Room 2a: The Waddesdon Bequest displays the collection of Renaissance and Baroque masterpieces left to the British Museum by Baron Ferdinand Rothschild MP at his death in 1898.
persona1_adapted:
The British Museum opened its doors to visitors in 1759. It was founded in 1753. The Museum tells the story of human culture through many different collections.

This room celebrates some of the collectors who helped shape the Museum over 400 years. It also honors people and groups who continue to shape its future today. The rooms next door explore other parts of collecting.

Room 1, called Enlightenment, shows how the early Museum gathered and sorted objects and knowledge. Room 2a, the Waddesdon Bequest, displays art from the Renaissance and Baroque periods. These masterpieces were left to the Museum by Baron Ferdinand Rothschild MP when he died in 1898.

CuratorialText: The British Museum has successfully raised £3.5 million, which means the Tudor Heart Pendant will remain on public display for generations to come.  In December 2019 a metal detectorist in Warwickshire found a spectacular Tudor Heart jewel associated with Henry VIII and his first wife Katherine of Aragon in the ground after centuries of rest. The Tudor Heart Pendant is unlike any object in the British Museum collection or elsewhere in the UK. This highly accomplished jewel with enamelled motifs is attached to a 75-link gold chain by a suspension clasp in the form of a hand emerging from a cloud. Few objects survive to tell the story of Henry's life with the devoutly Catholic Katherine, a consequence of the annulment of their marriage in 1533. Their union was the longest of all of Henry's, lasting 24 years it decisively shaped the first three decades of his reign.
persona1_adapted:
The British Museum raised £3.5 million. This means the Tudor Heart Pendant will stay on public display for many years to come.

In December 2019, a person using a metal detector in Warwickshire found a stunning Tudor heart jewel. It had been buried in the ground for centuries. The jewel is linked to Henry VIII and his first wife, Katherine of Aragon.

The Tudor Heart Pendant is unlike any other object in the British Museum or anywhere else in the UK. This highly skilled piece of jewelry has enamel designs. It hangs from a 75-link gold chain. A clasp shaped like a hand coming out of a cloud holds it in place.

Very few objects survive that tell the story of Henry’s life with Katherine, who was a devout Catholic. This is because their marriage was annulled, or legally ended, in 1533. Their marriage lasted 24 years—the longest of all Henry’s marriages. It strongly shaped the first 30 years of his rule as king.

CuratorialText: In the 18th century, the collections were divided into 'Natural and Artificial Rarities' – objects found in nature or made by people. Only a few rooms in the early Museum had manmade objects but case after case was filled with natural specimens from Sloane's collection and his library which included the 265 volumes of his herbarium. In 1735, the Swedish botanist Carl Linnaeus revolutionised the way plants, animals and other objects from the natural world were named and classified. His pupil, Daniel Solander, was a curator in the Museum and applied the system to Sloane's collections and to the natural history specimens Solander himself collected with Sir Joseph Banks on the first Cook voyage.  Many of these original specimens are included in the exhibition, as well as a number of fossils. By 1880 there were so many natural history specimens that they needed a museum of their own – the Natural History Museum in South Kensington.
persona1_adapted:
In the 1700s, the Museum's collections were split into two types: "Natural and Artificial Rarities." Natural rarities were objects found in nature, while artificial rarities were things made by people. Only a few rooms in the early Museum held man-made objects. Instead, case after case was filled with natural specimens from Sir Hans Sloane's collection. His library also included 265 volumes of pressed plants, called a herbarium.

In 1735, Swedish botanist Carl Linnaeus changed how scientists named and grouped plants, animals, and other natural objects. His student, Daniel Solander, worked as a curator at the Museum. Solander used Linnaeus's system to organize Sloane's collections. He also applied it to the natural history specimens he collected with Sir Joseph Banks on Captain Cook's first voyage. Many of these original specimens are in the exhibition, along with several fossils.

By 1880, the Museum had so many natural history specimens that they needed their own building. That became the Natural History Museum in South Kensington.

CuratorialText: Antiquaries collected books, manuscripts, drawings and artefacts from the past, and travelled the country studying ruins in order to learn more about Britain's Roman past and its earlier history. It was fairly easy to learn about medieval times because many of these objects had survived, including religious artefacts, armour, books and buildings. But antiquaries also began to survey and map Roman and earlier sites, such as Stonehenge, in a more scientific and systematic way and to learn more about the earliest Britons.  This knowledge, combined with the new study of rock strata, led archaeologists to question the accepted date of the world's creation – 4004 BC – calculated from readings of the Bible.
persona1_adapted: Antiquaries were people who collected old books, handwritten pages, drawings, and objects from the past. They traveled around the country studying ruins. They wanted to learn more about Britain's Roman past and its earlier history. Learning about medieval times was fairly easy. Many objects from that period had survived, like religious items, armor, books, and buildings. But antiquaries also began to survey and map Roman and even older sites, such as Stonehenge. They did this in a more scientific and organized way. This helped them learn more about the earliest Britons. This new knowledge, along with the study of rock layers, led archaeologists to question the accepted date of the world's creation. That date was 4004 BC, which had been calculated from readings of the Bible.

CuratorialText: In 1824, Charles Townley's famous collection of Greek and Roman sculptures, acquired on his Grand Tours in Italy, came to the British Museum. They joined the collections of prints and drawings, gems, coins, bronzes, vases and other classical antiquities from fellow connoisseurs such as Sir William Hamilton and Richard Payne Knight. These men studied each other's collections to learn about the 'progress' of art, from 'primitive' beginnings in early civilisations to what they considered to be the height of artistic achievement – the sculpture and architecture of classical Greece. This became the standard against which to measure all art and led, in late 18th-century Britain, to a classical revival in architecture and the decorative arts.
persona1_adapted:
In 1824, Charles Townley’s famous collection of Greek and Roman sculptures arrived at the British Museum. He had gathered these pieces during his Grand Tours in Italy. They joined other collections of prints, drawings, gems, coins, bronzes, vases, and ancient objects. These items came from other art lovers like Sir William Hamilton and Richard Payne Knight.

These men studied each other’s collections to understand how art had “progressed.” They believed art started with simple, early works from ancient civilizations. They thought it reached its peak in the sculpture and architecture of classical Greece. This Greek art became the standard for judging all other art. In late 1700s Britain, this idea sparked a revival of classical styles in buildings and decorative arts.

CuratorialText: Everything gathered in the 18th century had to be classified and organised so that it could be more easily understood and used.  King George III's library was presented to the nation by George IV and was housed in the room that is now the Enlightenment Gallery (his library is now in the British Library). The coins and medals collection was among the first part of the library to arrive at the Museum and they were arranged by country and ruler to help bring past history alive. George III was also keenly interested in the navy, navigation and scientific discoveries and collected instruments and working models. Sir William Hamilton arranged his vases found in the area around Naples according to the styles of painting, attempting to establish a chronology for them. He later published them in beautifully illustrated folios. In the early part of the century, Sir Hans Sloane filled his cabinets with curious 'artificial' (manmade) objects from around the world, including those collected from enslaved peoples in Jamaica. These objects were catalogued and displayed in many different ways – by type, material, date or purpose.
persona1_adapted: Everything collected in the 1700s had to be sorted and organized so it could be understood and used more easily. King George III’s library was given to the nation by George IV. It was kept in the room that is now the Enlightenment Gallery. (His library is now at the British Library.) The coins and medals were among the first items from the library to arrive at the Museum. They were arranged by country and ruler to help bring history to life. George III was very interested in the navy, navigation, and science. He collected tools and working models. Sir William Hamilton arranged his vases from the area near Naples by painting style. He tried to figure out the order in which they were made. Later, he published them in books with beautiful pictures. In the early 1700s, Sir Hans Sloane filled his cabinets with curious manmade objects from around the world. These included items collected from enslaved people in Jamaica. The objects were listed and shown in many different ways—by type, material, date, or purpose.

CuratorialText: The Enlightenment led to a fevered interest in deciphering the mysterious forms of Egyptian hieroglyphs and the cuneiform inscriptions found in lands described in the Bible as well as early forms of Sanskrit in India. Scholars 'cracked the codes' in the 19th century, opening up thousands of years of history. At the same time, antiquaries deciphered runes and other early forms of writing, revealing more of our own history. This was a period when significant numbers of antiquities, acquired from collectors, diplomats and through excavation, entered the British Museum.
persona1_adapted: The Enlightenment sparked a huge interest in decoding Egyptian hieroglyphs, cuneiform writing from Bible lands, and early Sanskrit in India. In the 1800s, scholars cracked these codes. This opened up thousands of years of history. At the same time, experts deciphered runes and other ancient scripts. This revealed more about our own past. During this time, many ancient objects came to the British Museum. They were collected from excavators, diplomats, and private collectors.

CuratorialText: Christian mistrust of superstition and idols, rituals and magic led to ignorance about other religions. Enlightenment scholars attempted to dispel this by studying the ancient and modern religions they encountered. They researched rituals and cults in different cultures, collected religious artefacts, grouped gods into families and examined their attributes and functions. They wanted to find similarities that might shed light on the origins of the world's religions. Much of this activity took place within the context of colonialism.
persona1_adapted: Christians distrusted superstition, idols, rituals, and magic. This led to ignorance about other religions. Enlightenment scholars tried to fix this. They studied ancient and modern religions they came across. They researched rituals and cults in different cultures. They collected religious objects. They grouped gods into families. They looked at what each god was like and what they did. Their goal was to find similarities. These similarities might explain where the world's religions came from. Much of this work happened during the time of colonialism.

CuratorialText: In this era of colonialism and global trade, travellers, traders, scientists, explorers and diplomats created collections of ethnographic objects, including ceremonial and everyday items, thought representative of customs and cultures of people around the globe. They collected artefacts and published illustrated accounts about the lives and cultures of people around the world, from a European perspective. Alongside Sir Hans Sloane's collection, the British Museum also houses objects from the voyages of Captain James Cook and Sir Joseph Banks to Australia, New Zealand, Tahiti and other Pacific islands. A significant number of objects were acquired through employees of the East India and Royal Africa Companies, whose wealth was made possible through the use of enslaved labour and the exploitation of people and resources.
persona1_adapted: During the time of colonialism and global trade, travelers, traders, scientists, explorers, and diplomats collected objects from different cultures. These objects included items used in ceremonies and daily life. People thought these objects showed the customs and cultures of people around the world. They collected these items and published books with pictures about the lives and cultures of people from a European point of view. Along with Sir Hans Sloane's collection, the British Museum also has objects from the voyages of Captain James Cook and Sir Joseph Banks. They traveled to Australia, New Zealand, Tahiti, and other Pacific islands. A large number of objects came from employees of the East India Company and the Royal Africa Company. These companies became wealthy by using enslaved labor and by exploiting people and resources.

CuratorialText: During his leadership, from 336–323 BC, Alexander the Great conquered the Greek city-states, the Persian Empire and founded more than 70 cities creating an empire that stretched across three continents and covered about two million square miles.  The Hellenistic period dates from the death of Alexander the Great in 323 BC to the defeat of Cleopatra VII and Mark Antony at Actium in 31 BC. After his death, Alexander's empire was divided up into kingdoms, ruled by his generals. The major dynasties sponsored major architectural programmes and were great patrons of the arts and culture. Objects on display in Room 22 include a colossal carved column drum from the Temple of Artemis at Ephesos (one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World), the Demeter from Knidos in south-west Turkey and Greek bronzes. Displays of smaller objects show the skill of ancient goldsmiths, metalworkers, potters and coroplasts (makers of clay or terracotta figurines) and gem cutters.
persona1_adapted:
Alexander the Great led his empire from 336 to 323 BC. He conquered the Greek city-states and the Persian Empire. He also founded more than 70 cities. His empire stretched across three continents and covered about two million square miles.

The Hellenistic period began when Alexander died in 323 BC. It ended when Cleopatra VII and Mark Antony were defeated at Actium in 31 BC. After Alexander's death, his generals split his empire into separate kingdoms. These major ruling families paid for large building projects. They also supported art and culture.

Room 22 displays many objects from this time. One is a huge carved column drum from the Temple of Artemis at Ephesos. That temple was one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World. Another object is the Demeter statue from Knidos in southwest Turkey. There are also Greek bronzes. Smaller displays show the skill of ancient goldsmiths, metalworkers, and potters. You can also see works by coroplasts, who made clay or terracotta figurines, and gem cutters.

CuratorialText: The exhibition is a dazzling celebration of the artistry and history of Hawaiʻi. It brings together remarkable objects – from feathered cloaks worn by chiefs and finely carved deities, to powerful shark-toothed weapons and bold contemporary works by Kānaka ʻŌiwi (Native Hawaiian) artists. In 1824, the young King Liholiho and Queen Kamāmalu travelled across vast oceans on a journey that would mark a turning point in the history of their kingdom, Hawaiʻi. The exhibition commemorates over 200 years since this ill-fated royal visit and sheds light on Hawaiʻi's history and culture through an exploration of the complex and enduring relationship between the Hawaiian and British nations.
persona1_adapted:
This exhibition celebrates the art and history of Hawaiʻi. It brings together amazing objects. You will see feathered cloaks worn by chiefs and finely carved gods. There are also powerful weapons made with shark teeth. Bold modern works by Kānaka ʻŌiwi (Native Hawaiian) artists are on display too.

In 1824, the young King Liholiho and Queen Kamāmalu traveled across the ocean. Their journey marked a turning point for their kingdom, Hawaiʻi. This exhibition honors over 200 years since that tragic royal visit. It explores Hawaiʻi's history and culture. It also looks at the deep and lasting bond between the Hawaiian and British nations.

CuratorialText: The exhibition is a dazzling celebration of the artistry and history of Hawaiʻi. It brings together remarkable objects – from feathered cloaks worn by chiefs and finely carved deities, to powerful shark-toothed weapons and bold contemporary works by Kānaka ʻŌiwi (Native Hawaiian) artists. In 1824, the young King Liholiho and Queen Kamāmalu travelled across vast oceans on a journey that would mark a turning point in the history of their kingdom, Hawaiʻi. The exhibition commemorates over 200 years since this ill-fated royal visit and sheds light on Hawaiʻi's history and culture through an exploration of the complex and enduring relationship between the Hawaiian and British nations.
persona1_adapted:
This exhibition celebrates the art and history of Hawaiʻi. It brings together amazing objects. You will see feathered cloaks worn by chiefs and finely carved statues of gods. There are also powerful weapons made with shark teeth and bold modern works by Kānaka ʻŌiwi (Native Hawaiian) artists.

In 1824, the young King Liholiho and Queen Kamāmalu traveled across the ocean. Their journey marked a turning point for their kingdom, Hawaiʻi. The exhibition honors over 200 years since this royal visit. It explores Hawaiʻi's history and culture by looking at the deep and lasting ties between the Hawaiian and British nations.

CuratorialText: This fashion show presents contemporary clothing of the Moana-Nui-a-Kiwa (the great ocean of Kiwa/the Pacific Ocean), which has been facilitated in the UK by Interisland Collective and curated by Lyall Hakaraia. The collection has been conceived and made by Māori designer Lyall Hakaraia in collaboration with venerated practitioners from across the Pacific. These practictioners are tohunga (experts) who are building and continue to grow Pacific cultural knowledge. They consider themselves as guides and strive to be 'good ancestors' through their daily practices, powerful intentions and vision. They are building resilient futures for mokopuna (grandchildren) and generations to come. The show will act as a focal point for the Pacific diaspora community as they activate a VĀ (portal) to their ancestors. The show is an offering to those ancestors that remain at the British Museum and are embodied through Pacific collections. It aims to 'warm' them in slumber. The collective will sing, chant and offer prayers to them during the show with Aroha (love) from across the Pacific.
persona1_adapted: This fashion show features modern clothing from Moana-Nui-a-Kiwa, also known as the great ocean of Kiwa or the Pacific Ocean. The show is organized in the UK by Interisland Collective and curated by Lyall Hakaraia. Māori designer Lyall Hakaraia created the collection with respected experts from across the Pacific. These experts are called tohunga, which means they are masters of their craft. They work to build and grow Pacific cultural knowledge. They see themselves as guides and try to be 'good ancestors' through their daily actions, strong intentions, and vision. They are creating strong futures for mokopuna, or grandchildren, and for generations to come. The show will be a gathering point for the Pacific diaspora community as they open a VĀ, or portal, to their ancestors. The show is an offering to those ancestors who remain at the British Museum and are represented through Pacific collections. It aims to 'warm' them while they rest. During the show, the group will sing, chant, and offer prayers to them with Aroha, or love, from across the Pacific.

CuratorialText: One half of the gallery presents the histories of China from 5000 BC to the present: from iconic Ming dynasty blue-and-white porcelain to delicate handscrolls, from magnificent Tang dynasty tomb figurines to modern works of art. The displays feature the richness of art and material culture in China, including painting, prints, jade, bronze, lacquer and ceramics.
persona1_adapted: One half of the gallery shows China's history from 5000 BC to today. You can see famous blue-and-white porcelain from the Ming dynasty and delicate handscrolls. There are also grand Tang dynasty tomb figurines and modern artworks. The displays highlight China's rich art and material culture. This includes paintings, prints, jade, bronze, lacquer, and ceramics.

CuratorialText: The other half of the gallery presents South Asia's many histories chronologically and by region, from early human occupation to the present. Highlights include seals from the Indus civilisation, superb south Indian sculptures of Shiva and one of the finest statues of the goddess Tara from Sri Lanka. Sophisticated paintings and objects from the courts of the Mughal emperors can be seen alongside 20th-century paintings, including by the Nobel laureate Rabindranath Tagore.
persona1_adapted: The other side of the gallery shows South Asia’s many histories in order of time and by region. It covers from early human settlement to today. Key objects include seals from the Indus civilization, beautiful sculptures of Shiva from south India, and one of the finest statues of the goddess Tara from Sri Lanka. You can see detailed paintings and objects from the courts of the Mughal emperors. These are displayed next to 20th-century paintings, including works by the Nobel Prize winner Rabindranath Tagore.

CuratorialText: China is one of the world's oldest civilisations. Today it covers a vast territory the size of Europe and is home to a quarter of the world's population. China has produced a highly distinctive culture with beautifully crafted objects made on an industrial scale from the earliest times. Room 33 examines the past 7,000 years of China's history, exploring the themes of writing systems, rituals, beliefs, war, international trade and more. Room 33 also explores South Asia's history, from 1.5 million years ago to the present day. Trade, exchange, migration and political power are some of the themes examined. South Asia spans India, Pakistan, Bangladesh, Bhutan, Nepal, the Tibetan plateau and Sri Lanka. The region is home to numerous ancient and modern languages, such as Sanskrit, Tamil, Hindi and Urdu. The Buddhist, Hindu, Jain, Sikh and Islamic faiths have inspired magnificent architecture, sculpture, painting, literature and music.
persona1_adapted:
China is one of the world's oldest civilizations. Today it covers a huge area, about the size of Europe. One out of every four people on Earth lives there. China developed a very unique culture. From early times, people there made beautiful objects in large numbers.

Room 33 looks at 7,000 years of Chinese history. It explores writing, rituals, beliefs, war, and international trade. The room also looks at South Asia's history, from 1.5 million years ago to today. Themes include trade, exchange, migration, and political power.

South Asia includes India, Pakistan, Bangladesh, Bhutan, Nepal, the Tibetan plateau, and Sri Lanka. Many ancient and modern languages are spoken there, like Sanskrit, Tamil, Hindi, and Urdu. Religions such as Buddhism, Hinduism, Jainism, Sikhism, and Islam inspired great architecture, sculpture, painting, literature, and music.

CuratorialText:
Africa's rich cultural heritage and histories are represented at the Museum both in Africa (Room 25), The Sainsbury Galleries, and across the galleries. Join our volunteer-led tours, developed in collaboration with community members, to explore their personal selection of objects ranging from the ancient world to the present day. Each guide will deliver their own personal tour exploring one of the following themes:
Out of Africa: stories of migration, materials and mythology
The Igbo worldview: artefacts from the past in the British Museum
African metal artefacts: tradition, technique and trade
Ancient objects from African kingdoms
persona1_adapted:
Africa’s rich culture and history are shown at the Museum in Africa (Room 25), The Sainsbury Galleries, and other galleries. Join our volunteer-led tours, made with help from community members. You can explore their personal choice of objects, from ancient times to today. Each guide gives their own tour on one of these topics:

- Out of Africa: stories of migration, materials, and mythology  
- The Igbo worldview: old objects from the past at the British Museum  
- African metal objects: tradition, skill, and trade  
- Ancient objects from African kingdoms

CuratorialText:
Step into spring and the month of May in this creative play session for children aged five and under.
Meet May Queens, Green Men and woodland creatures in this child-led, open-ended creative session. Stop and smell some floral scents, draw and scribble with a range of art materials and make crowns and masks to wear for spring festivities! Working at your child's pace, together we'll explore textures, colours and scents through a range of materials.
persona1_adapted: Step into spring and the month of May with this creative play session for kids ages five and under. You'll meet May Queens, Green Men, and woodland creatures in a child-led, open-ended activity. Stop and smell floral scents. Draw and scribble with different art materials. Make crowns and masks to wear for spring celebrations. We'll work at your child's pace. Together, we'll explore textures, colors, and scents through a variety of materials.

CuratorialText: Sennacherib was the king of Assyria from 704–681 BC and was famous for his building projects. The rooms and courtyards of his Neo-Assyrian Southwest Palace at Nineveh were decorated with a series of detailed carved stone panels. Many of these stone panels are on display in Room 9. The panels depict a variety of scenes, including the transport of huge sculptures of human-headed winged bulls (lamassu) that weigh up to 30 tons and were intended for the main entrances to the palace. These illustrations provide an insight into ancient quarrying and transport techniques, as well as Sennacherib's keen interest in his building projects. Other panels on display depict the king's military campaigns.
persona1_adapted: Sennacherib was the king of Assyria from 704 to 681 BC. He was famous for his building projects. His Southwest Palace at Nineveh was decorated with detailed stone carvings. Many of these carved stone panels are now on display in Room 9. The panels show different scenes. One scene shows workers moving huge stone sculptures of human-headed winged bulls. These bulls, called lamassu, weighed up to 30 tons. They were meant for the main entrances to the palace. These carvings give us a look at how people in ancient times quarried and moved heavy stone. They also show how much Sennacherib cared about his building projects. Other panels on display show the king's military campaigns.

CuratorialText: Split across three rooms, these galleries display life during this period in Assyrian culture. On display are lion hunting reliefs, the siege of the city of Lachish and scenes from inside the palace of King Sargon II at Khorsabad.  Visitors to Room 10a are greeted by a pair of colossal human-headed winged bulls. Intended as a supernatural guardians, these gigantic figures guarded the palace of King Sargon II at Khorsabad, and were intended to deter evil of all kinds from entering.
persona1_adapted: These galleries are spread across three rooms. They show what life was like in Assyrian culture during this time. You will see stone carvings of lion hunts, the attack on the city of Lachish, and scenes from inside the palace of King Sargon II at Khorsabad. In Room 10a, visitors are greeted by two huge winged bulls with human heads. These giant figures were meant to be supernatural guards. They protected the palace of King Sargon II at Khorsabad and were made to keep all kinds of evil from entering.

CuratorialText: In ancient Assyria, hunting lions was considered the sport of kings, symbolic of the ruling monarch's duty to protect and fight for his people. The sculpted reliefs in Room 10a illustrate the sporting exploits of the last great Assyrian king, Ashurbanipal (668–631 BC) and were created for his palace at Nineveh (in modern-day northern Iraq). The hunt scenes, full of tension and realism, rank among the finest achievements of Assyrian Art. They depict the release of the lions, the ensuing chase and subsequent killing.
persona1_adapted: In ancient Assyria, hunting lions was seen as the sport of kings. It showed that the ruler had a duty to protect and fight for his people. The stone carvings in Room 10a show the hunting adventures of the last great Assyrian king, Ashurbanipal (668–631 BC). These carvings were made for his palace in Nineveh, which is in modern-day northern Iraq. The hunting scenes are full of tension and look very real. They are some of the finest examples of Assyrian art. The carvings show the lions being released, the chase, and the final kill.

CuratorialText: Lachish was one of the chief cities of the kingdom of Judah in the southern Levant and in 701 BC it was captured by the Assyrian King Sennacherib (704–681 BC). The siege followed the refusal of Lachish to pay tribute to the Assyrian Empire (based in modern northern Iraq) and is mentioned in the Bible. Many of the relief sculptures on display in Room 10b depict the capture of the city, alongside a selection of items and weaponry used in the siege. A 'prism' inscribed with an Assyrian account of the campaign is also on show.
persona1_adapted: Lachish was one of the main cities in the kingdom of Judah, located in the southern Levant. In 701 BC, the Assyrian King Sennacherib (704–681 BC) captured it. The siege happened because Lachish refused to pay tribute to the Assyrian Empire, which was based in modern northern Iraq. The Bible also mentions this event. Many of the stone carvings on display in Room 10b show the capture of the city. They are shown alongside tools and weapons used during the siege. A clay object called a 'prism,' which has an Assyrian story of the campaign written on it, is also on display.

CuratorialText: The city and palace at Khorsabad (in modern northern Iraq), was built for the Assyrian King Sargon II (721–705 BC). The palace entrances were originally dominated by pairs of colossal human-headed winged bulls, which were intended as guardians, accompanied by protective spirits with magical powers. Two of these impressive statues now stand in Room 10c, along with carvings depicting the king and crown prince, royal courtiers and hunting scenes. Inscriptions on display in the gallery come from a similar winged bull from the palace of Sennacherib (704–681 BC) at nearby Nineveh and were badly burnt when the city was destroyed in 612 BC.
persona1_adapted: The city and palace at Khorsabad, in modern northern Iraq, was built for the Assyrian King Sargon II, who ruled from 721 to 705 BC. Huge statues of winged bulls with human heads once guarded the palace entrances. These statues were meant to protect the palace, along with magical spirit figures. Today, two of these impressive statues stand in Room 10c. You can also see carvings of the king, the crown prince, royal courtiers, and hunting scenes. Nearby, inscriptions on display come from a similar winged bull statue. That statue was from the palace of King Sennacherib, who ruled from 704 to 681 BC, at the city of Nineveh. The inscriptions were badly burned when the city was destroyed in 612 BC.

CuratorialText: Rooms 57–59 present the material culture of the region from the Neolithic farmers of the eighth millennium BC to the fall of the Neo-Babylonian Empire in 539 BC, within the context of major historical events. Objects on display illustrate the continuity of the Canaanite culture of the southern Levant throughout this period. They highlight the indigenous origins of both the Israelites and the Phoenicians. The display compares this culture with that of the people of central inland Syria, the Amorites and the Aramaeans.
persona1_adapted: Rooms 57–59 show objects from a region that spans a long period of history. This period starts with the first farmers of the Neolithic age, around 8000 BC, and ends with the fall of the Neo-Babylonian Empire in 539 BC. The items on display help us understand the major events of that time. They show how the Canaanite culture of the southern Levant stayed strong and lasted through the years. These objects also highlight that both the Israelites and the Phoenicians came from local people in this area. The display compares this culture with the people of central inland Syria, known as the Amorites and the Aramaeans.

CuratorialText: Ancient Anatolia and Urartu form an important land link between Europe and Asia. They lie where the modern Republic of Turkey, Armenia, Georgia and north-west Iran are located today.  Examples of Early Bronze Age craftsmanship on display include silver bull and cup. Business archives of Middle Bronze Age merchants illustrate trading between central Anatolia and Mesopotamia (modern Iraq). Delicate gold jewellery and figurines date from the Hittite period. Iron Age objects from Urartu, include winged bulls and griffins used to decorate furniture.
persona1_adapted: Ancient Anatolia and Urartu were a key land bridge between Europe and Asia. These lands are now part of modern Turkey, Armenia, Georgia, and northwest Iran. On display, you can see Early Bronze Age crafts, like a silver bull and cup. Business records from Middle Bronze Age merchants show trade between central Anatolia and Mesopotamia (modern Iraq). Delicate gold jewelry and figurines come from the Hittite period. Iron Age objects from Urartu include winged bulls and griffins. These were used to decorate furniture.

CuratorialText: Reaching back more than 2,000 years, this exhibition explored the origins of Hindu, Jain and Buddhist sacred art in the ancient and powerful nature spirits of India, and the spread of this art beyond the subcontinent.  One of the first major exhibitions in the world to look at the early devotional art of India from a multi-faith, contemporary and global perspective, it highlighted the inspiration behind now-familiar depictions of the deities and enlightened teachers of these world religions – and how they were shared across the Indian Ocean to Southeast Asia and along the Silk Roads to East Asia.
persona1_adapted: This exhibition looked back over 2,000 years. It explored the origins of sacred art for Hindus, Jains, and Buddhists. This art began with ancient nature spirits in India. The show also traced how this art spread beyond India. It was one of the first major exhibitions to study early Indian devotional art. It used a multi-faith, modern, and global view. The exhibition highlighted what inspired the familiar images of gods and enlightened teachers. It showed how these images traveled across the Indian Ocean to Southeast Asia. They also moved along the Silk Roads to East Asia.

CuratorialText: Colourful, multi-sensory and atmospheric, this exhibition was developed in collaboration with an advisory community panel of practising Hindus, Buddhists and Jains. These living religious traditions and their sacred art are now integral to the daily lives of almost two billion people around the world including in the UK. Key loans from our community partners helped to tell this contemporary story.
persona1_adapted: This exhibition is colorful, multi-sensory, and full of atmosphere. It was created with help from a community panel of Hindus, Buddhists, and Jains. These are living religious traditions. Their sacred art is part of daily life for nearly two billion people around the world, including in the UK. Important loans from our community partners helped tell this modern story.

CuratorialText: The exhibition showcased more than 180 objects – including sculptures, paintings, drawings and manuscripts – from the South Asian collection at the British Museum as well as generous loans from national and international partners. It highlighted provenance, examining the stories, from creation to acquisition by museums, of every object in the show.
persona1_adapted: This exhibition featured over 180 objects from the British Museum’s South Asian collection. It included sculptures, paintings, drawings, and manuscripts. Many items also came from museums and partners around the world. The show focused on provenance—the history of each object, from when it was made to how it ended up in a museum.

CuratorialText: From the symbolic footprints which preceded portrayals of the Buddha in human form to the cosmic serpents incorporated into Hindu art and the nature spirits who attend Jain enlightened teachers, this compelling exhibition told the ancient stories behind these living traditions.
persona1_adapted: This exhibition tells ancient stories behind three living traditions. Early Buddhist art used symbols like footprints instead of showing the Buddha as a person. Hindu art includes cosmic serpents. Jain art shows nature spirits who attend enlightened teachers.

CuratorialText: The first exhibition on Hiroshige to be held at the British Museum, and the first on the artist in London for more than a quarter of a century, this was a visually stunning portrait of a country about to change forever. Born during an unsettled time in Japan's history, Utagawa Hiroshige (1797–1858) went on to become one of the country's most talented, prolific and popular artists. As Japan confronted the encroaching outside world, Hiroshige's calm artistic vision connected with – and reassured – people at every level of society. From fashionable figures and energetic city views to remote landscapes and impressions of the natural world, Hiroshige captured many aspects of life in the Japan of his time. Stunning bird-and-flower prints reveal his poetic feeling for nature while his evocative landscapes reflected the growing interest in travel across Japan. Hiroshige portrayed his world sometimes as it was, but often the way he imagined it could be.
persona1_adapted: This is the first exhibition on Hiroshige at the British Museum. It is also the first show on the artist in London in over 25 years. The exhibition gives a stunning look at a country about to change forever. Hiroshige was born during a troubled time in Japan's history. He became one of the country's most talented, productive, and popular artists. As Japan faced the outside world moving in, Hiroshige's calm art connected with people from all levels of society. His work reassured them. He captured many parts of life in Japan during his time. This includes fashionable people, busy city views, faraway landscapes, and scenes from nature. His beautiful bird-and-flower prints show his poetic feeling for nature. His landscapes reflect the growing interest in travel across Japan. Hiroshige showed his world as it was, but often as he imagined it could be.

CuratorialText: Possessed of remarkable technical skills, both as a colourist and draftsman, Hiroshige had a sympathetic regard for people from all walks of life. Unlike most other print designers of his day, he came from a samurai family, but crossed social boundaries to devote himself to depicting popular customs. His work was affordable, too – along with celebrated landscape prints, he also designed hundreds of hand-held, disposable fans that were available to all.
persona1_adapted: Hiroshige was a highly skilled artist. He was great at using color and drawing. He also had a kind and understanding view of people from all backgrounds. Unlike most other print artists of his time, he came from a samurai family. But he crossed social lines to focus on showing everyday customs. His work was affordable. Besides his famous landscape prints, he also made hundreds of hand-held fans. These fans were cheap and available to everyone.

CuratorialText: The exhibition featured prints, drawings, illustrated books and paintings from the British Museum collection, as well as a significant gift and loan of prints from Alan Medaugh, a major US collector of Hiroshige's work, and other important loans. As well as exploring Hiroshige's incredible body of work, this show considered his global legacy, which spans from Japan's Edo period (1615–1868) through to Vincent van Gogh and contemporary artists such as Julian Opie.
persona1_adapted: This exhibition includes prints, drawings, illustrated books, and paintings from the British Museum. It also features a major gift and loan of prints from Alan Medaugh, a top US collector of Hiroshige’s work. Other important loans are included too. The show explores Hiroshige’s amazing body of work. It also looks at his global legacy. That legacy stretches from Japan’s Edo period (1615–1868) to artists like Vincent van Gogh and modern artists such as Julian Opie.

CuratorialText: A stone figure of 'Hara-Pārvatī' (Śiva seated with Pārvatī on his knee). Also known as 'Umamahesvaramurti'. Accompanied by a lion and a bull (Nandi) and surrounded by many flying and standing attendants (musicians, garland-bearers), there is also a row of ritual equipment: two conch-shell trumpets on stands, a lamp and a bell.
persona1_adapted: This stone carving shows the god Shiva sitting with his wife, Parvati, on his knee. This type of artwork is called "Umamahesvaramurti." A lion and a bull named Nandi stand nearby. Many flying and standing attendants surround them. These attendants include musicians and people carrying garlands. Below them is a row of ritual objects: two conch-shell trumpets on stands, a lamp, and a bell.

CuratorialText: Pair of bride's slippers (tarkasin) made of red-dyed leather embroidered in silk thread and decorated with metal studs. Tongues cut in the shape of the khamsa ('five') or 'Hand of Fatima'.
persona1_adapted: These are a pair of a bride's slippers, called *tarkasin*. They are made from red-dyed leather. The slippers are embroidered with silk thread and decorated with metal studs. The tongues of the slippers are cut in the shape of the *khamsa* (which means 'five'), also known as the 'Hand of Fatima'.

CuratorialText: Head and upper body of pink/grey granite monumental statue of Ramses II (one of a pair placed before the door of the Ramesseum) wearing nemes head-cloth and circlet of uraei (about half now lost), the sculptor has exploited the bichrome nature of the stone to emphasise the division between body and face; the dorsal pillar is inscribed with vertical registers of hieroglyphs - giving the name and titles of the king and part of a dedication to Amun-Ra; in 1817 it was noted that there were traces of colour upon the statue and it may have, therefore, been painted red in antiquity.
persona1_adapted: This is the head and upper body of a large statue made of pink and gray granite. It shows Ramses II, an ancient Egyptian king. The statue was one of a pair placed in front of the Ramesseum, a temple built for the king. He wears a royal headdress called a nemes and a circlet of cobras, which is a symbol of power. About half of the cobras are now missing. The sculptor used the two colors of the stone to make a clear difference between the body and the face. On the back pillar, there are vertical rows of hieroglyphs. These carvings give the king’s name and titles, plus part of a dedication to the god Amun-Ra. In 1817, people noticed traces of color on the statue. This means it was probably painted red in ancient times.

CuratorialText: Head from monumental red granite statue of Amenhotep III(?).
persona1_adapted: This head is from a huge statue of a pharaoh named Amenhotep III. It is made of red granite.

CuratorialText: Base and lid of the anthropoid wooden inner coffin of Hornedjitef, son of Nekhthorheb, who held a large number of priestly offices. Polychrome painted and gilded face, wig, collar and pectoral, winged scarab across breast, body inscribed with vertical registers of painted hieroglyphs, flanked by deities, the rest of the surface is unelaborated. Interior of lid decorated with a figure of Nut, surrounded by astronomical images of decan stars and constellations. Interior of base also decorated.
persona1_adapted: This is the base and lid of the inner wooden coffin of Hornedjitef. He was the son of Nekhthorheb and held many important priestly jobs. The face, wig, collar, and chest piece are painted in bright colors and covered with gold. A winged scarab beetle sits across the chest. The body of the coffin has vertical rows of painted hieroglyphs, with gods on either side. The rest of the surface is plain. Inside the lid is a picture of the sky goddess Nut. She is surrounded by images of stars and star groups called decans. The inside of the base is also decorated.

CuratorialText: Brass (heavily leaded zinc-brass) head cast using the lost wax (cire perdue) technique. The head is a little under life size and is made in a naturalistic style. It has a headdress, suggesting a crown, of complex construction. The main tubular section of the crown runs around the head in a three-layer composition. The upper layer has a band of four horizontal rectangles representing flat, discoidal beads surmounted by a red-painted tubular bead and a tassel. The central layer has a row of vertical rectangles representing tubular beads with tassels. The lower layer has a row of rosettes painted in red. The main tubular section of the crown also has a projecting arc around the forehead comprising small tubular beads edged with a row of red-painted feathers. At the back of the crown is a neck cover. The central part of the cover has eighteen vertical elements incised to indicate plaitwork with traces of black paint. The bottom and sides have a row of red-painted rosettes. A crest rises above the central crown at the front. It comprises a conical roundel with central boss surrounded by seven concentric rings, probably representing beads. A plaitwork element rises behind the roundel, it terminates in a pointed ovoid tip; both elements bear traces of black paint. The face is slightly elongated and has vertical incised markings (striations). Two lines of holes run between the lobes of the ears, one going through the angle of the neck and the jaw, the second crossing the jaw below the lower lip. A double line of holes runs across the upper lip. The almond-shaped eyes are small in proportion to the head and the eyebrow-ridge is sharply defined. The lips are not striated. There are grooves round the front of the neck representing skin creases. There are holes pierced through the neck at the front and at both sides. There is a large irregular hole on the right proper side of the jaw.
persona1_adapted:
This head was made from a type of brass that contains a lot of lead. It was cast using the lost wax method, a technique where a wax model is covered in clay, heated to melt the wax out, and then filled with molten metal. The head is a bit smaller than life-size and looks very natural.

It wears a complex headdress that looks like a crown. The main part of the crown wraps around the head in three layers. The top layer has four flat, rectangular beads. Above them is a red-painted tube-shaped bead with a tassel. The middle layer has a row of vertical rectangles that look like tube-shaped beads with tassels. The bottom layer has a row of rosettes painted red.

A curved section of the crown sticks out over the forehead. It is made of small tube-shaped beads edged with red-painted feathers. At the back of the crown is a neck cover. Its center has eighteen vertical lines that suggest braiding, with traces of black paint. The bottom and sides of the cover have a row of red-painted rosettes.

A crest rises from the front of the crown. It has a cone-shaped circle with a central bump, surrounded by seven rings that probably represent beads. Behind this circle is a braided element that ends in a pointed oval tip. Both parts have traces of black paint.

The face is slightly long and has vertical carved lines. Two rows of holes run between the earlobes. One row goes through the angle of the neck and jaw. The other crosses the jaw below the lower lip. A double row of holes runs across the upper lip. The almond-shaped eyes are small compared to the head, and the eyebrow ridge is sharply defined. The lips have no carved lines. There are grooves around the front of the neck that look like skin creases. There are holes pierced through the front and both sides of the neck. A large, irregular hole is on the right side of the jaw.

CuratorialText: Goddess. A standing figure of a female deity (Tārā) solid cast in bronze and gilded. The figure has a high tubular coiffure (jatamukuta) held in place by a medallion flanked by makara-s (water creatures). The medallion was probably set with stones, as were the eyes. The earlobes are elongated and the lower body dressed in a tight-fitting cloth knotted at the hips. The upper body is uncovered. The proper right hand is in varadamudrā (gesture of giving), the left hand in katakahastamudrā (the hand pose wherein the tips of the fingers are applied to the thumb so as to form a ring, as if to hold a flower). The two middle fingers of the right hand are missing as are toes from both feet.
persona1_adapted:
This is a statue of a goddess named Tārā. She is a female deity. The statue is made of bronze and covered with a thin layer of gold. 

Her hair is styled high on her head like a tube. A decorated circle, or medallion, holds the hair in place. On each side of the medallion are makaras, which are mythical water creatures. The medallion and the eyes were probably set with precious stones.

Her earlobes are long. She wears a tight cloth around her lower body, tied at the hips. Her upper body is bare. 

Her right hand makes a gesture of giving. Her left hand is in a pose where the tips of her fingers touch her thumb, forming a ring, as if holding a flower. 

Two middle fingers on her right hand are missing. Some toes are also missing from both feet.

CuratorialText:
The Virgin and child: cartoon; the Virgin half-length, Christ standing with his arms around his mother
Black chalk and/or charcoal, touches of white chalk, pricked and indented for transfer, on two conjoined sheets; framed
persona1_adapted:
This drawing is called "The Virgin and Child." It is a full-size study, known as a cartoon, for a larger artwork. The Virgin Mary is shown from the waist up. Baby Jesus stands with his arms around his mother.

The artist used black chalk and charcoal to make the drawing. He added touches of white chalk for highlights. Tiny holes were pricked along the outlines. This helped the artist transfer the design onto another surface. The drawing is made on two sheets of paper joined together. It is framed.

CuratorialText: Marble torso of Nike, originally winged, wearing a sleeveless chiton; probably part of an akroterion.
persona1_adapted: This marble sculpture shows the upper body of Nike, the Greek goddess of victory. She was originally carved with wings. She wears a sleeveless tunic called a chiton. This piece was likely part of an akroterion, a decorative statue placed on the roof of a temple.

CuratorialText:
Fragment of a marble corner palmetto with bird foot.
The small fragment originates from the standing, rectangular snapped off corner palmetto above a volute acroterion. One both sides of the middle leave, which protrudes from the corner, three lightly curved and hollow shaped leaves are visible and whose edges are accompanied by a bar. The round endings were emphasised on the outer side by tubercle. Darts appear between the leaves. On the left side of the fragment remains of a curved ovolo moulding of the volute is preserved. The upper and back side show a small pentagonal block with a 45^ (degrees) outstanding, discs-like tunnel. Leaves with a convex back site seem to grow out of the tunnel. On the block and on both sides of the tunnels two claws are visible with bird legs on each the edges
On the darts on the left side traces of red paint was found on the uppermost and the third. Traces of red and blue paint.
The light crystalline marble is fairly sanded on the surface, however, the underside is fractured.
persona1_adapted:
This is a fragment of a marble corner decoration shaped like a palm leaf, with a bird’s foot.

The piece comes from a standing, rectangular corner palmetto. A palmetto is a carved decoration that looks like a palm leaf. This one sat above a volute acroterion, which is a curled ornament placed on the corner of a roof.

On both sides of the middle leaf, which sticks out from the corner, you can see three slightly curved, hollow-shaped leaves. Their edges have a raised bar. The round tips of the leaves were emphasized on the outside by small bumps called tubercles. Darts, or small pointed shapes, appear between the leaves.

On the left side of the fragment, part of a curved ovolo moulding is still visible. An ovolo is a rounded, egg-shaped molding. The upper and back sides show a small pentagonal block. This block has a disc-shaped tunnel sticking out at a 45-degree angle. Leaves with a curved back seem to grow out of this tunnel. On the block and on both sides of the tunnels, you can see two claws with bird legs at their edges.

On the darts on the left side, traces of red paint were found on the topmost and third darts. There are also traces of red and blue paint.

The light, crystalline marble is fairly smooth on the surface, but the underside is broken.

CuratorialText: Chess piece; walrus ivory; king: bearded; wearing floriated crown, tunic, mantle and brooch; seated upon high-backed chair holding sheathed sword; chair back: two horizontal compartments separated by zigzag band; upper filled with interlaced arcading of round arches; lower filled with symmetrical foliate ornaments; chair sides decorated with interlaced patterns.
persona1_adapted: This chess piece is made from walrus ivory. It shows a king with a beard. He wears a crown decorated with leaves, a tunic, a cloak, and a brooch. The king sits on a high-backed chair and holds a sheathed sword. The back of the chair has two horizontal sections separated by a zigzag band. The top section is filled with a pattern of connected round arches. The bottom section has matching leaf-like decorations. The sides of the chair are decorated with interwoven patterns.

CuratorialText: Egyptian terracotta model palmette acroterion. The upper corner broken from a miniature altar, decorated on each side with a half-palmette. The altar was made in a multiple mould, probably of two pieces, but perhaps more. Traces of a matt red slip survive, inside and out and signs of burning within. Brown Nile silt with a light-brown core, abundant fine gold mica and some white inclusions.
persona1_adapted: This is a small Egyptian decoration made from baked clay. It was shaped like a palm leaf and sat on the corner of a miniature altar. The altar was made using a mold, likely in two pieces. Traces of a dull red coating remain on the inside and outside. There are also signs of burning inside. The clay is brown Nile silt with a light-brown center. It contains lots of fine gold mica and some white specks.

CuratorialText: Indigo resist-dyed cloth (adire eleko); on factory-made cotton; two panels machine sewn tgether. Each panel is divided into four by seven squares. Hand-painted patterns include two squares with the pillars of Mapo Hall (in Ibadan) between which are four pestles (or spoons).
persona1_adapted: This cloth is called *adire eleko*, a type of indigo resist-dyed fabric. It is made from factory-made cotton. Two panels are machine-sewn together. Each panel is divided into four rows of seven squares. The patterns are hand-painted. Two squares show the pillars of Mapo Hall in Ibadan. Between them are four pestles, which look like spoons.

CuratorialText:
Ticket addressed to A. W. Franks to see Falero's painting 'Nightmare' at the Gainsborough Gallery, with image of a creature behind the ticket, with a bat's wing, claw and bone rib cage. 1888
Wood-engraving and letterpress
persona1_adapted: This ticket let A. W. Franks see Falero’s painting *Nightmare* at the Gainsborough Gallery. The ticket shows a creature with a bat’s wing, a claw, and a bony rib cage. It was made in 1888 using wood-engraving and letterpress.

CuratorialText:
