Ticket to see Frith's painting 'The Railway Station', with central image of a steam train arriving at the station with figures; empty space at the top to add name. 1865
Lithograph
persona1_adapted: This ticket lets you see Frith’s painting *The Railway Station*. The main image shows a steam train pulling into the station, surrounded by people. There is empty space at the top for a name. The ticket is from 1865. It is a lithograph, which is a type of print made from a stone or metal plate.

CuratorialText:
Paper admission ticket. (whole)
Photographs of, left to right, Klimt painting, the Leopold Museum and Shiele painting; inscription in black above: multicolour print on white background.
Inscription, in black print on white background.
persona1_adapted: This is a paper admission ticket. It has photographs of three things, from left to right: a painting by Klimt, the Leopold Museum, and a painting by Schiele. Above the photographs, there is black text printed on a white background. The ticket also has a colorful design printed on a white background.

CuratorialText:
Paper admission ticket. (whole)
Photograph, view of the Basilica Cistern in Istanbul showing columns and arched roof; inscription to right in black: multicolour print on pink and white background.
Unidentified historical painting showing forces gathered, tree to left: multicolour print on white background.
persona1_adapted:
This is a paper admission ticket. It shows a photograph of the Basilica Cistern in Istanbul. You can see the columns and arched roof. On the right side, there is black writing. The ticket is a multicolor print on a pink and white background.

This is an unidentified historical painting. It shows a group of soldiers gathered together. A tree is on the left side. The painting is a multicolor print on a white background.

CuratorialText:
View of the interior of a tent with visitors gathered before Copley's huge history painting (Guildhall Art Gallery), elaborately framed with pillars and swags, including two young men at left flirting with young women, and two older men at right seated with papers, figures at a desk behind at left; facsimile of the admission ticket to the exhibition, from an engraving by Bartolozzi. c.1850s-60s
Photomechincal reproduction
persona1_adapted: This picture shows the inside of a tent. Visitors are gathered in front of a huge history painting by Copley. The painting has an elaborate frame with pillars and fabric decorations. On the left, two young men are flirting with young women. On the right, two older men sit with papers. Behind them on the left, people work at a desk. The image also includes a copy of the exhibition's admission ticket. This ticket comes from an engraving by Bartolozzi. The picture was made around the 1850s to 1860s. It is a photomechanical reproduction, which means it was printed using a photographic process.

CuratorialText:
Admission ticket to a performance organised by the Guild of Literature and Art, with figure representing the painter Richard Wilson at left, taking a painting to a pawn shop, and at right figure representing Daniel Defoe, with a manuscript of 'Robinson Crusoe' in his pocket, leaving the office of the publisher Edmund Curll with a sad expression on his face, met by his worried wife and daughter; books and pallette with paintbrushes lying in foreground; after Ward. 1851
Mixed method, including etching, aquatint and engraving, on chine collé
persona1_adapted: This ticket was for a show put on by the Guild of Literature and Art. On the left, a man represents the painter Richard Wilson. He is taking a painting to a pawn shop. On the right, a man represents the writer Daniel Defoe. He has the manuscript for "Robinson Crusoe" in his pocket. Defoe is leaving the office of the publisher Edmund Curll. He looks sad. His worried wife and daughter meet him. Books and a palette with paintbrushes lie in the foreground. This print was made after a design by Ward in 1851. The artist used a mix of methods: etching, aquatint, and engraving. It is printed on thin paper glued to a thicker sheet.

CuratorialText:
Admission ticket to a performance organised by the Guild of Literature and Art, with figure representing the painter Richard Wilson at left, taking a painting to a pawn shop, and at right figure representing Daniel Defoe, with a manuscript of 'Robinson Crusoe' in his pocket, leaving the office of the publisher Edmund Curll with a sad expression on his face, met by his worried wife and daughter; books and pallette with paintbrushes lying in foreground; after Ward. 1851
Mixed method, including etching, aquatint and engraving, on chine collé
persona1_adapted: This ticket was for a show put on by the Guild of Literature and Art. On the left, you see the painter Richard Wilson. He is taking a painting to a pawn shop. On the right, you see the writer Daniel Defoe. He has the manuscript for "Robinson Crusoe" in his pocket. Defoe is leaving the office of the publisher Edmund Curll. He looks sad. His worried wife and daughter meet him. In the front of the scene, there are books and a palette with paintbrushes. This artwork is based on a design by Ward. It was made in 1851. The artist used a mix of methods: etching, aquatint, and engraving. It is printed on a thin paper called chine collé.

CuratorialText:
Admission ticket to a performance organised by the Guild of Literature and Art, with figure representing the painter Richard Wilson at left, taking a painting to a pawn shop, and at right figure representing Daniel Defoe, with a manuscript of 'Robinson Crusoe' in his pocket, leaving the office of the publisher Edmund Curll with a sad expression on his face, met by his worried wife and daughter; books and pallette with paintbrushes lying in foreground; after Ward. 1851
Mixed method, including etching, aquatint and engraving, on chine collé
persona1_adapted: This is an admission ticket for a show put on by the Guild of Literature and Art. On the left, a figure represents the painter Richard Wilson. He is taking a painting to a pawn shop. On the right, a figure represents the writer Daniel Defoe. He has the manuscript for *Robinson Crusoe* in his pocket. He is leaving the office of the publisher Edmund Curll with a sad look on his face. His worried wife and daughter meet him. Books and a palette with paintbrushes lie in the foreground. This piece was made after an artwork by Ward in 1851. It uses a mix of methods, including etching, aquatint, and engraving, on a type of paper called chine collé.

CuratorialText:
Ticket to a special exhibition at the South Kensington Museum, with signature by James Beck; am emblem on the top left with the letters VR and a crown above. 1862
Woodcut and letterpress
persona1_adapted: This is a ticket for a special exhibition at the South Kensington Museum. It has a signature by James Beck. In the top left corner, there is an emblem with the letters "VR" and a crown above them. The ticket is from 1862. It was made using woodcut and letterpress printing.

CuratorialText:
Advertisement for "Aspinall's Enamel"; Princess Alexandra, full length, seated to right, painting a stool with Aspinall's Enamel; illustration to "The Queen, the Lady's Newspaper" (3 December, 1887).
Wood engraving
persona1_adapted: This is an ad for "Aspinall's Enamel." It shows Princess Alexandra sitting and painting a stool with the product. She is shown from head to toe, facing to the right. This picture was made for a newspaper called "The Queen, the Lady's Newspaper" on December 3, 1887. The artwork is a wood engraving, which is a type of print made by carving into a block of wood.

CuratorialText: Large glazed and framed oil painting on canvas depicting a swirling abstract composition dominated by crimson on a dark background. The central bright design has areas of thickly applied paint while in the black background the contracting of the paint surface has produced a textured effect.
persona1_adapted: This large oil painting on canvas is covered in glass and set in a frame. It shows a swirling, abstract design. The main color is bright red against a dark background. In the center, the paint is very thick. On the black parts, the paint has shrunk as it dried, creating a bumpy, textured surface.

CuratorialText: Painting. Oil painting on canvas. The adoration of the Śālagrāma is shown in the middle of the picture. To the left, is Balakṛṣṇa holding the butter ball. On the right, a female figure holds a parasol over a central throne which suggests an aniconic reverence to Viṣṇu, also suggested by the large conch positioned centrally in the foreground. Other ritual objects typically used in 'puja' can be seen too: a copper water-pourer and a bell.
persona1_adapted: This is an oil painting on canvas. In the center, people are worshiping a sacred stone called a Śālagrāma. On the left, the god Balakṛṣṇa holds a butter ball. On the right, a woman holds a parasol over an empty throne. This suggests that people are honoring the god Viṣṇu without showing his image. A large conch shell in the front also points to Viṣṇu. You can see other objects used in 'puja,' or Hindu worship: a copper water-pourer and a bell.

CuratorialText: Portrait of Lady Layard, wife of Henry Layard; oil painting on canvas in a carved and gilded wood frame. Lady Layard is seated on the balcony of Ca Capello, that Layards' house in Venice, overlooking the Grand Canal. She wears a closely-fitting belted red jacket and a black skirt with an enormously long train; she holds a fan in her right hand and leans on the balcony balustrade. Open French doors set with glass panels give onto an interior with potted plants; in the distance is a view across the Grand Canal.
persona1_adapted: Portrait of Lady Layard, wife of Henry Layard. It is an oil painting on canvas inside a carved and gold-painted wooden frame. Lady Layard sits on the balcony of Ca Capello, the Layards' house in Venice, which overlooks the Grand Canal. She wears a tight red jacket with a belt and a black skirt with a very long train. In her right hand, she holds a fan. She leans on the balcony railing. Open French doors with glass panels lead into a room with potted plants. In the distance, you can see a view across the Grand Canal.

CuratorialText: Painting in mixed media (?oil pencil) of a bearded head and shoulders of a male figure. This is one from the series 'Telingana Faces'. The figure wears a square amulet around his neck. Signed and dated by the artist. Mounted
persona1_adapted: This is a painting of a man’s head and shoulders. He has a beard. The artist used mixed media, meaning more than one material, possibly oil pencil. This work is part of a series called “Telingana Faces.” The man wears a square amulet around his neck. The artist signed and dated the piece. It is mounted.

CuratorialText: Portrait of Voltaire (1694-1778), T.Q.L., standing, body nearly in profile to r., head turned half r., and eyes looking full, wearing dark green coat trimmed with gold lace, and holding book in his l. hand.
persona1_adapted: This is a portrait of Voltaire, a famous writer and thinker who lived from 1694 to 1778. He stands with his body turned mostly to the right. His head is turned partway in that direction, and his eyes look straight ahead. He wears a dark green coat decorated with gold lace. In his left hand, he holds a book.

CuratorialText:
Painting; portrait of Se-Tan-Si-Tan, also known as Yellow Hawk a Dakota Sans Arc Chief, sitting. Wearing three feathers in his hair, ear and neck -ornaments, decorated shirt with feathers on upper sleeves and blanket wrap; holding a Pipe-tomahawk.
Oil on canvas
persona1_adapted: This painting shows a portrait of Se-Tan-Si-Tan, also called Yellow Hawk. He was a chief of the Dakota Sans Arc people. He sits for the portrait. He wears three feathers in his hair. He also has ear and neck ornaments. His shirt is decorated with feathers on the upper sleeves. He is wrapped in a blanket. He holds a pipe-tomahawk. The artist used oil paint on canvas.

CuratorialText:
Portrait of Johann Gutenberg (c. 1398-1468), H.L., turning half-r., the eyes looking full, wearing a cap, holding a wood block with the letters of the alphabet A-I.
Oil painting on canvas
persona1_adapted: This portrait shows Johann Gutenberg (about 1398-1468). He is turned halfway to the right, but his eyes look straight at us. He wears a cap and holds a wood block with the letters A through I carved into it. The artist painted this using oil paint on canvas.

CuratorialText:
Portrait of Isabella Clara Eugenia of Austria (1566-1633), Infanta of Spain, half-length turned slightly to left, wearing white dress trimmed with red rosettes etc., large ruff, and jewelled headdress; after Frans Pourbus II
Oil painting on canvas
persona1_adapted: This is a portrait of Isabella Clara Eugenia of Austria. She lived from 1566 to 1633 and was an Infanta of Spain, meaning she was a Spanish princess. In the painting, she is shown from the waist up and turned slightly to the left. She wears a white dress decorated with red rosettes, a large ruff (a stiff, frilly collar), and a jeweled headdress. This artwork is a copy of an original painting by Frans Pourbus II. It is an oil painting on canvas.

CuratorialText:
Portrait of Augustus II ('The Strong') (1670-1733), King of Poland and Elector of Saxony, three-quarter length, standing, body turned half-right, head half-left, wearing armour and ermine-trimmed cloak, and holding sceptre.
Oil painting on canvas
persona1_adapted: This painting shows Augustus II, also known as "The Strong." He was the King of Poland and Elector of Saxony, living from 1670 to 1733. In the portrait, he stands and faces slightly to the right, but his head turns a bit to the left. He wears armor and a cloak trimmed with ermine fur. In his hand, he holds a royal scepter. The artist used oil paint on canvas to create this work.

CuratorialText:
Portrait said to be of Mary Tudor (1516-58) as Princess; Head and shoulders, 3/4 face, eyes looking full. Wearing ruff, earring, embroidered dress, ?chain, headdress.
Oil painting on canvas
persona1_adapted: This portrait is believed to show Mary Tudor (1516–58) as a princess. She is shown from the head and shoulders, with her face turned slightly to the side. Her eyes look straight at the viewer. She wears a ruff, an earring, and an embroidered dress. She also has a chain and a headdress. The artist used oil paint on canvas.

CuratorialText: Portrait of Jacques (aka Jean, John, James) Rousseau (1630-93), Oval, head and shoulders turned half-r., eyes looking full, wearing dark coat and lace cravat. Oil on canvas.
persona1_adapted: This is a portrait of Jacques Rousseau. He lived from 1630 to 1693. In the painting, his head and shoulders are turned slightly to the right. His eyes look straight at the viewer. He wears a dark coat and a lace necktie. The artist used oil paint on canvas.

CuratorialText:
Portrait of Dr Robert G W Anderson. Head, profile, facing to sitter's right
Oil painting on panel. The subject is looking out of a window in the artist's studio at Talfourd Road, Peckham. Superimposed on the window is a section of the Sloane astrolabe. The tie is a version of the Great Court ties sold at the British Museum around the end of 2000, to mark the opening of the Great Court.
persona1_adapted: This is a portrait of Dr. Robert G. W. Anderson. It shows his head and profile, facing to the right. The painting is oil on a wooden panel. The subject looks out of a window in the artist’s studio on Talfourd Road in Peckham. Over the window, the artist added a section of the Sloane astrolabe. An astrolabe is an old tool used to measure the stars. The tie Dr. Anderson wears is a version of the Great Court ties. The British Museum sold these ties around the end of 2000 to celebrate the opening of the Great Court.

CuratorialText: Oil painting on canvas stretched and glued to back of stained wooden frame. Painting shows scene of an imaginary beach side village with huts and trees in the foreground, ocean and sky with full moon behind. Painting is signed.
persona1_adapted: This painting is made with oil paint on canvas. The canvas is stretched and glued to the back of a stained wooden frame. It shows an imaginary beachside village. In the front, there are huts and trees. Behind them, you can see the ocean and a sky with a full moon. The painting is signed by the artist.

CuratorialText: Oil painting; commercial sign-board made of plywood, used to advertise barbers' shop hairstyles. Six individual heads are depicted in profile, each numbered and displaying a different haircut. The heads face in different directions and are painted in bold black outlines on a white background; each head has a blue collar.
persona1_adapted: This is an oil painting on a plywood sign. The sign was used to advertise hairstyles at a barbershop. It shows six heads in profile, or from the side. Each head has a number and a different haircut. The heads face different directions. They are painted with bold black outlines on a white background. Each head also has a blue collar.

CuratorialText: A large oil painting on canvas depicting the slaying of the dragon by St George who is the central figure of the painting, mounted on a white horse and carrying a circular shield in one hand, a spear in the other. The dragon, with striped skin and small spotted wings, lies on its back beneath the horse's hooves, together with a little blue vanquished devil, his face in profile. To the right of the painting Biruwit is depicted in the branches of a tree; to the left stand a group of soldiers armed with spears and shields.
persona1_adapted: This large oil painting on canvas shows Saint George killing a dragon. George is the main figure in the painting. He rides a white horse and holds a round shield in one hand and a spear in the other. The dragon has striped skin and small spotted wings. It lies on its back under the horse's hooves. A small blue defeated devil lies there too, with his face shown from the side. On the right side of the painting, Biruwit is shown in the branches of a tree. On the left side, a group of soldiers stands holding spears and shields.

CuratorialText: Oil painting; wooden frame painted brown; matte of white mesh; at centre is miniature oil painting on white background with brown matte around it; painting shows a matador waving a red cape in front of a bull. Written on back: "El Paso del Desden: 1.8 cm x 2 cm ozeo (?) s/ P. Ingres Fabr. Guillermo Calaveo Lazaro 1 217 Oct de 1985"
persona1_adapted: This is an oil painting inside a wooden frame painted brown. The painting has a white mesh matte around it. At the center is a tiny oil painting on a white background, with a brown matte border. The painting shows a matador waving a red cape in front of a bull. On the back, it says: "El Paso del Desden: 1.8 cm x 2 cm ozeo (?) s/ P. Ingres Fabr. Guillermo Calaveo Lazaro 1 217 Oct de 1985."

CuratorialText:
Painting; pouches made from the skin of small animals; used for carrying tobacco, smoking barks and weeds. plate 13.
Oil on cardboard
persona1_adapted: This painting shows pouches made from the skin of small animals. People used them to carry tobacco, smoking barks, and weeds. It is labeled as plate 13. The artist painted it with oil on cardboard.

CuratorialText:
Painting; three Javanese men; wearing head-dress, shirt and sarong. Man at the front has a kris or sword, secured to his back with a waist wrap. House and trees are in the background.
oil on canvas
persona1_adapted: This painting shows three Javanese men. They wear head-dresses, shirts, and sarongs. The man in front has a kris, a type of sword, tied to his back with a cloth around his waist. A house and trees are in the background. The artist used oil paint on canvas.

CuratorialText:
Painting; full length portrait a Javanese woman; wearing head, neck, arm ornament and designed costume. People, trees and two thatched buildings are in the background.
oil on canvas
persona1_adapted: This painting shows a full-length portrait of a Javanese woman. She wears ornaments on her head, neck, and arms, along with a decorated costume. In the background, you can see people, trees, and two thatched buildings. The artist used oil paint on canvas.

CuratorialText:
Painting; full length portrait of a Javanese man holding a spear; wearing a helmet, striped shirt and leggings and sarong with waist wrap. He carries a kris either side of his waist wrap and a sword hanging on his left side. Trees and buildings, one with a large flag are in the background.
oil on canvas
persona1_adapted: This painting shows a full-length portrait of a Javanese man holding a spear. He wears a helmet, a striped shirt, leggings, and a sarong with a cloth wrapped around his waist. On each side of his waist wrap, he carries a kris, which is a type of traditional dagger. A sword also hangs on his left side. In the background, you can see trees and buildings. One of the buildings has a large flag. The artist used oil paint on canvas.

CuratorialText:
Painting; portrait of Wunagisa, also known as Moses Keokuk, Jr; was principal Chief of the Sacs & Fox Nation; wearing headdress with feathers, ear-rings, necklaces, one made of bear claws, shirt and a decorated blanket wrap; holding a Pipe-tomahawk and fan made of feathers.
Oil on canvas
persona1_adapted: This painting is a portrait of Wunagisa, also known as Moses Keokuk, Jr. He was the principal chief of the Sacs & Fox Nation. In the painting, he wears a feathered headdress, earrings, and necklaces. One necklace is made of bear claws. He also wears a shirt and a decorated blanket wrap. In his hands, he holds a pipe-tomahawk and a fan made of feathers. The artwork is oil on canvas.

CuratorialText:
Painting; from a collection of seventeen. An Aboriginal Australian woman from Spring Creek, near Port Fairy, Victoria; wearing head-band and possum skin cloak.
Oil on canvas.
persona1_adapted: This painting is part of a set of seventeen. It shows an Aboriginal Australian woman from Spring Creek, near Port Fairy, Victoria. She wears a headband and a cloak made from possum skin. The artist used oil paint on canvas.

CuratorialText:

Oil on canvas
persona1_adapted: This is a painting of a Native North American chief. We do not know his name. He wears two feathers in his hair and a neck ornament. His shirt has a decorated frill. A blanket is wrapped around him. He holds a pipe-tomahawk and a tobacco pouch. The artist used oil paint on canvas.

CuratorialText:
Painting; side view of a Javanese soldier holding a kris and circular shield; wearing head-dress, top garment and serong, with a kris-sheath secure to his back with his waist wrap. People, trees and the top of a building are in the background.
oil on canvas
persona1_adapted: This painting shows a Javanese soldier from the side. He holds a kris, which is a traditional dagger, and a round shield. He wears a headdress, a top garment, and a cloth called a serong. A sheath for his kris is tucked into the cloth wrapped around his waist. People, trees, and the top of a building appear in the background. The artist used oil paint on canvas.

CuratorialText:
Painting; five decorated pipe bowls featuring men, bears, and a woman; below is a decorated tomahawk pipe. plate 8.
Oil on cardboard
persona1_adapted: This painting shows five decorated pipe bowls. They are carved with images of men, bears, and a woman. Below the bowls is a decorated tomahawk pipe. This artwork is labeled "plate 8." It was made with oil paint on cardboard.

CuratorialText:
Framed painting; portrait of Sina Kavaliku, seated on the ground, wearing a western-style dress, her hair in plaits and a flower in her hair. Tonga. 1951.
Oil on canvas.
persona1_adapted: This is a framed oil painting on canvas from 1951. It shows a woman named Sina Kavaliku sitting on the ground. She wears a Western-style dress. Her hair is in braids, and she has a flower in her hair. The painting is from Tonga.

CuratorialText: Pottery Protocorinthian alabastron decorated with a lion and two griffins; wheel-made; depressed-ovoid body on a rounded base; small mouth with a sloping rim; pierced lug handle at the top; made of fine buff clay and slip, with painted decoration which has fired black; also added purple paint on the animal bodies; details of the figures are incised. The main frieze shows two griffins separated by stylised tree-like motif; a lion occupies the space between the two mythical beasts; filling motifs in the field. Above the main scene is a frieze of stylised floral designs; below simpler motifs composed of dots separated by S-shaped strokes. Simple rays on the vase, and tongues on the lip.
persona1_adapted:
This small pottery bottle, called an alabastron, was made in ancient Corinth. It has a round, egg-shaped body and a tiny mouth with a slanted rim. A small handle sticks out from the top. The artist used fine, light-colored clay and added a painted design that turned black when fired. Purple paint was also added to the animals' bodies, and details were scratched into the clay.

The main band of decoration shows two griffins—mythical creatures with the head of an eagle and body of a lion. Between them is a tree-like design. A lion stands between the two griffins. Small filler shapes fill the empty space. Above this scene is a band of flower-like patterns. Below are simpler designs made of dots and S-shaped lines. Simple ray patterns decorate the vase, and tongue shapes appear on the lip.

CuratorialText: IWM holds approximately 11 million photographs of modern conflict from the First World War to present day.
persona1_adapted: IWM has about 11 million photos showing modern conflicts, from World War I to today.

CuratorialText: The collection covers the activities of British and Commonwealth forces and international perspectives. Photographs from official, press, and private perspectives such as soldiers, nurses and civilians who participated in or witnessed conflict reveal the human impact of war. The collection includes notable photographers such as Olive Edis, Bill Brandt and Tim Hetherington.
persona1_adapted: This collection shows what British and Commonwealth forces did during wars. It also includes views from around the world. Photos come from official sources, newspapers, and private collections. Soldiers, nurses, and regular people took or appeared in these pictures. They saw war up close. Their photos reveal how war affects people. The collection features famous photographers like Olive Edis, Bill Brandt, and Tim Hetherington.

CuratorialText: The photographs can be viewed alongside original documents which help us understand how and why these photographs were taken.
persona1_adapted: You can look at the photographs next to original documents. These documents help us understand how and why the photos were taken.

CuratorialText:
Uniforms, Flags, Badges and Insignia
An important and international collection of flags and uniforms, the latter representing all of the armed services and institutions of not only Great Britain and the Commonwealth, but Allies as well as former enemies along with their associated badges and insignia.
persona1_adapted:
Uniforms, Flags, Badges, and Insignia

This is a large collection of flags and uniforms from around the world. The uniforms come from all branches of the military and other institutions. They represent not just Great Britain and the Commonwealth, but also allies and former enemies. Each uniform comes with its own badges and insignia.

CuratorialText:
Mementoes and Miscellanea
A diverse collection containing objects of personal significance, including escape and evasion items, prisoner of war and civilian internee items, trench art, battlefield souvenirs, commemorative objects, foodstuffs, currency, tobacciana, toys and games, Kindertransport and Holocaust related items, home-front and peace-time items.
persona1_adapted:
Mementos and Miscellanea

This collection holds many different objects that were important to people. It includes items used to escape or avoid capture, things from prisoners of war and civilian camps, and art made by soldiers in the trenches. You'll also find battlefield souvenirs, objects made to remember events, food, money, tobacco-related items, toys, and games. Some objects relate to the Kindertransport and the Holocaust. Others show what life was like on the home front and during peacetime.

CuratorialText:
Orders, Medals and Decorations
An important collection comprising orders, medal and decorations issued to service personnel and civilians.
persona1_adapted:
Orders, Medals, and Decorations  
This collection holds important awards given to military members and civilians. It includes orders, medals, and decorations.

CuratorialText:
Equipment
An extensive collection of equipment used in twentieth and twenty-first-century warfare, including radios, cipher machines, radars, cameras, medical equipment and even pigeon baskets.
persona1_adapted: This collection has a wide range of military equipment from the 1900s and 2000s. You will find radios, code-making machines, radars, and cameras. There is also medical gear and even baskets used to carry pigeons.

CuratorialText:
Weapons and Ammunition
A major reference collection that includes firearms, edged weapons, trench clubs or concussive weapons, ordnance and munitions.
persona1_adapted:
**Weapons and Ammunition**

This is a large collection of weapons and ammunition. It includes guns, knives, and clubs used in trench warfare. You will also find cannons, bombs, and other military supplies.

CuratorialText:
Vehicles, Aircraft and Ships
An internationally renowned collection including models, battle tanks, maritime equipment and aircraft components. Most of the collection of artillery and large military equipment is on show at IWM London or IWM Duxford.
persona1_adapted:
Vehicles, Aircraft, and Ships

This collection is famous around the world. It includes models, battle tanks, ship equipment, and aircraft parts. Most of the artillery and large military equipment is on display at IWM London or IWM Duxford.

CuratorialText: IWM’s Sound Collection is home to over 35,000 recordings relating to conflicts since 1914.  It is the largest oral history archive of its kind in the world, with more than 60,000 hours of material and interviews in over 15 different languages.  Beyond interviews it also includes speeches, sound effects, radio broadcasts and music. The collection covers the wartime experiences of ordinary men and women- from soldiers and activists, to factory workers and journalists. It also includes notable figures such as Field Marshal Bernard Montgomery and Vera Lynn.  Our unique collection of oral history interviews has formed the basis for significant academic research as well as featuring heavily in bestselling books, films, TV and radio broadcasts and podcasts. Many thousands of our audio recordings can be listened to via the IWM website, which is regularly updated with new additions.
persona1_adapted: IWM’s Sound Collection holds over 35,000 recordings about conflicts since 1914. It is the largest oral history archive of its kind in the world. Oral history means people tell their own stories out loud. The collection has more than 60,000 hours of material. It includes interviews in over 15 different languages. Besides interviews, it also has speeches, sound effects, radio shows, and music. The collection shares the wartime experiences of everyday people. This includes soldiers, activists, factory workers, and journalists. It also features famous figures like Field Marshal Bernard Montgomery and Vera Lynn. Our unique oral history interviews have helped shape important academic research. They also appear in bestselling books, movies, TV and radio shows, and podcasts. You can listen to thousands of these recordings on the IWM website. The site adds new recordings regularly.

CuratorialText:
IWM has managed a Film Archive for over a century. From its very beginnings as an institution IWM understood the importance of film as primary source material and its extensive collection illustrates the causes, course and consequences of conflict from the First World War to the present day. A remarkable resource for researchers and film-makers
Our constantly growing collection now extends to over 25,000 hours of moving images, representing a wide and diverse range of material from public and service information films, documentaries and unedited combat film, through to official newsreels and amateur films. Two substantial collections from NATO and the United Nations in the former Yugoslavia are also held. An ongoing film digitisation programme provides free online access, with newly-digitised media added as soon as it becomes available.
persona1_adapted:
IWM has run a Film Archive for over 100 years. From the very start, IWM knew that film is important as a primary source—a firsthand record of events. Its large collection shows the causes, course, and results of conflict from World War I to today. It is a great resource for researchers and filmmakers.

Our growing collection now includes more than 25,000 hours of moving images. It covers a wide range of material, such as public information films, service films, documentaries, unedited combat footage, official newsreels, and amateur films. We also hold two large collections from NATO and the United Nations in the former Yugoslavia. An ongoing film digitization program gives free online access. Newly digitized media is added as soon as it becomes available.

CuratorialText:
Private Papers
IWM holds over 20,000 individual collections of private papers, principally comprising unpublished diaries, letters and memoirs written primarily by British and Commonwealth servicemen and women since 1914 and by civilians mainly during the two world wars. We also hold many important documents from victims and survivors of the Holocaust.
persona1_adapted:
Private Papers

IWM keeps more than 20,000 personal collections. These are mostly unpublished diaries, letters, and memoirs. British and Commonwealth soldiers and nurses wrote them since 1914. Civilians wrote them mainly during the two world wars. We also hold important documents from people who survived or were victims of the Holocaust.

CuratorialText:
Foreign and Related Documents
We also look after a large collection of foreign documents, including the official British records of the major war crimes trials conducted at Nuremberg and Tokyo, together with related documents from the Second World War which mainly concern the German and Japanese war efforts.
persona1_adapted: We also take care of a big collection of documents from other countries. This includes the official British records of the major war crimes trials held in Nuremberg and Tokyo. It also has related papers from World War II that mostly deal with the German and Japanese war efforts.

CuratorialText: This exceptional collection is one of the most important representations of twentieth century British art in the world. It includes many great works of art from the British government war art schemes of the First and Second World Wars, which employed the greatest artists of their day, including leaders of the avant garde. These included Paul Nash, C R W Nevinson, John Singer Sargent and Sir William Orpen.  IWM also holds the unique War Artists Archive, which reveals the day-to-day running of the war art schemes and gives fascinating glimpses of the artists' experiences.
persona1_adapted: This collection is one of the most important displays of 20th-century British art in the world. It includes many great artworks from British government war art programs during World War I and World War II. These programs hired the best artists of their time, including leaders of the avant-garde—artists who tried new, bold styles. These artists included Paul Nash, C R W Nevinson, John Singer Sargent, and Sir William Orpen. IWM also holds the unique War Artists Archive. It shows how the war art programs ran day to day and gives fascinating glimpses into the artists' experiences.

CuratorialText: Our Library collection consists of over 150,000 items covering twentieth and twenty-first century conflict involving British and Commonwealth countries. Along with material on military units and campaign histories, we have biographies, manuals, items relating to the economic, social, and cultural aspects of war, journals, newspapers, ephemera, propaganda, and e-resources.
persona1_adapted: Our library has more than 150,000 items. They cover wars from the 1900s to today that involved Britain and Commonwealth countries. We have books about military units and battle histories. You can also find biographies, manuals, and materials about the economic, social, and cultural sides of war. The collection includes journals, newspapers, ephemera (items meant to be used briefly, like posters or tickets), propaganda, and online resources.

CuratorialText: image: HMS Laurentic is seen sinking in the background, with half of the ship submerged and her bow rising up out of the water. In the foreground is a lifeboat full of sailors illuminated against the dark by a small light. Another lifeboat is visible on the right and small dots of light in the background indicate further lifeboats. In the background on the left, the top of the German U-boat is visible. HMS Laurentic, originally a P&O liner, was equipped with 6" guns in 1939 and converted into an armed merchant cruiser. She was Worsley's first ship and he remained with her until she was sunk off Foreland, Ulster by the U-boat captain Otto Kretschmer (U-99) on the night of 3 November 1940. Worsley painted the original immediatly afterwards, while on survivors leave. The WAAC bought the picture and in August 1942 sent it to South America in a touring exhibition of war art. On the way, however, the ship was torpedoed and the present picture was painted as a replacement, at the request of the WAAC. (From exhibition catalogue, Burstow Gallery, Brighton, 1995)
persona1_adapted:
In this painting, you can see HMS Laurentic sinking in the background. Half of the ship is underwater, and its bow rises up out of the sea. In the front, a lifeboat full of sailors is lit by a small light against the dark night. Another lifeboat appears on the right. Small dots of light in the background show more lifeboats. On the left side, the top of a German U-boat (a type of submarine) is visible.

HMS Laurentic was first a passenger ship for the P&O company. In 1939, it was fitted with 6-inch guns and turned into an armed merchant cruiser. This was the first ship the artist, Worsley, served on. He stayed with it until it was sunk off the coast of Foreland, Ulster, by the German U-boat captain Otto Kretschmer on the night of November 3, 1940.

Worsley painted the original picture right after the event, while on leave as a survivor. The War Artists Advisory Committee (WAAC) bought the painting. In August 1942, they sent it to South America for a traveling exhibition of war art. But the ship carrying it was torpedoed on the way. So, at the WAAC’s request, Worsley painted this replacement version.

CuratorialText:
Object description
image: A reconstruction of the sinking of a number of merchant ships by German U-boats in the North Atlantic. Merchant sailors and debris from a destroyed ship are in the water in the foreground. persona1_adapted: This image shows what happened when German submarines attacked merchant ships in the North Atlantic. In the front, you can see sailors and wreckage floating in the water. To the right, a ship is sinking. Its back end is underwater, and its front end points up toward the sky. In the background, enemy fire hits other merchant ships.

CuratorialText: A rough for a furniture mural. whole: text is split between left, middle and right side, with separate images associated with each. image: (left) blueprint illustrating furniture with aircraft, tanks and ships in background. (middle) various types of furniture arranged over a blueprint of a house with factories in the background. (right) birds eye view of a port, mainly in yellow, with modern buildings and furniture floating above the buildings. text: FROM WAR PRODUCTION THROUGH AUSTERITY TO BETTER TIMES
persona1_adapted:
This is a rough sketch for a mural about furniture. The text is split into three parts: left, middle, and right. Each part has its own image.

On the left, there is a blueprint. It shows furniture with aircraft, tanks, and ships in the background.

In the middle, different types of furniture sit on top of a house blueprint. Factories are in the background.

On the right, there is a bird's-eye view of a port. The port is mostly yellow. Modern buildings and furniture float above the buildings.

The text reads: "From war production through austerity to better times."

CuratorialText: image: A view across a tree-lined road to a high wall bordering a railway cutting. The wall has grafitti on it which reads 'Nuclear state = Police state' followed by an anarchy symbol and then 'One World'. Behind the wall to the right is a large industrial building. Further to the right is a junction with a road leading off to the left.'In the summer of 1983 ... I discovered the old train station in Mildmay Grove and started what became a set of ten drawings of the section of line between Canonbury and Kingsland Road. It was while doing the second drawing that I realized the line I was looking at was used several times a week to take nuclear waste from Sizewell [nuclear power station] to Sellafield [nuclear fuel reprocessing plant]. Over the next six weeks, I found it impossible to escape that realization, and it inextricably meshed with every mark I made.' [Eric Rimmington, from the exhibition catalogue 'A World's Waste: Cumbria, Sellafield and Nuclear Reprocessing, A Brewery Arts Centre Touring Exhibition' 1987.] The artist made this series of drawings at a time of intense anti-nuclear protests against both nuclear weapons and the nuclear power industry. The 1979 decision by the British Government to allow American Cruise Missiles to be sited in the UK sparked a series of marches and protests. These included the Greenham Common Women's Peace Camp, started in 1981 and which was at its height in 1983 when the missiles finally arrived at the Berkshire base. The accident at Three Mile Island, a nuclear power station in Pennsylvania, USA, also in 1979, heightened fears about the safety of nuclear power. Nuclear waste and its transport and storage continues to be a focus of protest and anxiety. The Greater London Assembly commissioned a report into the safety of nuclear waste trains in 2001 for this reason.
persona1_adapted:
This drawing shows a view across a tree-lined road. On the other side is a high wall next to a railway cutting. The wall has graffiti that says "Nuclear state = Police state." It also shows an anarchy symbol and the words "One World." Behind the wall on the right is a large industrial building. Further right is a road junction with a road leading off to the left.

In the summer of 1983, the artist Eric Rimmington found an old train station in Mildmay Grove. He started making ten drawings of the railway line between Canonbury and Kingsland Road. While working on the second drawing, he realized that this train line was used several times a week. It carried nuclear waste from Sizewell nuclear power station to Sellafield, a nuclear fuel reprocessing plant. Over the next six weeks, he could not stop thinking about this fact. It became mixed into every mark he made.

Rimmington created this series during a time of strong anti-nuclear protests. People were protesting against both nuclear weapons and nuclear power. In 1979, the British Government decided to let American Cruise Missiles be placed in the UK. This decision sparked many marches and protests. One example was the Greenham Common Women's Peace Camp, which started in 1981. It was at its peak in 1983 when the missiles finally arrived at the Berkshire base.

Also in 1979, an accident happened at Three Mile Island, a nuclear power station in Pennsylvania, USA. This accident made people more afraid about the safety of nuclear power. Nuclear waste, along with how it is transported and stored, continues to cause protest and worry. In 2001, the Greater London Assembly ordered a report on the safety of nuclear waste trains for this reason.

CuratorialText:
Object description
image: Five children are seated around a table in room lit by a large window. An elderly woman sits at the centre with the youngest child on her lap. A large 'Spotted-Dick' pudding is placed on the table with several bowls and cutlery set around it. The woman serves a boy on the far right who holds out a bowl for his portion.
Label
'Mrs Norris, of Cookham, with five of her war family of seven children from London who had by January of 1941 been with her for sixteen months. Mrs Norris is the ideal foster-parent, and there are very many other householders like her. Over a million children have been moved in school parties from the evacuation areas, and with such large numbers, boarding out in billets must always be the principal means of finding homes for them. Given Mrs Norris' friendliness and care, town children readily adapt themselves to their new surroundings.' (title from folder cover, Children in Wartime).
persona1_adapted:
**Object Description**
Five children sit around a table in a room lit by a large window. An elderly woman sits in the middle with the youngest child on her lap. A large "Spotted-Dick" pudding sits on the table with bowls and forks around it. The woman serves a boy on the far right. He holds out a bowl for his portion.

**Label**
This photo shows Mrs. Norris of Cookham. She is with five of the seven children from London who were staying with her during the war. By January 1941, they had been with her for sixteen months. Mrs. Norris is the ideal foster parent. Many other householders were like her. Over a million children had been moved from evacuation areas in school groups. With so many children, placing them in private homes was the main way to find them places to stay. Because Mrs. Norris was friendly and caring, town children quickly got used to their new surroundings.

CuratorialText: image: portrait of a female factory worker operating a lathe. Miss Loftus had been brought to the attention of the War Artist's Advisory Committee as 'an outstanding factory worker'. Knight expected to do a studio portrait but the Ministry of Supply requested that she be painted at work in the Royal Ordnance Factory in Newport. Making a Bofors Breech ring was considered the most highly skilled job in the factory, normally requiring eight or nine years training. Loftus was aged 21 at the time of the painting and had no previous factory experience. Her ability to operate the machine presented a considerable publicity coup at the time. However, it has been suggested that she was placed at the machine precisely for this reason. Industrial machinery was a wholly new element in Knight's work but her technical accuracy was praised in contemporary reports: Knight, like Loftus, was proving herself in a traditionally male environment. Loftus was an outstanding factory worker who had mastered complex engineering skills in a very short space of time, and Knight was commissioned to paint her at work in the factory. Knight was fascinated by circus artistes and dancers, and she emphasises the balance and posture of her subject at work. Industrial machinery was a wholly new element in Knight's work but her technical accuracy was praised in contemporary reports: Knight, like Loftus, was proving herself in a traditionally male environment.
persona1_adapted:
This painting shows a young woman named Miss Loftus operating a lathe, a machine that shapes metal. She was known as an "outstanding factory worker." The artist, Laura Knight, first planned to paint a studio portrait. But the Ministry of Supply asked her to paint Loftus at work in the Royal Ordnance Factory in Newport.

Making a Bofors Breech ring was the most skilled job in the factory. It normally required eight or nine years of training. Loftus was only 21 years old and had no previous factory experience. Her ability to run the machine was a big win for publicity at the time. Some people suggest she was placed at the machine just for this reason.

Industrial machinery was new to Knight’s work. But her technical accuracy was praised in reports from that time. Like Loftus, Knight was proving herself in a field usually dominated by men. Knight was fascinated by circus performers and dancers. In this painting, she highlights the balance and posture of her subject at work.

CuratorialText: image: A view along a platform at Charing Cross station. In the lower left there are several wounded soldiers lying on the stretchers that are placed on the ground. They are attended by a nurse and there are uniformed Order of St John personnel, standing on the platform, and lifting stretchers from the train. In the centre there are a group of soldiers in overcoats, several with bandaged limbs. The top half of the composition shows the roof of the station filled with steam and smoke from the train below.
persona1_adapted: This image shows a view along a platform at Charing Cross station. In the lower left, several wounded soldiers lie on stretchers placed on the ground. A nurse attends to them. Uniformed members of the Order of St John stand on the platform. They lift stretchers from the train. In the center, a group of soldiers in overcoats stand together. Several have bandaged arms or legs. The top half of the picture shows the station roof. It is filled with steam and smoke from the train below.

persona1_adapted:

This artwork was first ordered for Wellington House. Later, it was moved to the Department and Ministry of Information. Finally, it came to the Imperial War Museum.

CuratorialText:
image: Indian troops, wearing distinctive Sikh turbans, move timber and provisions from two military trucks to a waiting train in the background. Closer to the foreground three Indian soldiers carry a few planks of wood towards the viewer. The scene is near Pozières on the Somme in northern France, behind the front line.
History note
Imperial War Museum commission
persona1_adapted:
Indian soldiers, wearing their traditional Sikh turbans, unload wood and supplies from two military trucks. They load these items onto a train waiting in the background. In the front, three soldiers carry planks of wood toward the viewer. This scene takes place near Pozières on the Somme in northern France, just behind the front line.

This photograph was created for the Imperial War Museum.

CuratorialText:
image: a group of Indian infantrymen, all wearing distinctive Sikh turbans, dismantle a strongpoint near Pozières on the Western Front. The strongpoint consists of earth, brick, sandbags and corrugated iron and is the prominent feature in a flat, largely desolate and battle scarred landscape.
History note
Imperial War Museum commission
persona1_adapted: A group of Indian infantrymen, all wearing their distinctive Sikh turbans, take apart a strongpoint near Pozières on the Western Front. A strongpoint is a small, fortified position built for defense. This one is made of earth, brick, sandbags, and corrugated iron. It stands out in a flat, empty, and battle-scarred landscape. This photograph was taken for the Imperial War Museum.

CuratorialText: image: A view along a platform at Charing Cross station. In the lower left there are several wounded soldiers lying on the stretchers that are placed on the ground. They are attended by a nurse and there are uniformed Order of St John personnel, standing on the platform, and lifting stretchers from the train. In the centre there are a group of soldiers in overcoats, several with bandaged limbs. The top half of the composition shows the roof of the station filled with steam and smoke from the train below.
persona1_adapted: This picture shows a view along a platform at Charing Cross station. In the lower left, several wounded soldiers lie on stretchers on the ground. A nurse attends to them. Uniformed members of the Order of St John stand on the platform. They lift stretchers from the train. In the center, a group of soldiers in overcoats stand together. Several have bandaged arms or legs. The top half of the scene shows the station roof. It is filled with steam and smoke from the train below.

CuratorialText: image: A half length portrait of a British officer wearing a red dress uniform jacket with seven medals on his chest, including the DSO and OBE. He also wears the insignia of the Order of the Indian Empire around his neck.
persona1_adapted: A half-length portrait shows a British officer in a red dress uniform jacket. He has seven medals on his chest, including the DSO and OBE. Around his neck, he wears the badge of the Order of the Indian Empire.

CuratorialText:
image: a skeletal Death drives a bulldozer through the rubble, debris and detritus of war. Bricks, metal struts and tyres litter the ground and there is a flaming object on the right.
Label
This is a fantasy of utter destruction, as death rides in on a bulldozer. Only the flags stand tall, criss-crossing but ultimately adding to the confusion. The only architectural remains are bricks and girders. Michael Sandle is primarily a sculptor and has produced a number of public monuments. A key theme of his work is warfare, influenced by his early experiences of air raids in Plymouth and subsequent National Service. This is one of a series of six prints entitled 'The Driver'.
persona1_adapted:
In this image, a skeleton representing Death drives a bulldozer through war ruins. Bricks, metal beams, and tires cover the ground. A flaming object burns on the right side.

This is a fantasy of total destruction, with Death riding in on a bulldozer. Only the flags still stand, crossing each other and adding to the chaos. The only building parts left are bricks and metal beams.

Michael Sandle is mainly a sculptor and has made several public monuments. A key topic in his work is war. This comes from his early memories of air raids in Plymouth and his time in the military. This print is one of six in a series called "The Driver."

CuratorialText: image: a view through a few trees across the large field of crosses of the British military cemetery at Etaples on the French coast. The coastline is bordered by trees and a sailing boat is visible in the English Channel.
persona1_adapted: You see a view through a few trees. Across a large field, there are rows of crosses. This is the British military cemetery at Etaples on the French coast. Trees line the coastline. A sailing boat is visible in the English Channel.

CuratorialText:
image: The Royal Navy destroyer HMS Eclipse in the process of sinking in the Aegean Sea. The ship appears to have broken in two, with only the stern and bow sections above water. The bow section is on fire, with a bright cloud of flames and smoke billowing up into the night sky. There is another ship in the background and two Greek islands are visible to the left and right.
Label
On 23 October 1943 the two destroyers HMS Eclipse and HMS Petard sailed along the Turkish coast bound for Leros, one of the Greek Dodecanese Islands, which was occupied by a British garrison. On board the Eclipse was Brigadier-General George Davy, the Director of Military Operations, Middle East, who was enroute to inspect the defences of Leros. The ships were also carrying troops, ammunition and supplies for the garrison. At 11.45pm the Eclipse struck a mine between Kos and Kalymnos, blew up and sank in only two minutes. In addition to the ship's complement of around 200 men, she was carrying another 200 soldiers, including a company of the Buffs. Only around 100 men were picked up by an air-sea rescue launch of the RAF dispatched from Leros and boats sent from HMS Petard, after spending over three hours in the water. Davy was amongst the survivors. He went onto become the Commander of Land Forces, Adriatic, retiring from the Army in 1949. He recorded this traumatic event 24 years after it had occurred.
persona1_adapted:
On October 23, 1943, two British destroyers—HMS Eclipse and HMS Petard—sailed along the Turkish coast. They were heading to Leros, a Greek island held by British troops. On board the Eclipse was Brigadier-General George Davy. He was the Director of Military Operations for the Middle East and was going to inspect Leros’s defenses. The ships also carried soldiers, ammunition, and supplies.

At 11:45 p.m., the Eclipse hit a mine between the islands of Kos and Kalymnos. The ship exploded and sank in just two minutes. Besides its crew of about 200 men, the Eclipse was carrying another 200 soldiers. Only about 100 people survived. They were rescued by an RAF boat from Leros and by boats from HMS Petard after spending over three hours in the water.

General Davy was among the survivors. He later became the Commander of Land Forces in the Adriatic and retired from the Army in 1949. He wrote about this terrible event 24 years after it happened.

CuratorialText: image: two soldiers lie side by side on the floor of a tent with their heads resting on khaki bags. Both are wearing dessert gear. The soldier furthest away is reading, but the man nearest the artist has fallen asleep with his book resting on his chest.
persona1_adapted: Two soldiers lie side by side on the floor of a tent. Their heads rest on khaki bags. Both wear desert gear. The soldier farther away is reading. The man closest to the artist has fallen asleep. His book rests on his chest.

CuratorialText:
image: A view across the river Spree with the sun rising over the city in the background. There are fortifications and an observation post on the opposite bank with a vessel moored below, and a British Military policeman with a vehicle on the near side.
History note
Artistic Records Committee Commission, December 1980
persona1_adapted:
A view across the River Spree shows the sun rising over the city in the background. On the far bank, there are fortifications and an observation post. A boat is tied up below them. On the near side, a British military policeman stands with a vehicle.

This artwork was commissioned by the Artistic Records Committee in December 1980.

CuratorialText: image: An abstract composition of coiled and twisted girders of all sizes. This drawing explores the distortions made by bombs to building structures. The violence of the blast is evident in the rhythm and the form of the girders. Paule Vézelay was born in Bristol and returned there during the Second World War after over 10 years as an artist in Paris. She never gained an official war artist commission due to her left wing associations and the abstract nature of her work. However with the help of Bristol Museum and Art Gallery she gained a permit to draw the ruins in Bristol.
persona1_adapted: This drawing shows a jumble of twisted metal beams of different sizes. It explores how bombs can warp and destroy building structures. The rhythm and shape of the beams make the force of the explosion clear. Paule Vézelay was born in Bristol. She returned there during World War II after spending over 10 years as an artist in Paris. She never got an official job as a war artist. This was because of her left-wing political views and the abstract style of her work. But with help from Bristol Museum and Art Gallery, she got a permit to draw the ruins in Bristol.

CuratorialText: image: In the foreground two sailors sit with their backs to the viewer on the deck of a ship. A third sailor stands behind them leaning back with his hands in his pockets. A gun protrudes out above them. They are watching two ships anchored ahead of them and clouds of smoke rising from the hills in the distance. The sky is dotted with barrage balloons. The artist has also made several other sketches of ships and sailors in the upper right corner of the image.
persona1_adapted: In the front of the picture, two sailors sit on a ship's deck with their backs to us. A third sailor stands behind them, leaning back with his hands in his pockets. A gun sticks out above them. They are watching two ships anchored ahead. Smoke rises from the hills far away. The sky is filled with barrage balloons—large balloons tied to the ground to protect against enemy planes. The artist also drew several other sketches of ships and sailors in the top right corner of the image.

CuratorialText: image: British soldiers are in action on a German street, crouching and firing their guns among the rubble of bombed buildings. Two soldiers are lying on a Nazi flag bearing a swastika, the left of the two in the process of firing a Bren Gun. Four other infantrymen stand directly behind them to the left, two armed with rifles and one with a Bren Gun. Other British soldiers are visible in the middle distance.
persona1_adapted: British soldiers are fighting on a German street. They crouch and fire their guns among the rubble of bombed buildings. Two soldiers lie on a Nazi flag with a swastika. The soldier on the left is firing a Bren Gun, a type of light machine gun. Four other soldiers stand behind them to the left. Two carry rifles, and one has another Bren Gun. More British soldiers can be seen in the distance.

CuratorialText: image: two British Royal Army Medical Corps stretcher-bearers stand with their stretchers in a shallow trench in the foreground. The trench leads up to the entrance of an Advanced Dressing Station, marked with an ADS sign, which is housed in the bomb damaged remains of a large house in a ruined village. Other heavily bomb damaged buildings are visible in the background.
persona1_adapted: Two British Army stretcher-bearers stand in a shallow trench in the front of the scene. They hold their stretchers, ready to carry wounded soldiers. The trench leads to the entrance of an Advanced Dressing Station, or ADS, marked by a sign. This station is set up inside a bombed-out house in a ruined village. In the background, you can see other buildings that have also been heavily damaged by bombs.

CuratorialText:
image: the interior of the designers' room in the Old Vic Theatre, Waterloo, London, after suffering the effects of an air raid. The windows have been blown out and internal walls damaged, with the room littered with disturbed papers, designs and a fallen mannequin. The window looks out roughly south towards Lambeth, with the roofs of buildings visible.
Label
Short text of working notes. Inscription verso reads: 'Designers Room - Old Vic Theatre September 1941'.
persona1_adapted:
This painting shows the inside of a room at the Old Vic Theatre in London. The room was hit by an air raid. The windows are blown out, and the walls are damaged. Papers, designs, and a fallen mannequin are scattered everywhere. The window faces south toward Lambeth, where you can see the roofs of buildings.

A short note on the back of the painting says: "Designers Room - Old Vic Theatre September 1941."

CuratorialText: image: Against a gloomy sunset and upon a grassy mound stands a smoking but unmanned French 75mm field gun, facing left. Below running the length of the composition lies the detritus of battle: a broken limber, four German corpses and two dead mules.
persona1_adapted: A smoking French 75mm field gun stands alone on a grassy hill. It faces left against a dark sunset. No one is operating the gun. Below it, stretching across the scene, lies the wreckage of battle: a broken ammunition cart, four dead German soldiers, and two dead mules.

CuratorialText: image: A convoy at sea being bombarded by enemy aircraft. In the centre of the composition is a large tanker, which has black smoke emanating from it. Other ships can be seen to the left and right with aircraft visible above. Large splashes of water in the sea show several near-misses.
persona1_adapted: A convoy at sea is under attack by enemy planes. In the middle of the scene, a large tanker is hit and black smoke pours out of it. Other ships are on the left and right, with aircraft flying above. Big splashes in the water show that several bombs barely missed their targets.

CuratorialText: Powder paint painting, hinged in corners onto a single sheet of white board. Bottom left hinge has previously been cut to allow access to object number. Neatly torn along top and right edges, rather than cut. Previously folded in half vertically.
persona1_adapted: This artwork is made with powder paint. It is attached at the corners to one sheet of white board. The hinge at the bottom left was cut open so someone could see the object number. The top and right edges are neatly torn, not cut. The piece was once folded in half vertically.

CuratorialText: image: eight internees stand behind a barbed wire fence at Hutchinson camp near Douglas on the Isle of Man. The word 'Parachutists' is on the wall behind them. Four of the men wear glasses and all but one of them appear to be wearing kippot, indicating their Jewish faith. The other man wears a bowler-style hat.
persona1_adapted: Eight people who were being held at a camp on the Isle of Man stand behind a barbed wire fence. The word "Parachutists" is painted on the wall behind them. Four of the men wear glasses. Most of them wear kippot, which are small caps that show they are Jewish. One man wears a bowler-style hat instead.

CuratorialText: Acknowledged as one of the greatest landscape artists of all time, Joseph Mallord William Turner (1775–1851) knew Oxford extremely well. From childhood, he made numerous drawings and watercolours of the streets and colleges and the picturesque views surrounding the city. His celebrated oil painting of the High Street, Oxford is on display in the 19th-Century Art Gallery. To mark the 250th anniversary of J.M.W. Turner's birth on 23 April 1775, explore this selection of his depictions of Oxford from the Ashmolean's collections. The Museum's Western Art Print Room houses just over 100 watercolours and drawings by Turner, as well as many landscape etchings. There are lively studies of animals, birds and fish, as well as evocative views of places that the artist visited on his travels in Britain and Europe, from Margate to Venice.
persona1_adapted: Joseph Mallord William Turner (1775–1851) is known as one of the greatest landscape artists ever. He knew Oxford very well. As a child, he made many drawings and watercolors of the city's streets, colleges, and beautiful views. His famous oil painting of the High Street in Oxford is on display in the 19th-Century Art Gallery. To celebrate the 250th anniversary of Turner's birth on April 23, 1775, explore this selection of his Oxford scenes from the Ashmolean's collections. The Museum's Western Art Print Room holds over 100 watercolors and drawings by Turner, plus many landscape etchings (prints made from engraved metal plates). There are lively sketches of animals, birds, and fish, along with striking views of places he visited in Britain and Europe, from Margate to Venice.

CuratorialText: In the second millennium BCE the island of Crete was home to a complex pre-Greek society known as ‘the Minoans’. When archaeologist Sir Arthur Evans started to excavate at the site of Knossos on Crete, in 1900, he frequently connected the things he found with ancient Cretan myths. He named the building at Knossos the ‘Palace of Minos’, after the mythical king of Crete, and connected it to the story of the Labyrinth. The Labyrinth was an ingenious maze commissioned by King Minos to imprison the ferocious Minotaur, a creature with the head of a bull and the body of a man. In the myth, the Minotaur is killed by Theseus, the famous semi-divine hero. Following Theseus's victory, he returned to Greece, and became king and the founder of Athens. The Cretan myths originated in stories which the Greek and Roman inhabitants of the city of Knossos would have told long after the Palace had been destroyed.
persona1_adapted: Around 4,000 years ago, the island of Crete was home to a group of people called the Minoans. They lived there before the Greeks. In 1900, an archaeologist named Sir Arthur Evans began digging at a place called Knossos on Crete. He often linked what he found to old Cretan myths. He named the building at Knossos the “Palace of Minos,” after the mythical king of Crete. He also connected it to the story of the Labyrinth. The Labyrinth was a tricky maze. King Minos ordered it to be built to trap the fierce Minotaur. The Minotaur had the head of a bull and the body of a man. In the myth, a hero named Theseus kills the Minotaur. Theseus was the son of a god and a human. After his victory, Theseus returned to Greece. He became king and founded Athens. The Cretan myths came from stories that Greek and Roman people in Knossos told long after the Palace was destroyed.

CuratorialText:
Landscape painting has a long tradition in East Asian art. However, landscape did not become an important feature of Japanese woodblock prints until the early 1800s. By this time, Japanese citizens were taking part in more outdoor sightseeing activities, such as temple visits and boat trips, and there was a growing demand for souvenir images. In the competitive world of the publishing industry, publishers were constantly trying to develop new products; landscape prints were an appealing novelty.

Technical factors also played an important part in the emergence of the landscape print. Particularly significant was the introduction of the new imported pigment Prussian Blue. Unlike earlier Japanese blues, which faded very quickly, this brilliant colour gave artists more freedom of expression, especially in the depiction of sky and water

Here we feature a selection of highlights from the master of landscape prints, Utagawa Hiroshige, as well as selected works by Hokusai and Kuniyoshi.
persona1_adapted:
Landscape painting has a long history in East Asian art. But landscape did not become a major part of Japanese woodblock prints until the early 1800s. By then, Japanese people were taking more outdoor sightseeing trips, like visiting temples and going on boat rides. This created a growing demand for souvenir images. In the competitive world of publishing, companies were always looking for new products. Landscape prints became a popular and fresh idea.

Technical changes also helped landscape prints emerge. A key factor was a new blue pigment called Prussian Blue, which was imported from abroad. Unlike older Japanese blues that faded quickly, this bright color gave artists more freedom to express themselves, especially when painting sky and water.

Here we show a selection of highlights from the master of landscape prints, Utagawa Hiroshige, along with selected works by Hokusai and Kuniyoshi.

CuratorialText:
The collection of antiquities includes internationally renowned archaeological collections from Prehistoric Europe, the Ancient Near East, Ancient Egypt and Sudan, Minoan Aegean, Mycenaean and Classical Greece, Ancient Cyprus, the Roman world, Medieval and later Europe, as well as the museum's Founding Collection and historic archives.

It also includes one of the oldest, largest and best-preserved collections of casts of Greek and Roman sculpture in the UK.
persona1_adapted:
The museum’s collection of ancient objects is famous around the world. It includes items from prehistoric Europe, the Ancient Near East, Egypt, Sudan, Greece, Cyprus, and the Roman world. It also covers medieval and later Europe. The museum holds its original Founding Collection and historical archives.

The museum also has one of the oldest, largest, and best-preserved collections of plaster casts of Greek and Roman statues in the UK.

CuratorialText: In our Eastern Art collections you will find sculpture, textiles, ceramics and paintings from the Islamic Middle East, China, Japan, Southeast Asia, India and the Himalayas.
persona1_adapted: In our Eastern Art collections, you can see sculptures, fabrics, pottery, and paintings. These works come from the Islamic Middle East, China, Japan, Southeast Asia, India, and the Himalayas.

CuratorialText: The Museum’s Heberden Coin Room is one of the leading international coin cabinets with particular strengths in the fields of Greek, Roman, Celtic, Byzantine, Medieval, Islamic and Chinese coinages. It also holds collections of paper money, tokens, jetons and commemorative art medals.
persona1_adapted: The museum’s Heberden Coin Room is one of the world’s top coin collections. It is especially strong in Greek, Roman, Celtic, Byzantine, Medieval, Islamic, and Chinese coins. The room also holds collections of paper money, tokens, and special art medals made to honor people or events.

CuratorialText: The Western Art collections contain outstanding European fine and decorative arts from the Renaissance to the present day. They include Old Master and later paintings, drawings, watercolours, and prints; English and continental ceramics, sculpture, silver, watches, rings and musical instruments. The Ashmolean's exceptional print collection contains around 25,000 drawings and over 250,000 prints and many are accessible through our Western Art Print Room.
persona1_adapted: The Western Art collections feature amazing European fine and decorative arts, from the Renaissance period to today. You can find paintings, drawings, watercolors, and prints by Old Masters and later artists. There are also English and European ceramics, sculptures, silver items, watches, rings, and musical instruments. The Ashmolean’s print collection is special. It holds about 25,000 drawings and over 250,000 prints. Many of these are available to see in our Western Art Print Room.

CuratorialText: The painting resulted from an important early commission for two watercolours to be engraved for the annual calendar published by the University Press, the Oxford Almanack. They were so successful that Tuner eventually completed ten watercolours between 1798 and 1804. This work and one other were later painted in oils for the local print-seller and frame-maker, James Wyatt. Turner consulted Wyatt on the details of the architecture included in the view. He also introduced figures, members of the University and clergy, and some women ‘for the sake of colour’. The painting is one of the most fully documented of all Turner’s works because Wyatt kept his correspondence with the artist which was included in his posthumous sale. The original purpose of the commission was to have the design engraved. Wyatt settled on an oil painting, instead of a watercolour, half the size of Turner’s normal canvases, at a cost of 100 guineas. The painting was exhibited at the Royal Academy in 1812. When, in the 1830s, Turner was choosing views of Oxford for his greatest series of watercolours, he rejected the High Street. He felt that in this painting he had achieved an unparalleled view of technical mastery that he could not repeat.
persona1_adapted:
This painting started as an early, important job. Turner was asked to create two watercolors for the *Oxford Almanack*, a yearly calendar published by the University Press. The watercolors were so well-liked that Turner ended up making ten of them between 1798 and 1804.

Later, Turner turned this work and one other into oil paintings. He did this for a local print-seller and frame-maker named James Wyatt. Turner asked Wyatt for advice on the details of the buildings in the scene. He also added people, like university members and clergy, plus some women “for the sake of color.”

This painting is one of Turner’s most well-documented works. That’s because Wyatt kept the letters Turner sent him. Those letters were later sold after Wyatt’s death.

The original goal was to engrave the design. But Wyatt decided on an oil painting instead of a watercolor. It was half the size of Turner’s usual canvases and cost 100 guineas. The painting was shown at the Royal Academy in 1812.

In the 1830s, Turner chose views of Oxford for his greatest series of watercolors. He did not pick the High Street. He felt that in this painting, he had reached a level of technical skill he could not match again.

CuratorialText: Together with a St Francis (Christ Church, Oxford) and a Risen Christ (National Gallery of Wales), WA1850.13 and WA1850.13.1 come from a lost altar-piece by Granacci, painted around 1520. The four panels may have been placed in the pilasters. Granacci was a follower of Ghirlandaio who was particularly interested in the art of Fra Bartolomeo and Raphael.
persona1_adapted: These four panels were part of a lost altar-piece painted by Granacci around 1520. An altar-piece is a large painting placed behind an altar in a church. The panels may have been set into the pilasters, which are flat columns on the sides of the altar. Granacci was a follower of the artist Ghirlandaio. He was especially interested in the work of two other artists, Fra Bartolomeo and Raphael.

CuratorialText: These are pinnacle panels that once belonged to the same unknown altar-piece, which may have been crowned with a central pinnacle panel showing Christ on the Cross. The artist was Florentine, working in a late Gothic manner and an attribution to Bartolomeo di Fruosino has recently been suggested. A painter and miniaturist, Bartolomeo was a pupil of Agnolo Gaddi.
persona1_adapted: These pointed panels were once part of the same unknown altar piece. The top of the altar piece may have had a central panel showing Christ on the Cross. The artist was from Florence, Italy. He worked in a late Gothic style, which means his art had a lot of decoration and detail. Experts recently suggested that the artist might be Bartolomeo di Fruosino. He was a painter and a miniaturist, which means he painted small, detailed pictures. Bartolomeo was a student of another artist named Agnolo Gaddi.

CuratorialText: As a nocturnal landscape and as a brilliantly structured composition, The Hunt in the Forest is a highly original painting. Hunting was an aristocratic pastime with its own rituals, but the idea of a hunt at night is playful and may be symbolic rather than realistic. In preparing this work, the artist Paolo di Dono first mapped out a grid on the panel's surface, fixing a central vanishing point to draw our gaze deep into the composition. In painting the subject, he incorporated details including the huntsmen's spears, the cut branches, logs and the water in the foreground as devices to bring focus to this space and to the receding forms of men and animals. Gold flecks in the foliage of the trees would have complemented the bright colours of the figures against the dark forest, and would have glistened and become animated when illuminated by candlelight. Paolo di Dono (1397-1475) was celebrated in his lifetime as a master of perspective, animals and landscape; his nickname, Uccello (‘Bird’), alludes to his depictions of the natural world. He was a versatile designer, working at times on mosaic and stained-glass commissions and all of these skills have been cleverly combined to create this masterpiece. The Hunt is what is known as a spalliera painting, which would have been mounted at shoulder height – whether as a backboard of a decorated chest or set into the wooden panelling of a room. The original client is unknown, but the painting was clearly intended for a Renaissance palace, perhaps in Urbino where Uccello worked for a time from 1465, or in Florence about 1470.
persona1_adapted:
