The Hunt in the Forest is a very original painting. It shows a nighttime landscape with a strong, clear structure. Hunting was a hobby for rich people, with its own special customs. But the idea of a night hunt is playful. It might be more symbolic than realistic.

To make this work, the artist Paolo di Dono first drew a grid on the wooden panel. He fixed a single vanishing point in the center. This pulls our eyes deep into the scene. He added details like the hunters’ spears, cut branches, logs, and water in the front. These elements focus our attention on the space and on the people and animals moving away from us.

Gold specks in the leaves of the trees would have matched the bright colors of the figures against the dark forest. When lit by candlelight, the gold would have sparkled and seemed to move. Paolo di Dono (1397–1475) was famous in his time as a master of perspective, animals, and landscapes. His nickname, Uccello (which means “Bird”), refers to his paintings of nature. He was a versatile designer. He also worked on mosaics and stained glass. He combined all these skills to create this masterpiece.

The Hunt is a type of painting called a spalliera. It would have been placed at shoulder height. It could have been the backboard of a decorated chest or set into the wooden wall of a room. We don’t know who first bought it, but the painting was clearly made for a Renaissance palace. It might have been in Urbino, where Uccello worked from 1465, or in Florence around 1470.

CuratorialText: The portrait corresponds generally to a number of paintings and prints of Louix XIII of France on a prancing horse. From the type and handling of the paint, it seems to be either by or after a Flemish painter. The appearance of the king suggests a date of about 1622.
persona1_adapted: This portrait is similar to several paintings and prints of King Louis XIII of France riding a prancing horse. Based on the style and use of paint, it was likely made by a Flemish painter or someone copying that artist. The king’s appearance in the painting suggests it was created around 1622.

CuratorialText: The Magdalen is recognisable by her jar of ointment, a reference to her traditional identification with the woman who anointed the feet of Christ and dried them with her hair (Luke 7: 36-50). Possibly cut from a larger composition, this small panel has been enlarged by being fitted into another and the figure extended. The artist may have worked in Parma in the early 17th century in the circle of Bartolomeo Schedoni.
persona1_adapted: You can identify the Magdalen by her jar of ointment. This refers to the woman who anointed Christ’s feet and dried them with her hair (Luke 7: 36-50). This small painting was probably cut from a larger artwork. Someone later added extra wood to make it bigger and extended the figure. The artist may have worked in Parma in the early 1600s. He was part of the group around Bartolomeo Schedoni.

CuratorialText: The landscape is reminiscent of Éragny. It may have been painted in the summer of 1893 or 1894, before Félix Pissarro left Éragny for Belgium. In: Jon Whiteley, Nineteenth-century French Paintings in the Ashmolean Museum, (Oxford: Ashmolean Museum 2022)
persona1_adapted: This landscape looks like the area around Éragny. It was probably painted in the summer of 1893 or 1894. That was just before Félix Pissarro left Éragny to move to Belgium.

CuratorialText: A free copy, dating from the late 17th or early 18th century, of the fresco in one of the spandrels of the painted Loggia di Psiche in the Villa Farnesina, Rome. Raphael and his studio decorated this space in 1518-19 with scenes from the story of Cupid and Psyche. The spandrel from which WA1859.1 was copied may have been painted by Raphael's senior assistant, Giulio Romano. The copyist has altered the format to a square shape.
persona1_adapted:
This is a freehand copy of a fresco, or wall painting. The original was made in the late 1600s or early 1700s. It copies a painting in the Villa Farnesina in Rome. That painting is in a part of the ceiling called a spandrel. The spandrel is part of the painted Loggia di Psiche.

Raphael and his workshop decorated this space in 1518-19. They used scenes from the story of Cupid and Psyche. The spandrel that WA1859.1 copies may have been painted by Giulio Romano. He was Raphael’s senior assistant. The copyist changed the shape from a triangle to a square.

CuratorialText: Three of the objects in this still life are found elsewhere in Renoir’s late work: the silver beaker appears alongside a sugar bowl and knife in a small still life said to have been painted at Cagnes in 1910 and again in a similar composition without the knife, also said to have been painted in 1910; the bronze-coloured child on the column was included in a small study, dated c.1905 by the Daubervilles. The faience double salt, a type produced in quantity in the nineteenth century at Desvres in the Pas-de-Calais and at Quimper in Brittany, appears in at least four other sketches. A disembodied image of the same figure, seen from the back, hovers in upper right, probably a reflection in a mirror. Renoir, who worked as a painter at a ceramic manufactory in his early days, seems to have owned a number of pieces of faience of this popular type⁵ and used a variety of coloured and decorated ceramic containers in his flower paintings.
persona1_adapted: Three objects in this still life also appear in other late works by Renoir. The silver beaker shows up with a sugar bowl and knife in a small still life, said to have been painted at Cagnes in 1910. It appears again in a similar painting without the knife, also from 1910. The bronze-colored child on the column was included in a small study, dated around 1905 by the Daubervilles. The faience double salt—a type of glazed ceramic made in large numbers in the 1800s at Desvres and Quimper in France—appears in at least four other sketches. A floating image of the same figure, seen from the back, hovers in the upper right. This is probably a reflection in a mirror. Renoir started his career painting at a ceramic factory. He seems to have owned several pieces of this popular type of faience. He used many different colored and decorated ceramic containers in his flower paintings.

CuratorialText: Although damaged, this portrait of an unknown woman is of fine quality and has affinities with the work of Andrea in the 1520s. Apart from a short stay in Rome c.1516, Andrea spent most of his career in Siena and in Florence, where he collaborated with his brother Raffaello Piccinelli.
persona1_adapted: This portrait of an unknown woman is damaged, but it is still high quality. It shares features with Andrea’s work from the 1520s. Andrea spent most of his career in Siena and Florence. He worked with his brother Raffaello Piccinelli. He only briefly stayed in Rome around 1516.

CuratorialText: After 1739, Ruysch painted mainly small-scale paintings of bouquets and posies, inscribed with her age, as here. The arrangement of curving stems, full, rounded roses and a number of smaller flowers looks back to the work of Willem van Aelst. This painting was stolen from the Museum in March 1949 and has not been recovered
persona1_adapted: After 1739, Ruysch mostly painted small bouquets and flower bunches. She wrote her age on each one, like in this painting. The flowers curve and bend. The roses are full and round. Smaller flowers fill the spaces between them. This style was inspired by the artist Willem van Aelst. This painting was stolen from the Museum in March 1949. It has never been found.

CuratorialText: This panel comes from the predella of a large altar-piece of 1433-35 for S. Niccolò in Cafaggio, Florence, whose parts are now scattered in various collections. The Virgin and Child with Four Angels in the centre was flanked by pairs of saints (Sts Benedict and Nicholas of Bari and St John the Baptist and an Evangelist, probably St Matthew). The predella showed scenes from the life of St Nicholas, of which five are known: the birth of the saint; pilgrims at the shrine of the saint; St Nicholas resuscitating three youths; the saint providing dowries for three sisters (three gold balls, his traditional attribute); and banishing the storm. The panels probably originally had quatrefoil frames. Bicci based the design of WA1850.26 on Gentile da Fabriano's Quaratesi altar-piece of 1425 for S. Niccolò oltr'Arno.
persona1_adapted:
This panel was once part of the bottom section of a large altar-piece. The artwork was made between 1433 and 1435 for a church called S. Niccolò in Cafaggio, Florence. Today, the different parts of the altar-piece are spread across various collections.

The center of the altar-piece showed the Virgin and Child with Four Angels. On each side were pairs of saints: Sts Benedict and Nicholas of Bari, and St John the Baptist with an Evangelist, probably St Matthew.

The bottom section showed scenes from the life of St Nicholas. Five of these scenes are still known today. They include: the saint's birth, pilgrims at his shrine, St Nicholas bringing three youths back to life, the saint providing dowries for three sisters (three gold balls became his symbol), and calming a storm. The panels likely had frames shaped like four-leaf clovers.

The artist, Bicci, based his design on an earlier altar-piece by Gentile da Fabriano from 1425. That work was made for another church in Florence called S. Niccolò oltr'Arno.

CuratorialText: This is clearly the portrait of a professional sculptor, shown in his studio with some of his work on the shelf behind. He holds a terracotta figure of a saint, perhaps the Virgin Mary. The painting has been tentatively attributed to the circle of the de Critz family.
persona1_adapted: This is a portrait of a professional sculptor. He is in his studio, with some of his work on the shelf behind him. He holds a clay figure of a saint, possibly the Virgin Mary. Experts think this painting was made by someone in the de Critz family’s workshop.

CuratorialText: The pages of calligraphy, all by one hand, mostly include kanshi (Chinese poems), and are signed with variations of the name Sessō Keigi. Two of the poems bear dates of Meiji 10 (1878) and Meiji 12 (1880), and a few identify the source from which the poem came or where the poem was composed. If the calligraphy was done at the same time as the paintings, this album would be an early work by Sobun before he began participating in the national and international expositions.
persona1_adapted: These calligraphy pages were all written by the same person. Most of them include kanshi, which are Chinese poems. The artist signed them using different versions of the name Sessō Keigi. Two of the poems have dates: Meiji 10 (1878) and Meiji 12 (1880). A few others mention where the poem came from or where it was written. If the calligraphy was made at the same time as the paintings, then this album would be an early work by Sobun. He made it before he started showing his art in national and international exhibitions.

CuratorialText: The genesis of this painting began in 1857, when it was exhibited as The Mother's Grave. The original composition, known from a drawing in the Ashmolean, showed the boy desolate over the grave of his mother. The landscape was begun in the summer of 1856 in the old churchyard at Chingford, Essex. Around 1862, Hughes altered the background and added the figure of the sister, for which the artist's wife, Tryphena, posed. The detail is used to reinforce the pathos of the subject, so that the ephemeral nature of spider's webs, dew drops, dog roses and dandelion seeds all emphasise the theme of transience. The boy's loss is retold in the lamb separated from its mother by the barrier of the tomb.
persona1_adapted:
This painting first appeared in 1857 with the title *The Mother's Grave*. A drawing in the Ashmolean Museum shows the original version. In that version, a boy stands alone, sad, at his mother’s grave.

The artist began the landscape in the summer of 1856. He painted it in an old churchyard at Chingford, Essex. Around 1862, Hughes changed the background. He also added the figure of the sister. The artist’s wife, Tryphena, posed for that figure.

Small details in the painting make the sadness stronger. Spider webs, dew drops, dog roses, and dandelion seeds all show that life is short and passes quickly. The boy’s loss is also shown by a lamb. The lamb is separated from its mother by the barrier of the tomb.

CuratorialText: John Tradescant the Younger (1608-1662) succeeded his father as Keeper of the King's Gardens at Oatlands Palace and further enriched the family collection of specimens of natural and man-made curiosity. He is shown here with his friend, Roger Friend, a brewer (for which Zythepsa is the Greek) from Lambeth, who may have contributed the shells to Tradescant's museum.
persona1_adapted: John Tradescant the Younger (1608–1662) took over his father’s job as Keeper of the King’s Gardens at Oatlands Palace. He added many more natural and man-made objects to the family’s collection of curiosities. In this portrait, he is shown with his friend Roger Friend, a brewer from Lambeth. (The Greek word for brewing is “Zythepsa.”) Friend may have given the shells that were part of Tradescant’s museum.

CuratorialText: As one of the leading members of post-war generation of St Ives artists, Lanyon was primarily interested in the representation of landscape. This work epitomises the tension between abstraction and figuration in an otherwise carefully descriptive work.
persona1_adapted: Lanyon was a key artist in the group from St Ives after World War II. He mainly focused on showing landscapes in his work. This painting shows the push and pull between abstract shapes and realistic details, even though the scene is carefully described.

CuratorialText: Bundi, Rajasthan, c.1760-70. Gouache with gold on paper. Attended by a drowsy maid, a young noble couple sleep in close embrace on a palace terrace, sheltered by a shamiana or awning. The young prince’s sword and arrows lie close to hand.
persona1_adapted: This painting is from Bundi, Rajasthan, made around 1760-1770. The artist used gouache (a type of thick, opaque paint) and gold on paper. A young noble couple sleeps in a close embrace on a palace terrace. A drowsy maid attends to them. They are sheltered by a shamiana, which is a decorative awning or canopy. The young prince’s sword and arrows lie nearby, ready to use.

CuratorialText: This painting was described as A Nun Playing a Piano when it appeared at Christie’s in 1919 and when it was sold by Colnaghi’s to Mrs Weldon in 1936, no doubt because the sitter is wearing a long veil. However, there is no reason otherwise to suppose the sitter is a nun. It is almost certainly a portrait of Desirée-Marie Ribot, known as Louise, the artist’s daughter whom he often used as a model. Born in 1857, her youthful appearance supports the evidence of style which suggests a date in the late 1860s or early 1870s before Ribot’s touch became thicker and more painterly. The dark background and domestic theme recall the work of François Bonvin (1817–1887), an early supporter of Ribot and of Alphonse Legros (1837–1911), who shared Bonvin’s interest in Spanish art.
persona1_adapted: This painting was once called *A Nun Playing a Piano*. That name was used when it was sold in 1919 and again in 1936. The reason is simple: the woman in the painting wears a long veil. But there is no real proof she is a nun. In fact, the sitter is almost certainly Desirée-Marie Ribot, known as Louise. She was the artist’s daughter, and he often used her as a model. She was born in 1857. Her young look matches the painting’s style, which suggests a date in the late 1860s or early 1870s. That was before the artist’s brushwork became thicker and more textured. The dark background and everyday scene remind us of the work of François Bonvin. He was an early supporter of Ribot. It also recalls the work of Alphonse Legros, who shared Bonvin’s interest in Spanish art.

CuratorialText: The inscription on this painting reads ‘Wind from the west is blowing through layers of curtains on doors, the proud [chrysanthemum] are falling but their beauty remains. In fading moonlight I [stayed] with these flowers while they cooled, versing through this cool evening in autumn.’
persona1_adapted: The words on this painting say: "Wind from the west blows through layers of curtains on doors. The proud chrysanthemums are falling, but their beauty stays. In fading moonlight, I stayed with these flowers as they cooled. I wrote poems through this cool autumn evening."

CuratorialText: Sinnott is well known for his complex figurative compositions. He often deals with the human figure in a context. Although the same fluid brush-strokes can be seen in the background and in the figures, the two wrestling men can be distinguished by a lighter touch and a slight variation in the use of paint. The underpainting, in places, appears through the upper layers, accentuating the sense of speed in the painting process.
persona1_adapted: Sinnott is famous for his detailed paintings of people. He often shows the human body in a setting. You can see the same smooth brushstrokes in both the background and the figures. But the two wrestling men stand out because of a lighter touch and a small difference in how the paint is used. In some spots, the first layer of paint shows through the top layers. This makes the painting look like it was done very quickly.

CuratorialText: The only securely documented painting by the artist is an altar-piece of 1447 now in the Museo Civico, Padua. A follower of Michele Giambono, Francesco may have collaborated with Antonio Vivarini in Venice. Together with WA1850.10, this panel was once part of an unknown altar-piece possibly for a church in Padua or Venice. St Catherine, of royal birth, wears a crown and carries her martyr's palm; the wheel refers to earlier attempts at her martyrdom.
persona1_adapted: The only painting that experts are sure was made by this artist is an altar-piece from 1447. It is now in the Museo Civico in Padua. Francesco was a follower of Michele Giambono. He may have worked with Antonio Vivarini in Venice. This panel, along with WA1850.10, was once part of an unknown altar-piece. It was possibly made for a church in Padua or Venice. St. Catherine was born into a royal family. She wears a crown and carries a palm leaf, which is a symbol of her martyrdom. The wheel in the painting refers to earlier attempts to kill her.

CuratorialText: The grief-stricken figures of the Virgin and St John the Evangelist make this small panel a powerful devotional image; it may have been the right wing of a portable diptych, although no hinge-marks are now visible. The anonymous artist was very close to Jacopo di Cione (to whom WA1921.3 has also been attributed) and would have trained in the large workshop of Orcagna, the leading artist in mid 14th-century Florence
persona1_adapted: This small panel shows the grieving figures of the Virgin Mary and St John the Evangelist. It was likely the right wing of a portable diptych, which is a two-panel painting that folds shut like a book. No hinge marks are visible today. The artist is unknown, but their style is very close to Jacopo di Cione. This artist would have trained in the large workshop of Orcagna, who was the leading artist in Florence during the mid-1300s.

CuratorialText: Veronese's celebrated Last Supper of 1573 for the refectory of SS Giovanni e Paolo, Venice, caused him to be examined by the Inquisition on the grounds of blasphemy; he changed the title to The Feast in the House of Levi, a subject which allowed for much colourful detail (Luke 6: 27-30). Numerous copies are known of this large painting, now in the Accademia, Venice. WA1855.159 is a reduced copy of fine quality by a mid-18th-century Venetian artist, probably Francesco Fontebasso.
persona1_adapted: In 1573, Veronese painted a famous version of *The Last Supper* for a dining hall in a Venetian monastery. The painting got him in trouble with the Inquisition, a church court, because they thought it was disrespectful. To fix this, Veronese changed the title to *The Feast in the House of Levi*. This new subject allowed him to keep the colorful details he had painted. Many copies of this large painting exist. The original is now in the Accademia museum in Venice. WA1855.159 is a smaller, high-quality copy. It was made in the mid-1700s by a Venetian artist, probably Francesco Fontebasso.

CuratorialText: A companion to Opie's portrait of the sitter's husband, Francis Smith (1764-1815), WA1934.422. Both paintings were exhibited at the Royal Academy in 1800.
persona1_adapted: This painting is a companion to Opie’s portrait of the sitter’s husband, Francis Smith (1764–1815), which is also in the museum’s collection. Both artworks were shown at the Royal Academy in 1800.

CuratorialText: Painted in 1886 at a time when both Camille and Lucien Pissarro were working with the pointillist technique. The view, taken from a bend on the path leading from the Pré du Chêne across the Epte from Éragny to Bazincourt, includes the spire of Saint-Martin in Éragny in the centre with the sixteenth-century brick manor house on the right. The Epte runs along the line of trees that crosses the composition. In 1887 Lucien painted an almost identical view in watercolour and bodycolour. The buildings seen from this stretch of the path are now largely concealed by the trees. However, it is clear that in both works Lucien has greatly enlarged the size of the church and manor and suppressed the farm buildings that lie in front of the manor. His father, as Lucien must have known, had painted the same view from the same spot in 1884, using the more improvised style of the early 1880s.
persona1_adapted:
This painting was made in 1886. At that time, both Camille Pissarro and his son Lucien were using a technique called pointillism. In pointillism, artists apply small dots of color to create a picture.

The scene shows a view from a bend in a path. The path goes from Pré du Chêne to Bazincourt, crossing the Epte River near Éragny. In the center of the painting, you can see the spire of Saint-Martin church in Éragny. On the right, there is a brick manor house from the 1500s. The Epte River runs along the line of trees that cuts across the composition.

In 1887, Lucien painted a very similar view using watercolor and bodycolor. Bodycolor is a type of paint that is not transparent, like gouache. Today, the buildings seen from this part of the path are mostly hidden by trees. But it is clear that in both works, Lucien made the church and manor much larger. He also left out the farm buildings that stand in front of the manor.

Lucien must have known that his father had painted the same view from the same spot in 1884. Camille used a looser, more spontaneous style from the early 1880s.

CuratorialText: The painting shows a distant view of the village of Lauris in the Vaucluse, with its distinctive eighteenth-century château and garden terrace on the left and the mountains of the Petit Luberon in the background. Guigou was seated or standing on one of the many sandbanks interspersed with the channels of the river Durance as it passes between Lauris and La Roque-d’Anthéron during the dry season. As in most of the artist’s Provençal landscapes, the air is clear and the sunlight strong. The example of Corot or Daubigny may have had some influence on the choice of the panoramic view that Guigou seems to have favoured above all others, but his manner of painting is distinctive. The sky, sand and river are thinly brushed and enlivened with spots of impasto and small, short strokes with the tip of the brush in the artist’s characteristic manner.
persona1_adapted:
This painting shows a distant view of the village of Lauris in the Vaucluse region. On the left, you can see the village’s eighteenth-century castle and its garden terrace. In the background are the mountains of the Petit Luberon.

The artist, Guigou, sat or stood on one of the many sandbanks in the Durance River. These sandbanks appear when the river passes between Lauris and La Roque-d’Anthéron during the dry season. Like most of Guigou’s landscapes from Provence, the air is clear and the sunlight is strong.

Guigou seems to have preferred wide, panoramic views. Artists like Corot or Daubigny may have influenced this choice. But Guigou’s painting style is unique. He painted the sky, sand, and river with thin layers of paint. He then added life to the scene with small, thick dabs of paint and short strokes made with the tip of his brush. This was his special way of working.

CuratorialText: This painting, which is a cosmic landscape with Daoist undertones, was produced in 1988 for Khoan Sullivan (1919-2003) who wished for something with a predominantly blue colour. The inscription reads ‘To Mr. and Mrs. Sullivan. Affectionately, Chu Teh-Chun 1988.’
persona1_adapted: This painting shows a cosmic landscape with ideas from Daoism. It was made in 1988 for Khoan Sullivan (1919-2003), who wanted something mostly blue. The writing on the painting says, “To Mr. and Mrs. Sullivan. Affectionately, Chu Teh-Chun 1988.”

CuratorialText: One of Lucien's most extrovert works, painted in June 1925 and illustrating the broom in full flower at Le Brusq.
persona1_adapted: One of Lucien's most outgoing paintings, made in June 1925, shows the broom plant in full bloom at Le Brusq.

CuratorialText: Joseph Warton (1722-1800) was educated at Oriel College, Oxford, before entering the church. He was Headmaster of Winchester College between 1766 and 1793 and an accomplished poet. This portrait was painted and exhibited at the Royal Academy in 1777, the year in which Warton was also elected to 'The Club'. He was the brother of Thomas Warton, the Poet Laureate, whose portrait Reynolds also painted (Trinity College, Oxford; Mannings 2000, no. 1838).
persona1_adapted: Joseph Warton (1722-1800) went to Oriel College, Oxford, before becoming a church leader. He served as Headmaster of Winchester College from 1766 to 1793 and was a skilled poet. This portrait was painted and shown at the Royal Academy in 1777. That same year, Warton was chosen to join 'The Club'. He was the brother of Thomas Warton, who was the Poet Laureate. The artist Reynolds also painted Thomas Warton's portrait (Trinity College, Oxford; Mannings 2000, no. 1838).

CuratorialText: The influence of Vuillard is strongly marked. There is a less finished version of the same composition on the back
persona1_adapted: Vuillard's style clearly influenced this painting. On the back, there is a rougher version of the same scene.

CuratorialText: Bollongier was one of the few painters of the first half of the 17th century in Haarlem who specialised in painting flowers. His work seems to have been influenced by the Bosschaert school but with an added naturalism in the arrangement of the flowers. The glass container in this painting was a favourite accessory. It appears first in a work of 1627 and for the last time in a flower piece of 1639 (Rijksmuseum, Amsterdam).
persona1_adapted: Bollongier was one of the few painters in Haarlem during the early 1600s who focused on painting flowers. His work was influenced by the Bosschaert school, but he made his flower arrangements look more natural. The glass container in this painting was a favorite item for him. It first appeared in a painting from 1627 and last appeared in a flower piece from 1639 (Rijksmuseum, Amsterdam).

CuratorialText: When Pharaoh ordered all new-born Hebrew sons to be killed, Moses's mother put him in an ark of bulrushes in the Nile. His sister stayed to watch while Pharaoh's daughter came to the river with her maidens and found the child (Exodus 2: 2-4). WA1950.169 is striking for its emotional intensity, with the focus on Moses's grief-stricken parents. The joyful scene of the finding of Moses was a popular one, whereas this subject is far less common (Poussin earlier treated it c. 1627). Painted in 1654 for Jacques Stella, WA1950.169 was greatly admired by contemporaries for the artist's learned treatment of the subject, his skill in painting reflections and the beauty of the landscape. Pharoah's city is based on Pirro Ligorio's reconstruction of some of the most famous buildings of ancient Rome. A sphinx accompanied by a river-god and cornucopia identifies the Nile. Different explanations, based on Poussin's antiquarian interests and wide-ranging visual references, have been proposed for the significance of the pipes of Pan that hang from a tree behind this group. Two painted copies are recorded in the 17th century; other copies and variations are known (including a tapestry version of the right side) and three engraved versions were made, the first by Stella's niece, Claudine Bouzonnet Stella, in 1672.
persona1_adapted:
When Pharaoh ordered that all newborn Hebrew boys be killed, Moses’s mother placed him in a small boat made of reeds and set it in the Nile River. His sister stayed nearby to watch. Pharaoh’s daughter came to the river with her servants and found the baby (Exodus 2:2-4).

This painting is striking because of its strong emotions. It focuses on Moses’s grieving parents. The joyful scene of finding Moses was a popular subject for artists, but this sadder moment is much rarer. The French painter Nicolas Poussin created this work in 1654 for a man named Jacques Stella. People at the time admired it because Poussin showed deep knowledge of the story, skillfully painted reflections, and created a beautiful landscape.

Pharaoh’s city in the background is based on an artist’s reconstruction of famous buildings from ancient Rome. A sphinx, a river-god, and a cornucopia (a horn-shaped basket filled with food) identify the Nile River. Behind this group, pipes of Pan hang from a tree. Scholars have offered different explanations for what these pipes mean, based on Poussin’s interest in ancient history and his use of many visual references.

In the 1600s, two painted copies of this work were recorded. Other copies and variations also exist, including a tapestry version of the right side. Three engraved versions were made, the first by Stella’s niece, Claudine Bouzonnet Stella, in 1672.

CuratorialText: The brother of Masaccio, Lo Scheggia was an eclectic artist who painted numerous works for domestic settings. This type of decorative, ceremonial subject is often found on cassone panels. Although the picture is much abraded, it has sufficient affinities with the mature work of Lo Scheggia to make the attribution convincing.
persona1_adapted: Lo Scheggia was the brother of the artist Masaccio. He was a versatile painter who created many works for people's homes. This kind of decorative, ceremonial scene often appears on cassone panels—these were painted chests used for weddings or special occasions. Even though the painting is very worn, it still shows enough similarities to Lo Scheggia's later work to make the attribution believable.

CuratorialText: Lemon studied in Rome and in Paris with Carolus-Duran, but spent his early years in California. He exhibited at the Royal Academy from 1880 and came into contact with Giovanni Costa and his circle of Etruscans in Italy. This slight sketch from nature probably dates from the last years of the 19th century.
persona1_adapted: Lemon studied in Rome and Paris with the artist Carolus-Duran. But he spent his early years in California. Starting in 1880, he showed his work at the Royal Academy. In Italy, he met Giovanni Costa and his group of artists called the Etruscans. This small sketch from real life was probably made in the late 1890s.

CuratorialText: The subject, the flat and dry handling and the monumental quality of the figures show Shannon's debt to Puvis de Chavannes. The painting has been dated to c.1910 and is a variation on one of the artist's favourite themes.
persona1_adapted: The subject, the flat and dry painting style, and the large, impressive quality of the figures show that Shannon was influenced by Puvis de Chavannes. This painting was made around 1910. It is a version of one of the artist's favorite themes.

CuratorialText: Views in and around the village of Berck in northern France appear in many of the artist's paintings from 1870 until his death in 1898. This view across the dunes is precisely dated. The unbroken horizon and solitary figure in the foreground emphasize the windswept emptiness of the site.
persona1_adapted: The artist painted many scenes of Berck, a village in northern France, from 1870 until he died in 1898. This painting shows a view across the sand dunes, and it has a specific date. The flat horizon and the single person in the front highlight how empty and windy the place is.

CuratorialText: Traditionally but wrongly said to be a portrait of Antonio Stradivari, A863a is a portrait of a musician or instrument-maker of the late 16th century and identifications of Claudio Monteverdi (1567-1643) and Gasparo da Salo (1540-1609) have been proposed.
persona1_adapted: People often say this painting shows Antonio Stradivari, but that is not true. It actually shows a musician or instrument maker from the late 1500s. Some experts think it might be Claudio Monteverdi (1567-1643) or Gasparo da Salo (1540-1609).

CuratorialText: During his later years, Calvert produced many small works in thin oil colours on paper, the restrained colours and muted harmonies evocative of a dream world. They are generally peopled with figures from Greek mythology. Painted on a pink ground.
persona1_adapted: In his later years, Calvert created many small paintings on paper. He used thin oil paints and soft, quiet colors. These colors feel like a dream world. Most of the paintings show figures from Greek myths. He painted on a pink background.

CuratorialText: Traditionally attributed to Guido Reni as the Head of a Sibyl, WA1926.9 was recently identified as a copy after Cerrini. The same expressive head appears in his altar-piece of The Holy Family with Sts Catherine of Alexandria and Agnes painted for San Carlo alle Quattro Fontane, Rome (consecrated in 1646) and now in the convent there.
persona1_adapted: This painting was once thought to be by Guido Reni and called *Head of a Sibyl*. But experts recently found that it is actually a copy of a work by another artist, Cerrini. The same expressive face appears in Cerrini’s altar-piece, *The Holy Family with Sts Catherine of Alexandria and Agnes*. He painted that work for a church in Rome called San Carlo alle Quattro Fontane, which was consecrated in 1646. Today, that painting is kept in the convent there.

CuratorialText: One of Linnell's most important religious paintings, this was exhibited at the Royal Academy in 1835 and sold to the Art Union for the purposes of engraving in 1838. It was based on sketches made in 1829, when Linnell was closely associated with the 'Ancients' at Shoreham. Samuel Palmer was the model for the disciple on the right. The source is Luke 24: 13-35.
persona1_adapted: This is one of John Linnell’s most important religious paintings. It was shown at the Royal Academy in 1835. In 1838, it was sold to the Art Union so it could be turned into an engraving (a printed copy). Linnell based the painting on sketches he made in 1829. At that time, he was working closely with a group of artists called the “Ancients” in Shoreham. The disciple on the right was modeled after the artist Samuel Palmer. The story comes from the Bible, Luke 24: 13-35.

CuratorialText: Design is an impulse that unifies us across time and culture. This installation unifies two traditionally disparate collecting fields, fashion and textiles with decorative and industrial arts to better understand what underlies our motivations and capacity for design. Ensembles from the Iris Apfel Rare Bird of Fashion collection celebrate the exuberant remixing and inventive styling of one of the world’s most prominent fashion icons, while constellations of unique and culturally significant works of design, fashion, and textiles explore distinctive and resourceful forms of creative expression. A recent gallery highlight is the display of 38 vibrant and flamboyant ensembles from the collection of Boston-based entrepreneur and fashion icon Yolanda Cellucci. Yolanda’s, based in Waltham, Massachusetts, was a hotspot for lavish evening and wedding ensembles and that became a household name throughout the region, and a shorthand for glitz and glamor in the late 20th century. Cellucci generously donated 57 works of fashion and accessories to PEM in 2021, along with archival photographs and materials related to the legacy of her bridal shop.
persona1_adapted:
Design connects people across time and cultures. This exhibit brings together two areas that are usually separate: fashion and textiles, along with decorative and industrial arts. The goal is to explore what drives us to create and design things.

Outfits from the Iris Apfel Rare Bird of Fashion collection show the bold mixing and creative styling of one of the world’s most famous fashion icons. At the same time, groups of unique and culturally important works of design, fashion, and textiles highlight creative and clever ways of expressing ideas.

A recent highlight of the gallery is the display of 38 bright and flashy outfits from the collection of Yolanda Cellucci, a business owner and fashion icon from Boston. Her shop, Yolanda’s, was located in Waltham, Massachusetts. It was a popular place for fancy evening gowns and wedding dresses. In the late 1900s, the store became well-known across the region and was a symbol of glamour and sparkle. In 2021, Cellucci generously donated 57 fashion pieces and accessories to PEM, along with old photos and materials about the history of her bridal shop.

CuratorialText: Born in Greenbush, New York in 1844, Lewis became the first sculptor of Black and Indigenous (Mississauga) descent to achieve international recognition. Beginning her career in Boston in 1863, she traveled to Rome in 1866 to join the leading American sculptors of her generation, breaking international, racial and gender barriers. "Sometimes the times were dark and the outlook was lonesome, but where there is a will, there is a way,” Lewis recalled in 1878. “That is what I tell my people whenever I meet them, that they must not be discouraged, but work ahead until the world is bound to respect them for what they have accomplished.” Following her death in 1907, Lewis’ legacy endured in Black communities, yet her contribution to American sculpture has largely been underrecognized. Some of her great masterpieces were rediscovered decades later, while others remain lost today. Her vivid, naturalistic stone sculptures feature abolitionists and social reformers, engage with Indigenous identity and give new life to religious and mythological figures.
persona1_adapted:
Edmonia Lewis was born in Greenbush, New York, in 1844. She became the first sculptor of Black and Indigenous (Mississauga) descent to win worldwide fame. She started her career in Boston in 1863. In 1866, she moved to Rome to join the top American sculptors of her time. She broke through barriers of race, gender, and nationality.

In 1878, Lewis said, “Sometimes the times were dark and the outlook was lonesome, but where there is a will, there is a way. That is what I tell my people whenever I meet them, that they must not be discouraged, but work ahead until the world is bound to respect them for what they have accomplished.”

After she died in 1907, Lewis’ legacy lived on in Black communities. But her work as a sculptor was largely overlooked. Some of her greatest masterpieces were rediscovered decades later. Others are still missing today. Her stone sculptures are vivid and lifelike. They show abolitionists and social reformers, explore Indigenous identity, and give new life to religious and mythological figures.

CuratorialText: Home to 3.7 million people, 85 species of animals, 32,000 species of insects and 2 billion migratory birds, the boreal forest spans nearly the entire Northern Hemisphere, just below the Arctic Circle, and makes up one of the world’s last remaining stretches of true wilderness. Discover the biodiversity and global importance of our northernmost forests through first-person stories, commissioned objects, interactive experiences and exquisite photography and videography. The boreal forest is critical to the health of our entire planet. It helps regulate Earth’s climate, is the world’s largest land biome, is a critical water reservoir and holds two to three times as much carbon as tropical forests.
persona1_adapted:
The boreal forest stretches across the Northern Hemisphere, just below the Arctic Circle. It is home to 3.7 million people, 85 species of animals, 32,000 species of insects, and 2 billion migratory birds. This forest is one of the last true wilderness areas left on Earth.

Through first-person stories, specially made objects, hands-on activities, and stunning photos and videos, you can explore the biodiversity and global importance of these northern forests.

The boreal forest is vital to the health of our planet. It helps control Earth’s climate and is the world’s largest land biome—a biome is a large natural area with its own climate and plants. It also stores two to three times more carbon than tropical forests and acts as a critical water reservoir.

CuratorialText: Salem’s connections to the Declaration of Independence center around Ezekiel Russell, a local printer who played a key role in publishing and disseminating the Declaration in 1776. This exhibition coincides with the 250th anniversary of the American Revolution as well as the 400th anniversary of the European settlement of Salem. Works on view in the intimate James Duncan Phillips Trust Gallery include two of the earliest broadside editions of the Declaration of Independence alongside Revolutionary-era manuscripts, newspapers, pamphlets and broadsides that showcase the nation’s founding values of democracy: freedom, liberty and equality.
persona1_adapted: Salem’s link to the Declaration of Independence comes through Ezekiel Russell. He was a local printer. In 1776, he helped print and spread the Declaration. This exhibition marks the 250th anniversary of the American Revolution. It also marks 400 years since Europeans first settled in Salem. The show is in the small James Duncan Phillips Trust Gallery. It features two of the earliest large printed copies of the Declaration. You will also see letters, newspapers, pamphlets, and other prints from the Revolutionary time. These items show the nation’s founding ideas of democracy: freedom, liberty, and equality.

CuratorialText: These films honor the lesser-known stories of Salem and offer a behind-the-scenes look at what makes this city so remarkable. Produced over the course of more than seven years by Perry Hallinan, Joe Cultrera and many other filmmakers for presentation at the Salem Film Fest, these short films span topics from breakfast at Red’s to the annual Black Picnic at Salem Willows. For longtime Salem residents, these stories might spark nostalgia; for new residents and tourists, they illuminate Salem’s vibrant communities, bygone businesses and traditions that local filmmakers have long worked to record and preserve. Beyond the Broom showcases the vibrant community of Salem, one with an identity that extends beyond its witch trials history. This exhibition is part of the 2026 celebration of Salem 400+, which commemorates the quadricentennial of the city’s settlement while honoring the preexisting Indigenous community. The initiative aims to engage the Salem community and ensure a vibrant and sustainable city for generations to come.
persona1_adapted:
These films tell the lesser-known stories of Salem. They also show what makes this city so special. Filmmakers Perry Hallinan, Joe Cultrera, and many others made them over seven years. The films were shown at the Salem Film Fest. They cover many topics, from breakfast at Red’s to the annual Black Picnic at Salem Willows.

For people who have lived in Salem a long time, these stories may bring back memories. For new residents and tourists, they reveal Salem’s lively communities, old businesses, and traditions. Local filmmakers have worked hard to record and save these stories.

Beyond the Broom shows that Salem is a vibrant community. Its identity goes beyond the witch trials history. This exhibition is part of the 2026 celebration of Salem 400+. This event marks 400 years since the city was settled. It also honors the Indigenous people who lived there before. The goal is to bring the Salem community together and help create a strong, lasting city for future generations.

CuratorialText: Explore several hundred fascinating objects inside the historic hall, representing a sampling of the many collections that were displayed in the museum during its earliest years. This installation gives voice to early collectors, trading partners and museum visitors about these objects — with some accounts extracted from records in the museum’s vast archive — and shares perspectives from a range of people living today. These captivating stories and works that reflect local and global history, as well as the impact of maritime trade, recenter East India Marine Hall as the heart of the museum. From its inception, this space and the objects in it have elicited empathy, curiosity and wonder and transported visitors to places around the world. This project has been made possible in part by a grant from the National Endowment for the Humanities: Democracy demands wisdom. Major support is provided by Mr. Ulf B. Heide, Tim and Joanie Ingraham, Alex Ingraham, and Sam Ingraham. Generous support has been provided by Haven Trust, Mr. and Mrs. James B. Hawkes, Mr. and Mrs. Howard B. Hodgson Jr., Angus and Leslie Littlejohn, Chip and Susan Robie, Mr. and Mrs. Stuart W. Pratt, Katie Huddleson and Alan Kraning, Edward A. Studzinski and Anne G. Studzinski, the Salem Marine Society, and Henry Birdseye Weil and Ann Uppington. Additional support has been provided by The Lee and Juliet Folger Fund, Jonathan B. Loring, Walter C. Meibaum III, Jean Verbridge, and Connie and Stan Grayson.
persona1_adapted:
Step inside this historic hall and explore hundreds of fascinating objects. They show a sample of the many collections that were on display here in the museum’s earliest years.

This exhibit shares the voices of early collectors, trading partners, and museum visitors. Some of their stories come from records in the museum’s vast archive. It also includes perspectives from a range of people living today.

These captivating stories and objects reflect local and global history. They also show the impact of maritime trade. Together, they place East India Marine Hall back at the heart of the museum.

From the very beginning, this space and the objects in it have sparked empathy, curiosity, and wonder. They have transported visitors to places around the world.

This project was made possible in part by a grant from the National Endowment for the Humanities: Democracy demands wisdom. Major support comes from Mr. Ulf B. Heide, Tim and Joanie Ingraham, Alex Ingraham, and Sam Ingraham. Generous support has been provided by Haven Trust, Mr. and Mrs. James B. Hawkes, Mr. and Mrs. Howard B. Hodgson Jr., Angus and Leslie Littlejohn, Chip and Susan Robie, Mr. and Mrs. Stuart W. Pratt, Katie Huddleson and Alan Kraning, Edward A. Studzinski and Anne G. Studzinski, the Salem Marine Society, and Henry Birdseye Weil and Ann Uppington. Additional support has been provided by The Lee and Juliet Folger Fund, Jonathan B. Loring, Walter C. Meibaum III, Jean Verbridge, and Connie and Stan Grayson.

CuratorialText: Renowned for its expansive holdings of decorative arts and paintings, PEM’s American art collection spans four centuries of creative expression in the United States. Rooted in our historic holdings that celebrate the rich and diverse artistic and cultural heritage of Essex County, Massachusetts, the collection has continued to grow to embrace a broader representation of the art of this country. The artists represented in this Seamans gallery use a variety of materials — wood, metal, glass, clay, paint, fiber and more — to convey their creative ambitions. Many of the artistic practices and decorative inspirations behind these works have long histories, with their origins traceable to Europe, Asia and the Americas. These examples of formal perfection, domestic utility and luxury, and innovative twists on cultural traditions tell a larger story of our global connectedness. Together, these works tell compelling stories of American life and the ongoing cultural exchanges between the people of the region, the country and the wider world. Located in the Seamans Gallery.
persona1_adapted:
PEM’s American art collection is famous for its large number of decorative arts and paintings. It covers four centuries of creative work in the United States. The collection started with historic pieces that celebrate the rich and diverse art and culture of Essex County, Massachusetts. Over time, it has grown to include a wider range of American art.

The artists in this Seamans gallery use many different materials—like wood, metal, glass, clay, paint, and fiber—to express their creative ideas. Many of the art styles and decorative ideas behind these works have long histories. Their origins can be traced back to Europe, Asia, and the Americas.

These objects show formal perfection, everyday use and luxury, and new twists on cultural traditions. Together, they tell a bigger story about how we are all connected around the world. They also share compelling stories of American life and the ongoing cultural exchanges between people of this region, the country, and the wider world. This gallery is located in the Seamans Gallery.

PEM’s American art collection is famous for its large number of decorative arts and paintings. It covers four hundred years of creative expression in the United States. The collection started with historic pieces that celebrate the rich and diverse art and culture of Essex County, Massachusetts. Over time, it has grown to include a wider range of American art.

The artists in this Seamans gallery use many different materials—like wood, metal, glass, clay, paint, and fiber—to show their creative ideas. Many of the art techniques and decorative styles in these works have long histories. Their origins can be traced back to Europe, Asia, and the Americas.

CuratorialText: Persian and Turkish architecture, textiles and miniature paintings inspire the precise, stylized floral forms that compose Anila Quayyum Agha’s sculptural chamber of light and shadow. This luminous installation located in the Wheatland Family gallery provides an opportunity to contemplate the differences and commonalities that shape our lives and relationships. Originally from Lahore, Pakistan, and now living in the US, Agha is acutely attuned to the social codes that inform the lives of Muslim women and all immigrants. She describes this work as her effort to create a sense of how women can reclaim and safely open up private space to welcome others. Located in the Wheatland Family Gallery.
persona1_adapted:
Anila Quayyum Agha’s artwork is a room filled with light and shadow. It is made from a carved wooden box with a light inside. The patterns on the box come from Persian and Turkish art. They look like flowers and leaves, and they are very precise and stylized. This means the shapes are simplified and arranged in a decorative way.

The installation is in the Wheatland Family Gallery. It asks us to think about what makes us different and what we share. Agha was born in Lahore, Pakistan, and now lives in the United States. She knows the rules that shape the lives of Muslim women and immigrants. She says this work is her way of showing how women can take back private space and open it up to welcome others.

CuratorialText:
Merchants across the globe went to great lengths to acquire these spectacular commodities. Now known as Asian export art, these objects connected societies and created a complex global economy that continues to shape our world to this day.

PEM’s Asian Export Art collection, foremost in the world, explores cross-cultural exchange as a catalyst for creativity and celebrates the interplay of commerce and creative expression. The gallery features more than 200 works of art made in diverse media by artists in China, Japan, and South Asia. These transcultural objects demonstrate the beauty and ingenuity that can be created through blending artistic traditions, materials, and technologies. Porcelain, textiles, tea, ivory, and silver were the focus of intensive trade activity between Asia and the rest of the world. This installation in the Sean M. Healey Family gallery also addresses the uncomfortable truth that many of these works of art were originally purchased with profits derived from the illegal opium trade. During the 1800s, millions of Indian and Chinese lives were devastated by opium, a foreshadowing of today’s opioid crisis.
persona1_adapted:
Merchants around the world went to great lengths to get these amazing goods. Today we call them Asian export art. These objects connected different societies. They also created a complex global economy that still affects our world today.

PEM’s Asian Export Art collection is the best in the world. It shows how cross-cultural exchange—when people from different cultures share ideas—sparks creativity. The collection also celebrates the link between trade and art. The gallery has more than 200 works of art. They were made in many different materials by artists from China, Japan, and South Asia. These objects show the beauty and cleverness that come from mixing different art styles, materials, and techniques.

Porcelain, textiles, tea, ivory, and silver were the main items traded between Asia and the rest of the world. This exhibit in the Sean M. Healey Family gallery also talks about a hard truth. Many of these artworks were bought with money from the illegal opium trade. In the 1800s, millions of people in India and China suffered from opium addiction. This was an early sign of today’s opioid crisis.

CuratorialText: This exhibition explores America as a place where disparate viewpoints are brought together: America as both longed for and greatly revered. Previously on view in the context of PEM's 2019 Jacob Lawrence: The American Struggle exhibition, Bethany Collins, America: A Hymnal presents a solo exhibition of Collins’ art as an immersive experience. Explore 100 different versions of the popular patriotic anthem “My Country ‘Tis of Thee,” rewritten between the 18th and 20th centuries in support of various American causes. Versions of the lyrics are bound together chronologically in Collins’ artist book, where she burns away the musical notes with a laser — chronicling an idea of America through the history of one song’s varied verses. A range of these powerful voices and expressions are heard throughout the gallery in a sound piece, what Collins calls, “a familiar chaos of dissenting versions of what it means to be … American bound together.” The gallery is wallpapered with a newly commissioned work of flocked flower silhouettes. Each flower contains a meaning as the artist uses floriography, the symbolic language of flowers, to relay messages of hope, longing and loss to further illustrate the evolution and complexity of American experiences. In addition to the artwork on view, a study center offers deeper dives into Collins' artistic approaches and historical sources. Through interactives, PEM collection objects and media components, Bethany Collins, America: A Hymnal inspires us to activate our own civic mindset. Located in the Jurrien Timmer Gallery.
persona1_adapted:
This exhibition looks at America as a place where different viewpoints come together. It shows America as both a dream and something deeply respected. The show was first part of PEM’s 2019 exhibition *Jacob Lawrence: The American Struggle*. Now, *Bethany Collins: America: A Hymnal* is a solo show of Collins’ art that you can walk into and experience.

Explore 100 different versions of the patriotic song “My Country ‘Tis of Thee.” People rewrote this song between the 1700s and 1900s to support many different American causes. Collins bound these lyrics together in order by date in her artist book. She used a laser to burn away the musical notes. This shows an idea of America through the history of one song’s many different verses.

In the gallery, you can hear a range of these powerful voices in a sound piece. Collins calls it “a familiar chaos of dissenting versions of what it means to be … American bound together.” The walls are covered with a new artwork made of flocked flower silhouettes. Each flower has a meaning. Collins uses floriography, which is the symbolic language of flowers, to send messages of hope, longing, and loss. This helps show how American experiences have changed and grown more complex.

A study center in the gallery lets you dive deeper into Collins’ artistic methods and historical sources. Through hands-on activities, objects from PEM’s collection, and media pieces, *Bethany Collins: America: A Hymnal* inspires us to think about our own role as citizens. The exhibition is in the Jurrien Timmer Gallery.

CuratorialText: Encompassing everyday objects and the fine arts, the museum’s Japanese collection is distinguished for its range, from paintings and sculpture to decorative arts and costumes and textiles. Spanning Hokkaido in the North to Okinawa in the South, and prehistory to the present, these objects illuminate the varied artistic and cultural traditions of Japan. Toward the end of the 19th century, Edward Sylvester Morse, director of the Peabody Academy of Science (a precursor to PEM), became one of the first Western scholars to visit modern Japan after it opened to travelers. Morse was highly influential in encouraging American interest in Japanese art and culture, and helped create this remarkable Japanese collection.
persona1_adapted:
The museum’s Japanese collection includes everyday objects and fine art. It stands out for its wide range, from paintings and sculptures to decorative arts, costumes, and textiles. The collection covers Japan from Hokkaido in the north to Okinawa in the south, and from prehistoric times to today. These objects show the many artistic and cultural traditions of Japan.

In the late 1800s, Edward Sylvester Morse was the director of the Peabody Academy of Science, an earlier version of PEM. He became one of the first Western scholars to visit modern Japan after it opened to travelers. Morse helped spark American interest in Japanese art and culture. He also played a key role in building this remarkable collection.

CuratorialText: From the late Joseon dynasty (1392–1910) to today, PEM’s exceptional collection explores art, culture, and life in Korea during a time of significant transition and global connection. PEM’s early Korean collection was primarily formed by Edward Sylvester Morse, director of the Peabody Academy of Science (a predecessor organization to PEM), who proactively pursued Korean acquisitions during his tenure from 1880 to 1914. Among his most significant acquisitions from this period is a set of eight Korean musical instruments played at the 1893 World’s Columbian Exposition. PEM’s connection with Korea was also strengthened by Yu Kil-Chun (1856-1914), the reformist scholar and politician who came to the U.S. as a member of the first Korean delegation and lived near Salem for nearly two years. Yu Kil-Chun and other early travelers between the U.S. and Korea donated many meaningful and foundational works to the museum’s collection. PEM’s Yu Kil-Chun Gallery of Korean Art and Culture brings to life the compelling stories of these early pioneers, presented alongside the museum’s renowned Korean textile collection, exquisite 19th-century paintings — including the Welcoming Banquet of the Governor of Pyeongan screen — and inspiring contemporary works by Nam June Paik (1932–2006) and other Korean and Korean American artists.
persona1_adapted:
The late Joseon dynasty (1392–1910) was a time of big change in Korea. PEM’s collection shows the art, culture, and daily life from that period up to today. It also highlights Korea’s growing connections with the world.

Much of PEM’s early Korean collection came from Edward Sylvester Morse. He was the director of the Peabody Academy of Science, one of the museums that later became PEM. From 1880 to 1914, he actively collected Korean objects. One of his most important finds was a set of eight Korean musical instruments. These instruments were played at the 1893 World’s Columbian Exposition in Chicago.

PEM’s link to Korea grew stronger thanks to Yu Kil-Chun (1856–1914). He was a scholar and politician who wanted to reform Korea. Yu came to the U.S. as part of the first official Korean delegation. He lived near Salem, Massachusetts, for almost two years. Yu and other early travelers between the U.S. and Korea donated many important items to the museum.

The Yu Kil-Chun Gallery of Korean Art and Culture at PEM tells the stories of these early pioneers. The gallery also displays the museum’s famous Korean textile collection. You can see beautiful 19th-century paintings there, like the *Welcoming Banquet of the Governor of Pyeongan* screen. And you will find inspiring modern works by Nam June Paik (1932–2006) and other Korean and Korean American artists.

CuratorialText: PEM’s Maritime art collection, which is the finest in the country, frames the sea as an enduring source of opportunity as well as peril, a force that inspires creativity and innovation, and encourages engagement with the wider world. This installation located in the Byrne Family gallery offers a global perspective on our creative response to the sea, from a 17th-century brass Pakistani astrolabe to Valerie Hagerty’s monumental Shipwrecked Armoire with Barnacles (2012) and artworks that reflect Salem’s rich history of maritime trade and exploration. Immersive digital media amplify compelling stories that lay behind unassuming objects, like a calendar stick from 1803 whose carved notches record the long days that Rhode Island native, James Drown, spent shipwrecked and left for dead on Tristan da Cunha, a remote speck island in the South Atlantic.
persona1_adapted: PEM’s Maritime art collection is the best in the country. It shows the sea as both a source of opportunity and danger. The sea inspires creativity and new ideas, and it encourages people to connect with the wider world. This exhibit in the Byrne Family Gallery looks at how people around the world have responded to the sea through art. You’ll see a brass astrolabe from 1600s Pakistan, a tool used for navigation. You’ll also see Valerie Hagerty’s large sculpture *Shipwrecked Armoire with Barnacles* (2012). Other artworks reflect Salem’s rich history of sea trade and exploration. Digital displays bring powerful stories to life. For example, a calendar stick from 1803 has carved notches. Those notches record the long days that James Drown, a man from Rhode Island, spent shipwrecked and left for dead on Tristan da Cunha, a tiny island in the South Atlantic.

CuratorialText: Bringing two extraordinary collections of Native American and American art together for the first time in our institution's history, this long-term installation celebrates artistic achievements across time, space, and worldviews. Located in the Nancy and George Putnam Gallery and Barbara Weld Putnam Gallery, the 250 artworks on display span in time from 10,000 years ago to the present, and demonstrate a range of voices, modes of expression, cultures, and media including: sculpture, paintings, textiles and costumes, furniture, decorative arts, works on paper, installations, video, and a re-envisioned period room. The gallery also includes original documents and objects from the Salem witch trials, one of the most iconic dramas in American history. Between May 1692 and March 1693, the court sentenced 25 innocent people in the Salem region to death. The new installation will be responsive to these urgent times by giving our public an opportunity to grapple with the links, continuities, and disjunctions of our complex histories in America in order to shape a more connected and empathetic present and future. By rethinking America’s histories, visitors are asked to consider how we might envision a better future together.
persona1_adapted:
This new, long-term exhibit brings together two amazing collections—Native American art and American art—for the first time in our museum’s history. It celebrates artistic achievements across different times, places, and ways of seeing the world. Located in the Nancy and George Putnam Gallery and the Barbara Weld Putnam Gallery, the show features 250 artworks. These pieces span from 10,000 years ago to today. They include sculptures, paintings, textiles, costumes, furniture, decorative arts, works on paper, installations, video, and a reimagined period room.

The gallery also includes original documents and objects from the Salem witch trials, one of the most famous events in American history. Between May 1692 and March 1693, the court sentenced 25 innocent people in the Salem area to death. This new installation responds to our urgent times. It gives visitors a chance to explore the links, continuities, and breaks in our complex American histories. The goal is to help shape a more connected and understanding present and future. By rethinking America’s histories, visitors are asked to consider how we might imagine a better future together.

CuratorialText: These figures, from disparate cultures around the world and across time, embody power as both a fundamental social dynamic and a part of our innate wiring to respond to figures and faces. Curved niches encourage visitors to engage with each artwork, decluttering the field of view and focusing attention. Poetically concise labels heighten the emotional tenor of the experience. Located in the Louise Du Pont Crowninshield Gallery.
persona1_adapted: These figures come from different cultures around the world and from different time periods. They show power as a basic part of how societies work. They also tap into our natural instinct to react to faces and bodies. Curved wall spaces help visitors focus on each artwork. This design clears away distractions and directs your attention. Short, poetic labels make the emotional experience stronger. This gallery is named the Louise Du Pont Crowninshield Gallery.

CuratorialText: The exhibition starts at “A is for Always Indigenous” to acknowledge the Native communities who have lived for millennia on the land where the museum now sits. It ends with Z is for Zoology and coincides with the return to the galleries of a leatherback turtle specimen captured in 1885, a favorite of longtime visitors. Salem is a city with many stories of local, national and international significance. Alexander Graham Bell completed the first successful long-distance telephone call from Salem in 1877. Parker Brothers produced Monopoly here. And in 2013, President Obama signed legislation recognizing the city as the birthplace of the United States National Guard. Using selections from PEM’s vast collection, Salem Stories features more than 100 works, including paintings, sculpture, textiles, decorative arts, photographs, natural history specimens, manuscripts, posters, books, eyewitness accounts, and even a murder weapon. The A–Z structure creates an accessible and entertaining way to engage with Salem’s history, from past to present day. This is an evolving story, one that will continue to unfold throughout the life of the exhibition. Located in the Peggy and Lloyd Hilborn Gallery.
persona1_adapted:
The exhibition starts with “A is for Always Indigenous.” This part honors the Native communities who have lived on this land for thousands of years. It ends with “Z is for Zoology.” That section includes the return of a leatherback turtle specimen caught in 1885. It is a favorite of longtime visitors.

Salem is a city with many important stories. Some are local, and some are national or global. In 1877, Alexander Graham Bell made the first successful long-distance phone call from Salem. Parker Brothers created the game Monopoly here. In 2013, President Obama signed a law naming Salem the birthplace of the United States National Guard.

The exhibition uses items from PEM’s large collection. *Salem Stories* includes more than 100 works. You will see paintings, sculptures, textiles, decorative arts, photographs, natural history specimens, manuscripts, posters, books, eyewitness accounts, and even a murder weapon.

The A–Z format makes it easy and fun to explore Salem’s history, from the past to today. This story is still growing. It will keep changing as the exhibition continues. The show is located in the Peggy and Lloyd Hilborn Gallery.

CuratorialText: Bridging myth with social and political history, the Chester and Davida Herwitz Gallery tells the story of nation-building and self-discovery through works by India's most celebrated artistic geniuses of the 20th century. Following independence from British rule in 1947, artists in India aimed to uncover a visual language that was uniquely Indian in inspiration to convey their experiences, struggles, ambitions and dreams. The Prashant H. Fadia Foundation and Deshpande Foundation Gallery features a selection of objects from PEM's extensive collection of historical material from India. Focused primarily on the 19th century, the gallery considers India's long and complex history of foreign occupation, and its troubling impact on the representation of Indian people in art. Featuring some of the earliest objects to come to PEM, including unfired clay sculpture, mica paintings and Kalighat paintings, the gallery considers and questions the timeless tropes of India that persist even today.
persona1_adapted:
The Chester and Davida Herwitz Gallery connects myth with social and political history. It tells the story of building a nation and finding identity through works by India's most famous 20th-century artists. After India gained independence from British rule in 1947, artists wanted to create a visual language that was truly Indian. They used this language to share their experiences, struggles, hopes, and dreams.

The Prashant H. Fadia Foundation and Deshpande Foundation Gallery shows objects from PEM's large collection of historical Indian art. This gallery focuses mainly on the 19th century. It looks at India's long and complex history of foreign rule and how that affected the way Indian people were shown in art. The gallery includes some of the earliest objects at PEM, like unfired clay sculptures, mica paintings, and Kalighat paintings. It asks viewers to think about the lasting images of India that still exist today.

CuratorialText: This diverse selection of contemporary glass represents the work of more than 40 of the most influential glass artists from the 20th century to today. Donated to PEM in 2022 by New York-based collectors Carl and Betty Pforzheimer, this collection is remarkable for its range of forms, scale and techniques in glass. The Pforzheimers’ gift significantly expands PEM’s noted historic glass collection while celebrating the extraordinary aesthetic possibilities that arise from the combination of sand and fire.
persona1_adapted: This selection of modern glass shows the work of over 40 of the most important glass artists from the 1900s to today. New York collectors Carl and Betty Pforzheimer gave these pieces to PEM in 2022. The collection stands out because of its wide range of shapes, sizes, and glass-making methods. The Pforzheimers’ gift greatly adds to PEM’s well-known historic glass collection. It also celebrates the amazing beauty that comes from mixing sand and fire.

CuratorialText: The Pod is part of PEM's Dotty Brown Art & Nature Center, which investigates our place in nature through contemporary art, memorable objects and interactive experiences. Designed for visitors of all ages, the family-friendly center features installations, changing exhibitions, hands-on displays and art-making spaces. Grow your awareness of nature, try on new ways of being, and tap into your own creativity. Discover a life-size wire tree by sculptor Elizabeth Keithline modeled on a live tree from PEM’s campus. Watch dancer Laura Kathrein move as different animals and embody local habitats. And create your own imaginary creature inspired by real animals and fantastical beasts from around the globe. What creative sense will be sparked in you on your visit to The Pod? What comes to mind when you think of a pod? A pea pod, an iPod or maybe a futuristic orb? Jane Winchell, the Sarah Fraser Robbins Director of the Art & Nature Center and curator of The Pod, describes her idea of a pod and what you’ll encounter when you visit our new multisensory exhibition space. There is something for everyone here, ranging from fabulous, nature-inspired contemporary and historic artworks to stations where you can explore nature up close or make your own art. Families with young children will discover the Wonder Zone which includes activity bins, a light table and picture books just for them
persona1_adapted:
The Pod is part of PEM's Dotty Brown Art & Nature Center. This space explores our connection to nature through modern art, interesting objects, and hands-on activities. It is designed for visitors of all ages. The center includes art installations, changing exhibits, interactive displays, and areas where you can make your own art.

Grow your awareness of nature. Try new ways of thinking and being. Tap into your own creativity. Discover a life-size wire tree by sculptor Elizabeth Keithline. She modeled it after a real tree from PEM’s campus. Watch dancer Laura Kathrein move like different animals and show how local habitats feel. Then create your own imaginary creature. Get inspired by real animals and fantastical beasts from around the world.

What creative sense will be sparked in you during your visit to The Pod? What comes to mind when you think of a pod? A pea pod, an iPod, or maybe a futuristic orb? Jane Winchell is the Sarah Fraser Robbins Director of the Art & Nature Center and curator of The Pod. She shares her idea of a pod and what you’ll find in this new multisensory exhibit space.

There is something for everyone here. You’ll see amazing nature-inspired artworks, both modern and historic. You can explore nature up close at different stations. Or you can make your own art. Families with young children will discover the Wonder Zone. It includes activity bins, a light table, and picture books just for them.

CuratorialText: This extraordinary series of events between June 1692 and March 1693 led to the deaths of 25 innocent women, men and children who were wrongfully convicted of crimes. More than 300 years later, the personal tragedies and grievous wrongs that occurred still provoke us to reflect and reckon with the experiences of those involved. Learn the true story of this tragedy as told through the voices and with the possessions of those directly involved. This ongoing installation tells this story through court documents and authentic historic objects presented as tangible fragments directly tied to people in Salem and nearby communities in the late 17th century. A handwritten petition, a carved loom, a walking stick — each illuminates an aspect of individuals who lived through Salem’s witch trials and serves as a reminder of the real people impacted by these harrowing events. The Salem Witch Trials 1692 is organized by the Peabody Essex Museum. This exhibition is made possible by Carolyn and Peter S. Lynch and The Lynch Foundation. We thank Jennifer and Andrew Borggaard, James B. and Mary Lou Hawkes, Chip and Susan Robie, and Timothy T. Hilton as supporters of the Exhibition Innovation Fund. We also recognize the generosity of the East India Marine Associates of the Peabody Essex Museum.
persona1_adapted:
Between June 1692 and March 1693, a series of terrible events took place. Twenty-five innocent people—women, men, and children—were wrongly convicted of crimes and died. More than 300 years later, these personal tragedies still make us think about what happened to those involved.

Learn the true story of this tragedy. It is told through the voices and belongings of the people who lived through it. This ongoing exhibit uses court documents and real historic objects. These items are direct links to people in Salem and nearby towns in the late 1600s.

A handwritten petition, a carved loom, a walking stick—each object shows a part of someone’s life during the Salem witch trials. They remind us that real people were affected by these frightening events.

The Salem Witch Trials 1692 is organized by the Peabody Essex Museum. This exhibition is made possible by Carolyn and Peter S. Lynch and The Lynch Foundation. We thank Jennifer and Andrew Borggaard, James B. and Mary Lou Hawkes, Chip and Susan Robie, and Timothy T. Hilton for their support of the Exhibition Innovation Fund. We also recognize the generosity of the East India Marine Associates of the Peabody Essex Museum.

CuratorialText: The artist researched the museum’s vast and varied holdings, diving deep to find unexpected connections across time, cultures, and materials. Platacis turned her findings into 210 canvas stencils—all drawn and cut by hand with X-Acto blades. After applying the stencils to the walls, she and her assistant used a variety of spray-paint and graffiti techniques, including drop shadows, high contrast, and layering, to apply color to the walls and give dimensionality and life to her forms. Organic forms and curvilinear lines emerged as unifying design motifs that speak to the natural world. As you move deeper into these galleries, floral forms give way to enchanting fauna and larger-than-life presentations of familiar and unexpected objects. Platacis’ labor-intensive contemporary approach to painting connects to the artistry and skill embedded within the historical objects in PEM’s collection, creating a fascinating interplay between artistic practice then and now. What discoveries await? Located in the H.A. Crosby Forbes and Pamela Cunningham Copeland galleries.
persona1_adapted: The artist studied the museum’s huge and varied collection. She searched for surprising links between different times, cultures, and materials. Platacis turned what she found into 210 canvas stencils. She drew and cut each one by hand using X-Acto blades. After placing the stencils on the walls, she and her assistant used spray-paint and graffiti methods. They added drop shadows, high contrast, and layers of color. These techniques gave depth and life to her shapes. Curving lines and organic forms became the main design themes. They remind us of the natural world. As you walk deeper into the galleries, flower shapes give way to magical animals and oversized versions of familiar and unexpected objects. Platacis’s careful, hands-on painting style connects to the skill and artistry found in the historical objects at PEM. This creates a fascinating link between art made long ago and art made today. What discoveries await you? Find them in the H.A. Crosby Forbes and Pamela Cunningham Copeland galleries.
