CuratorialText: As one of the 20th century’s most critically acclaimed and genre-blurring artists, Noguchi created a remarkable design legacy encompassing sculpture, lighting, furniture, ceramics and designs for public spaces such as gardens, playgrounds and plazas. Organized by the High Museum of Art in Atlanta, Isamu Noguchi: "I am not a designer" shines a light on the artist’s limitless creativity and social consciousness. Discover the evolution of Noguchi’s design work, particularly the way he created environments through theatrical set design, furniture and inclusive public spaces — all of which were intentionally designed to improve the way people lived. Many of his designs remain incredibly popular, such as the celebrated coffee table and Akari light sculptures that you may recognize in stores today. As Noguchi once said, “Everything is sculpture. Any material, any idea without hindrance born into space, I consider sculpture.” Noguchi also excelled at working collaboratively and forming close relationships with other creative professionals, particularly with modern dancer and choreographer Martha Graham, American architect R. Buckminster Fuller and Romanian sculptor and painter Constantin Brâncuși. These fruitful creative relationships are also highlighted within the exhibition.
persona1_adapted:
Isamu Noguchi was one of the most celebrated artists of the 1900s. He mixed different styles and created many kinds of art. His work includes sculptures, lamps, furniture, pottery, and designs for public spaces like gardens, playgrounds, and plazas. The exhibition *Isamu Noguchi: “I am not a designer”* was organized by the High Museum of Art in Atlanta. It shows Noguchi’s endless creativity and his concern for society.

The exhibition explores how Noguchi’s design work changed over time. He created environments through theater sets, furniture, and public spaces that were open to everyone. He designed these things on purpose to make people’s lives better. Many of his designs are still very popular today. For example, you might recognize his famous coffee table or his Akari light sculptures in stores.

Noguchi once said, “Everything is sculpture. Any material, any idea without hindrance born into space, I consider sculpture.” He also worked well with others. He formed close friendships with other creative people, such as dancer and choreographer Martha Graham, architect R. Buckminster Fuller, and sculptor Constantin Brâncuși. These important working relationships are also featured in the exhibition.

CuratorialText: In the nearly two decades since PEM featured the crowd-pleasing exhibition Origami Now!, contemporary origami has continued to expand into exciting new styles of expression and invention. This multigenerational exhibition in the Dotty Brown Art & Nature Center highlights origami as not just an exciting art form, but also an important influence on new innovations in science, engineering, math and more. See works from leading origami artists, including Michael G. LaFosse, one of the world’s top origami masters and a Massachusetts resident. In addition to showcasing masterworks of origami, PEM will highlight what is emerging in contemporary origami and design, ranging from incredibly complex artistic models and folding nano structures, to origami-inspired emergency shelters. Investigate origami-inspired breakthroughs in robotics, medicine and sustainability. Interactive displays and video tutorials will give visitors of all ages hands-on opportunities to investigate and participate in the artistic process of origami.
persona1_adapted: It has been almost 20 years since PEM showed the popular exhibition *Origami Now!* Since then, origami has grown into exciting new forms of art and invention. This exhibition brings together artists of different ages in the Dotty Brown Art & Nature Center. It shows that origami is not just a cool art form. It also inspires new ideas in science, engineering, math, and more. See works from top origami artists like Michael G. LaFosse. He is one of the world’s best origami masters and lives in Massachusetts. Along with masterpieces, PEM will show what’s new in origami and design. This includes very complex paper models, tiny folded structures, and even emergency shelters inspired by origami. Explore how origami has led to breakthroughs in robotics, medicine, and sustainability. Hands-on displays and video tutorials let visitors of all ages try origami and take part in the creative process.

CuratorialText: View iconic contemporary artworks that reach back into millennia-old art practices. The Stars We Do Not See: Australian Indigenous Art is the largest exhibition of Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander art ever presented in North America, representing the incredible breadth and depth of Contemporary Indigenous Australian art. The exhibition charts watershed moments in Australian Indigenous art, celebrating a vivid history of creativity that predates the arrival of Europeans by many thousands of years. The exhibition features nearly 200 artworks by more than 130 artists, among them several undisputed masterworks from the National Gallery of Victoria’s collection. Many of these significant works have never before left the country. The Stars We Do Not See is curated especially to introduce North American audiences to customary forms and styles, such as the conceptual map paintings of the Central and Western Desert Regions (sometimes colloquially referred to as “dot paintings”), ochre on bark paintings from far Northern Australia and ambitious experimental weavings. The exhibition also presents powerful works in neon, video and photography that acknowledge and innovate upon tradition.
persona1_adapted: View amazing modern artworks that connect to art traditions thousands of years old. *The Stars We Do Not See: Australian Indigenous Art* is the largest show of Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander art ever held in North America. It shows the huge range and depth of today's Indigenous Australian art. The exhibition marks key turning points in Australian Indigenous art. It celebrates a long history of creativity that started many thousands of years before Europeans arrived. The show includes nearly 200 artworks by more than 130 artists. Several of these are famous masterpieces from the National Gallery of Victoria’s collection. Many of these important works have never left Australia before. *The Stars We Do Not See* is designed to introduce North American audiences to traditional art forms and styles. These include map-like paintings from the Central and Western Desert Regions, sometimes called "dot paintings." The show also features paintings made with ochre on bark from far Northern Australia, and bold experimental weavings. In addition, the exhibition presents powerful works in neon, video, and photography that honor and build on tradition.

CuratorialText: Come and experience the liveliness of a drinking party, the opulence of a royal wedding and poetic evocation of spring on a delicate dish. Discover plants and animals, myths and symbols and decipher the Chinese character for "Double Happiness." With more than 30 highlights from the museum's wide-ranging Chinese collection spanning 3,000 years, this exhibition celebrates China's artistic achievements crystallized in seasonal festivals, religious ceremonies and celebrations. Located in the Yin Yu Tang Interpretive Gallery.
persona1_adapted: Come see the energy of a drinking party, the luxury of a royal wedding, and a poem about spring on a delicate dish. Explore plants, animals, myths, and symbols. Find out what the Chinese character for "Double Happiness" means. This exhibit shows over 30 top pieces from the museum’s Chinese collection. These objects span 3,000 years. They celebrate China’s art in seasonal festivals, religious ceremonies, and celebrations. The exhibit is in the Yin Yu Tang Interpretive Gallery.

CuratorialText:
Thomas Mifflin (1744-1800) and his wife, Sarah Morris Mifflin (1747?-1790), were the only Philadelphians painted by John Singleton Copley, the greatest artist in the American colonies prior to the Revolution. Mifflin was an ardent patriot and by the time this portrait was made, had established himself as a successful merchant; later he rose to the rank of major general in the Continental Army, and was elected the first governor of Pennsylvania after the United States achieved independence. Sarah Morris, who married Thomas in 1767, was an accomplished, witty, and supportive partner. In the portrait, Sarah is weaving a decorative fringe on a portable loom, which may symbolize the couple's endorsement of the colonists' boycott of English goods.
Famous as one of Copley's finest works soon after it was painted, this portrait shows the artist at the height of his powers. Born in Boston in 1738 into a poor immigrant family, Copley was self-taught. He developed a highly finished style that rendered the features, costumes, and settings of his subjects with remarkable accuracy. Copley always kept a keen eye on his competition, and he may have been inspired to paint both Sarah and Thomas Mifflin on a single canvas by his contemporary Charles Willson Peale, who had recently completed a group portrait of the Philadelphia Cadwalader family that was admired for its portrayal of family unity and affection. Here, Copley depicts not only the features and costumes of his sitters with his famed skill, but creates an image of marriage as an affectionate, equal partnership--an innovative concept in American portraiture at the time.
persona1_adapted:
Thomas Mifflin (1744-1800) and his wife, Sarah Morris Mifflin (1747?-1790), were the only people from Philadelphia painted by John Singleton Copley. Copley was the best artist in the American colonies before the Revolution. Mifflin was a strong supporter of American independence. By the time this portrait was made, he had become a successful merchant. Later, he became a major general in the Continental Army. After the United States won its freedom, he was elected the first governor of Pennsylvania. Sarah Morris married Thomas in 1767. She was talented, witty, and a supportive partner. In the portrait, Sarah is weaving a decorative fringe on a small, portable loom. This may show that the couple supported the colonists' boycott of English goods.

This portrait was famous as one of Copley's best works soon after it was painted. It shows the artist at the peak of his skill. Copley was born in Boston in 1738 into a poor immigrant family. He taught himself to paint. He developed a very detailed style. He painted faces, clothes, and backgrounds with amazing accuracy. Copley always watched his rivals closely. He may have been inspired to paint both Sarah and Thomas Mifflin in one picture by another artist, Charles Willson Peale. Peale had just finished a group portrait of the Philadelphia Cadwalader family. People admired that painting for showing family unity and love. Here, Copley does more than just paint faces and clothes with his famous skill. He creates an image of marriage as a loving, equal partnership. This was a new idea in American portraits at the time.

CuratorialText: Better known as a patriot during the American Revolution, Paul Revere was the most prolific and accomplished silversmith in late 1700s Boston. His shop produced domestic and ecclesiastical objects for clients belonging to the same civic, military, political, and religious organizations as Revere. The teapot’s inverted pear shape and engraved ornament of C-scrolls, foliage, and shells epitomizes the Rococo style of his early career. Equally renowned as an engraver of bookplates, trade cards, magazine illustrations, and political prints, Revere probably executed this decoration himself. John Singleton Copley’s famous 1768 portrait of Revere (Museum of Fine Arts, Boston) depicts the silversmith with engraving tools, holding a teapot with the same low, domed cover and slightly pointed, pinecone finial as this example. Could they be one and the same?
persona1_adapted:
Paul Revere is best known as a patriot from the American Revolution. But he was also the most skilled and productive silversmith in Boston during the late 1700s. His shop made objects for both homes and churches. His customers were part of the same civic, military, political, and religious groups as Revere himself.

This teapot has an upside-down pear shape. Its engraved decoration includes C-shaped curves, leaves, and shells. These details are typical of the Rococo style, which Revere used early in his career. Revere was also famous as an engraver. He made bookplates, business cards, magazine pictures, and political prints. He probably did the engraving on this teapot himself.

A famous 1768 portrait of Revere by John Singleton Copley shows the silversmith holding engraving tools. In the painting, he also holds a teapot with a low, domed lid and a pointed, pinecone-shaped knob on top. This teapot has the same features. Could they be the same teapot?

CuratorialText: Human rights and social issues often figure in Pippin’s work, but this painting is particularly overt in its treatment of racism. Clouds hover over a hooded member of the Ku Klux Klan and a burly white man holding a noose. The two menacing figures stand opposite the Statue of Liberty, here painted brown instead of green. At the center, Mr. Prejudice drives a wedge into a symbolic V for victory, segregating white and black machinists and servicemen. Pippin includes himself as one of the soldiers, wearing a World War I uniform, with his wounded right arm hanging at his side.
persona1_adapted: Pippin often painted about human rights and social issues. This painting is very direct about racism. Dark clouds hang over a hooded Ku Klux Klan member and a large white man holding a noose. These two scary figures stand across from the Statue of Liberty, which is painted brown instead of green. In the center, a character called Mr. Prejudice drives a wedge into a V-for-victory symbol. He is splitting apart white and Black factory workers and soldiers. Pippin painted himself as one of the soldiers. He wears a World War I uniform, and his wounded right arm hangs at his side.

CuratorialText: While the bed chambers of chic early Philadelphia houses often featured a high chest (for storing textiles; 1957-129-1) and dressing table (for cosmetics and hair accessories; 2012-59-1), this pairing represents the pinnacle of design, cabinetmaking, and carved ornament. The raised space underneath the high chest distinguishes it as a confection of North America since the British had abandoned that design by the 1730s. While most high chests and dressing tables fit within well-defined groups, the proportions and shaping of the rails and the legs of this pairing relate to only one other dressing table, making it difficult to identify the maker. The choice mahogany, with an even surface and swirling figure, signifies the status of the commission. And a highly skilled carver ornamented it profusely—most conspicuously with a narrative from Aesop’s fable of “The Fox and the Grapes” on the central bottom drawers. Aesop’s moralistic tales were the only fiction Quakers were allowed to read.
persona1_adapted:
In the bedrooms of stylish early Philadelphia homes, you would often find a high chest (for storing clothes and linens) and a dressing table (for cosmetics and hair items). This particular set is a top example of design, furniture making, and carved decoration. The open space under the high chest shows it was made in North America, because British makers had stopped using that design by the 1730s.

Most high chests and dressing tables fit into clear groups, but the shape and curves of the rails and legs on this set match only one other dressing table. That makes it hard to know who made it. The high-quality mahogany wood, with its smooth surface and swirling grain, shows that this was a special, expensive piece. A very skilled carver added lots of decoration—most noticeably a scene from Aesop’s fable “The Fox and the Grapes” on the bottom center drawers. Aesop’s moral stories were the only fiction that Quakers were allowed to read.

CuratorialText: Pennsylvania-made Kleiderschranken (clothes presses) functioned, as did the earlier models in northern Europe, to hold hanging clothes and store cherished textiles. This schrank is notable for its extraordinary decoration—bold architectural paneling, carving, and lettering that records the name of its owner, Georg Huber of Manheim, Pennsylvania, and the date it was made, 1779, only a year before he married Barbara Oberholtzer. Rather than a lightwood inlay, the lettering and floral designs were made by pouring molten sulfur into channels carved in the walnut. Swiss and German designs inspired the decorative motifs, many of which are commonly found on other Pennsylvania German furniture as well as ceramics and fraktur, and helped to connect the owners and makers to their cultural roots. The wardrobe’s monumental size, bold composition, and profuse ornament demonstrate not only Huber’s aesthetic taste and wealth but also the skills and sensibilities of the Germanic artisans who made it.
persona1_adapted: This clothes press, called a Kleiderschrank, was made in Pennsylvania. Like earlier versions from northern Europe, it held hanging clothes and stored treasured fabrics. This piece stands out because of its amazing decoration. It has bold wood panels, carvings, and lettering. The lettering tells us the owner’s name—Georg Huber of Manheim, Pennsylvania—and the year it was made, 1779. That was just one year before he married Barbara Oberholtzer. The letters and flower designs were not made with light wood inlay. Instead, the maker carved channels into the walnut wood and poured hot sulfur into them. The patterns were inspired by Swiss and German designs. You can find similar designs on other Pennsylvania German furniture, as well as on ceramics and fraktur, a type of decorated writing. These designs helped connect the owners and makers to their cultural background. The wardrobe’s huge size, bold look, and rich decoration show Huber’s taste and wealth. They also show the skill and style of the German artisans who built it.

CuratorialText: Inscribed (translation): 1786 G. H. From the earth with sense the potter makes everything. Cadarina Raeder, her dish. German-speaking immigrants to Pennsylvania maintained the artistic traditions they brought from Europe, often retaining art forms and styles over several generations. This dish features decoration made using the centuries-old sgraffito technique, achieved by cutting through a slip coating to expose the red clay underneath. The verse in German around the border, extolling the skill of the potter, also shows an adherence to tradition: language provided an immediate means of holding onto ethnic identity. With such rich ornamentation, this dish was clearly not intended for use.
persona1_adapted:
This dish was made in 1786 by a potter named G.H. The potter shaped it from clay, giving it form and meaning. It belonged to Cadarina Raeder.

German-speaking immigrants who moved to Pennsylvania kept the art traditions they brought from Europe. They often passed down these styles for many generations.

The decoration on this dish uses a technique called sgraffito (skrah-FEE-toh). In this method, the potter cuts through a thin layer of light clay to reveal the red clay underneath.

A verse in German runs around the border. It praises the potter's skill. Using the German language helped these immigrants hold onto their cultural identity.

Because of its rich decoration, this dish was clearly not meant for everyday use.

CuratorialText: In 1795 the Philadelphia artist Charles Willson Peale painted this illusionistic portrait to showcase his artistic skills. At the same time, he wished to represent the roles played by two of his sons at Peale’s Museum, the first American museum created to educate and entertain the public. Titian Ramsay Peale, who crafted natural science displays, gestures at the top of the stairs as his older brother, Raphaelle, an artist with his palette in hand, strides upward just above a real step attached to the base of the canvas. The life-size portrait is known to have startled and delighted visitors, reputedly even George Washington, before the Peale Museum’s collection was dismantled and finally sold in 1854. Acquired by the Philadelphia Museum of Art in 1945, the picture continues to engage and welcome visitors.
persona1_adapted: In 1795, Philadelphia artist Charles Willson Peale painted this lifelike portrait to show off his artistic talent. He also wanted to show the jobs of two of his sons at Peale’s Museum. That museum was the first in America created to teach and entertain the public. Titian Ramsay Peale made displays about natural science. He points at the top of the stairs. His older brother, Raphaelle, is an artist holding his paint palette. He walks up the stairs just above a real step attached to the bottom of the canvas. This life-size portrait surprised and delighted visitors. It is said that even George Washington was amazed by it. The Peale Museum’s collection was taken apart and finally sold in 1854. The Philadelphia Museum of Art bought this painting in 1945. It still welcomes and interests visitors today.

CuratorialText: A rare early representation of ethnic and religious diversity in the United States, and an outstanding example of Peale’s late naturalistic style, the picture is distinguished by the direct and sympathetic encounter between the artist and his subject and a skilled rendering of details. Yarrow’s knit cap suggests a kufi, a hat traditionally worn by African Muslim men to assert their religion or African identity, but Peale artfully employed its yellow band to highlight his sitter’s steady gaze with its glint of humor and wisdom. Seventy-seven years old when he created this portrait, Peale was seeking to record the traits that he believed supported a long life. In his writings and museum displays, Peale celebrated making wise choices to maintain good health and a positive attitude. He perceived Yarrow’s perseverance in the face of racism and enslavement as a model of resourcefulness, industriousness, and sobriety.
persona1_adapted:
This painting is a rare early example showing ethnic and religious diversity in the United States. It is also a great example of Charles Willson Peale’s later naturalistic style. Naturalistic means the artist tried to paint things as they really looked.

The picture stands out because of the direct and kind connection between the artist and his subject. Peale also skillfully painted small details. Yarrow’s knit cap looks like a kufi, a hat that African Muslim men often wear to show their religion or African identity. Peale cleverly used the cap’s yellow band to draw attention to Yarrow’s steady gaze, which shows both humor and wisdom.

Peale was seventy-seven years old when he made this portrait. He wanted to record the qualities that he believed helped people live a long life. In his writings and museum displays, Peale praised making wise choices to stay healthy and keep a positive attitude. He saw Yarrow’s strength in the face of racism and slavery as a model of resourcefulness, hard work, and self-control.

CuratorialText: This chair is part of the elegantly painted furniture Latrobe designed for the Walns' drawing room. Decorated in rich red, yellow, and gilt on a black ground and incorporating classical motifs and symbols, the set of drawing room furniture consisted of a pier table, Grecian sofas, a pair of card tables, window benches, and chairs. Its legs that curved inwards make the chair the earliest American example of a Klismos chair, a forerunner of the classical style that enjoyed widespread popularity in the late 1810s and 1820s. In addition to this chair from the Waln set, the Museum's collection includes six more chairs, a Grecian sofa, a card table, and a pier table with a mirrored back.
persona1_adapted: This chair is part of a set of painted furniture. Latrobe designed it for the Waln family’s drawing room. The set is decorated in rich red, yellow, and gold on a black background. It also includes classical designs and symbols. The set had a pier table, Grecian sofas, card tables, window benches, and chairs. The chair’s legs curve inward. This makes it the earliest American example of a Klismos chair. A Klismos chair is a type of chair with curved legs. It was a forerunner of the classical style. That style became very popular in the late 1810s and 1820s. Besides this chair, the Museum’s collection has six more chairs, a Grecian sofa, a card table, and a pier table with a mirrored back.

CuratorialText:
The Gross Clinic is recognized as one of the greatest American paintings ever made. The young and little-known Eakins created it specifically for Philadelphia’s 1876 Centennial Exhibition, intending to showcase his talents as an artist and to honor the scientific achievements of his native Philadelphia.
Choosing the city’s world-famous surgeon and teacher Dr. Samuel Gross as his subject, Eakins sets the scene in Jefferson Medical College’s surgical amphitheater. Dr. Gross is shown leading a clinic of five doctors operating on the left thigh of a patient. At the same time Gross is demonstrating to students the relatively new surgical procedure he had developed to treat bone infections. In contrast to the recoiling woman to the left—traditionally identified as the patient’s mother—Gross embodies the confidence that comes from knowledge and experience. Casting himself as a witness, Eakins can be seen seated to the right of the tunnel railing, sketching or writing.
persona1_adapted:
The Gross Clinic is known as one of the greatest American paintings ever made. The young and little-known artist Eakins created it for Philadelphia’s 1876 Centennial Exhibition. He wanted to show off his skills as an artist and honor the scientific achievements of his hometown, Philadelphia.

Eakins chose Dr. Samuel Gross, a world-famous surgeon and teacher from the city, as his subject. The scene takes place in a surgical amphitheater at Jefferson Medical College. Dr. Gross leads a team of five doctors as they operate on a patient’s left thigh. At the same time, he teaches students about a new surgery he had developed to treat bone infections.

On the left, a woman recoils in shock—she is often thought to be the patient’s mother. In contrast, Dr. Gross shows the confidence that comes from knowledge and experience. Eakins places himself in the scene as a witness. You can see him seated to the right of the railing, sketching or writing.

CuratorialText: The dramatic rescue from a foundering ship shown here was made possible by a recent innovation in lifesaving technology, the breeches buoy. Secured firmly to ship and shore, the device permitted the transfer of stranded passengers to safety by means of a pulley that was hauled back and forth by crews at either end. Cropped down to its essentials, Homer's composition thrusts us into the midst of the action with massive waves rolling past, drenching the semiconscious woman and her anonymous savior. The Life Line was immediately recognized by critics as a major contribution to American art, portraying a heroic, contemporary subject with both painterly virtuosity and detailed observation.
persona1_adapted: This dramatic rescue from a sinking ship was made possible by a new lifesaving tool, the breeches buoy. The device was a pulley system tied tightly to both the ship and the shore. Crews on each end pulled the pulley back and forth. This allowed stranded passengers to be moved to safety. Homer crops the scene down to its most important parts. Huge waves roll past, soaking the half-conscious woman and her unknown rescuer. We feel like we are right in the middle of the action. Critics immediately saw *The Life Line* as a major work of American art. It shows a heroic, modern event with both skillful painting and careful detail.

CuratorialText: Mary Cassatt’s flair for digging into an ordinary moment and distilling its emotions stands out in this portrayal of three individuals lulled by a carriage ride into silent reverie. Once titled simply Driving, the painting depicts an outing in the Bois de Boulogne, a large wooded park on the western edge of Paris. The driver of the carriage is Lydia Cassatt, sister of the artist, and the girl seated primly beside her is Odile Fèvre, niece of the painter Edgar Degas. The bored-looking groom is unidentified. Usually associated with domestic interiors and the private lives of women and children, here Cassatt explored an outdoor setting and a degree of autonomy enjoyed by upper-class women. Driving in the park was something the Cassatt sisters enjoyed, particularly after 1879, when the family acquired a small carriage and Bichette, the horse partly depicted here
persona1_adapted: Mary Cassatt had a talent for capturing everyday moments and bringing out their emotions. This painting shows three people quietly lost in thought during a carriage ride. It was once called *Driving*. The scene takes place in the Bois de Boulogne, a large wooded park on the western edge of Paris. The driver is Lydia Cassatt, the artist’s sister. Sitting primly beside her is Odile Fèvre, the niece of painter Edgar Degas. The bored-looking groom is not identified. Cassatt is known for painting indoor scenes and the private lives of women and children. But here, she explores an outdoor setting and the freedom that upper-class women enjoyed. Driving in the park was something the Cassatt sisters loved, especially after 1879, when their family bought a small carriage and a horse named Bichette, who is partly shown in this painting.

CuratorialText: Tanner painted The Annunciation soon after returning to Paris from a trip to Egypt and Palestine in 1897. The son of a minister in the African Methodist Episcopal Church, Tanner specialized in religious subjects, and wanted to experience the people, culture, architecture, and light of the Holy Land. Influenced by what he saw, Tanner created an unconventional image of the moment when the angel Gabriel announces to Mary that she will bear the Son of God. Mary is shown as an adolescent dressed in rumpled Middle Eastern peasant clothing, without a halo or other holy attributes. Gabriel appears only as a shaft of light. Tanner entered this painting in the 1898 Paris Salon exhibition, after which it was bought for the Philadelphia Museum of Art in 1899, making it his first work to enter an American museum.
persona1_adapted: Tanner painted *The Annunciation* soon after returning to Paris from a trip to Egypt and Palestine in 1897. His father was a minister in the African Methodist Episcopal Church. Tanner focused on religious subjects and wanted to see the people, culture, buildings, and light of the Holy Land. Inspired by what he saw, Tanner created a different kind of image of the moment when the angel Gabriel tells Mary she will give birth to the Son of God. Mary is shown as a young teenager wearing wrinkled Middle Eastern peasant clothes. She has no halo or other holy symbols. Gabriel appears only as a beam of light. Tanner entered this painting in the 1898 Paris Salon, a major art exhibition. In 1899, the Philadelphia Museum of Art bought it. This was the first of his works to enter an American museum.

CuratorialText: A father’s love radiates from this iridescent painting of sunlight and youth. Shaped by the realist tradition of Thomas Eakins at the Pennsylvania Academy of the Fine Arts, Daniel Garber adopted Impressionist color and handling during a two-year sojourn in Europe. Returning in 1907, he settled into a farm along Cuttalossa Creek in Bucks County, Pennsylvania, where he harmoniously merged nature, art, and domestic life. In this portrait of his daughter, Tanis, light from the garden outside his studio renders her smock translucent, while the composition’s serene geometry captures the shimmer of an endless summer afternoon.
persona1_adapted: A father’s love shines through this glowing painting of sunlight and youth. Daniel Garber learned from the realist tradition of Thomas Eakins at the Pennsylvania Academy of the Fine Arts. During a two-year stay in Europe, he adopted Impressionist color and style. When he returned in 1907, he moved to a farm along Cuttalossa Creek in Bucks County, Pennsylvania. There, he blended nature, art, and family life. In this portrait of his daughter, Tanis, light from the garden outside his studio makes her smock see-through. The calm, balanced design captures the sparkle of a never-ending summer afternoon.

CuratorialText: Georgia O’Keeffe once remarked, “What is my experience of the flower if not color?” This painting of two calla lilies is an extraordinary example of her floral compositions, made of sweeping, broad waves of subtly blended hues. The white petals, highlighted in green, are arranged against a pink backdrop, and from each one emerges a bright yellow pistil. Many have interpreted O’Keeffe’s depictions of floral anatomy in relation to sexuality and gender, but the artist always resisted these interpretations, considering them too specific and limiting.
persona1_adapted: Georgia O’Keeffe once said, “What is my experience of the flower if not color?” This painting of two calla lilies is a great example of her flower art. It uses wide, flowing brushstrokes with softly blended colors. The white petals have green highlights and stand out against a pink background. A bright yellow pistil, the flower’s central part, rises from each bloom. Many people have linked O’Keeffe’s flower paintings to ideas about sexuality and gender. But the artist always disagreed with those readings. She felt they were too narrow and limited the meaning of her work.

CuratorialText: Wharton Esherick’s carved interiors are among his most important early sculptural work. Inspired by the angular shapes and intersecting lines of the Cubist movement, the artist incorporated the natural patterns and grains of assorted woods into his pieces to emphasize their strong geometric and organic forms. Born in Philadelphia in 1887, Esherick studied painting at the Pennsylvania Academy of the Fine Arts from 1908–10, and discovered wood as a medium for artistic expression when he began to make carved frames for his canvases. By the mid-1920s he was fully committed to sculpture and extended his definition of the medium to the carving of furniture and architectural interiors. His work is often seen as a forerunner to the contemporary studio furniture movement.
persona1_adapted:
Wharton Esherick’s carved interiors are some of his most important early sculptures. He was inspired by the sharp shapes and crossing lines of Cubism, an art style that breaks objects into geometric forms. Esherick used the natural patterns and grains of different types of wood to highlight the strong shapes and natural forms in his work.

Born in Philadelphia in 1887, Esherick studied painting at the Pennsylvania Academy of the Fine Arts from 1908 to 1910. He first discovered wood as a way to make art when he started carving frames for his paintings. By the mid-1920s, he had fully turned to sculpture. He expanded his idea of sculpture to include carving furniture and even entire rooms.

Many people see his work as an early step toward the modern studio furniture movement.

CuratorialText: Andrew Wyeth shared the realist approach of many mid-twentieth-century painters of the American scene, although his process of simplification and synthesis often led to haunting, surrealist effects. His picture of his neighbor Karl Kuerner’s light-filled kitchen holds an air of something missing. Wyeth explained that the painting was about “the winter light, the dishes ready for Karl Kuerner’s lunch—that is, peace, yes, but to me behind it is violence suppressed.” The snarling log outside the window, like the knife on the table, connected in the artist’s mind to Kuerner’s wartime experience, his violent dog, and his strong hand on the entire farm. Said Wyeth, “I wanted to get down to the very essence of the man who wasn’t there.”
persona1_adapted:
Andrew Wyeth was a realist painter. Like many American artists in the mid-1900s, he painted everyday scenes. But Wyeth simplified and combined details in a way that often created haunting, dreamlike effects.

This painting shows his neighbor Karl Kuerner’s kitchen, filled with light. Yet the room feels like something is missing. Wyeth said the painting was about “the winter light, the dishes ready for Karl Kuerner’s lunch—that is, peace, yes, but to me behind it is violence suppressed.”

The snarling log outside the window and the knife on the table were connected in Wyeth’s mind to Kuerner’s wartime experiences, his violent dog, and his strong control over the farm. Wyeth explained, “I wanted to get down to the very essence of the man who wasn’t there.”

CuratorialText: In November 1943 this painting was included in Jackson Pollock's stunning solo exhibition at Art of This Century, the innovative New York art gallery run by legendary art patron Peggy Guggenheim. As its title suggests, the work depicts a male figure, embodied in the black columnar form at right, with its mysterious arithmetic graffiti, and a curvy female figure at left, with marvelous catlike eyelashes. Standing on tiny triangular feet, both figures are surrounded by splashed and smeared oil paint that enhances the sense of energy inherent in the two vertical forms, while also foreshadowing the famous "drip" technique that Pollock would develop later that decade.
persona1_adapted: In November 1943, this painting appeared in Jackson Pollock's amazing solo show at Art of This Century. That was a cutting-edge New York art gallery run by famous art supporter Peggy Guggenheim. As the title suggests, the work shows a male figure. He is the black column-like shape on the right, covered in mysterious number-like marks. On the left is a curvy female figure with striking cat-like eyelashes. Both figures stand on tiny triangle-shaped feet. Splashed and smeared oil paint surrounds them. This paint adds to the energy of the two upright forms. It also hints at the famous "drip" technique Pollock would develop later in the 1940s.

CuratorialText: Here you will find Benjamin Franklin as you’ve never seen him before. Philadelphia-born artist Roberto Lugo riffs on the traditional representations of Franklin found throughout the city—ones that celebrate him as one of the Founding Fathers of the United States. You can see one such stately portrayal of Franklin directly behind you, in gallery 384. Lugo places Franklin within a constellation of songs, symbols, and rappers’ names—ones that shaped his childhood and adult life. The artist also includes his own self-portrait on the other side of the vase, creating an imagined dialogue between himself and Franklin around the themes of money, history, violence, and success.
persona1_adapted: Here you will see Benjamin Franklin in a way you’ve never seen him before. Artist Roberto Lugo, who was born in Philadelphia, plays with the usual images of Franklin found around the city. Those images celebrate Franklin as one of the Founding Fathers of the United States. You can see one of those formal portraits of Franklin behind you in gallery 384. Lugo places Franklin inside a mix of songs, symbols, and rappers’ names—things that shaped his own childhood and adult life. On the other side of the vase, the artist also includes a self-portrait. This creates an imagined conversation between himself and Franklin about money, history, violence, and success.

CuratorialText: Florine Stettheimer set this scene like a stage, framed by a grand red staircase at left and a long red drape mimicking a theater curtain at right. The depicted customers of Bendel’s, the famous Manhattan department store, lack the decorum and manners expected of women in this period. Instead, they frantically dive for clearance items, change clothes, inspect garments, and examine their mirrored reflections. Stettheimer lets us see "behind the curtain" to observe these women during a moment of unabashed liberation. The action-packed canvas contains an astonishing thirty-two people and two dogs, including the Pekingese at the forefront who serves as a surrogate for the artist, as indicated by the "FS"-monogrammed sweater. Henri Bendel, the store’s queer owner, stands in the left foreground. Opposite is an androgynous salesperson, whose appearance and pose—slicked-back platinum hair, rosy lips, and delicate hand gesture—align with how white gay men often presented themselves at the time.
persona1_adapted:
Florine Stettheimer designed this scene like a stage. On the left, a large red staircase frames the picture. On the right, a long red curtain looks like one you’d see in a theater.

The shoppers at Bendel’s, a famous department store in Manhattan, do not act the way women were expected to at the time. Instead, they grab sale items, change clothes, check the fabric, and look at themselves in mirrors. Stettheimer shows us what happens “behind the curtain” when these women feel free to act without rules.

The busy painting includes thirty-two people and two dogs. One dog, a Pekingese in the front, stands in for the artist. You can tell because the dog wears a sweater with the letters “FS” on it.

Henri Bendel, the store’s owner, who was gay, stands on the left in the front. Across from him is a salesperson who looks androgynous—neither clearly male nor female. This person has slicked-back platinum hair, rosy lips, and a delicate hand gesture. At the time, that look was common among white gay men.

CuratorialText: Three female immortals wear cloaks and skirts made of grasses and leaves. Their hairy bodies identify them as the Feathered Women (mao nü) of Daoist lore, who live deep in the mountains searching for herbs. Here they show each other what they have gathered. The figure at the far left holds a lingzhi fungus (thought to bestow longevity if ingested) and a basket of plants is draped over her arm. The focus of the painting is on a large health-benefiting ginseng root displayed in the hand of the central immortal. Other herbs depicted include leaves of banana and palm tree carried in the baskets on the women’s backs. The representation of Feathered Women exhibiting precious medicinal items functions as a special talisman, protecting the owner from illness.
persona1_adapted:
Three female immortals wear cloaks and skirts made of grasses and leaves. Their hairy bodies mark them as the Feathered Women (mao nü) from Daoist stories. These women live deep in the mountains, searching for healing herbs. In this painting, they show each other what they have found.

The figure on the far left holds a lingzhi fungus. People believed eating this fungus could give a long life. A basket of plants hangs over her arm.

The main focus of the painting is a large ginseng root. The central immortal holds it in her hand. Ginseng was thought to be good for health. Other herbs in the painting include banana and palm leaves, carried in baskets on the women's backs.

The image of Feathered Women showing precious medicinal items acts as a special talisman. It was meant to protect the owner from getting sick.

CuratorialText:
This delicate porcelain lantern is so light in the hand that it feels almost weightless. Candles placed within it would have glowed and flickered through the intricate lattice designs, which required great skill to achieve due to the extremely thin clay. It was not until the early 1700s that artists developed this piercing technique.

Auspicious Decoration

The four flowers within the central medallions on the six sides—the camellia, rose, peony, and lily—convey peace, unity, and longevity.
persona1_adapted:
This delicate porcelain lantern is so light it feels almost weightless in your hand. Candles placed inside would have glowed and flickered through the intricate lattice patterns. The clay was extremely thin, so making these designs required great skill. Artists did not develop this piercing technique until the early 1700s.

Auspicious Decoration

The four flowers inside the central medallions on the six sides—the camellia, rose, peony, and lily—represent peace, unity, and longevity.

CuratorialText:
Various deva, or guardian deities, are depicted on this fragment from the outer section of a large mural that once decorated the hall of a Buddhist temple. Murals representing such figures, richly dressed in costumes and adorned with jewelry, are typical of northern Chinese temples built from the late Yuan (1271–1368) to the early Ming dynasties (1368–1644), when the ruling elite embraced a mix of Chinese and Tibetan Buddhism. Although there is some loss of color, we can identify several of the deities:
(Far left) The guardian of Buddhist temples and protector of the faith, Weituo (Skanda in Sanskrit), wears a winged helmet. His diamond sword destroys demons and Buddhism’s enemies.
(Center) The Heavenly King of the West brandishes a snake, while the Heavenly King of the North holds an umbrella and stupa/pagoda.
(Right) The protective goddess Mārīcī has three faces—one frontal and two in profile—and multiple arms.
persona1_adapted:
This fragment comes from a large mural that once decorated the hall of a Buddhist temple. It shows several deva, or guardian deities. These are protective gods in Buddhism.

Murals like this one are common in northern Chinese temples. They were built from the late Yuan dynasty (1271–1368) to the early Ming dynasty (1368–1644). During that time, the ruling elite followed a mix of Chinese and Tibetan Buddhism. The figures are richly dressed in costumes and jewelry.

Even though some of the color has faded, we can still identify several deities:

(Far left) Weituo (called Skanda in Sanskrit) is the guardian of Buddhist temples and protector of the faith. He wears a winged helmet. His diamond sword is used to destroy demons and enemies of Buddhism.

(Center) The Heavenly King of the West holds a snake. The Heavenly King of the North carries an umbrella and a stupa, which is a type of pagoda or sacred building.

(Right) The protective goddess Mārīcī has three faces—one facing forward and two in profile—and multiple arms.

CuratorialText: The Zhihua monastery is one of the great Buddhist temple compounds in the Chinese capital of Beijing. Conceived on a grand scale, the compound was built in the early 1400s by the eunuch Wang Zhen. This ceiling comes from the second of five main halls on the monastery's central axis—the Hall of Great Wisdom. To give visitors a sense of the original context, the Museum installed the ceiling with architectural elements reconstructed from measured drawings made at the original site. In the center is a writhing imperial dragon surrounded by clouds, bracketing, and eight canted panels, each bearing a smaller dragon among clouds. Lotuses, apsaras (Buddhist flying musicians), and other Buddhist religious symbols are carved in the surrounding panels. The Chinese name for this central part of the ceiling is tianjing, or "well of heaven." While most of the original red lacquer is well preserved, much of the rich overlay of gold leaf has been lost. A series of model temples supported by cloud-decorated brackets encompass the central element; within these miniature structures are delicately carved sculptures of Buddhas and bodhisattvas. The remainder of the ceiling consists of square panels painted with stylized lotus flowers that show the influence of Tibetan Buddhism (Lamaism). Each bears a character from a North Indian script (called Lantsha by the Tibetans) that symbolically recreates the universe. The model temples and panels may have been added during repairs made in the Kangxi period (1662–1722).
persona1_adapted:
The Zhihua monastery is one of the great Buddhist temple complexes in Beijing, China. It was built on a large scale in the early 1400s by a man named Wang Zhen, who worked as a eunuch in the emperor’s court. This ceiling comes from the second of five main halls along the monastery’s central path—the Hall of Great Wisdom. To help visitors imagine the original setting, the Museum installed the ceiling with parts of the building rebuilt from drawings made at the actual site.

In the center of the ceiling is a twisting imperial dragon surrounded by clouds, brackets, and eight angled panels. Each panel holds a smaller dragon among clouds. The surrounding panels are carved with lotuses, apsaras (Buddhist flying musicians), and other Buddhist religious symbols. The Chinese name for this central part of the ceiling is *tianjing*, which means "well of heaven." Most of the original red lacquer is still in good shape, but much of the gold leaf that once covered it has worn away.

Around the center is a series of model temples held up by cloud-decorated brackets. Inside these tiny buildings are carefully carved statues of Buddhas and bodhisattvas (enlightened beings). The rest of the ceiling is made of square panels painted with stylized lotus flowers. These show the influence of Tibetan Buddhism (also called Lamaism). Each panel has a character from a North Indian script (known as Lantsha to Tibetans) that symbolically represents the universe. The model temples and panels may have been added during repairs in the Kangxi period (1662–1722).

CuratorialText: Educator and collector Ernest Fenollosa installed this monumental painting by Kano Hōgai in his home in Tokyo, where it held pride of place. The artist’s highly naturalistic rendering of intertwined dragons reflects a fusion of East and West. While the subject of dragons dates back to ancient China, the three-dimensionality and foreshortening of the animals’ forms reflect Hōgai’s familiarity with the Western use of perspective.
persona1_adapted: Ernest Fenollosa was both a teacher and an art collector. He placed this huge painting by Kano Hōgai in his home in Tokyo, where it had the most important spot. The artist painted the dragons in a very lifelike way. This style mixes ideas from both Eastern and Western art. Dragons have been a subject in art since ancient China. But the dragons here look three-dimensional and are shown at sharp angles. This shows that Hōgai knew how Western artists use perspective.

CuratorialText: Fifty-eight sixteenth-century granite architectural elements are presented within the museum gallery in an arrangement designed to resemble a South Indian temple hall (mandapam). In 1913 these pieces lay discarded at the small Madanagopalaswamy Temple in Madurai, where a Philadelphia couple purchased them while on their two-year-long honeymoon. But recent research supports that the majority were created for the large Kudal Alagar Temple nearby. There they had been part of a shrine to the goddess Lakshmi, wife of the Hindu deity Vishnu. This shrine had become dilapidated, and the temple authorities dismantled it by 1907, completing its replacement by 1923. Stone slabs between the lion brackets depict several scenes from the Ramayana, the epic tale of one of Vishnu's earthly incarnations. They once formed parts of a carved frieze showing the entire story. The life-size figures projecting from pillars are deities and mythological characters, including from the Ramayana. The many small images on the pillars include gods and heroes as well as humans (like an architect with his measuring stick).
persona1_adapted: Fifty-eight granite pieces from the 1500s are on display in the museum. They are arranged to look like a South Indian temple hall, called a mandapam. In 1913, these pieces were left unused at the Madanagopalaswamy Temple in Madurai. A couple from Philadelphia bought them while on their two-year honeymoon. But recent research shows that most were made for the large Kudal Alagar Temple nearby. There, they were part of a shrine to the goddess Lakshmi, wife of the Hindu god Vishnu. That shrine had fallen into poor condition. The temple authorities took it apart by 1907 and finished replacing it by 1923. Stone slabs between the lion-shaped brackets show scenes from the Ramayana. The Ramayana is an epic story about one of Vishnu’s earthly forms. These slabs were once part of a carved band that told the whole story. Life-size figures on the pillars are gods and mythical characters, including some from the Ramayana. The many small images on the pillars include gods, heroes, and humans, like an architect holding his measuring stick.

CuratorialText: Enslaved Black and free white craftsmen worked side by side across the southern landscape.  Over the past thirty years researchers at MESDA have identified nearly 6500 craftsmen who enslaved people.  In fact, this fact is among the most common things we know about an identified craftsman.  Often anonymous, those who were enslaved by craftsmen were often at work on building sites, at the potters’ wheels, in cabinet shops, at iron foundries, behind the counters of silversmiths’ shops, and at dozens of other trades.  Their skills are revealed in the objects that they helped to fashion.  Their identities are often lost to history. A line in a legal document, or a brief mention in an advertisement is often all we know about an enslaved craftsman – but careful reading of period documents by MESDA researchers has identified nearly 3000 enslaved craftsmen by name.  The exhibition “Black and White all Mix’d Together”: The Hidden Legacy of Enslaved Craftsmen looks at icons of southern decorative arts through the lens of research conducted by MESDA over more than thirty years.  It recovers the stories of the many named and nameless individuals whose hidden hands shaped the southern landscape.
persona1_adapted: Enslaved Black and free white craftsmen worked side by side across the southern United States. Over the past thirty years, researchers at MESDA have found nearly 6,500 craftsmen who enslaved people. In fact, this is one of the most common facts we know about a craftsman from that time. Often, their names are unknown. The people enslaved by craftsmen worked on building sites, at potters’ wheels, in cabinet shops, at iron foundries, behind the counters of silversmiths’ shops, and in many other trades. Their skills show up in the objects they helped make. But their identities are often lost to history. Sometimes, all we know about an enslaved craftsman is a line in a legal document or a short mention in an advertisement. Still, by carefully reading old documents, MESDA researchers have found nearly 3,000 enslaved craftsmen by name. The exhibition “Black and White all Mix’d Together”: The Hidden Legacy of Enslaved Craftsmen looks at famous objects from the southern decorative arts. It uses research from MESDA’s work over more than thirty years. The exhibition brings back the stories of the many named and unnamed people whose hidden hands shaped the southern landscape.

CuratorialText: North Carolina’s Piedmont region is known around the world for the manufacture of fine furniture.  However, long before the factories of Lexington, Thomasville, Hickory, and High Point came into existence, the Piedmont was home to a diverse group of cabinetmakers who crafted distinctive regional furniture. By the middle of the eighteenth century, immigrants from Pennsylvania, Maryland, and Virginia began pouring into the North Carolina Piedmont in search of inexpensive yet fertile land.  By 1800 about 250,000 Scotch-Irish, English, and German settlers called the region home.  Cabinetmakers immediately utilized the abundant walnut, cherry, pine and poplar trees to produce well-made, stylish furniture for their homes.  In most cases, these craftsmen combined national trends with local idiosyncratic preferences to produce regional furniture with a distinctive style and appearance. This exhibit is drawn from MESDA’s extensive collection of Piedmont North Carolina furniture made between 1780 and 1860.
persona1_adapted:
North Carolina’s Piedmont region is famous around the world for making fine furniture. But long before the factories in Lexington, Thomasville, Hickory, and High Point existed, the Piedmont was home to many different cabinetmakers. These craftspeople built unique furniture for their area.

By the mid-1700s, people from Pennsylvania, Maryland, and Virginia began moving to the North Carolina Piedmont. They came looking for cheap, fertile land. By 1800, about 250,000 Scotch-Irish, English, and German settlers lived there.

Cabinetmakers quickly used the area’s plentiful walnut, cherry, pine, and poplar trees. They made well-built, stylish furniture for their homes. Often, these workers mixed national trends with local tastes. This created regional furniture with a distinct look and feel.

This exhibit comes from MESDA’s large collection of Piedmont North Carolina furniture. The pieces were made between 1780 and 1860.

CuratorialText: Masterfully decorated slipware, sculptural bottles, refined creamware, and faience are but part of the rich artistic legacy of North Carolina’s first earthenware potters. During the last half of the eighteenth century, artisans of European descent introduced a variety of Old World ceramics traditions to the Carolina backcountry. From both an artistic and technical standpoint, the work of these craftsmen often surpassed that of their Middle Atlantic and New England contemporaries. North Carolina potters transformed the simplest of materials into vessels of practical utility, astonishing beauty, and deep cultural significance. This exhibit is the first major survey of this work an the context in which it was created.
persona1_adapted: North Carolina's first earthenware potters left behind a rich artistic legacy. They made beautifully decorated slipware, sculptural bottles, refined creamware, and faience. In the last half of the 1700s, European craftsmen brought Old World pottery traditions to the Carolina backcountry. Their work often surpassed that of potters in the Middle Atlantic and New England regions, both in art and skill. These North Carolina potters turned simple materials into useful vessels. Their creations were not only practical but also stunningly beautiful and deeply meaningful. This exhibit is the first major survey of their work and the world in which it was made.

CuratorialText: Salem was the largest of North Carolina’s Moravian communities and from the moment of arrival the earliest settlers documented their landscape with a variety of detailed maps, surveys, drawings, and paintings. Salem was founded in 1766 as a congregation town with the Church overseeing all activities—financial, social, and spiritual. The Church owned all land and leased it to individuals who owned only the improvements. Salem was a bustling trade center with a mill, pottery, tannery, brewery, and store. But the earliest landscapes emphasize the centrality of the Church. They depict the town’s conscious design around a central square, the heart of the community. The square was home to the Gemein Haus (and later Home Church), where religious services were held; the large communal dwellings for Single Brothers and Single Sisters; the schools for young boys and girls; and the store. Gardens and fields appear nestled in and around the most prominent buildings in early views, suggesting the town’s growing success both agriculturally and commercially. As the nineteenth century progressed, the residents lobbied for more personal control. In 1849, the Church agreed to sell land to create the neighboring town of Winston, and in 1856 it abandoned the lease-hold system in favor of private land ownership. Manufacturing enterprises grew, and the railroad’s arrival in 1873 set the stage for tremendous growth in local industries. Salem—once closed to outsiders—became closely intertwined with the town of Winston. The textile and tobacco industries flourished, and by 1900 the twin cities of Winston and Salem had become the largest metropolitan area in North Carolina.
persona1_adapted:
Salem was the largest Moravian community in North Carolina. From the very beginning, the first settlers made detailed maps, surveys, drawings, and paintings of the land around them. Salem was founded in 1766 as a church-run town. The Church controlled everything—money, social life, and spiritual life. The Church owned all the land and rented it to people. Those people only owned the buildings and improvements they made.

Salem was a busy trade center. It had a mill, a pottery, a tannery, a brewery, and a store. But the earliest pictures of the town focus on the Church. They show that the town was carefully designed around a central square. This square was the heart of the community. It held the Gemein Haus (later called Home Church), where religious services took place. It also had large homes for Single Brothers and Single Sisters, schools for boys and girls, and the store.

In early views, gardens and fields appear around the most important buildings. This suggests the town was growing in both farming and business. As the 1800s went on, residents asked for more personal control. In 1849, the Church agreed to sell land to create the nearby town of Winston. In 1856, it ended the lease system and let people own land. Manufacturing grew, and when the railroad arrived in 1873, local industries boomed.

Salem—once closed to outsiders—became closely linked with Winston. The textile and tobacco industries thrived. By 1900, the twin cities of Winston and Salem had become the largest urban area in North Carolina.

CuratorialText:
James River Valley pottery has been the subject of archaeological and collector interest for over half a century.  The James River and its tributaries were home to more than a dozen potteries before the Civil War.  Potters hailing from New York, New Jersey, Pennsylvania, Maryland, Ireland and England, created a rich  hybrid tradition with forms and decorations adapted from their regional styles and wrought in local clays dug from Kaolin to Claymount.

Transcending their status as utilitarian objects, these pots, many exhibited here for the first time, provide a remarkable record and a unique glimpse into a significant and long-lived concentration of pottery manufacturing in eastern Virginia.  Viewed collectively, they present a bold decorative aesthetic rivaling long-recognized and celebrated schools of production seen elsewhere in the commonwealth and the Nation.
Today, many of these important historic sites are threatened by development.  Fortunately, archaeologists and decorative arts scholars are working ahead of the bulldozers to document and understand the important legacy of the James River Potteries before they are lost forever.  At the end of 2013 the Chipstone Foundation journal Ceramics in America will publish an extensive article about the James River Valley stoneware potteries based on this ongoing research.
This exhibition was on view at MESDA from November 2012 through March 2012. It was co-curated by Kurt Russ, Oliver Mueller-Heubach, Marshall
persona1_adapted:
People have studied and collected James River Valley pottery for over fifty years. Before the Civil War, more than a dozen potteries were located along the James River and its smaller rivers. The potters came from New York, New Jersey, Pennsylvania, Maryland, Ireland, and England. They mixed their own regional styles with local clays, like Kaolin and Claymount, to create a rich new tradition.

These pots are more than just useful objects. Many are shown here for the first time. They give us a special look at a large and long-lasting pottery industry in eastern Virginia. When seen together, they show a bold decorative style that matches famous pottery schools in other parts of Virginia and the United States.

Today, many of these important historic sites are in danger from new buildings. Luckily, archaeologists and decorative arts experts are working ahead of the bulldozers. They are studying and recording the important history of the James River Potteries before it is lost forever. At the end of 2013, the Chipstone Foundation journal *Ceramics in America* will publish a long article about these stoneware potteries, based on this ongoing research.

This exhibition was on view at MESDA from November 2012 through March 2012. It was co-curated by Kurt Russ, Oliver Mueller-Heubach, and Marshall.

CuratorialText:
An Irish schoolmaster. A German Jewish silversmith. An African Muslim scholar. A multi-national female pastelist. A Scottish cabinetmaker. A potter, a carpenter, an engraver. The lives of these individuals share a common thread: they were “lately from” somewhere else and came to the American South in the 18th and early 19th centuries.

This exhibition features objects created by these craftspeople whose origins spanned continents and oceans. As much as possible, it connects those objects with the words of their makers through letters, inscriptions, autobiographies, account books, advertisements, and even drawings. Together, they tell the story of journeys taken, communities forged in new homes, responses to changing political climates, and the creative blending of traditions into something new.
persona1_adapted:
An Irish schoolmaster. A German Jewish silversmith. An African Muslim scholar. A female pastel artist from several countries. A Scottish cabinetmaker. A potter, a carpenter, an engraver. These people all share one thing: they were "lately from" somewhere else. They came to the American South in the 1700s and early 1800s.

This exhibition shows objects made by these craftspeople. Their origins spanned continents and oceans. Where possible, it connects those objects with the makers' own words. These words come from letters, inscriptions, autobiographies, account books, ads, and even drawings. Together, they tell the story of journeys taken, communities built in new homes, responses to changing politics, and the creative mixing of traditions into something new.

CuratorialText: Early Southerners drank a lot — nearly twenty gallons of alcohol each — every year. They drank when they woke up in the morning, as they ate and worked during the day, and when they socialized at night. But how people drank was as important as what they drank. From imported Madeira sipped at mahogany tables to local whisky out of redware jugs, this exhibit explores the decorative arts that defined the early Southern drinker.
persona1_adapted: Early Southerners drank a lot—almost twenty gallons of alcohol per person each year. They drank when they woke up, while eating and working during the day, and when they socialized at night. But how people drank mattered just as much as what they drank. This exhibit looks at the decorative objects that defined early Southern drinkers. These range from imported Madeira wine sipped at fancy mahogany tables to local whiskey poured from redware jugs.

CuratorialText: this exhibit in MESDA’s Whitehall Gallery brings together rare maps from the 18th and early 19th centuries to explore how maps were made, used, and did work in the early American South. Rivers of Ink, Mountains of Type: Maps at Work in the Early American South includes maps from the MESDA and Old Salem collections as well as major loans from private collections, Salem Congregation, and the Moravian Archives of the Southern Province. The exhibit includes two early surveys of the Wachovia Tract made by Andreas Höger; a rare first-state copy of Joshua Fry and Peter Jefferson’s map of Virginia; two copies of Lewis Evans’s General Map of the Middle British Colonies produced at Ben Franklin’s press in Philadelphia; and surveys by George Washington of lands he owned along the Ohio River.
persona1_adapted: This exhibit in MESDA’s Whitehall Gallery brings together rare maps from the 1700s and early 1800s. It shows how people made maps, used them, and put them to work in the early American South. The show is called *Rivers of Ink, Mountains of Type: Maps at Work in the Early American South*. It includes maps from MESDA and Old Salem collections. It also has major loans from private collections, Salem Congregation, and the Moravian Archives of the Southern Province. The exhibit features two early surveys of the Wachovia Tract made by Andreas Höger. It also includes a rare first-state copy of Joshua Fry and Peter Jefferson’s map of Virginia. Two copies of Lewis Evans’s *General Map of the Middle British Colonies* are on display. These were printed at Ben Franklin’s press in Philadelphia. Finally, the exhibit shows surveys by George Washington of lands he owned along the Ohio River.

CuratorialText: Alabama’s pottery traditions reflect the distinctive combination of people “thrown together” across its landscape. This exhibition explores the human hands that made, held, and preserved these objects. Across the present-day state of Alabama, Indigenous people including the Muscogee (Creek), Choctaw, Cherokee, and Chickasaw, practiced ceramic traditions brought by their ancestors into the region over 10,000 years ago. Colonial and American settlers were both a threat and a market for these craftspeople, who produced hybrid wares that meshed traditional techniques and designs that appealed to these foreign consumers. The geography of Alabama influenced how and where diverse potters and people came together. A tail of the Appalachian Mountains runs from south to north across the middle of the state. Most of the 19th century potters north of the mountains came from Tennessee, Virginia, and the Ohio River Valley. South of the mountains, the majority arrived from Georgia or South Carolina. The Gulf Coast was the site of early interactions between French, Spanish and British colonists and the Indigenous people of Alabama. Whether they came from the north, east, or across the Atlantic, immigrants brought with them enslaved people of African descent, including skilled ceramists. Together, their hands shaped the clay of the American South.
persona1_adapted:
Alabama’s pottery traditions show how different groups of people came together in one place. This exhibition looks at the human hands that made, used, and saved these objects. Across what is now Alabama, Indigenous peoples like the Muscogee (Creek), Choctaw, Cherokee, and Chickasaw made pottery using methods passed down by their ancestors for over 10,000 years. Colonial and American settlers were both a danger and a source of business for these potters. The potters created mixed-style wares that combined old techniques with designs that appealed to these new customers.

Alabama’s geography shaped where potters and people met. A tail of the Appalachian Mountains runs from south to north through the middle of the state. Most 19th-century potters who lived north of the mountains came from Tennessee, Virginia, and the Ohio River Valley. South of the mountains, most potters came from Georgia or South Carolina. The Gulf Coast was an early meeting place for French, Spanish, and British colonists and the Indigenous people of Alabama. Whether they came from the north, east, or across the Atlantic, immigrants brought enslaved people of African descent with them, including skilled potters. Together, their hands shaped the clay of the American South.

CuratorialText:
The Carolyn and Mike McNamara Gallery at the Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts (MESDA) exhibits some of the furniture, ceramics, paintings, prints, metalwork, and textiles from the early American South.  From fine art to folk art, these objects tell the stories of the diverse craftsmen and consumers who called the South home for more than four centuries.

The early South is usually divided into three major cultural regions: the Chesapeake, the Lowcountry, and the Backcountry.  The rivers, roads, and natural resources of these regions defined their economic and settlement patterns.  Each was home to a unique blend of ethnicities and religions: Native Americans, British Anglicans and Quakers, Scotch-Irish Presbyterians, French Huguenots, Moravians, German Lutherans and Reformed, and free and enslaved peoples of African descent.  The dynamic interactions between and among these distinct groups flavored the decorative arts of the early South.
persona1_adapted:
The Carolyn and Mike McNamara Gallery at the Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts (MESDA) shows furniture, pottery, paintings, prints, metalwork, and textiles from the early American South. These objects range from fine art to folk art. They tell the stories of the many different makers and buyers who lived in the South for over 400 years.

The early South is usually split into three main cultural areas: the Chesapeake, the Lowcountry, and the Backcountry. The rivers, roads, and natural resources in these areas shaped how people lived and where they settled. Each area had its own mix of ethnic groups and religions. These included Native Americans, British Anglicans and Quakers, Scotch-Irish Presbyterians, French Huguenots, Moravians, German Lutherans and Reformed Christians, and both free and enslaved people of African descent. The lively exchanges among these groups gave the decorative arts of the early South their unique character.

CuratorialText:
The William C. and Susan S. Mariner Southern Ceramics Gallery is home to the most comprehensive collection of antebellum pottery made in Maryland, Virginia, the Carolinas, Georgia, Kentucky, and Tennessee.

Among the decorative arts, pottery is the one media that is most intimately connected to place.  The southern pottery in this gallery reflects the interplay between maker, consumer, culture, and geography.  It is a tangible reflection of how potters responded to specific cultural needs to craft vessels out of local materials that were both useful and beautiful.

This virtual gallery offers two ways to explore the southern ceramics on exhibit in the Mariner Gallery.  The first is through a series of virtual galleries curated by the noted ceramics scholar Robert Hunter, Editor of Ceramics in America that explore important themes—tradition, design, expression, purpose, faith, war, and freedom—as seen through the lens of southern pottery.  You can also browse the objects in the Mariner Gallery by media: earthenware, salt-glazed stoneware, and alkaline glazed stoneware.  Within these pages you are also able to filter the objects by maker, place, and time
persona1_adapted:
The William C. and Susan S. Mariner Southern Ceramics Gallery holds the largest collection of pottery made in the South before the Civil War. This pottery comes from Maryland, Virginia, the Carolinas, Georgia, Kentucky, and Tennessee.

Among decorative arts, pottery is the type most closely linked to a specific place. The southern pottery in this gallery shows how makers, buyers, culture, and geography all work together. It is a physical example of how potters met local needs. They used materials from their area to create vessels that were both useful and beautiful.

This virtual gallery gives you two ways to explore the southern ceramics on display. First, you can visit a series of online galleries put together by Robert Hunter, a well-known ceramics scholar and editor of *Ceramics in America*. These galleries explore important themes—tradition, design, expression, purpose, faith, war, and freedom—through the lens of southern pottery. You can also browse the objects by type: earthenware, salt-glazed stoneware, and alkaline glazed stoneware. On these pages, you can filter the objects by maker, place, and time.

CuratorialText: The Thomas A. Gray Rare Book and Manuscript Collection is home to rare and important works about the early American South. The collection represents a lifetime of connoisseurship for Tom Gray, a former director of the Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts and cousin of MESDA founder Frank L. Horton. What began as an collection focused on Tom’s home state of North Carolina gradually expanded to include important texts from all seven of MESDA’s southern states. With books and manuscripts spanning four centuries – from 1585 to 1865 – the Gray collection is a perfect complement to the MESDA and Old Salem collections.  Books and manuscripts from the collection are exhibited in the MESDA galleries and are available to researchers.  A selection of highlights are shown below.
persona1_adapted:
The Thomas A. Gray Rare Book and Manuscript Collection holds rare and important works about the early American South. Tom Gray built this collection over his lifetime. He was a director of the Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts, or MESDA. He was also the cousin of MESDA’s founder, Frank L. Horton.

At first, Tom focused on books from his home state, North Carolina. Over time, he added important texts from all seven southern states that MESDA covers. The collection includes books and manuscripts from 1585 to 1865—that’s four centuries of history.

The Gray collection fits perfectly with the MESDA and Old Salem collections. Some books and manuscripts are on display in the MESDA galleries. Researchers can also use them. Below are a few highlights from the collection.

CuratorialText: MESDA’s collection includes architectural woodwork collected from houses in Maryland, Virginia, North Carolina, South Carolina, and Georgia.  This collection was originally assembled by Frank L. Horton to serve as backdrops for period room installations.  Today, in addition to providing scale and context for our study galleries, MESDA’s architectural collections are also studied as important Southern objects in their own right that that can tell stories of style, craftsmanship, migration, ethnicity, and race.
persona1_adapted: MESDA’s collection has woodwork from houses in Maryland, Virginia, North Carolina, South Carolina, and Georgia. Frank L. Horton first gathered these pieces to use as backgrounds for room displays. Today, these woodworks help set the size and feel of our study galleries. Experts also study them as important Southern objects. They tell stories about style, skill, movement of people, and race.

CuratorialText: Old Salem Museums & Gardens and its Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts are home to one of country’s most important collections of early Southern longrifles, swords, and related objects.  Longrifles were some of the most complicated objects crafted in the early American South.  Artistic masterworks, they required craftsmen skilled in woodworking, silversmithing, ironworking, and the mechanical arts.  The rich tradition of early Southern rifle making is kept alive today by the talented craftsmen of the Timothy Vogler Gun Shop, located directly across the street from the Frank L. Horton Museum Center at Old Salem Museums & Gardens.
persona1_adapted: Old Salem Museums & Gardens and its Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts hold one of the country’s most important collections of early Southern longrifles, swords, and related items. Longrifles were some of the most complex objects made in the early American South. These artistic masterpieces needed craftsmen skilled in woodworking, silversmithing, ironworking, and mechanical arts. The rich tradition of early Southern rifle making continues today. Talented craftsmen at the Timothy Vogler Gun Shop keep it alive. The shop is located directly across the street from the Frank L. Horton Museum Center at Old Salem Museums & Gardens.

CuratorialText: The Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts and Old Salem Museums & Gardens is home to an exceptional collection of Southern bedcoverings including quilts and coverlets.  The collection also has a significant number of textiles made and used by the women of Salem, North Carolina.
