persona1_adapted: The Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts and Old Salem Museums & Gardens has a special collection of Southern bedcoverings, like quilts and coverlets. The collection also includes many textiles made and used by the women of Salem, North Carolina.

CuratorialText: The Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts at Old Salem Museums & Gardens is home to an extensive collection of rare books and manuscripts prints that document the people and places of the early American south.  Many of these books and manuscripts are part of the Thomas A. Gray Rare Book and Manuscript Collection.
persona1_adapted: The Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts at Old Salem Museums & Gardens has a large collection of rare books and handwritten documents. These items tell the story of the people and places of the early American South. Many of these books and documents are part of the Thomas A. Gray Rare Book and Manuscript Collection.

CuratorialText: MESDA is home to an extensive collection of southern brass, copper, and pewter.
persona1_adapted: MESDA has a large collection of brass, copper, and pewter objects from the American South.

CuratorialText: Old Salem Museums & Gardens, its Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts, and The William C. and Susan S. Mariner Southern Ceramics Collection are home to the most comprehensive collection of Southern ceramics in existence.
persona1_adapted: Old Salem Museums & Gardens, along with its Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts and the William C. and Susan S. Mariner Southern Ceramics Collection, holds the largest collection of Southern ceramics in the world.

CuratorialText: MESDA is home to a collection of clocks, surveyors compasses, and sundials that span all seven of our constituent states.
persona1_adapted: MESDA has a collection of clocks, surveyors' compasses, and sundials. These objects come from all seven states in our region.

CuratorialText: German speaking communities throughout the South created richly illuminated documents celebrating births, baptisms, marriages, and deaths.
persona1_adapted: German-speaking communities in the South made beautifully decorated documents. These documents celebrated births, baptisms, marriages, and deaths.

CuratorialText: For over forty years the Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts has collected many of the finest examples of southern-made furniture.  These objects demonstrate how the early South evolved, inform us about the diverse peoples who came here, and illuminate the artisans who were integral to the process. Whether they were English, Scottish, Irish, German, African, or French, Southern cabinetmakers utilized their materials at hand, both native and imported, to create furniture that speaks to us about their heterogeneous backgrounds and the multifaceted aspects of their everyday lives. These craftsmen contributed to the dynamic amalgamation of art, culture, and history that forms the basis for MESDA’s ongoing study of the early South.
persona1_adapted:
For over forty years, the Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts has collected some of the finest furniture made in the South. These objects show how the early South changed over time. They also tell us about the different people who came to live there. And they help us learn about the skilled workers, or artisans, who made the furniture.

These furniture makers came from many places, like England, Scotland, Ireland, Germany, Africa, and France. They used materials they found nearby, as well as materials brought from other countries. The furniture they made reflects their different backgrounds and the many parts of their daily lives. These craftspeople helped create a rich mix of art, culture, and history. That mix is the foundation of MESDA’s ongoing study of the early South.

CuratorialText: MESDA’s collection of iron represents the work of furnaces and blacksmiths across all seven southern states.
persona1_adapted: MESDA’s iron collection shows the work of furnaces and blacksmiths from all seven southern states.

CuratorialText: The Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts at Old Salem Museums & Gardens is home to one of the most important collections of southern of maps.  On the surface maps appear scientific and authoritative, useful devices for governmental officials and colonial expansionists to claim ownership of land and exert power and authority over competing nationalities.  Below the surface, however, maps often provide tremendous insights into a myriad of social, political, and economic forces that shaped Southern culture.
persona1_adapted: The Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts at Old Salem Museums & Gardens holds one of the most important collections of southern maps. On the surface, maps look scientific and trustworthy. Government officials and colonial leaders used them to claim land and show power over other groups. But if you look deeper, maps often reveal a lot about the social, political, and economic forces that shaped Southern culture.

CuratorialText: Music is an important part of Southern culture.  In Salem, North Carolina, music was intrgal to Moravian worship and daily life.  Salem had its own orchestra by the 1780s and brass bands played regularly as part of religious services and to announce births and deaths in the community.  Old Salem Museums & Gardens collection of musical instruments and related materials reflects the important role played by music in the community.  Other objects in the collection reflect musical craftsmanship elsewhere in the American South.
persona1_adapted: Music plays a big role in Southern culture. In Salem, North Carolina, music was a key part of Moravian worship and everyday life. Salem had its own orchestra by the 1780s. Brass bands often played during religious services. They also announced births and deaths in the community. Old Salem Museums & Gardens has a collection of musical instruments and related items. This collection shows how important music was to the community. Other objects in the collection highlight musical craftsmanship from other parts of the American South.

CuratorialText: The Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts has led the way in researching and collecting work by Southern artisans, allowing us to better understand the rich cultural complexity of artistic production in the South. While it is true that with only a handful of significant urban centers, fewer painters settled and remained in the South, the painting collection at MESDA ably demonstrates the diversity of training and styles with works by European-trained and native-born artists living in urban and rural settings
persona1_adapted: The Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts, or MESDA, has led the way in studying and collecting work by Southern artists. This helps us better understand the rich and complex cultures that shaped art in the South. It is true that only a few major cities existed in the South, so fewer painters settled and stayed there. Still, MESDA’s painting collection shows a wide range of training and styles. It includes works by artists trained in Europe and artists born in America, living in both cities and the countryside.

CuratorialText: The Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts at Old Salem Museums & Gardens is home to an extensive collection of prints that document the people and places of the early American south.  These views documented English perceptions of America and served as vehicles to convey a host of attitudes and values and provide tremendous insights into a myriad of social, political, and economic forces that shaped Southern culture.
persona1_adapted: The Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts at Old Salem Museums & Gardens has a large collection of prints. These prints show the people and places of the early American South. They recorded how the English saw America. They also carried many different attitudes and values. These images give us great insight into the social, political, and economic forces that shaped Southern culture.

CuratorialText: Pastel portrait of Frances L’Escott. Half-length pastel portrait of young woman with body turned slightly to the right and head slightly to the left. She has auburn hair, brown eyes, with loosely fitted ecru and blue gown. The original wood frame retains its original spruce back with the following inscriptions: “Henrietta Johnston Fecit/ South Carolina Ano 1716.” “I/ Frances Lescott/ Married Peter Villep—.”

She has auburn hair and brown eyes. She wears a loose-fitting gown in ecru and blue. The original wood frame still has its original spruce back. On the back, there are these words: “Henrietta Johnston Fecit/ South Carolina Ano 1716.” And also: “I/ Frances Lescott/ Married Peter Villep—.”

CuratorialText: Portrait of man, bust length, seated in a red upholstered chair, body half-left, eyes and head forward, brown hair, blue-gray eyes, ruddy complexion, wearing a black coat, yellow vest and white stock and tie, brass buttons. The background is a greenish-gray.
persona1_adapted: This is a portrait of a man. He is shown from the chest up, sitting in a red chair. His body is turned slightly to the left, but he looks straight ahead. CuratorialText: Portrait of man in brown coat and yellow vest with white stock. He has blue-gray eyes and dark hair. He is a half-length figure, facing half left, posed with hand in his vest. He stands in front of a column and what must be a balustraded porch with green trees and a greenish ground in the distance.
persona1_adapted: This is a portrait of a man. He wears a brown coat, a yellow vest, and a white stock around his neck. He has blue-gray eyes and dark hair. The painting shows him from the waist up. In the distance, you can see green trees and a green landscape.

CuratorialText: Portrait of a woman seated in a Chippendale type chair, half-length, facing forward and dressed with a white high crowned cap, gray dress and white neck piece with a black ribbon and jewel at her throat. She has brown eyes, dark hair. She holds a reddish-brown book in her right hand and is seated at a column base to the right, with red drapery and tassel, and with a balustraded rail and trees in the background to the left.
persona1_adapted: This portrait shows a woman sitting in a fancy wooden chair. We see her from the waist up, facing forward. She wears a tall white cap and a gray dress. CuratorialText: Full length portrait of a man in blue uniform with red trim at lapel and cuffs, white vest and breeches with black boots trimmed with gold at the top, white epaulets and neck piece, and black tircorner hat decorated with silver cord. He is posed with sword in right upraised hand and a dagger-like knife in the left, facing half-left, with a tree at either side to frame a fight between American troops and Indians in the background. The soldiers in the back wear round hats, short blue jackets with red facings, belly boxes, and linen overalls. The retreating Indians to the left rear of the painting appear with topknots or scalp locks, and wear battle dress of breechcloths and leggings.
persona1_adapted:
This is a full-length painting of a man in a blue uniform. The uniform has red trim on the lapel and cuffs. He wears a white vest and white breeches (short pants). His black boots have gold trim at the top. He also has white epaulets (shoulder decorations) and a white neck piece. On his head is a black three-cornered hat decorated with silver cord.

The man stands facing half-left. He holds a sword up in his right hand and a dagger-like knife in his left hand. Trees on each side frame the scene. In the background, a fight is happening between American troops and Native Americans.

The soldiers in the back wear round hats and short blue jackets with red trim. They also have belly boxes (small pouches worn on the stomach) and linen overalls. On the left side of the painting, the Native Americans are retreating. They have topknots or scalp locks in their hair. They wear battle dress made of breechcloths and leggings.

CuratorialText: Watercolor on paper of a plantation with an inscription at the base of the painting. A path leads to the house, and two men, one mounted upon a horse, the other standing below him are talking to one another. A body of water is in the background.

CuratorialText: Oil on canvas portrait of a woman facing half-right. The subject has dark hair combed upward to expose her left ear, and pearls in her hair that hang down over her right shoulder. CuratorialText: A watercolor “en grisaille” of Williamsville, a home in Hanover County, Virginia. The house was built between 1794 and 1803 for William Pollard, II, the second Clerk of Court of Hanover County. It passed to his son George William Pollard in 1840 and remained in the Pollard family until after the death of Harry Todd Pollard, the amateur artist who drew and signed the watercolor.
persona1_adapted: This is a watercolor painting of Williamsville, a home in Hanover County, Virginia. The painting is done in shades of gray, a style called "grisaille." The house was built between 1794 and 1803 for William Pollard, II. He was the second Clerk of Court for Hanover County. In 1840, the house passed to his son, George William Pollard. The Pollard family kept the home until after the death of Harry Todd Pollard. He was the amateur artist who drew and signed this watercolor.

CuratorialText: Painting, done in oil on mahogany panel, of a young man facing half-right. The gentleman has dark hair and side-burns, and is dressed in a white shirt, tie and vest, and a black coat supporting a high collar. There is an inscription on the back of the panel.
persona1_adapted: This painting is made with oil paint on a mahogany wood panel. It shows a young man facing slightly to the right. He has dark hair and sideburns. He wears a white shirt, a tie, a vest, and a black coat with a high collar. There is writing on the back of the panel.

CuratorialText: Oil on canvas painting of a man facing half-left. The work is a half-length portrait. The subject has gray hair, dark gray eyes, wears a dark brown coat with a high turned-down collar, a yellow vest, and white stock at the neck.
persona1_adapted: This painting is an oil on canvas portrait of a man. He faces half-left, and the painting shows him from the waist up. The man has gray hair and dark gray eyes. He wears a dark brown coat with a high, turned-down collar. Underneath, he has a yellow vest and a white neckcloth.

CuratorialText: This painting depicts a bend in the Cumberland River near the Hermitage, the home of Andrew Jackson (1767-1845). It was a scene that the artist returned to regularly. Another version of this composition is in the collection of the Greenville County Museum of Art.
persona1_adapted: This painting shows a bend in the Cumberland River near the Hermitage, the home of Andrew Jackson (1767-1845). The artist painted this scene many times. Another version of this artwork is in the Greenville County Museum of Art.

CuratorialText: Painting, oil on canvas, of Charles Town showing the city waterfront as seen from across the Cooper River. A large sailing ship is in the left foreground, the opposite shore in foreground with various figures along the shore.
persona1_adapted: This painting shows the waterfront of Charles Town. The view is from across the Cooper River. A large sailing ship sits in the left foreground. On the opposite shore, you can see several people along the water's edge.

CuratorialText: Painting, oil on canvas, waist-length portrait of a lady wearing a gray satin dress with gray stomacher in front and white lace at neck and sleeves, her hair is pulled tightly back, a loop of pearls in it on the left side of the portrait. Her right hand holds a pink rose.
persona1_adapted: This is an oil painting on canvas. It shows a woman from the waist up. She wears a gray satin dress with a matching stomacher, a decorative panel, at the front. White lace trims her neck and sleeves. Her hair is pulled back tightly. On the left side of the portrait, a loop of pearls is woven into her hair. In her right hand, she holds a pink rose.

CuratorialText: Painting, oil on canvas, of a man facing half-left and seated in a green painted chair. The man has a high foreheard, dark hair, dark blue eyes, ruddy complexion, and is wearing a dark coat mounted with gray buttons, a white stock, and a yellow vest. A red and pearl pin is on his neck cloth. The painting is signed in the lower left corner.
persona1_adapted: This is an oil painting on canvas. It shows a man sitting in a green chair, facing slightly to the left. He has a high forehead, dark hair, and dark blue eyes. His skin is reddish. He wears a dark coat with gray buttons, a white neck cloth, and a yellow vest. A red and pearl pin is attached to his neck cloth. The artist signed the painting in the lower left corner.

CuratorialText: Painting, oil on canvas, of lady sitting half right, face to front. She wears a high dark brown hair style, has brown eyes, and wears a dark high gathered dress with puff short sleeves, a loose arrangement of net at a square cut neck line, a red paisley shawl over her left hand and a red cloth at her right hand in the lower left corner. The painting is signed in the lower left hand corner.
persona1_adapted: This is an oil painting on canvas. It shows a woman sitting slightly to the right, but facing forward. She has a tall, dark brown hairstyle and brown eyes. Her dress is dark, high-waisted, and gathered, with short puffy sleeves. A loose piece of netting sits at the square neckline. A red paisley shawl is draped over her left hand. A red cloth rests near her right hand in the lower left corner. The artist signed the painting in that same corner.

CuratorialText: Pastel portrait on paper of a young man. He sits in profile to the left, and he has light brown hair, a black coat, a white shirt and stock. The work was done on white paper. The side profile view of this portrait seems inspired by the work of British-born artist James Sharples (d. 1811), instead of the three-quarter views preferred by his son Felix.
persona1_adapted: This is a pastel portrait of a young man on paper. He sits facing to the left. He has light brown hair, a black coat, a white shirt, and a stock. The artist made this work on white paper. The side profile view looks like it was inspired by British-born artist James Sharples (died 1811). His son Felix preferred three-quarter views, but this portrait uses a different angle.

CuratorialText: A bust lenth portrait in crayon/ pastel on grey-pink paper of a young woman in a three-quarter pose to the left. She has brown eyes, brown hair in a bun held high on her head with a black comb, and wears a high organdy collar and white blouse. The portrait is marked on the back with the inscription “Miss Lucy Ann Bou….” and “H.E. H.”.
persona1_adapted: This is a bust-length portrait, meaning it shows the head and upper chest. It was made with crayon and pastel on gray-pink paper. The young woman in the picture is turned slightly to the left. She has brown eyes and brown hair. Her hair is in a bun held high on her head with a black comb. She wears a high organdy collar and a white blouse. On the back of the portrait, there is writing that says “Miss Lucy Ann Bou….” and “H.E. H.”

CuratorialText: Pastel on white paper, of young woman, three-quarter pose to the right. She has brown hair, brown eyes, and wears a pink dress of a low cut design. It is marked on the back with the sitter’s name, “Miss Susan Whiting.” In a lower corner, upside down to the sitter’s name is another inscription which notes that the portrait was done at “North End” on “November 21, 1814.” North End was home of artist Hester Eliza Henrietta (Van Bibber) Tabb, whose initials “H.E.H.V.B.” are also inscribed on the verso.
persona1_adapted: This pastel portrait on white paper shows a young woman facing slightly to the right. She has brown hair and brown eyes. She wears a pink dress with a low neckline. On the back of the artwork, the sitter’s name is written: “Miss Susan Whiting.” In one corner, upside down compared to the name, there is another note. It says the portrait was made at “North End” on “November 21, 1814.” North End was the home of the artist, Hester Eliza Henrietta (Van Bibber) Tabb. Her initials, “H.E.H.V.B.,” are also written on the back.
